###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXPERIENCED Fujitsu Powercobol Developer needed

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-12-01T00:28:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DE95AE9.3DD81A22@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<49973e5b.0211271222.24572ed1@posting.google.com> <as5oh4$err$1@panix1.panix.com> <m63duuchjfur0bh771euijqiuovlr5610f@4ax.com> <3DE691BB.70807@nycap.rr.com> <eSDF9.35959$hK4.3135833@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3DE7E1E0.8030601@nycap.rr.com> <7uUF9.30460$vM1.2371161@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <gT2G9.4047$Kw6.2500460@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <3DE9223B.7798AE1C@shaw.ca> <3DE9239C.9050209@nycap.rr.com>`

```


Robert Graham wrote:

> "HUNG" is an acceptable past form of "HANG", as is "HANGED",
> at least according to my dictionary [Webster's International
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> >

But - you are missing the point of Michael's comment. "He was HANGED, drawn and
quartered", and then, "His body parts were HUNG for display on the Tower of London
walls".

Judge Roy Bean had people "hanged" not "hung".

I guess that's one of the tortuous subtleties of the English language. I checked
my Concise Oxford, but it is so small I need binoculars to read it !. So I turned
to my wife's library, (she's a crossword nut) and she has the Webster's 'Third
College Edition', 1988. That says "hung - see hang". (Thank God it didn't include
"hang -  see  hung". <G> )

Under hang it includes 'hung' but says 'hanged is the preferred'. Plus being an
American dictionary it includes 'hang a left (or right)", where we would be
directing somebody to go "kiddy corner from here...". (First time my sister-in-law
heard that she was perplexed,. "What kitty corner ?).

If your Webster's is an 1894 Edition - no wonder you are in trouble <G>.

They may drop their 'aitches', but even Cockneys can get it right :-

'arry went to 'ampstead,
'arry lost 'is 'at
Mum said to 'arry,
"Where's your 'at ?",
'arrry said to Mum,
"'angin' on the 'anger in the 'all"

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0212011207.10189a75@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<49973e5b.0211271222.24572ed1@posting.google.com> <as5oh4$err$1@panix1.panix.com> <m63duuchjfur0bh771euijqiuovlr5610f@4ax.com> <3DE691BB.70807@nycap.rr.com> <eSDF9.35959$hK4.3135833@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3DE7E1E0.8030601@nycap.rr.com> <7uUF9.30460$vM1.2371161@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <gT2G9.4047$Kw6.2500460@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <_1kG9.38335$hK4.3390792@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
"Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote
 
> My personal preference is for the phrase I used, a sentiment
> which a Google search shows to be the majority opinion
> by a vote of 11,300 to 6,080.

According to 'majority opinion' on the internet, when I like
something, I should be spelling my opinion 'kewl'.

Also how did you reduce your search to just the reference to hanging
as a form of capital punishment or suicide ?  The only and correct use
of 'hanged' is when someone is killed using a rope, all other past
tense of 'hang' is 'hung'.

eg:

He was hanged and then his body was hung over the city gateway.

When someone is 'hung, drawn and quartered' they are not 'hanged' in
that the hanging is not to kill them, the quartering eventually does
that, but is just to raise them so that the public can see the fun.
```

> > > Simple answer - determine which compiler you are going to use to earn
your
> > > bread and butter and use their appropriate Screen Section features.
> > >
> >
> > I agree with you, but in my case I have to port quickly a bunch of
programs,
> > some of them written in cobol,
> > others in IDMS (ADS) to oracle + cobol.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Firstly, there's the learning curve to use DS and then the problem of
getting
> your screens into DS format. Additionally DS is designed to have 'self
contained
> code' to validate fields etc., and automatically also draws off OO GUI
classes
> for support where necessary, plus it also an event driven process, i.e.,
> Windows.
>
> There's nothing wrong with DS as a tool, but it just doesn't seem to fit
your
> criteria of a quick 'importing' tool.
>
> Your reference to IDMS (ADS) - any chance you can read from input source
and
> output line sequentials as dummy screen source ?. You would  still have to
do
> some editing of  the output to get your final result.
>
> I believe it was 'Gazloo' who offered some other suggestions - might be
worth
> asking Micro Focus support for ideas.
>
> Jimmy, Calgary AB
>
I found an old DOS based Cobol Screen builder program that is Shareware from
1991.  It's on a 5 � floppy that I got to try out but never used.  I was
trying to build a screen for a friends business but only got as far as the
text portion and not the in/out.

This first part is the screen design. 80 x 24.

                             ONTARIO TRUCK PARTS
                                SUPPLIER LIST

COMPANY NAME:
STREET ADDRESS:
MAILING STATION 1:
MAILING STATION 2:
CITY:
PROV/STATE:           POSTAL CODE:              COUNTRY:

CONTACT NAME:
LAST:                          FIRST:                       INIT:
           PHONE:              EXT:           FAX:

BACKUP NAME:
LAST:                          FIRST:                       INIT:
           PHONE:              EXT:           FAX:

----------------------------------------------------------------------------
---

  TYPE OF PARTS: CODE:
                 DESCRIPTION:

F1 - ADD    F2 - READ   F3 - NEXT RECORD    F4 - UPDATE    F5 - MAIN MENU



###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Looking for tool to create/generate screen-sections (screen painter?)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Donald Tees <Donald_Tees@Sympatico.ca>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<as5ho3$99c$1@gaudi2.rug.ac.be> <3DE66C6D.B8F6C9F7@shaw.ca> <as7ge9$t8b$1@gaudi2.rug.ac.be> <3DE7ABE6.3AD4E673@shaw.ca> <xCvG9.193705$oRV.177128@news04.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>`

```
I have source for one of those I wrote myself if you want a copy.  It 
might take me a day to find it in my library.
The last time I ran it, it was generating Fujitsu Version 4.0 code, and 
compiled in that language.  I thought of changing
it to write SP2 compable GUI code, but the event driven stuff  just did 
not lend itself to the approach.

If I was going to do it today, I would have it generate screen objects. 
 The better way would be to read
the original screen sections, and create a current "screen object" that 
ran in a DOS window.  That way
you could open as many of them as you wanted, and get a very "windowy" 
look. If the old screen sections
were generated, the code should be very easy to read, and something 
along that line could be hacked together
in a couple weeks of coding.

Donald

I am not quite sure what you mean.  The program started with a 
background screen that could be
read from a text file and allowed you to place overlays on top of it. 
 You could either edit that within
the screen development program, or dump it to a text file and edit it 
with and editor, then re-load
it to the screen file data base.  When desired you would press a key and 
it would write the screen
sections.

Donald

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Looking for tool to create/generate screen-sections (screen painter?)

- **From:** Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-02T17:17:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<205nuu83jq1i9no7chg2jkut7mj82osbcm@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<as5ho3$99c$1@gaudi2.rug.ac.be> <3DE66C6D.B8F6C9F7@shaw.ca> <as7ge9$t8b$1@gaudi2.rug.ac.be>`

```
Gunther:

1.  COBOL sp2 is 100% COBOL compiler independent.  We work with ALL
the COBOL compilers without change of screen handling source code or
screen definitions.

2.  COBOL sp2 allows you to create full GUI screens dynamically from
COBOL CALL statements so that you can create your own custom
conversion programs.

3.  COBOL sp2 also fully supports the ability to integrate COM (Active
X Objects) components into your COBOL application.

4.  COBOL sp2 is also tightly integrated with our Thin Client and Web
Client Internet/intranet deployment products, so you can run your
application in just about any environment (including LINUX and VAX
server platforms) you prefer.

The best part is that everything is accomplished using ANSI standard
COBOL CALL USING statements.  COBOL and nothing but COBOL.

Contact me if I can be of further assistance.


"Gï¿½nther De Vogelaere" <Gunther.DeVogelaere@killspammersrug.ac.be>
wrote:

>Thx for your reply

…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Bob Wolfe
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

```

Bill:

Do I use the term, "only" in the COBOL FAQ?  If so, I'll be happy to
change it.

I'm going to ask Tracey to check all of our web pages too so that any
incorrect statements can be eliminated.

I may be incorrect, but I'm not sure if we ever used the term, "only"
because Norcom also uses a CALL USING approach to GUI screen handling,
which certainly falls into the category of ANSI standard syntax.

If we have used it, then you are absolutely correct that we certainly
are NOT the only approach which uses ANSI standard syntax and we'll
make the change post haste.

<a id="t-b521af732f"></a>
## Thread: data sorting — 20 msgs

### data sorting

- **From:** "CG" <chhguan@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-01T17:02:04-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ase4de$37n$1@news.doit.wisc.edu>`

```
I am writing a  program that requires first to verify the data file, then to
sort the good ones.  I can do them in two programs, but I can not combine
them into one.How can I do?
Thank you.
minghui
```

#### ↳ Re: data sorting

- **From:** "Ron Schnatzmeyer" <NoSoy@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2002-12-01T20:46:03-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<asehhc$jdb$1@ngspool-d02.news.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<ase4de$37n$1@news.doit.wisc.edu>`

```
I suggest that you use an Input Procedure and an Output Procedure.
```

#### ↳ Re: data sorting

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-12-02T03:07:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DEACC8A.7ABA9D1A@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<ase4de$37n$1@news.doit.wisc.edu>`

```


CG wrote:

> I am writing a  program that requires first to verify the data file, then to
> sort the good ones.  I can do them in two programs, but I can not combine
> them into one.How can I do?
> Thank you.
> minghui

You are going to get replies, "Show us what you have done so far". Yes, you can
do it in one program using different Paragraphs for different operations.. Hint
- the lines below represent separate paragraphs  :-

1. Definitions :-
- define Infile (assuming you are not reading from a table)
- define OutFile (your 'good' records')
- define sort file

    (Hint - chances are all three files have the same format, unless your
instructor has
    told you otherwise)

2. Procedures :-
- open files
- read input records
- validate your incoming records to see if they are 'good' records
- write 'good' records to the OutFile
- what are you supposed to do with the bad records ?
- now close files
-  sort your 'good' OutFile.

-  I could give you the code for the sort in about 4 lines, but backtrack to the

    Sort example you instructor must have already given you

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: data sorting

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2002-12-02T07:47:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DEB0FFF.4087956@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<ase4de$37n$1@news.doit.wisc.edu> <3DEACC8A.7ABA9D1A@shaw.ca> <122041.AOROHRKU@news.doit.wisc.edu>`

```
CG wrote:
> 
> On Mon, 02 Dec 2002 03:07:13 GMT "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:
…[46 more quoted lines elided]…
> Minghui

What do you have to do to "verify" your records?

I would probably read every record during the INPUT procedure, and
RELEASE each verified record to the SORT.  Then in the OUTPUT
procedure I would RETURN every verified record and WRITE each one to
the output file.

Any reporting on the input file (both good and bad records) would be
done within the INPUT procedure.  Any reporting on the verified
records only would be done in the OUTPUT procedure.  

Perhaps I don't completely understand the assignment, but it should be
possible to do it in one program rather than two.  Does the input
(verified and unverified records) file need to be sorted?  It's also
possible to have more than one SORT statement in a program. 
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: data sorting

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-12-02T20:26:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<BlPG9.4469$Kw6.2918517@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<ase4de$37n$1@news.doit.wisc.edu> <3DEACC8A.7ABA9D1A@shaw.ca> <122041.AOROHRKU@news.doit.wisc.edu>`

```
Note that you can mix USING...GIVING with RELEASE..RETURN.. so you could
have something like..

SORT infile  ON ASCENDING KEY keyname
  INPUT PROCEDURE IS  procedure-name
  GIVING  outfile

Me, I'd just use INPUT and OUTPUT procedures with something like...

SORT infile ON ASCENDING KEY keyspecs
 INPUT PROCEDURE   IS input-Procedure-name
 OUTPUT PROCEDURE is output-procedure-name

INPUT-PROCEDURE-NAME.
   (get record from input, qualify for good-bad)
   IF Good-record
      RELEASE to sort
  ELSE
     WRITE BadFile-Record
  END-IF
 ...

OUTPUT-PROCEDURE-NAME

RETURN good-record
  you will get good records in sorted order here.. and you can do whatever
you want with them


This does not provide the bad-records in sorted order (left as an exercise),
but USING..GIVING looks like a good way to handle those.


MCM

#### ↳ Re: data sorting

- **From:** Robert Graham <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-02T11:39:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DEB4895.1080808@nycap.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<ase4de$37n$1@news.doit.wisc.edu>`

```
Sort the file first, next verify the data, then write out 
the good records.

SORT FILEA INPUT PROCEDURE CHECK-DATA
             OUTPUT PROCEDURE REWRITE-GOOD-DATA.

You should be able to take it from there.  The INPUT 
PROCEDURE is where you read in the file and check for the 
good data.  The OUTPUT PROCEDURE is where you write out the 
good records already sorted.

CG wrote:
> I am writing a  program that requires first to verify the data file, then to
> sort the good ones.  I can do them in two programs, but I can not combine
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

In article <ase4de$37n$1@news.doit.wisc.edu>, chhguan@yahoo.com wrote:

>I am writing a  program that requires first to verify the data file, then to
>sort the good ones.  I can do them in two programs, but I can not combine
>them into one.How can I do?

How about using a program with an internal SORT. The INPUT procedure
tests the records and the ones which pass the test are released. The
OUTPUT procedure then returns the records and writes them to a file 
containing the good records.
```

- **From:** ronglaz6@aol.comnojunk (Ron Glazier)

- **References:** `<SBycnbwvAOJnTJqjXTWcoA@comcast.com>`

```
run the first action and you of course will have to open files and close them
at the end after all validated.  the resultant created file with only those
that were found to be valid will be closed. (after reaching end of file = the
last record done).
   next, open the file again.  if you open it again it will reset itself to the
first record and you can now start but this time you are only working with the
validated records and at the first record (not at the end).
       Ron
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: data sorting

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-12-24T13:08:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2%YN9.3046$qU5.2273198@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<SBycnbwvAOJnTJqjXTWcoA@comcast.com> <20021224015002.09098.00000217@mb-ct.aol.com>`

```
"Ron Glazier" <ronglaz6@aol.comnojunk> wrote in message
news:20021224015002.09098.00000217@mb-ct.aol.com...
> I would do it in 2 programs instead of one as simpler.  But others would
prefer
> to combine into one program.

In the mainframe environment i've seen this a lot: smaller programs, many
job steps. My personal preference is for fewer job steps, which of course
means more complex programs.

When I first started working in mainframe environments (1994-95) I was
somewhat overwhelmed by JCL, so maybe that was a natural reaction, but even
as I got proficient with JCL, I never changed. I still like fewer job steps.

As I understand it, the main reason for the predominance of the 'many small
steps' method is to facilitate restart .. no sense restarting a multi-hour
batch job from the beginning if the first two hours' work were already
completed successfully. But between better hardware (fewer errors and faster
speeds) and better software (we learned new techniques to speed up
processing), this consideration recedes. Today the reason is likely twofold:
first, ease of program maintenance by contractors and others less familiar
with existing systems, and that old standby, inertia ("we've always done it
that way.").

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: data sorting

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-24T18:56:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<X52O9.77498$hK4.6341710@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<SBycnbwvAOJnTJqjXTWcoA@comcast.com> <20021224015002.09098.00000217@mb-ct.aol.com> <2%YN9.3046$qU5.2273198@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```

Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message news:2%YN9.3046$qU5.2273198@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com...
> "Ron Glazier" <ronglaz6@aol.comnojunk> wrote in message
> news:20021224015002.09098.00000217@mb-ct.aol.com...
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> that way.").

You are overlooking arguably the most important factor.  Job scheduling.

If you have one job with many steps which requires multiple databases
to be open in exclusive update mode, then such a job can play havoc
with the remainder of the production batch schedule, as there will normally be
many other jobs which could run concurrently with a better overall usage plan.

Many other batch jobs which could be run (if the behemoth were broken up)
have to sit in the queue until Godzilla completes successfully, especially if
earlier steps and later steps both demand exclusive access to widely used databases.

Another important factor is that smaller existing production jobs can be more easily
integrated into special runs, such as at month-end, fiscal year-end, calendar year-end
and also during conversion projects, when you might only wish to run certain steps.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: data sorting

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** jnjsle@aol.com (Jnjsle)
- **Date:** 2002-12-24T21:33:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20021224163319.29199.00000296@mb-cn.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<X52O9.77498$hK4.6341710@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
A small point, but I wouldn't sort first since the verification phase will
eliminate some recs, thereby reducing the sort burden.

BTW, they say another reason to consider multiple steps is the potential for
faster I/O when SORTing using sort, rather than COBOL I/O routines. From what
I've heard, but not verified, this performance gain is bought in spite of the
additional data pass required. 

Has anyone verified/debunked this assertion?

Regards, Jack.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: data sorting

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-12-27T15:41:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<auhsbo$rhs$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<SBycnbwvAOJnTJqjXTWcoA@comcast.com> <20021224015002.09098.00000217@mb-ct.aol.com> <2%YN9.3046$qU5.2273198@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```

On 24-Dec-2002, "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:

> As I understand it, the main reason for the predominance of the 'many small
> steps' method is to facilitate restart .. no sense restarting a multi-hour
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> that way.").

That is an advantage.  But the main advantage is that small, simple components
are easier to maintain than bigger, more complex components.  Look at which jobs
take the longest to maintain and enhance in your shop.   I bet it's the ones
with the big complex programs rather than similar jobs with more steps doing the
same stuff.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: data sorting

- **From:** ronglaz6@aol.comnojunk (Ron Glazier)
- **Date:** 2002-12-31T04:52:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20021230235202.06965.00000431@mb-cj.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<SBycnbwvAOJnTJqjXTWcoA@comcast.com>`

```
       identification division.
       program-id.    PSORT.
       Author.        Ron Glazier.
       Installation.  Selby.
       date-written.  10 Nov 1996.
       date-compiled.
       security.      non-classified.

      ***********************************************************
      *  This program produces sorted records
      *
      ***********************************************************

       environment division.
       input-output section.
           select account-file       assign to "psort.dat"
           organization is line sequential.

           select sort-file          assign to disk.

           select sorted-accounts    assign to "psort.rep"
           organization is line sequential.

       data division.
       file section.
       fd  account-file
           label records are standard
           data record is account-record.
       01  account-record.
           02 region-number                pic x(05).
           02 filler                       pic x(01).
           02 seller-number                pic x(05).
           02 filler                       pic x(01).
           02 account-number               pic x(05).
           02 filler                       pic x(01).
           02 sale-amount                  pic 9(05)v99.


       sd  sort-file.
       01  sort-work-record.
           02 sort-region-number           pic x(05).
           02 filler                       pic x(01).
           02 sort-seller-number           pic x(05).
           02 filler                       pic x(01).
           02 sort-account-number          pic x(05).
           02 filler                       pic x(01).
           02 filler                       pic 9(05)v99.


       fd  sorted-accounts
           label records are standard
           data record is sorted-account-record.
       01  sorted-account-record.
           02 sorted-region-number         pic x(05).
           02 filler                       pic x(01).
           02 sorted-seller-number         pic x(05).
           02 filler                       pic x(01).
           02 sorted-account-number        pic x(05).
           02 filler                       pic x(01).
           02 sorted-sale-amount           pic 9(05)v99.



      /
       procedure division.
       main-para.
           display " ".
           display " start of program ".

           sort sort-file
                on ascending key sort-region-number
                on ascending key sort-seller-number
                on ascending key sort-account-number
           with duplicates in order
           using account-file
           giving sorted-accounts.

           display " ".
           display " end of program ".

           stop run.




      ******************************************
      *                    end of program
      *******************************************




*********** I had some others around but cant find them at the moment.
```

---

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Java

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-12-03T00:15:18
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3debf92b_3@news.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<3DE28F46.8FB67DBB@shaw.ca> <as4ua0$ot8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <as5gj5$hmo$1@reader13.wxs.nl> <m3EF9.35964$hK4.3136620@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```

Hugh Candlin <no@spam.com> wrote in message
news:m3EF9.35964$hK4.3136620@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net...
>
> Danny Maijer <info@liveartists.com> wrote in message
news:as5gj5$hmo$1@reader13.wxs.nl...
>
> > In my opinion modern COBOL will survive all of us in here.....
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The flaw in this argument is that it assumes that things will continue as
they always have.

Indications are that they won't.

The Market Place is demanding new (and better/faster/cheaper) ways of
developing commercial computer systems. Procedural code simply won't hack it
into this century.

The trillions of lines which exist currently will be "mined" into
encapsulated functions, or will be rewritten with tools that can do the job
many times faster than the original hand coding took. The "conversion" of
existing code and systems is a "one time" thing. It will take thousands of
man years but in elapsed time, around 7 - 10 years. (If the Gartner figure
quoted elsewhere (1.7 million programmers...3 million just under a year
ago...) is even halfway accurate, there is plenty of scope and necessity to
move out of COBOL.)

Don't think for one moment that an existing investment of "trillions of
lines" will save COBOL. This, coupled with a declining rate of programmer
availability, could be the very thing to hasten its demise.


> When just one person - yours truly - has generated millions of lines,
> the actual global number is inestimable with even a modicum of accuracy.
>
> Anyone care to attempt to extrapolate a total?  I couldn't.
>
It doesn't matter...see above.

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Java

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Gazaloo" <gaz@a.loo>
- **Date:** 2002-12-03T01:29:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yNTG9.6459$pN3.488@sccrnsc03>`
- **References:** `<3DE28F46.8FB67DBB@shaw.ca> <as4ua0$ot8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <as5gj5$hmo$1@reader13.wxs.nl> <m3EF9.35964$hK4.3136620@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3debf92b_3@news.newsgroups.com>`

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:
> The flaw in this argument is that it assumes that things will continue as
> they always have.

The flaw in this argument is that it assumes that things will not continue
as they always have.

> Indications are that they won't.

Indications are that they will.

Apologies for being flippant; though you should consider apologizing too :)

I'm not actually on one side of this particular argument or t'other, but
I've heard this FUD for almost 20 years (I'm sure others here have heard it
twice as long as that). Nothing to say you ain't right; you may well be, but
prophecie's will always manifest themselves at some point in time. Sure,
things change, things evolve; but rarely do they become extinct in the 7-10
year timeframe you are quoting (how far back in time could I find a similar
quote from you? just kidding Pete :)

> The Market Place is demanding new (and better/faster/cheaper) ways of
> developing commercial computer systems. Procedural code simply won't hack
it
> into this century.
>
> The trillions of lines which exist currently will be "mined" into
> encapsulated functions, or will be rewritten with tools that can do the
job
> many times faster than the original hand coding took.

If the code has been "mined", then in my book it's still hacking it.

> The "conversion" of
> existing code and systems is a "one time" thing. It will take thousands of
> man years but in elapsed time, around 7 - 10 years. (If the Gartner figure
> quoted elsewhere (1.7 million programmers...3 million just under a year
> ago...) is even halfway accurate, there is plenty of scope and necessity
to
> move out of COBOL.)

Data snippets taken out of context unless you were referring to a report
that was specifically citing the 1.3m decline in COBOL programmers in 12
months? If not, seems a pretty desparate attempt to spin data to support an
opinion.

> Don't think for one moment that an existing investment of "trillions of
> lines" will save COBOL. This, coupled with a declining rate of programmer
> availability, could be the very thing to hasten its demise.

I don't see the connection between the 2 sentences. Giga's (IMO very
sensible) response to this FUD, is simply to recognize market supply &
demand. If there's demand for COBOL skills, then the market will supply.

Gazaloo.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Java

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Peter E. C. Dashwood)
- **Date:** 2002-12-03T03:23:36-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3638c46.0212030323.329226b7@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3DE28F46.8FB67DBB@shaw.ca> <as4ua0$ot8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <as5gj5$hmo$1@reader13.wxs.nl> <m3EF9.35964$hK4.3136620@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3debf92b_3@news.newsgroups.com> <yNTG9.6459$pN3.488@sccrnsc03>`

```
"Gazaloo" <gaz@a.loo> wrote in message news:<yNTG9.6459$pN3.488@sccrnsc03>...
> "Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:
> > The flaw in this argument is that it assumes that things will continue as
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Then we obviously are seeing different indications <G>

> Apologies for being flippant; though you should consider apologizing too :)

I have no problem with you being flippant and do not require an
apology. By the same token, I see nothing offensive in what I wrote
and have no idea what you are requesting me to apologize for...<G>
> 
> I'm not actually on one side of this particular argument or t'other, but
> I've heard this FUD for almost 20 years (I'm sure others here have heard it
> twice as long as that). Nothing to say you ain't right; you may well be, but
> prophecie's will always manifest themselves at some point in time. 

Er...I don't think so. Dire prophecies around the Year 2000 did not
manifest themselves at ANY point in time (that's just one
example...history is full of prophecies which did not eventuate).
Anyway,I was not attempting to prophesy, just extrapolate from what I
perceive now. (I agree it's a fine point...)

1. I talk to other managers in the Business.
2. I read widely on computer related subjects.
3. I try not to limit my vision to the technicalities, but to also
look at what is succeeding and failing in the Market Place and why. It
is also interesting to look at what the Market Place is demanding.

When I do this is see an inevitable decline in the use of procedural
languages to develop computer systems.

> Sure,
> things change, things evolve; but rarely do they become extinct in the 7-10
> year timeframe you are quoting (how far back in time could I find a similar
> quote from you? just kidding Pete :)

You could go back 30 years and you will find no similar quote from me.
I am not saying that COBOL will become extinct. I am saying it will
not occupy the place it has always occupied on centre stage for
commercial development. Within 10 years COBOL will not be used to
develop commercial computer systems. You can quote me on that. (By the
way, I will get no pleasure if I am proved right...) If you were to
look around with an unbiased viewpont, you'd see that it is almost
true NOW... How many Corporate COBOL developments do you know of
personally that are in progress right now? Maybe 3, 5, 10? Balance
that against the entire universe of development that is happening in
your area.

I'm not trying to bully you into agreeing with me, I just want you to
recognise that this is not "FUD" it is the new reality. The situation
now is different from what it has been at any other time in history.
The rate of change is higher than it has ever been in the history of
Mankind. That is why these events can (and in my opinion, will...)
occur within the timeframe I mentioned.

> 
> > The Market Place is demanding new (and better/faster/cheaper) ways of
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
I was referring to the current decline in COBOL programming. No spin
was intended (or required); the figures speak for themselves (assuming
they are accurate of course...). It goes further than that, though.
You only have to run a search on Jobserve and see how many COBOL jobs
are advertised. Then look at how many of those are for new
development...

> > Don't think for one moment that an existing investment of "trillions of
> > lines" will save COBOL. This, coupled with a declining rate of programmer
> > availability, could be the very thing to hasten its demise.
> 
> I don't see the connection between the 2 sentences.

Try looking at it from a management pont of view, instead of as a
COBOL programmer who feels under threat. You may then see the
connection. Could you, as a responsible Manager, recommend that the
Company put it's data assets into a technology which declined 50% in
the last year? You MIGHT, if there was no other option. The fact is
that the "other options" are becoming better and better and there are
more of them all the time.

> Giga's (IMO very
> sensible) response to this FUD, is simply to recognize market supply &
> demand. If there's demand for COBOL skills, then the market will supply.
> 

No argument from me on that. I agree 100%. Check out the demand for
COBOL skills in the next 7 - 10 years.

Finally, I am not adopting this position in a COBOL newsgroup because
I want to spread doom and despondency. I am hopeful that many bright
and talented young programmers will wake up and work on expanding
their skill sets. If my posts achieve that, I will consider the time
well spent. It isn't about winning arguments, it is about the survival
of people.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Java

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Gazaloo" <gaz@a.loo>
- **Date:** 2002-12-04T05:08:00+00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<Q4gH9.21325$pN3.1519@sccrnsc03>`
- **References:** `<3DE28F46.8FB67DBB@shaw.ca> <as4ua0$ot8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <as5gj5$hmo$1@reader13.wxs.nl> <m3EF9.35964$hK4.3136620@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3debf92b_3@news.newsgroups.com> <yNTG9.6459$pN3.488@sccrnsc03> <b3638c46.0212030323.329226b7@posting.google.com>`

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> Then we obviously are seeing different indications <G>

A recent analyst quote for you to consider:

"The COBOL development tools market has been sorely overlooked as a result
of the Software Industry's enthusiasm for 'newer' and 'cooler' technologies.
I have many clients that are still building COBOL applications, and who want
tools to help them embrace new business models, notably e-commerce, without
having to go through the costly and perilous process of rip and replace" --
Gary Barnett, research director at Ovum.

> I have no problem with you being flippant and do not require an
> apology.

Fair enough -- I'll check mark "Flippant" on the "Behavior" tab of your
contact details!

> By the same token, I see nothing offensive in what I wrote
> and have no idea what you are requesting me to apologize for...<G>

For being flippant. (Oops, did I do it again? Oops, doesn't matter! :)

> When I do this is see an inevitable decline in the use of procedural
> languages to develop computer systems.

Hmmm. You might be right. You might be wrong. I guess the problem is that I
can distill no compelling case to suggest one outcome over the other. I
can't even put any weight on whether or not the "procedural and/or OO" even
matters.

Descriptions for business processes do seem to be becoming more & more
abstract from processor instruction sets (not much OO here!). XML for
data -- procedural or OO? BPEL4WS
http://www-106.ibm.com/developerworks/library/ws-bpel/ -- procedural or OO?

> I am not saying that COBOL will become extinct. I am saying it will
> not occupy the place it has always occupied on centre stage for
> commercial development.

I don't think COBOL or any language has ever occupied center stage for
commercially developed software packages. Perhaps COBOL was the closest 1
particular language came to that mantle, but (reading your statement
literally) that time has passed (or more likely, never was). It's not a
prediction.

Within an enterprise (and I'm drawing a distinction from "commercial") and
in particular on the mainframe, then yes, COBOL is still massive and, dare I
say, dominant (perhaps "entrenched" is a better term). I see no compelling
driver that will see that position erode significantly in 10 years. Many
quoted Y2K as the "right time", but not even something that forced
organizations to "confront their brutal reality", made much of an impact
(and there's a case to be made to say it actually did the opposite -- the
process actually made the business processes encoded in COBOL logic more
valueable simply because they were made visible and understood better).

> Within 10 years COBOL will not be used to
> develop commercial computer systems. You can quote me on that. (By the
> way, I will get no pleasure if I am proved right...)

Depends how strict you want to be about the term "used". I wouldn't be at
all surprised if many of the to be developed commercial applications
continue to be made up of composite technologies (including COBOL); again,
just as many are today.

> If you were to
> look around with an unbiased viewpont, you'd see that it is almost
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> your area.

Pete, go talk to the large: SI's, vendors & corporates and come back and
answer your own question. There's not much point in me answering it because
you simply wouldn't believe me. Perhaps you & I are simply in different
circles. Either that or the rate of change you speak of has caught you
napping.

> Try looking at it from a management pont of view, instead of as a
> COBOL programmer who feels under threat.

Someone once told me that it's dangerous to assume because it makes an "ass"
out of "u" and "me".

I am not a COBOL programmer.

> You may then see the
> connection. Could you, as a responsible Manager, recommend that the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> more of them all the time.

The point is that none of these "other options" explicitly exclude COBOL (or
any technology) from participating (assuming that the vendors continue to
respond and enable those technologies). Applications are made from composite
technologies. They always have been. They always will be. Economics dictates
this. Adopting a policy of rewriting your applications every time you get a
new dose of "englightenment" does not bode well for survival (interesting
you mention this in your last para).

> Finally, I am not adopting this position in a COBOL newsgroup because
> I want to spread doom and despondency. I am hopeful that many bright
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> of people.

Hear hear.

Gazaloo.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Java

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Richard Riehle <richard@adaworks.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-13T19:44:59-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DFAA93B.B8487628@adaworks.com>`
- **References:** `<3DE28F46.8FB67DBB@shaw.ca> <as4ua0$ot8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <as5gj5$hmo$1@reader13.wxs.nl> <m3EF9.35964$hK4.3136620@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3debf92b_3@news.newsgroups.com> <yNTG9.6459$pN3.488@sccrnsc03> <b3638c46.0212030323.329226b7@posting.google.com> <Q4gH9.21325$pN3.1519@sccrnsc03> <asl4uh$g2t$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:

> The process used to displace CoBOL will be when an enterprise replaces its
> billing system with a third party billing system, or when it outsources billing,
> and only writes web pages.  Replacing CoBOL programs with JAVA programs will be
> rare indeed.

Not only rare, but downright irresponsible.   The same can be said of those
idiots who think they should replace COBOL with C++.   As of this moment,
the only two languages well-suited to replacing COBOL are COBOL and
Ada 95.  The reason Ada works is because someone in the Ada community
with a lot of experience with COBOL ramrodded a decimal data type
through during the last standardization and because Ada has a pre-defined
library with COBOL-like data management and editing capabilities.

I really like Smalltalk.  One might suggest that it is the most fun programming
language around.   Even so, we are doing this stuff for fun.  We are trying
to create robust and maintainable software.  I agree that old-time COBOL
(68 and 74) programs tended to evolve into a pile of dry rot held up by
flying buttresses.  However, contemporary COBOL is much improved.  Now
it is C++ code that evolves into dry rot the more it is subjected to maintenance
efforts.

Still, COBOL, as of the ANSI 85 standard, COBOL is an appropriate choice when
one needs a good tool for business data processing problems.

Richard Riehle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Java

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lgYG9.41110$hK4.3645037@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3DE28F46.8FB67DBB@shaw.ca> <as4ua0$ot8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <as5gj5$hmo$1@reader13.wxs.nl> <m3EF9.35964$hK4.3136620@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3debf92b_3@news.newsgroups.com>`

Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message news:3debf92b_3@news.newsgroups.com...
>
> Hugh Candlin <no@spam.com> wrote in message
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> they always have.

Every side of an argument has flaws, otherwise there would be no argument.
One person would simply present a litany of facts, and the rest of us would keel over -
dead of boredom.

> Indications are that they won't.

And other indications are that they will.  Such as the necessity of not placing
a company in harms way by undertaking a possibly ill-advised system conversion.
>
> The Market Place is demanding new (and better/faster/cheaper) ways of
> developing commercial computer systems. Procedural code simply won't hack it
> into this century.

Please!  Businesses make specific demands on other businesses all the time.
I just finished such a project.  If you've got a quicky-clicky development tool
which provides one-size-fits-all solutions, I'll be very surprised.
BTW  The last comment a CEO of a very large company made to me about SAP was
"There are only 2 problems with SAP.  It cost too much, and it doesn't work".

> The trillions of lines which exist currently will be "mined" into
> encapsulated functions, or will be rewritten with tools that can do the job
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
That existing investment cost a titanic amount of money.
Companies today are cutting costs every which way they can,
an indisputable fact to which the long line of uemployed can attest.
>
> > When just one person - yours truly - has generated millions of lines,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> It doesn't matter...see above.

It does matter.  It is of paramount importance.

However, punch card was of vital importance in its time, and was supplanted.
Eventually, you will probably, but not assuredly, be correct, but a viable, safe
and compelling, repeat compelling, COBOL replacement does not beckon brightly enough.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Java

Hugh Candlin wrote:
> 
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Much to my regret I must agree with Pete about the demise of 
Cobol.  In the Mid-Atlantic States of the US, where probably 
half of all COBOL code resides, there is no shortage of 
Cobol programmmers, but a great shortage of Cobol jobs. 
Many Cobol shops are migrating to the "newer/cheaper/faster" 
products and the Cobol positions are there to babysit the 
old system until it is put to sleep.

So, this Cobol programmer, with 20+ years of experience, has 
been out of work since March.  But I am not resting on my 
laurels, instead I am trying to pick up some new languages 
as fast as I can.  The problem there is that I have little 
or no experience in them, making me a junior programmer all 
over again (at junior pay). :(

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Java

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Peter E. C. Dashwood)
- **Date:** 2002-12-04T10:50:54-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3638c46.0212041050.115adcb1@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3DE28F46.8FB67DBB@shaw.ca> <as4ua0$ot8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <as5gj5$hmo$1@reader13.wxs.nl> <m3EF9.35964$hK4.3136620@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3debf92b_3@news.newsgroups.com> <lgYG9.41110$hK4.3645037@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3DECA63F.6030805@nycap.rr.com>`

```
Robert Graham <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com> wrote in message news:<3DECA63F.6030805@nycap.rr.com>...
> Hugh Candlin wrote:
> > 
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> over again (at junior pay). :(

Robert,

I was extremely sorry to see this. Hope something good comes along for
you soon.
(At least you are demonstrating the right attitude; it is only a
matter of time...)

I started thinking about the points in your last paragraph, and it
seems to me that, if there aren't already, there will be an increasing
number of COBOL people facing this dilemma. (Unless they take action
right away. The question is: "What action should I take?")
Here are my thoughts as to what might be useful in this situation (I
have written it impersonally, but maybe you can use some of it
yourself):

1. A COBOL guy with many years experience has been exposed to just
about every kind of system that the Business use computers for... If
not, certainly the core systems. Along the way he is VERY LIKELY to
have acquired Analysis skills which may well be better than some of
the people designated as "Business Analysts".

Maybe it is worth applying for some Analyst type jobs which may not
necessarily involve COBOL. I would try to get an interview based on
overall IT exposure and experience. Then discuss the Business
Applications you are experienced with, rather than the specific
programming.

As an employer, I would rather have an ex-COBOL programmer with say,
5+ years in Business, than a newly graduated BSc, as a Business
Analyst. (In practise, I would employ both or either, depending on how
the interview went, but my point is that COBOL people have
automatically acquired a lot of useful ancilliary knowledge about
Business Processes without even realising they have it. This knowledge
could be worth money.)

2. As I have said many times: "Expand your skill set."

If you are currently working as a COBOL programmer look to where the
Company is going. Should you press for a move to Analysis? Whether you
decide that analysis is for you or not, I strongly advise you to make
an effort to get to grips with "new technology".

The Web is not difficult and children learn HTML without difficulty.
(The trick is NOT to approach it as if it were a programming language
(which it arguably, isn't...) but rather as a form of advanced Word
Processing.)Once you have the basics and have placed a few pages onto
your own Web Site,you can graduate into Styles and XML and server side
programming.

Get a grounding in OO (whether you believe the people here who say it
isn't necessary, or not, can it hurt to have an understanding of it?).
I believe the most useful way to do this is by learning Java, but that
certainly isn't the ONLY way...

3. Have a look at Management as another option. Maybe some
extra-curricular study towards a Management Qualification, together
with decent in-house applications experience (maybe gained as a COBOL
programmer), and a couple of projects as a Team Leader or PM, could
all add up to a more worthwhile package than simply "COBOL
Programmer". This is a good one for those who are a bit older as well,
as Managers are expected to have maturity. (I am the exception to this
rule <G>...well, they expect it of me...but they're usually
disappointed <G>).

4. Do you have some specific niche knowledge that you can package and
sell? If you are the guy who maintains the Fixed Assets system, for
example, you might do better selling yourself as an Asset Register
wizard, than as a COBOL programmer. Alternatively, if you have been
responsible for maintaining a specific application that is not widely
used (and therefore unlikely to have software houses queuing up to
write it) you could maybe write a simple package and advertise it in a
trade magazine. (I know two people who made fortunes in this way and I
kick myself I didn't do it when I had the chance...)

Finally, coming to Robert's point about the new skill not being worth
much... This is, sadly, true. But you can package it with your COBOL
skills and the years of experience you've had, to make it much more
attractive.

Remember, the next job you get, starts with what you do in this one...

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Java

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-12-02T16:24:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<asg1h3$89c$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3DE28F46.8FB67DBB@shaw.ca> <as4ua0$ot8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <3DE65EB5.1594968E@shaw.ca>`

```

On 28-Nov-2002, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:

> Yes, components are the right way to go and no, COBOL need not die if people
> get
> up off their arses and positively participate in the evolvement of the
> language,
> coupled with a use of the power offered by OO.

Question - how much effort should the average CoBOL programmer spend to make
sure CoBOL survives?  Most CoBOL programmers seem more interested in making sure
they can adapt to the future, rather than adapting the future to them.   We have
our opinions about how the future SHOULD be, but these opinions aren't often
about what we think the future will be.   When we place our bets, which way do
we go?

(Forget fogies such as myself who can count on retirement as being an option -
we are talking about a longer term future than this).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Java

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-12-02T20:09:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DEBC113.76C968AD@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3DE28F46.8FB67DBB@shaw.ca> <as4ua0$ot8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <3DE65EB5.1594968E@shaw.ca> <asg1h3$89c$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```


Howard Brazee wrote:

> On 28-Nov-2002, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> we are talking about a longer term future than this).

Unfortunately you are not the only old fogey Howard <G>. I can only guess that the
majority of COBOL users fit into the two or three decade category - and we've seen
messages such as 'It won't bother me, I'll be retired'.

(BTW - the mythical Gartner figure of 3 million COBOL programmers - from Micro Focus
white papers it now reads 1.7 million COBOL programmers).

COBOL wont continue to exist just because some write complacently, "It's been around
forever and will be around after I'm gone". Really ? People in US having difficulty
finding COBOL jobs and Pete's reference to some 60 jobs for COBOL v 1,000 for Java
etc., at job sites in the UK. Plus as he has indicated, not just himself, but there
are other managers with a mindset , "COBOL has had it'.

So COBOL can survive because you can use tools like SP2 which take the angst out of
GUIs. And of course our friend Thane smiling smugly to himself <G>. Question for him
- as you've got GUIs covered with SP2 are you currently using or can you justify
using OO syntax ?

The SP2 solution, we know from the many recommendations, is a good one. Now you are
a young programmer versed in OO and the language du jour. Somebody suggests you
should learn and do it in COBOL. Immediate response, "Can I do all I need in COBOL
?". "Sure, it has its own built in file handling, and SQL pre-processors if you want
to handle DBs. You want GUIs - ahhh ! AcuCOBOL, Fujitsu and Micro Focus offer three
different flavours, but of course they aren't part of any COBOL standard. Then
again, you could always use SP2 or Screen IO".

Does some researching and thinks to himself, 'Hmmmm GUIs are not a standard part of
the language. OK so I can spend $3,??? on a COBOL compiler - Jeez !
Now I've a choice of using either Fujitsu or Micro Focus because they have OO,
( which is still not the TODAY standard for our 'current' compilers, even though
Thane says the COBOL 2002 is the 'current' standard)  Alternatively I can go SP2 for
an additional $?,???'.

Then he remembers he uses 'lists' in his language. Sure Fujitsu and Micro Focus have
their own versions of collections/dictionaries - but again not part of any standard.
Before Thane jumps in with "We (J4) are looking........" - question what sort of
target date for concluding this complex module ? I may have missed it, but I haven't
noted one yet. I hate the thought, but I suspect 'Business as usual'. (Note by the
way, that 'lists' are only a segment of the problem - they are part and parcel of a
set of Standard Class untilities, available in other OO languages).

Our young programmer concludes, 'Get out of here I'll stick with Java or C++'. And
not only him, but his bosses too !

It's worth reflecting that people for different reasons, (both inside and outside of
J4), over the past years have rejected/resisted any suggestion that GUIs, (and from
that flows the Internet connection),. should be part of a COBOL standard.  Like it
or not, lack of that GUI connection built directly into the language, as is the
case, I believe, with most other OO languages, will be the major death knell for
COBOL.
(Am I suggesting it should happen now ?. Too bloody late !).

Java - OK apart from Sun there's also the Microsoft version and HP's - although I
get the impression Sun's is the major player. You can use each of Acu, F/J or M/F
COBOL compilers complete with their respective whistles and bells extensions, (and
those extensions specifically are Screen Section, GUIs and OO)  - but they are NOT
PORTABLE when you do that.

>  When we place our bets, which way do go?

I would be thrilled to find out I was wrong - but don't put your money on COBOL.
With a few exceptions, and Bill. K. is one of them, I just see a lack of the passion
needed to make the language survive.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Java

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Gazaloo" <gaz@a.loo>
- **Date:** 2002-12-02T23:09:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4LRG9.211775$QZ.32755@sccrnsc02>`
- **References:** `<3DE28F46.8FB67DBB@shaw.ca> <as4ua0$ot8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <3DE65EB5.1594968E@shaw.ca> <asg1h3$89c$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3DEBC113.76C968AD@shaw.ca>`

```
"James J. Gavan" wrote:
> It's worth reflecting that people for different reasons, (both inside and
outside of
> J4), over the past years have rejected/resisted any suggestion that GUIs,
(and from
> that flows the Internet connection),. should be part of a COBOL standard.
Like it
> or not, lack of that GUI connection built directly into the language, as
is the
> case, I believe, with most other OO languages, will be the major death
knell for
> COBOL.
> (Am I suggesting it should happen now ?. Too bloody late !).

Jimmy, is it not a fair statement to make that Browsers are the defacto UI
platform? Even if that's not reflective of the reality today in terms of
actual application code, this direction seems to be very much the way to go
(at least that's how my sensors have distilled the "noise" here).

That being the case (?) --

    exec html
        <b>The value of COBOL data item named FRED is :FRED</b>
    end-exec

-- is an example where GUI has been built directly into the COBOL language.
I'm well aware that the potential "richness" of a Browser UI vs. a "native"
one is not much of a competition, but for most, the benefits seem to
outweigh such constraints.

> Java - OK apart from Sun there's also the Microsoft version and HP's -
although I
> get the impression Sun's is the major player. You can use each of Acu, F/J
or M/F
> COBOL compilers complete with their respective whistles and bells
extensions, (and
> those extensions specifically are Screen Section, GUIs and OO)  - but they
are NOT
> PORTABLE when you do that.

I think both Acu and Micro Focus have a reasonable position to take on the
portability issue. Both vendors have long been committed to "cross-platform"
support for their feature sets. Infact, the code snippet above (under Micro
Focus) not only runs on multiple platforms, but also in multiple different
ways -- CGI, NSAPI and/or ISAPI -- all without any modifications being
necessary to the code (can Java do this too?).

I do find the COBOL standards process a disappointing one. COBOL language
innovations (SQL, multi-threading, XML, Web services) come from the vendors
in response to needs. The standard seems somewhat disconnected from needs
and is getting further & further behind as time marches on.

Should the COBOL vendors take the lead here and define the next standard?

Gazaloo.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Java

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-12-03T13:26:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<di2H9.4725$Kw6.3049116@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<3DE28F46.8FB67DBB@shaw.ca> <as4ua0$ot8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <3DE65EB5.1594968E@shaw.ca> <asg1h3$89c$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3DEBC113.76C968AD@shaw.ca> <4LRG9.211775$QZ.32755@sccrnsc02>`

```
"Gazaloo" <gaz@a.loo> wrote in message
news:4LRG9.211775$QZ.32755@sccrnsc02...
> Jimmy, is it not a fair statement to make that Browsers are the defacto UI
> platform? Even if that's not reflective of the reality today in terms of
> actual application code, this direction seems to be very much the way to
go
> (at least that's how my sensors have distilled the "noise" here).

From what I have seen, the browser interface is quite popular, especially
among firms with one or more networks/intranets installed. Perhaps the
reason is the availability of software development tools which make the
deployment  of a browser-based solution a pretty straightforward thing. The
tools are in turn fueled by Microsoft's commitment to them; and one cannot
dismiss the power of the combination of MS's cash plus its willingness to
spend it.

Or maybe it's because basic training time is next to nil for entry-level
employees, most have whom have never known a world *without* "the browser."

Not to say it's a universal fit, but all in all the browser-based solution
is now at least worth a look for any distributed application.

My $0.02

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Java

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-03T15:04:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<m7gpuu0odh0oa2bcejtad0cfgbb74qn0n8@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3DE28F46.8FB67DBB@shaw.ca> <as4ua0$ot8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <3DE65EB5.1594968E@shaw.ca> <asg1h3$89c$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3DEBC113.76C968AD@shaw.ca>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:

>Howard Brazee wrote:
>
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
>using OO syntax ?

Jimmy...check with Don Tees.  He uses sp2 in conjunction with OO COBOL
programming.

>The SP2 solution, we know from the many recommendations, is a good one. Now you are
>a young programmer versed in OO and the language du jour. Somebody suggests you
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>an additional $?,???'.

Nobody disputes the higher expense, but in many cases the COBOL
compilers and affiliated tools help the programmer create programs
much faster than they may have been able to using a mixed language or
some other approach....or even a different language.

In these cases, the programmer is simply making a business decision
(which is a *good* thing for any programmer to do).  The decision is
something like:

If I can bill my time out at $75 to $100 per hour (or my time costs
the company $50 to $75 per hour...or more) and these compilers and
tools help me be 5% more productive, then I am making/saving between
$5,000 and $10,000 per year because I choose to use compilers and
tools with which I am comfortable and familiar.

We won't add the additional factors of language stability and
maintainability.  These factors would likely increase the savings/make
more money.

>Then he remembers he uses 'lists' in his language. Sure Fujitsu and Micro Focus have
>their own versions of collections/dictionaries - but again not part of any standard.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>set of Standard Class untilities, available in other OO languages).

I assume that it is possible to license a COM object which provides
this type of function.  Some compilers provide built-in support for
these objects.  So does sp2....so you can accomplish this with any
COBOL compiler.

>Our young programmer concludes, 'Get out of here I'll stick with Java or C++'. And
>not only him, but his bosses too !

Yes, but only if they have not performed due diligence on the
compilers and tools available.

Or if they are hell-bent on scrapping COBOL, because the sum total of
their research was an article written by a journalist who writes for
PC-Weenie magazine and advises against the use of evil COBOL.....yet
the sum total of the author's programming experience was a Quick Basic
program at Wassamatta U.

(with apologies for the horrific run-on sentence and my jaded
sarcasm!)

>It's worth reflecting that people for different reasons, (both inside and outside of
>J4), over the past years have rejected/resisted any suggestion that GUIs, (and from
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>COBOL.

I have no problem with a GUI standard syntax in COBOL, because we
would simply modify our code generation template to generate the
proper syntax.  I think that the issue is in the implementation.
Despite the fact that all GUIs are similar (OS/2, Windows 16 bit,
Windows 32 bit, Motif, Open Look, Mac, KDE, Gnome, etc.) there are
certainly major difference bewteen the X11 stuff, the Windows stuff
and the Mac stuff.  Based upon our 12 years of GUI support, we have
seen things change radically.  I think that perhaps the reluctance is
in the fact that a standard would be created for a rapidly moving
target, thereby rendering the standard useless in short order.

>(Am I suggesting it should happen now ?. Too bloody late !).

I don't think that it's too late.  Pundits back in 1980 were saying
that it's too late for COBOL in general.  I think that they may have
been incorrect.

>Java - OK apart from Sun there's also the Microsoft version and HP's - although I
>get the impression Sun's is the major player. You can use each of Acu, F/J or M/F
>COBOL compilers complete with their respective whistles and bells extensions, (and
>those extensions specifically are Screen Section, GUIs and OO)  - but they are NOT
>PORTABLE when you do that.

Agreed 100%.  Portability should be a major consideration.  We speak
with folks each and every day who lament their choice of a
compiler-specific interface because they need to change
compilers...for whatever reason...and have a major re-write task.

>>  When we place our bets, which way do go?

I know which way I would go....but then , again, I am certainly
biased!!

;-)

>I would be thrilled to find out I was wrong - but don't put your money on COBOL.
>With a few exceptions, and Bill. K. is one of them, I just see a lack of the passion
>needed to make the language survive.

I'm not sure that passion is necessary.  In fact, I think that it can
be detrimental.  I would think that passion for a language can blur
practicality.

Language choice should be primarily based upon business criteria, not
emotion.  When a thorough analysis of the pros and the cons of a
language choice is performed, the one which benefits the business over
the long run should be chosen.  Sometimes that will be COBOL and
sometimes it will not be COBOL.

But basing the choice on how passionate the programming staff is over
VB, Java, C++ or COBOL should only be one factor in the overall
analysis.



Bob Wolfe
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Java

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-12-03T16:02:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<asikj2$s0q$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3DE28F46.8FB67DBB@shaw.ca> <as4ua0$ot8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <3DE65EB5.1594968E@shaw.ca> <asg1h3$89c$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3DEBC113.76C968AD@shaw.ca> <m7gpuu0odh0oa2bcejtad0cfgbb74qn0n8@4ax.com>`

```

On  3-Dec-2002, Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com> wrote:

> Nobody disputes the higher expense, but in many cases the COBOL
> compilers and affiliated tools help the programmer create programs
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> tools with which I am comfortable and familiar.

Which is fine for us old-timers.  Newcomers are not comfortable and familiar
with these tools.

> We won't add the additional factors of language stability and
> maintainability.  These factors would likely increase the savings/make
> more money

These factors matter if the contractor is skilled in both tools.   But entry
level programmers has different cost/benefit analyses.  "Which tool shall I
learn to use"?     "I will learn to use the tool that I can afford."    Of
course this gets modified by "I will learn to use the tool for which I can find
jobs", but that modification is not in CoBOL's favor.

If the tool is expensive, few individuals will choose it without overriding
reasons.   Corporations have different values of "expensive", but one of their
values is how expensive it is to find and retain skilled programmers.
```

happens to be COBOL.

It is becoming more and more necessary for people in this situation to
expand their skill sets.

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Java

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-04T14:02:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f12suugbgfojsddc8oaajojju2h8b5pmoj@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3DE28F46.8FB67DBB@shaw.ca> <as4ua0$ot8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <3DE65EB5.1594968E@shaw.ca> <asg1h3$89c$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3DEBC113.76C968AD@shaw.ca> <m7gpuu0odh0oa2bcejtad0cfgbb74qn0n8@4ax.com> <asikj2$s0q$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote:

>
>On  3-Dec-2002, Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com> wrote:
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>with these tools.

Howard:

You are absolutely correct, depending upon the tool.  Those tools
which use COBOL or COBOL-like syntax to display the GUI are more
difficult for newcomers with no COBOL experience or training.  But
those newcomers who have had some training in COBOL generally adapt to
the COBOL-oriented tools in reasonable fashion.

>> We won't add the additional factors of language stability and
>> maintainability.  These factors would likely increase the savings/make
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>jobs", but that modification is not in CoBOL's favor.

Entry level programmers generally don't make the decision on which
tool to use.  The employer usually makes those decisions.  But you are
100% correct that the employer should take into consideration the
cost/benefit of using each and every tool based upon the skills and
capabilities of the entire programming group.

>If the tool is expensive, few individuals will choose it without overriding
>reasons.   Corporations have different values of "expensive", but one of their
>values is how expensive it is to find and retain skilled programmers.

You're right......few individuals will spend thousands of dollars on a
programming tool, unless it provides them with a virtual guaranteed
return on the investment.  I also agree that corporations absolutely
should include the cost to hire and retrain programmers who may need
to be re-trained.  But that's not a COBOL-only factor.  That is a
factor which involves each and every programming language considered
for a new project.



Bob Wolfe
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Ok Mr. Troll, I'll pay the toll... payment below..

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message news:<3DEBC113.76C968AD@shaw.ca>...
> 
> Unfortunately you are not the only old fogey Howard <G>. I can only guess that the
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Fortunately many of us live in the real world as opposed to the
microcosm of discontent and rabble rousing rempresented in this
partucular forum of eclectic individuals.  I've worked regularly with
COBOL programmers of < 10 years experience.  Many of them coming to
COBOL from other languages.  The demand for Java may seem high, but
the pay is so low as to "get what they pay for".  It takes a ton of
Java programmers with that level of talent to do what the 1 in 1000
exceptional programmers can do.  ME - I'm a COMPUTER programmer.  The
numbers cited above might as well be pulled from the proverbial air. 
No one really knows.  Read on Mc Troll.

> COBOL wont continue to exist just because some write complacently, "It's been around
> forever and will be around after I'm gone". Really ? People in US having difficulty
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

And me thinks you are behind the pendulum.... I've seen the harsh pen
of reality swing back in COBOL's corner - even to the point where
those saying "Isn't COBOL Dead" whispering - thank God it's not!
 
> So COBOL can survive because you can use tools like SP2 which take the angst out of
> GUIs. And of course our friend Thane smiling smugly to himself <G>. Question for him
> - as you've got GUIs covered with SP2 are you currently using or can you justify
> using OO syntax ?
> 

You asked - I pay the toll...  It's all a matter of economics.  I've
not found it economically feasable to undertake a ground up OO
approach.  I dearly love OO - I find it a fantastic method of
development and for NEW development and added functionality it's
stellar - it's grand and it's the holy grail of new development. 
Using it for black box components is the way to go.  Having, however,
had the opportinity to utilize and maintain other persons and
companies OO code - written in other languages, I find that there is a
reason for the "redevelop" rather than "maintain" mentality that seems
to pervade these areas.... the stuff is hardly maintainable.  Once one
understands what the current set of code does, one has forgotten the
question.  I dearly hope that OO COBOL is more maintainable.... and
I'm sure you will twist my words into a comdenation of OO COBOL -- do
not, that's not what I'm saying.  I have high hopes because of the
lack of necessity for low level class libraries that OO COBOL is
maintainable.  I think that currently the only way these "others" will
be maintainable is when UML and UML tools are mature enough to do all
the source code generation -- and the programmer won't have to
understand it -- the UML (Or whatever is better that superceded UML)
tools will generate what you need for you using whatever language you
(or it) desires.  Is that the death of COBOL - hardly.  Its it new
life?  New life for what?  COBOL's not dead, it's not dying ----it's
NOT EVEN RESTING.

> The SP2 solution, we know from the many recommendations, is a good one. Now you are
> a young programmer versed in OO and the language du jour. Somebody suggests you
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Surely you jest?  Have you not seen the light under yon bridge young
troll?  There is a great drive to divorce the UI from the process. 
Web Services.  Client server all over again.  Stand alone process
servers.  Nothing new, and everthing new.... These have no UI.  They
have an API And you stick whatever UI on them you feel is appropriate
---does that mean that the language on back end that is processing the
web service is dying?

> Does some researching and thinks to himself, 'Hmmmm GUIs are not a standard part of
> the language. OK so I can spend $3,??? on a COBOL compiler - Jeez !
> Now I've a choice of using either Fujitsu or Micro Focus because they have OO,
> ( which is still not the TODAY standard for our 'current' compilers, even though

Show me ONE standardized programming language where the GUI is part of
the standard.  Just one.  SWING in Java is an add on Java library. 
It's not part of the language.  C?  C++?  No Gui there.  SmallTalk? 
Anything... IT DOES NOT BELONG.  Not in ANY of them.  There's no built
in GUI in C++.  Whichever C++ compiler you use you either use a third
party library, API calls, or whatever kludge or tool the vendor has
built into it.  All of them are different.  Borland C++ GUI is
incompatible with Microsoft....

> Thane says the COBOL 2002 is the 'current' standard)  Alternatively I can go SP2 for
> an additional $?,???'.
> 

If you want to play - you gotta pay.

> Then he remembers he uses 'lists' in his language. Sure Fujitsu and Micro Focus have
> their own versions of collections/dictionaries - but again not part of any standard.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> set of Standard Class untilities, available in other OO languages).

You take your lack of knowledge, spew it as FACT and twist mis and
disinformation into something of which you know not.  Yes, J4 is
working on a standardized OO Collection class library.  "Business as
usual" may not be bad - it may not be good or it may not be.  I tire,
continuously of your bashing for no productive reason.  You want it
faster --- help in the development work, the actual physical technical
work.  If you are not willing to help in this detail level, then you
have no place in the criticism pool.  I acknowledge that YOU have
submitted high level papers - that were seriously considered in
determining the direction.  Now it's time to do the nitty gritty work.

> 
> Our young programmer concludes, 'Get out of here I'll stick with Java or C++'. And
> not only him, but his bosses too !
> 

I've seen many a C++, Visual Basic (less Java) project fail.  I've
never witnessed such a COBOL Failure.  That's just my experience. 
Other toll payers may have a different expereience.

> It's worth reflecting that people for different reasons, (both inside and outside of
> J4), over the past years have rejected/resisted any suggestion that GUIs, (and from
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Two issues here - somehow you think other LANGUAGES have GUI built in.
 Tools for other languages certainly do - as do the tools for COBOL -
but certainly the languages THEMSELVES do not.
 
> Java - OK apart from Sun there's also the Microsoft version and HP's - although I
> get the impression Sun's is the major player. You can use each of Acu, F/J or M/F
> COBOL compilers complete with their respective whistles and bells extensions, (and
> those extensions specifically are Screen Section, GUIs and OO)  - but they are NOT
> PORTABLE when you do that.

Nor is C++ or any of the others.  COBOL CAN BE.  I have COMMON COBOL
source code running on MicroFocus, DEC/VMS, IBM Visual Age COBOL,
AcuCOBOL, Fujitsu, Realia, RM, ICOBOL, PerCOBOL - all doing GUI, all
with the SAME EXACT CODE, on NUMEROUS platforms.... And somehow -
magically, the lack of a standardized or commonly implemented GUI has
been no hinderance at all.....

> 
> >  When we place our bets, which way do go?
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

It's not passion that will "save" COBOL.  COBOL doesn't need your
help.  COBOL isn't even in trouble.  You are looking in the bottom of
the ocean at what is reflecting on the top of the water because it is
in flight over your head.  I hear under the bridge is warm this time
of year.

Shoot - just looked at my watch -- I could have made > $200.00 coding
COBOL instead of taking the time for this stupid reply...

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Java

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Gazaloo" <gaz@a.loo>
- **Date:** 2002-12-04T04:53:41+00:00

Entertaining response, Thane.

Some quick comments, below...

Thane Hubbell <thaneh@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:bfdfc3e8.0212032014.7e163d95@posting.google.com...

Hey! Steady on... you're not the only person here that lives in the "real

thing...it makes us re-examine stuff.

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

computer programmer. I have always adopted that approach throughout my
career. It requires a "Best tools for the job" and an open mind, to achieve
it, though.

> > COBOL wont continue to exist just because some write complacently, "It's
been around
> > forever and will be around after I'm gone". Really ? People in US having
difficulty
> > finding COBOL jobs and Pete's reference to some 60 jobs for COBOL v
1,000 for Java

himself, but there
