> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
 I don't believe "COBOL has had it". I wrote some yesterday <G>... I DO
believe it is in decline as a viable commercial solutions language. (I've
cited my reasons for this here and elsewhere for some time now. I also
accept that I could be wrong.) I have to say that the days when COBOL was
King (or any source based procedural language for that matter) are gone, and
I don't believe any swing of the pendulum is likely to change that. This is
one of the very few times when I would LOVE to be wrong. However, I can't
ignore the evidence.

> > So COBOL can survive because you can use tools like SP2 which take the
angst out of
> > GUIs. And of course our friend Thane smiling smugly to himself <G>.
Question for him
> > - as you've got GUIs covered with SP2 are you currently using or can you
justify
> > using OO syntax ?
> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Using it for black box components is the way to go.

Absolutely. Agree 100%.

>  Having, however,
> had the opportinity to utilize and maintain other persons and
> companies OO code - written in other languages, I find that there is a
> reason for the "redevelop" rather than "maintain" mentality that seems
> to pervade these areas.... the stuff is hardly maintainable.

Agree again. However, this is being recognised now and standards and
controls are emerging to address it. It was the same with COBOL in the
mid/late sixties. There were no standards or even "best practice" we wrote
stuff and it did what we wanted. We learned the hard way that if you wanted
to maintain it, it was a good idea to implement some standards...

> Once one
> understands what the current set of code does, one has forgotten the
> question.

Hahaha! This is SO true..!! I had to maintain one of my early C attempts
about 6 onths after I wrote it and had EXACTLY this problem. I had used all
sorts of "powerful" stuff (just because I was trying to get to grips with it
and, besides, it's fun, right?) then forgotten most of what I'd used and had
to start over 6 months later. I believe that if one was working in that
environment every day, it would be less of a problem, but I still see C as a
"write only" language...<G>

>  I dearly hope that OO COBOL is more maintainable.... and
> I'm sure you will twist my words into a comdenation of OO COBOL -- do
> not, that's not what I'm saying.

My indications are as follows (these are all purely subjective, and I
wouldn't pretend that EVERYBODY will have the same experience):

1. OO COBOL is easier to maintain than  C++.
2. OO COBOL is easier to mantain than Java (in some circumstances)
3. OO COBOL takes longer to write than either of the above.
4. OO COBOL does not have the Class Library support that the other 2 do. (I
don't see this as a problem. These days I use whatever Classes I need from
whatever languages and simply package the function as a component.)

It should also be noted that I use COBOL almost exclusively for building
components and the occasional CGI/ISAPI server side code. I no longer
develop large applications in COBOL. (It takes too long.) Commercially in
the place where I work, COBOL is simply not an option.


> I have high hopes because of the
> lack of necessity for low level class libraries that OO COBOL is
> maintainable.

yes, I would endorse that...see above.

> I think that currently the only way these "others" will
> be maintainable is when UML and UML tools are mature enough to do all
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> > The SP2 solution, we know from the many recommendations, is a good one.
Now you are
> > a young programmer versed in OO and the language du jour. Somebody
suggests you
> > should learn and do it in COBOL. Immediate response, "Can I do all I
need in COBOL
> > ?". "Sure, it has its own built in file handling, and SQL pre-processors
if you want
> > to handle DBs. You want GUIs - ahhh ! AcuCOBOL, Fujitsu and Micro Focus
offer three
> > different flavours, but of course they aren't part of any COBOL
standard. Then
> > again, you could always use SP2 or Screen IO".
> >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Web Services.

Yes, there is. And personally, I think it is good. It comes down to the 3
tier model and the use of the presentation layer. We use the Web as the
standard presentation layer for all of our commercial systems. When "new
world" arrives in the form of Siebel, I'll be interested to see whether this
concept is carried over.

> Client server all over again.  Stand alone process
> servers.  Nothing new, and everthing new.... These have no UI.  They
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

standard part of

have OO,

even though
>

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Agree with the above.

> > Thane says the COBOL 2002 is the 'current' standard)  Alternatively I
can go SP2 for
> > an additional $?,???'.
> >
>
> If you want to play - you gotta pay.
>

Fair enough...but if the amount you are required to pay is more than on the
other funfair, you may well vote with your feet...

> > Then he remembers he uses 'lists' in his language. Sure Fujitsu and
Micro Focus have
> > their own versions of collections/dictionaries - but again not part of
any standard.
> > Before Thane jumps in with "We (J4) are looking........" - question what
sort of
> > target date for concluding this complex module ? I may have missed it,
but I haven't
> > noted one yet. I hate the thought, but I suspect 'Business as usual'.
(Note by the
> > way, that 'lists' are only a segment of the problem - they are part and
parcel of a
> > set of Standard Class untilities, available in other OO languages).
>
> You take your lack of knowledge, spew it as FACT and twist mis and
> disinformation into something of which you know not.

(I wish I'd said that <G>. However, I was much gentler in my response to
Jmmy's last outburst <G>)

> Yes, J4 is
> working on a standardized OO Collection class library.  "Business as
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
It is fair to say "put up or shut up" Thane, but this is a bit OTT... Maybe
the whole thing is getting to you? I haven't seen "continual bashing" from
Jimmy. I probably did more of this than he did but I have stopped. (Stopped
a long time ago when I realised it would achieve nothing.) My opinion of the
Standards body hasn't changed, but I accept that there are at least some
people on it who are doing their best. It is also my opinion that they are
now an irrelevance anyway. My faith is in the vendors, not the Standards
committee. (We can agree to disagree on that one <G>)
> >
> > Our young programmer concludes, 'Get out of here I'll stick with Java or
C++'. And
> > not only him, but his bosses too !
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

It depends what you mean by "failure"... Things are usually salvaged to at
pleast some extent. I can't honestly say I have never seen a COBOL project
that failed to deliver what it promised. I think this has a lot to do with
the Project Management than with the Language used. I hate the Waterfall and
beieve it has consistently failed to deliver. I love RAD and use it every
chance I get. It is currently delivering excellent results on the site where
I work, and COBOL is not involved. (Other people will have exactly opposite
experience. The reasons are very complex. I am thinking about this in the
context of a forthcoming book.)


> > It's worth reflecting that people for different reasons, (both inside
and outside of
> > J4), over the past years have rejected/resisted any suggestion that
GUIs, (and from
> > that flows the Internet connection),. should be part of a COBOL
standard.  Like it
> > or not, lack of that GUI connection built directly into the language, as
is the
> > case, I believe, with most other OO languages, will be the major death
knell for
> > COBOL.
> > (Am I suggesting it should happen now ?. Too bloody late !).
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Java - OK apart from Sun there's also the Microsoft version and HP's -
although I
> > get the impression Sun's is the major player. You can use each of Acu,
F/J or M/F
> > COBOL compilers complete with their respective whistles and bells
extensions, (and
> > those extensions specifically are Screen Section, GUIs and OO)  - but
they are NOT
> > PORTABLE when you do that.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Agree with what you said, but... you can make ANYTHING platform independent
in ANY language if you stick with the component approach. The problem Jimmy
has with Class compatibility is because he is using GUI Classes, rather than
GUI components. The components are "pluggable" because they are packaged to
be that way; the Classes are not.

> >
> > >  When we place our bets, which way do go?
> >
> > I would be thrilled to find out I was wrong - but don't put your money
on COBOL.
> > With a few exceptions, and Bill. K. is one of them, I just see a lack of
the passion
> > needed to make the language survive.
> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Agree that the degree of passion is (almost) irrelevant, but I can't agree
that COBOL is not in trouble. That smacks of denial to me, Thane. All the
wishing and myopia in the World won't change the facts. The World has moved
on (and is doing so ever more rapidly). Scoffing at the argument because
you've heard it all before is also dangerous. The situation now is different
from what it has ever been before. The future is offering undreamed of
computer power on our desks within the next 4 years. The general level of
computer literacy is rising exponentially. The Market Place is expanding and
demanding much more than ever before. Given a demand, virtually unlimited
power, and the availability of more and more bright young minds unfettered
by history, it would seem highly unlikely that we will continue to develop
software they way we did for the latter part of the 20th century.

I believe we need to meet the reality and ensure that the COBOL people of
today are equipped for it.

That means expanding the skill sets, not just saying "Everything in COBOL is
all right...nothing to see over here, people..." like the cop in South
Park...

> Shoot - just looked at my watch -- I could have made > $200.00 coding
> COBOL instead of taking the time for this stupid reply...

Well, if you'd written it at work, you would've <G>

(This response was NOT written at work and I am very happy to donate my time
(which is at least as valuable as yours <G>) to the general welfare of the
COBOL community. <G>)

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Java

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Gazaloo" <gaz@a.loo>
- **Date:** 2002-12-05T02:08:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VyyH9.237637$WL3.81344@rwcrnsc54>`
- **References:** `<3DE28F46.8FB67DBB@shaw.ca> <as4ua0$ot8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <3DE65EB5.1594968E@shaw.ca> <asg1h3$89c$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3DEBC113.76C968AD@shaw.ca> <bfdfc3e8.0212032014.7e163d95@posting.google.com> <3dee0a43_1@news.newsgroups.com>`

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:
>  I don't believe "COBOL has had it". I wrote some yesterday <G>... I DO
> believe it is in decline as a viable commercial solutions language. (I've
> cited my reasons for this here and elsewhere for some time now. I also
> accept that I could be wrong.) I have to say that the days when COBOL was
> King (or any source based procedural language for that matter) are gone,
and
> I don't believe any swing of the pendulum is likely to change that. This
is
> one of the very few times when I would LOVE to be wrong. However, I can't
> ignore the evidence.

Any thoughts on this article?

http://www.computerworld.com/news/2002/story/0,11280,74368,00.html

Having read it, I think I understand better what you're saying Pete. You're
observing this decline (spurred by the pickup of packaged apps) and drawing
the conclusion that it will continue to spiral (at least in this domain).
Hayes suggests another possible outcome and it's one that I see happening
today; only difference is that it's not happening on the mainframe anymore.
The uptick (and it is significant) is occuring on distributed platforms
(being fed by mid/large system migrations) -- the general theme here is one
of composite applications and reuse.

Gazaloo.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Java

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Peter E. C. Dashwood)
- **Date:** 2002-12-05T09:58:01-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3638c46.0212050958.3d0c01a2@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3DE28F46.8FB67DBB@shaw.ca> <as4ua0$ot8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <3DE65EB5.1594968E@shaw.ca> <asg1h3$89c$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3DEBC113.76C968AD@shaw.ca> <bfdfc3e8.0212032014.7e163d95@posting.google.com> <3dee0a43_1@news.newsgroups.com> <VyyH9.237637$WL3.81344@rwcrnsc54>`

```
"Gazaloo" <gaz@a.loo> wrote in message news:<VyyH9.237637$WL3.81344@rwcrnsc54>...
<snip> 
> Any thoughts on this article?
>

Yes, it is a good article but I believe he has gone too far in trying
to be conciliatory.

I agree that we will use systems comprised of various "parts"
("composite applications" as you termed them...)but I don't see
procedural languages being used much longer. There is a huge (and
rising) uptake on packages and his point that this does not permit the
tailoring of specific competitive advantage is, in my opinion, a
feeble one... It will be the components embedded in these solutions
that will provide the competitive advantage, not specifically written
"glue" that holds them together.


> http://www.computerworld.com/news/2002/story/0,11280,74368,00.html
> 
> Having read it, I think I understand better what you're saying Pete. You're
> observing this decline (spurred by the pickup of packaged apps) and drawing
> the conclusion that it will continue to spiral (at least in this domain).

Yes, based on what I see going on around me. However, I believe
everything he describes is in the imminent future (say 2 years away).
He is probably right that within that timescale we will continue to
use procedural code as part of composite systems (we shall certainly
continue to build components with it...).

In the longer term I foresee more radical changes.

Around 5 years from now completely new technology (like Extended UV
Lithography) will have boosted the power of our desktops by around 50
times. This will enable the processing of natural language as a
"normal" interface.

Given that, it is a logical extrapolation to have users simply
interact with the system in natural language until it does what they
require. Once it has "learned" what is required it will never
forget... (I first wrote this scenario around three years ago, in much
more detail. At that time I gave it 15 years... It looks to me like it
is right on schedule <G>)

In fact, responding to your post prompted me to search the archives
and find out when I did actually suggest this scenario...here's me
responding to Jimmy's plea for Open COBOL in August 1999...

===================== begin fairly long quote
=============================

Hmmm...who cares? Most of today's COBOL programmers don't even
understand the beginnings of what is generated. The days when we used
to
patch object code are long gone. The skills of yesteryear (whether we
like it or not) are now irrelevant.

As for how it interacts with specific operating systems, again, who
cares? As long as it does. I have written COBOL on every major
mainframe
(IBM and the BUNCH), at least 5 intermediate range machines, and PCs
using everything from DOS and OS/2 to NT. I have never worried about
the
operating system. My code runs. I don't use vendor-specific
constructs;
the same code runs across the range. Re-compile and away you go.  As
far
as COBOL compilers go...I have worked on 2: 1 for Burroughs (remember
them? they were the half of Unisys that WASN'T Sperry...), the other
for
a very obscure CDC machine. In the first case I was actually patching
machine code and disassembling object code to determine how the
compiler
(which had been written for Burroughs on contract and for which they
had
no source and no-one capable of maintaining it) worked. I then
modified
it and improved it. Eventually, I was getting letters from Detroit
Michigan asking me (in Auckland NZ) what pass 7 of their compiler did,
and could I please fix it to take magnetic input, etc. All very
interesting and exciting at the time, but in the long run a total
waste
of time. Today you will find the B500 in museums or, very
occasionally,
as a comms server somewhere. I wrote stuff like syntax scanners which
parsed using transition diagramming and JIT keyword extraction, and
all
sorts of interesting stuff...for what? It had a useful life of around
5
years and it took a year to do.

I believe the whole question is academic as "computer programming" as
we
know it has a limited remaining life span. History will show that
"computer programming" was a phenomenon of the latter half of the 20th
Century. By 2020 there was no longer a requirement for it. (Just look
at
all the rubbish surrounding standard laguages.  1st, 2nd, and 3rd
generation languages (4th generation is horseshit, and 5th generation
exists in the alcohol befuddled minds of some marketing men who never
programmed a computer in their lives and wouldn't know a compiler if
it
bit them in the arse) like COBOL, C, BASIC, and Assembler, are already
in their twilight years. OO, CORBA, COM compliance (ActiveX, OCX) will
not push back the frontiers (although they are all steps in the right
direction and bring closer the scenario I shall describe shortly.))

It is too costly, labour intensive, and time-consuming.  OO is
included
in this category. Once all the necessary objects and classes are
established the rest is just stamp collecting. The next step is to
develop intelligent classes which can spawn their own methods using
firmware building blocks.

No, it's NOT Science Fiction and I AM serious! The advent of
artificial
intelligence, voice activated interfaces in standard English, and more
and more "user-friendliness" will enable non-technical people to
interact easily and effectively with a computer until it does exactly
what they want.

Imagine the following scenario:

Small businessman with no technical knowledge, goes to corner shop and
buys "small business" computer. Brings it home unpacks and connects it
using extensive colour handbook and video. Switches on and says:
"Print
me an invoice."

computer: "What's an invoice?"

user: "An invoice is a document which should be created whenever we
SELL
PRODUCTS to a CUSTOMER. It shows what we sold and how many and how
much
they cost. It also shows the taxes and other sundries which the
CUSTOMER
has to PAY."

computer: "OK, I know what PRODUCTS and CUSTOMERS are and I recognise
the SALE and PAYMENT as standard business transactions, so I know when
to trigger this INVOICE. Shall I print a sample INVOICE using my
sample
CUSTOMER and PRODUCT databases?"

user: "Yes, please"

The machine produces a very poor attempt at an invoice. The user
interacts with it and explains that the figures need to be in columns
and what should go on the document (like, description of the product,
quantity, unit cost, and the necessary calculations.) After about 20
minutes the machine is getting very close and within half an hour the
user has his invoice. 

There are 2 fundamental things which make the above scenario possible:
1. The CONCEPTS of CUSTOMER, PRODUCT, TRANSACTION etc. can be built
into
firmware as standard intelligent agents. By today's standards this
will
require some interesting programming using techniques derived from OO,
neural networking and heuristics, but there is no doubt that it is
achievable. (I used the INVOICE as an example. In fact, the "small
business" machine would almost certainly know how to produce an
invoice.
It would start from a standard template and allow the user to modify
it
by interaction.) Machines for all kinds of applications would be
produced simply by installing the appropriate firmware. LAN/WAN
connection and connectivity would be fundamental, built in, and almost
completly transparent to the user.

2. The use of ITERATION. Trial and error. The learning curve. Don't
even
try and get it right first time (that is one of the major failures in
the way we currently develop systems...waste a ton of time getting it
"right" then find it all has to be changed anyway....(you may have
guessed I am a devotee of RAD for project management...<grin>))
Charles
Darwin showed that trial and error has been the way we have developed
over the last 3,000,000 years. It works. Compare what we are today
with
what we were 3,000,000 years ago and you start to see what I mean.
Then
translate it into computer terms. 3,000,000 years against the 15
billion
year life of the Universe is the blink of an eye. For a computer, the
same blink of an eye can be 10 minutes. Hence I make no apology for
claiming the system can produce an invoice within half an hour. Not
only
that, but the invoice will be EXACTLY what the user wants because he
has
specified it interactively himself.

So, goodbye "computer programming" with its attendant mystique,
jargon,
tedium,  cost, and intensive mental effort. I believe we will see
systems like the one above within the next 15 years. The technicians
of
the future may study COBOL the same way we study Latin and Classical
Greek; purely as academic exercises which help to grasp the concepts
of
what is going on. The man in the street will have no interest
in, or need to learn, computer programming as we know it.

Nowadays, I see COBOL as purely recreational to allow me to develop
useful and fun applications for today's primitive operating systems.
Why? I've done it all my working life (since 1965) and I enjoy it. It
also means I can generate a few quid on the side, but I am under no
illusions as to the future of it. My career (what's left of it) does
not
lie in computer programming. It lies in managing the technology,
assisting users to come to grips with it, and showing Management how
it
may be of benefit to the business. It lies in applying imagination to
technical projects and designing things carefully and iteratively, in
close co-operation with end-users. 

======================= end quote from August, 1999
===========================

Since I wrote that,I have become a bit disenchanted with AI (the Japs
spent billions on it and got nowhere...I believe it will come but it
will be 50 years away...), however, the intelligence of the human user
can be put into the loop through interaction and iteration, so
computer intelligence is NOT a prerequisite.

> Hayes suggests another possible outcome and it's one that I see happening
> today; only difference is that it's not happening on the mainframe anymore.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Yes, I agree in the short term. In the medium term, procedural code
will be obviated. That includes COBOL. End Users with little technical
knowledge (but probably excellent Application knowledge) will simply
get computers to produce what is needed.

" 'Tis a consummation devoutly to be wished..." (so long as you are
not a computer programmer <G>)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Java

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-05T16:12:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<spsuuuosnagkr77jqoce39s5oafpe8ll84@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3DE28F46.8FB67DBB@shaw.ca> <as4ua0$ot8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <3DE65EB5.1594968E@shaw.ca> <asg1h3$89c$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3DEBC113.76C968AD@shaw.ca> <bfdfc3e8.0212032014.7e163d95@posting.google.com> <3dee0a43_1@news.newsgroups.com>`

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

> I don't believe "COBOL has had it". I wrote some yesterday <G>... I DO
>believe it is in decline as a viable commercial solutions language. (I've
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>ignore the evidence.

Peter:

What evidence?

The market is the best motivational factor for compiler and tool
vendors.  Frankly, the COBOL programming community has not
overwhelmingly embraced OO COBOL.  I'm sure that you speak with quite
a few COBOL programmers, but I speak with at least a dozen or two
different COBOL programmers each and every day....some are customers
and some are prospective customers.  The vast majority of the folks
with whom I speak still write procedural code (both prospective and
current clients) and have no intention of changing.

Perhaps that's why only 2 compiler vendors offer OO COBOL compilers.
I think that it's great that they do offer more choices for
programmers, but none of the other vendors seem to be doing much in
the way of object orientation.  I believe that it is because they are
listening to their customers and prospective customers.

[snip]
>
>1. OO COBOL is easier to maintain than  C++.

Agreed.

>2. OO COBOL is easier to mantain than Java (in some circumstances)

Agreed.

>3. OO COBOL takes longer to write than either of the above.

But don't forget the 80/20 rule.  Only 20% of the effort generally
goes into the initial development.  Eighty percent is spent
maintaining the code over the long haul, therefore the smart manager
will place far more emphasis on maintainability than on speed of new
development.  This, as most will agree, is a strength of COBOL, a
reason for it's longevity and a reason for its continued usage.

[snip]

>> If you want to play - you gotta pay.
>>
>
>Fair enough...but if the amount you are required to pay is more than on the
>other funfair, you may well vote with your feet...

And apples may cost more or less than oranges....but each is
different.  Basing a choice of programming languages purely on cost of
acquisition of the compiler without consideration of any of the
multitude of other factors is myopic.

[snip]


Bob Wolfe
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<asoac3$n9p$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3DE28F46.8FB67DBB@shaw.ca> <as4ua0$ot8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <3DE65EB5.1594968E@shaw.ca> <asg1h3$89c$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3DEBC113.76C968AD@shaw.ca> <bfdfc3e8.0212032014.7e163d95@posting.google.com> <3dee0a43_1@news.newsgroups.com> <spsuuuosnagkr77jqoce39s5oafpe8ll84@4ax.com>`

```

On  5-Dec-2002, Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com> wrote:

> And apples may cost more or less than oranges....but each is
> different.  Basing a choice of programming languages purely on cost of
> acquisition of the compiler without consideration of any of the
> multitude of other factors is myopic.

A company doesn't care about the cost of the compiler, but the company does care
about the supply of programmers who can use that compiler.  If the cost of that
compiles effects this supply then there is an indirect concern.
```

Reading this Bob, it's like you never read anything I wrote. I guess I
really am wasting my time here... <sigh>

I have covered all of these points in depth and at length in various
previous posts, but, I'll go once more around the houses again...

Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com> wrote in message
news:spsuuuosnagkr77jqoce39s5oafpe8ll84@4ax.com...
> "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >King (or any source based procedural language for that matter) are gone,
and
> >I don't believe any swing of the pendulum is likely to change that. This
is
> >one of the very few times when I would LOVE to be wrong. However, I can't
> >ignore the evidence.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

 I've covered this many times, but once again...

1. Jobserve for a start. Today there are 53 COBOL jobs out of 15,000. 3
years ago there were around 500, 5 years ago there would have been 3 - 4
thousand.

2. Major corporates who you would EXPECT to use COBOL (utilities, banks) are
NOT doing so. I am currently working for a major UK Utility that has no
COBOL or procedural code and I am in contact with similar businesses that
don't have any either.

3. I am in communication with other IT managers in other businesses. NOBODY
would consider going back to COBOL (or PL/1) or procedural code. Packages
like SAP, Siebel, Chordiant etc. are the "new world". SOME managers have
existing legacy code and are actively looking at how to salvage it or
replace it.

> The market is the best motivational factor for compiler and tool
> vendors.  Frankly, the COBOL programming community has not
> overwhelmingly embraced OO COBOL.

Nope, that's right. And that is one reason that the death of COBOL as a
viable commercial development language is inevitable. I have covered all of
this elsewhere.

> I'm sure that you speak with quite
> a few COBOL programmers,

Not as many as I used to... most of my COBOL contacts are people I
previously trained who are now seriously concerned about their futures.

> but I speak with at least a dozen or two
> different COBOL programmers each and every day....some are customers
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Exactly. In what way is that representative of the IT market as a whole? You
are looking at a niche because that is where your products fit. Good luck.
My point is that, whether COBOL programmers can see it or not, the asteroid
is in the sky...

Maybe my attempt here is pointless...maybe reptiles can never become
mammals... I dunno. I do know that I am getting more mail from people who
have learned to deal with the Web, or learned to write Java, or acquired SQL
Server skill, and none of them are sorry they added this to their COBOL.


> Perhaps that's why only 2 compiler vendors offer OO COBOL compilers.
> I think that it's great that they do offer more choices for
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I won't argue this one. You might be right. I contend that both "the other
vendors" and their customers will be sorry within 10 years... That's if
anybody is still selling COBOL in 10 years...

> [snip]
> >
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> development.

 That is a criminal twisting of the 80/20 rule.

The "smart manager" (I count myself in this domain) will place NO EMPHASIS
AT ALL on maintaining code if he builds systems that don't need to have
their source code maintained. The idea of writing source code and
maintaining it is just plain archaic in this day and age.

Again, it is very hard for COBOL people to grasp this. If you use
configurable software and components that you can simply manipulate (OBJECT
code; NOT SOURCE CODE!!!!) there is no requirement for maintainability of
source. Again, I have covered this with full length articles (accessed by
thousands but not by you, apparently) and debate here until I am tired of
it. But your interest is in selling COBOL oriented products, so you ignore
it and then challenge one post...

If you understood my arguments, I would be happy to debate them with you.
But you don't. So this is really pointless...(at least for me).

> This, as most will agree, is a strength of COBOL, a
> reason for it's longevity and a reason for its continued usage.
>

OK... I give up...<G>

Really, Bob, believe whatever you want to. I really wish you every success
(seriously).


> [snip]
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >Fair enough...but if the amount you are required to pay is more than on
the
> >other funfair, you may well vote with your feet...
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

It isn't if all you need is fruit, and you are quite happy with apples or
oranges. Once more you missed the point entirely...

I won't be responding to further posts unless you can quote something I
actually said, and adopt a position on it that demonstrates you understand
it, but disagree.

Pete.

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-12-04T15:00:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<asl5b3$gdu$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3DE28F46.8FB67DBB@shaw.ca> <as4ua0$ot8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <3DE65EB5.1594968E@shaw.ca> <asg1h3$89c$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3DEBC113.76C968AD@shaw.ca> <bfdfc3e8.0212032014.7e163d95@posting.google.com>`

```

On  3-Dec-2002, thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell) wrote:

> Show me ONE standardized programming language where the GUI is part of
> the standard.  Just one.  SWING in Java is an add on Java library.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> incompatible with Microsoft....

We don't have universal GUIs.  If Microsoft had include high level interfaces
instead of its low level APIs, it might have been different.   But if you need
to know the OS in order to do simple GUI tasks, your GUI commands will have to
be OS specific.  Or, if Borland has to develop its own GUI commands because
there is no open standard, the same thing happens.

What really is needed here is an ANSI GUI interface that works like a printer
does, standard to all applications.
```

"James J. Gavan" wrote:

> Our young programmer concludes, 'Get out of here I'll stick with Java or C++'. And
> not only him, but his bosses too !

In an upcoming issue of "Crosstalk" a publication from the Hill AFB Software
Technology Center in Utah, I have an article on programming language selection.
I explicitly state that the rush to Java and C++ by COBOL shops is not very
smart.  C++, in particular, is so error-prone that one wonders why so many
otherwise intelligent people choose it at all.

Crosstalk is free for most software developers.  You can find out more about
it at,

                              http://www.stsc.hill.af.mil/crosstalk/

Also, you will be able to download a copy of my article sometime in late
February or early March.

Richard Riehle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Java

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Richard Riehle <richard@adaworks.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-11T17:03:30-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DF7E062.AC9F2EA3@adaworks.com>`
- **References:** `<3DE28F46.8FB67DBB@shaw.ca> <as4ua0$ot8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <3DE65EB5.1594968E@shaw.ca> <asg1h3$89c$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3DEBC113.76C968AD@shaw.ca> <3DF551E1.88931112@adaworks.com> <eelJ9.6463$Kw6.4291795@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Michael Mattias wrote:

> "Richard Riehle" <richard@adaworks.com> wrote in message
> news:3DF551E1.88931112@adaworks.com...
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> your use of the term COBOL "shop."

I currently do most of my programming in Ada.  What a delight!  None of
the silly errors that come so easily in C and C++.   Consider a language
that lets one simply compute an index off the end of an array without
giving any warning.   That and hundreds of other entertaining aspects
of C++ are missing from Ada.  Yet, I can as easily solve any programming
problem in Ada as I can in C++ or C.

COBOL also does more error checking than C++.  It is explicitly designed
to handle accounting data (as also is Ada) and lets the programmer
do simple things in simple ways.   Granted, the more elderly standards
such as COBOL 68 and COBOL 74 were a bit clumsy sometimes, but
beginning with the 85 standard, COBOL evolved into a language with
excellent support for data processing applications.   C++ has never
been as effective for data processing as COBOL.

Worse, C++ is the "strike anywhere match" of programming languages.
Just as those kind of matches are inappropriate for children, C++ is
inappropriate for anyone not extremely skilled in the art of reliable
programming practices.

Richard Riehle
```

solution (if you are going to utilise objects I reckon you might as well
write OO code) but I have to admit, it is certainly viable. And I would
agree that for this solution COBOL is no more inappropriate than any other
language. (You might as well use Assembler...<G>)

Pete.

Paul Barnett <paul.barnett@nospam.microfocus.com> wrote in message
news:asamfe$cif$1@hyperion.microfocus.com...
> Ken,
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I agree that this is a reasonable approach. I tried it a couple of years ago
and was happy with it. The tricky bit was RMI (which I never got to work
with COBOL...<G>). For Methods that were local it worked fine. (This was
with Fujitsu COBOL). I also managed to get Java to invoke methods of a
component written in Fujitsu COBOL, and got VB to invoke methods of the same
component.

> I would use COBOL classes and/or dialog system for an application where
GUI
> is not required to work over the net.
>
The last time I used DIALOG system it was based on PANELS. It worked fine. I
didn't like having a separate language to describe the GUI event processes
in, but that was my only grumble. Nowadays, I use PowerCOBOL which allows
the events to be processed in COBOL.Although there appears to be a shadow
over the future of PowerCOBOL (according to another thread in this NG) I
don't see this as a problem. These days I tend to write components, and the
presentation layer is external to the component, and tends to be XML.

I'm not really bothered whether I use PowerCOBOL forms or Windows forms for
Windows desktop applications.

> Another appropriate system of doing remote procedure calls over the net is
> Web Services, now supported in COBOL by Micro Focus.
>
Yes, this is the approach I favour. I have been doing this in Fujitsu COBOL
for around a year now, using SOAP and XML. Thane was doing it almost as soon
as it was available...<G> (It was mainly his enthusiasm that got me into
it).

It seems to me that MicroFocus have taken the right approach. COBOL and Java
are good. So are COBOL and Web Services.

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Java

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-12-02T23:46:52
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3debf923_3@news.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<3DE28F46.8FB67DBB@shaw.ca> <as4ua0$ot8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <as5v03$1lf$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net>`

```

Ken Mullins <**Ken**Mullins**@**mindspring.com** remove **'s> wrote in
message news:as5v03$1lf$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net...
> "Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@nospam.microfocus.com> wrote>
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Why exactly is it about a GUI interface and COBOL that warrants using
JAVA?
> Why is JAVA more appropriate to use over COBOL?
>
Because the Java language has Classes that support GUI, and COBOL doesn't.

GUI needs OO. (All the GUI controls are Objects with their own methods and
attributes; that is the basis of both the Windows and Unix GUI systems.
Actually, I believe the Mac system is also Object Oriented, but I haven't
used it so can't be sure.) It is this object orientation that allows GUI
screens to be built quickly with drag and drop, re-using buttons,
tree-lists, menus, drop downs, and so on... and then incorporated into
applications as components. Otherwise it is necessary to utilise GUI Classes
and instantiate the objects as you want to use them. (This is a much more
long-winded process.)

Either way, you will need OO COBOL. Although OO COBOL is good, it was added
to COBOL many years after the language was invented so it is not seamless.
Java, on the other hand, was designed from day one as an OO language. It has
not ONE set of GUI Classes included in it, but SEVERAL. (Paul mentioned the
"swing" class, there is also another class that gives a metallic looking
finish called "metal" and both of these are in addition to the "Standard"
presentation class for Applets, Servlets and Beans...) As far as I know,
Fujitsu OO COBOL does not provide any GUI classes, but I haven't browsed the
6.1 Class libraries and I could be wrong.

Of course, if you are using OO COBOL the fact that there are no intrinsic
GUI classes available (or not) is fairly unimportant. You can reference
standard Windows objects and use the standard Windows FORMS objects,
referencing them as components. Or you can use the Java Classes if you
package them as beans or components.

So the answer to your question, Ken is that Java is well served when it
comes to GUI and COBOL isn't.

You could also conclude from the very superficial summary above that, unless
you are into OO, you are not going to get into GUI. You would be right. The
only tool I know that lets you get into GUI without OO knowledge is
PowerCOBOL. It simply hides the OO stuff from you. (I guess M/F DIALOG does
a similar function...)

This is WHY we need "the best tool for the job". COBOL is NOT the best tool
when it comes to a Graphic User Interface. Java is not the best tool when it
comes to internal data manipulation and processing...

Neither of them is much chop when it comes to complex relational DB
processing... (So we wrap them around SQL, or we set up RDB procedures that
can be triggered by them). And so it goes on...

The best tools for the job...

Pete.

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL v Java

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-03T15:17:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<16ipuucufh43a7t9gh37r5taiso9pepp5d@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3DE28F46.8FB67DBB@shaw.ca> <as4ua0$ot8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <as5v03$1lf$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net> <3debf923_3@news.newsgroups.com>`

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

[snip]
>
>You could also conclude from the very superficial summary above that, unless
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>a similar function...)

Peter, this is an incorrect statement.  I do not care to enter into a
long, drawn-out discussion with you on this, but the facts show
otherwise.

We have supported GUI screen display from COBOL since 1989 (UNIX Open
Look) and Windows 3.0 in 1990.  No OO required.

My friend, Kevin Hansen from Norcom in Alaska supports GUI screen
display from COBOL.  No OO required.

Acucorp's screen syntax supports GUI screens in COBOL.  No OO
required.

As you mentioned Micro Focus Dialog System supports GUI screens in
COBOL.  No OO required.

I'm not suggesting that OO is bad.  It's simply a choice made by the
programmer......but it is not a requirement to display a GUI screen.

>This is WHY we need "the best tool for the job". COBOL is NOT the best tool
>when it comes to a Graphic User Interface. Java is not the best tool when it
>comes to internal data manipulation and processing...

That's why we write all of our DLL's in native C and then provide a
COBOL programming interface to allow the programmer to manipulate the
GUI using COBOL syntax.

We've been doing this since 1986.  C allows us to be very portable at
the API level, very compact and very fast.  But...that doesn't mean
that a simple procedural COBOL interface cannot be written to control
our C language DLL.



Bob Wolfe
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

Sorry Bob, I didn't mention every tool that is available and I understand
SP2 is excellent. I did mention the two I have used personally.

It is also true that you can utilise GUI (at least on Windows platforms) by
simply calling the API.

So my statement is incorrect.

However, it doesn't alter the fact that the GUI objects themselves are
Object Oriented and the OS sees them that way. If you intend to get the most
out of GUI programming you will do best to treat the GUI as a set of Objects
(components) and manipulate their properties and invoke their methods and
events. To do THAT, requires some understanding of OO programming.

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Options for "Inspect" command

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-12-02T15:31:22-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0212021531.ce16235@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<asfsl8$gu$1@newsreader.mailgate.org> <l10nuuorureq4sros54k2m7misq2v27vlh@4ax.com> <asfvec$36s$1@newsreader.mailgate.org>`

```
"Capellone" <Capellone76@libero.it> wrote

> Can you be clearer please?I know the String command but the unstring, how
> should I use it, with less word: what's the complete instruction :) ?
> 
> I need to replace  the string xxxxxxxxxx01 with 09/09/1976 in a field for
> example.

You may be better with reference notation:

     MOVE ZERO   TO I1  
     INSPECT string1 TALLYING I1 FOR CHARACTERS BEFORE INITIAL "xxxxxxxxxx01"
     MOVE string1(1:I1)   TO string2
     MOVE "09/09/1976"    TO string2(I1+1:)
     MOVE string1(I1+11:) TO string2(I1+11:)
of course unstring and string could also be done.

     MOVE ZER        TO I1 I2
     UNSTRING string1
         DELIMITED BY "xxxxxxxxxx01"
         INTO work1 COUNT IN I1
              work2
     MOVE SPACES     TO string2
     STRING Work1(1:I1)
            "09/09/1976"
            Work2
         DELIMITED BY SIZE
         INTO string2
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: File migration RM85->Fujitsu

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <frederico_fonseca@eudoramail.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-03T21:20:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2o7quukcrl94ehbqsqd39cl7j3j6dob36g@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<as7v39$7kr$1@reader13.wxs.nl> <i1nfuu4ukr19n2kg94lqhsd8j7t04erkqk@4ax.com> <49973e5b.0212031141.714fd461@posting.google.com>`

```
On 3 Dec 2002 11:41:44 -0800, cobolnotdead@hotmail.com (Kate Parker)
wrote:

>>Frederico Fonseca <frederico_fonseca@eudoramail.com> wrote in message 
>> No need for conversion unless you use COMP-6 within the record FD.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>under Fujitsu AND RM!
That fine.

But a word of advice.

1- You need to be carefull in the type of numerics you use in the
file.
As an example 
COMP-5 (Fuji) does not (yet) have a corresponding in RM.
and COMP-6 (RM) does not have the Fuji counterpart.

2- Do not try to use the file concurrently. File lock mechanism
will most probably be implemented differently over a network.

3- One thing I have not tried is to have a big file (> 2GB) being
create by Fuji and then accessed by RM. I do not know if this is
implemented in the same way.

Apart from the above you should not have any problem.
```

On 28-Nov-2002, "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote:


RE:  "The moving hand, having writ, moves on never to return."


> > Arrrghhhh! No, I simply can't stand to see one of my favourite pieces
> > mis-quoted and claimed for Scotland...<G>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> You got the date wrong by around 500 years.

So the original quote comes from around the year 1200 or 2100...

Check out http://www.bartleby.com/41/623.html  (Rubaiyat of Omar Khayyam of
Naishapur (1048?-1122)).  I'm not sure I would quibble about spelling over that
time (why does my spell checker give only one spelling for Shakespeare, if the
bard used a bunch of spellings?)

                  LXXVI
The Moving Finger writes; and, having writ,
Moves on: nor all your Piety nor Wit
  Shall lure it back to cancel half a Line,
Nor all your Tears wash out a Word of it.

Could you point us to the quote from around the year 100
```

>

"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:asg37h$9ok$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On 27-Nov-2002, "Tim Scrivens" <tim.scrivens heavy mung@nz.invalideds.com>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> We all know one from Cats.   Where's the 2nd?

Easy meat - difficult to hum in Usenet though - too quick - Jellicle cats.

But as far as Cats goes, I have a totally unfair advantage, as the original
book was part of my childhood library.  Advantages of having an early
childhood teacher for a mother - everyone else was brought up with "See spot
run", and my childhood was full of Huck Finn, Narnia (where the religious
overtones TOTALLY escaped me (I was 6)), the Hobbit, and books Mum liked
without worrying if they were "good" for me or not.  Traditional Sunday
morning, with all three kids invading the parent's bedroom so Mum could read
to them for a couple of hours.
```

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

_Thread started [2002-11](2002-11.md#t-af29778c9b)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraph Recursion with IBM Cobol for OS/390?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-12-02T17:07:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<asg41i$a54$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<dcedb8d0.0211200724.708c48f2@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0211201327.1b0cc14@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0211260800.58c92360@posting.google.com> <as07hf$gkv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0211262016.1ae9945f@posting.google.com> <wb4F9.63070$e%.990887@news20.bellglobal.com> <as2n6a$58l$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <ebpcuus9kuv2bkl6e2jbc748vqb75jr3bt@4ax.com>`

```

On 28-Nov-2002, Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com> wrote:

> But what if:
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> precompiler, methinks.....

END-OBTAIN is not a command the pre-compiler recognizes.   The pre-compiler was
written before CoBOL had END- commands.

But the main problem is that we don't see the inserted code.   It is easier for
a maintenance programmer to make sure each IDMS command is followed by a period
and that he does not count on the invisible code to be of any format.   What
actually happens above is that the OBTAIN is replaced by a CALL - we don't need
to know the call syntax.  So what we need to know is the OBTAIN syntax and
remember that this gets translated into combinations of CALL, IF & ELSE logic.
 Knowing this, we either need to know exactly how it gets translated, or we need
to make sure it doesn't matter.   A full stop period makes sure it doesn't
matter.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraph Recursion with IBM Cobol for OS/390?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2002-12-04T11:18:04-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dcedb8d0.0212041118.c444951@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<dcedb8d0.0211200724.708c48f2@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0211201327.1b0cc14@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0211260800.58c92360@posting.google.com> <as07hf$gkv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0211262016.1ae9945f@posting.google.com> <wb4F9.63070$e%.990887@news20.bellglobal.com> <as2n6a$58l$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <ebpcuus9kuv2bkl6e2jbc748vqb75jr3bt@4ax.com> <asg41i$a54$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message news:<asg41i$a54$1@peabody.colorado.edu>...
> On 28-Nov-2002, Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com> wrote:
> 
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> matter.

It is a reasonably simple matter to write a Cobol pre-processor that
will convert END-OBTAIN or other scope terminators into "." for you.

Not only would your code look better and be easier to read, but you
would avoid the less than desirable effects of random '.' scattered
throughout code.

Is IDMS still sold, supported and maintained?  Why are they not
adopting the now two decade old standard with scope terminators?
```

stop is required is at the end of a paragraph or section.  What
ambiguity are you refering to?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraph Recursion with IBM Cobol for OS/390?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-04T17:20:08-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<asm2j9$hbe$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<dcedb8d0.0211200724.708c48f2@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0211201327.1b0cc14@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0211260800.58c92360@posting.google.com> <as07hf$gkv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0211262016.1ae9945f@posting.google.com> <wb4F9.63070$e%.990887@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
In the recently approved ANSI/ISO Standard, a period (or full-stop) is
pretty much required to tell where the last statement in a paragraph ends -
and where the next paragraph name starts.  This is due (in part) to the
removal of the A-margin/B-margin "rules".

Consider:

"traditional code"

Name-1.
        Move Name-2 to Name-3 Name-4
          .
Name-5.
        Continue
          .

"newly conforming" (no A-Margin/B-margin rules)

Name-1. Move Name-2 to Name-3 Name-4. Name-5. Continue.

Now consider this withOUT periods:

Name-1 Move Name-2 to Name-3 Name-4 Name-5 Continue


The question arises:
  Is Name-5 a data-name (receiving item for MOVE) or paragraph name?

    ***

Some comments:

 A) I am NOT saying that the "newly conforming" source code is GOOD coding
technique (much less easy to maintain)

B) I am NOT saying that removing the A-Margin/B-margin rules is (or is  not)
a good thing.

C) In fact, if the compiler (if not the programmer) "looked at" the entire
source code, they SHOULD be able to figure out whether Name-5 is or is not
ever defined (Global or Locally) as a data-name or if it ever referenced as
a paragraph name (remembering that non-referenced paragraph names need NOT
be unique).  However, this is the reason why I think that "periods are
needed".

Final Note:
  There is a suggestion for a POSSIBLE enhancement to a future Standard to
introduce an
        END-SENTENCE
construct to do what a period does today.  (I don't see this as a great
enhancement - but someone else might)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraph Recursion with IBM Cobol for OS/390?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-12-06T17:23:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DF0DD26.B9506FE@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<dcedb8d0.0211200724.708c48f2@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0211201327.1b0cc14@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0211260800.58c92360@posting.google.com> <as07hf$gkv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0211262016.1ae9945f@posting.google.com> <wb4F9.63070$e%.990887@news20.bellglobal.com> <asm2j9$hbe$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:

> a paragraph name (remembering that non-referenced paragraph names need NOT
> be unique).  

Referenced paragraphs also need not be unique, they should be unique
within a SECTION.  Referenced from within a section they need not even
be qualified.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraph Recursion with IBM Cobol for OS/390?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-04T17:29:45-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<asm35b$hc4$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<dcedb8d0.0211200724.708c48f2@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0211201327.1b0cc14@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0211260800.58c92360@posting.google.com> <as07hf$gkv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0211262016.1ae9945f@posting.google.com> <as2mvk$552$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <fcd09c56.0211291122.5b97ce5b@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0211291601.75b80f58@posting.google.com>`

```
Richard,
  Not disagreeing entirely, but a "missing scope terminator" *is* usually
(or at least often) detected by the compiler - at least when running in
ANSI-conforming mode.

Try compiling (for example)

  Read ABC
      At End
          If A = B
              Display "END-IF is missing below"
       Not At End
             Display "Won't compile cleanly"
   End-Read

There are compilers that allow this (as an extension) and there are also
cases where something will compile (but not provide the expected results).
However, I think that in MOST cases using a "single period per paragraph"
*and* use of scope terminators avoids many/most "logic" problems.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraph Recursion with IBM Cobol for OS/390?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-12-05T11:59:52-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0212051159.30395e34@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<dcedb8d0.0211200724.708c48f2@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0211201327.1b0cc14@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0211260800.58c92360@posting.google.com> <as07hf$gkv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0211262016.1ae9945f@posting.google.com> <as2mvk$552$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <fcd09c56.0211291122.5b97ce5b@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0211291601.75b80f58@posting.google.com> <asm35b$hc4$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote

>   Not disagreeing entirely, but a "missing scope terminator" *is* usually
> (or at least often) detected by the compiler - at least when running in
> ANSI-conforming mode.

While it is true that some scope terminators are required (such as
END-PERFORM on an in-line PERFORM) and will produce compiler
diagnostics, others are optional and can be masked.

See "Strange Cobol behaviour" for a perfect example provided quite
serendipitously.

> There are compilers that allow this (as an extension) and there are also
> cases where something will compile (but not provide the expected results).
> However, I think that in MOST cases using a "single period per paragraph"
> *and* use of scope terminators avoids many/most "logic" problems.

Yes, you are agreeing with me. The issue was ensuring the correct
number of scope terminators rather than using a 'catch-all' full stop
that will partially mask the lack of them.

I prefer not to mask the lack. For example in the thread indicated if
there had been 3 END-IF (4 are required) the test would have been
successful for many cases including '05'.  If a test using '10' had
only been casually checked it may have missed the one signal that it
had failed in some way, wheras if the full stop had been ommitted it
may have been far more obvious that there was a logic problem (with
the IF XXXX not executing).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraph Recursion with IBM Cobol for OS/390?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2002-12-04T11:12:11-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dcedb8d0.0212041112.4d7fe3c9@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<dcedb8d0.0211200724.708c48f2@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0211201327.1b0cc14@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0211260800.58c92360@posting.google.com> <as07hf$gkv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0211262016.1ae9945f@posting.google.com> <as2mvk$552$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <fcd09c56.0211291122.5b97ce5b@posting.google.com>`

```
robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones) wrote in message news:<fcd09c56.0211291122.5b97ce5b@posting.google.com>...
><snip lots of quotation>
> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> much is done wrong during overruns.

I'm confused.  It sounds as if you thing full stops provide you some
service and protection.

If you ONLY use scope delimiters then there can be no unintended run
time consequences because the compiler will alert you to any that are
missing.  The full stop IS the problem as it can mask the abscense of
one or more scope delimiters.

If you limit yourself to a single full stop per paragraph, and you
avoid that horrid perform-thru construct you will not have any
accidental execution.  Ever.  Period.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aso0h5$f2p$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<dcedb8d0.0211200724.708c48f2@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0211201327.1b0cc14@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0211260800.58c92360@posting.google.com> <as07hf$gkv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0211262016.1ae9945f@posting.google.com> <as2mvk$552$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <fcd09c56.0211291122.5b97ce5b@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0212041112.4d7fe3c9@posting.google.com> <aslp6h$smo$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <dcedb8d0.0212050701.8c0f7dc@posting.google.com> <asnt81$b52$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:asnt81$b52$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
<snip>
> I don't think I have EVER seen an END-COMPUTE, or an END-ADD.
>

Howard,
  How would you code the following (do you use "switches" or performs of
paragraphs - or something else for this type of logic)


If A = B
   Display "Before ADD"
   Add +10 to Num1
       On Size
              Display "Size error on ADD"
    End-Add
    Display "After ADD"
Else
   Display "Before Compute"
  Compute Num2 =   Num3 * Num4
      Not on Size
            Move "OK" to Good-Rec
   End-Compute
   Display "After Compute"
End-If

Don't your programs ever test for ON SIZE and NOT ON SIZE in nested
conditionals?

P.S.  In response to NOT seeing any compile-time errors for missing scope
terminators try compiling:

   Read  ABC
         At End
             Call  Dynamic-File-Name
                On Exception
                   Display "Program: " Dynamic-File-Name " not found"
         Not at End
             Add 1 to Good-Rec
   End-Read

which is medium-common program logic and which REQUIRES the "END-CALL to be
insserted before the "Not at End" phrase - to make the CALL statement
imperative rather than conditional.
```

On  5-Dec-2002, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:

> If A = B
>    Display "Before ADD"
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> End-If

When necessary I have used declaratives.   But the need for this has been very
rare.

> Don't your programs ever test for ON SIZE and NOT ON SIZE in nested
> conditionals?

No.  Do you have END-SUBTRACT and END-COMPUTE on every one of your SUBTRACT and
COMPUTE commands?  If so, a compiler that pointed out when you leave them out
would be useful.  Otherwise it turns into noise.

> P.S.  In response to NOT seeing any compile-time errors for missing scope
> terminators try compiling:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> imperative rather than conditional.

Well sure we will see it here.  But I haven't had a compiler show me missing
END-IF's.   And that's what we're talking about here.
```

>I don't think I have EVER seen an END-COMPUTE, or an END-ADD.

In other words, you have never seen anyone code  ON OVERFLOW on an ADD
or COMUTE statement?   Weird, I would consider it good programming if
ON OVERFLOW were included (and then it would require
END-ADD/END-COMPUTE

Or, if you are not coding ON OVERFLOW, how _do_ you cater for the
OVERFLOw case?


01 MY-SALARY PIC 9(5).
01 THE-BONUS PIC 9(4).

MOVE 98000 TO MY-SALARY
MOVE 9800  TO THE-BONUS
ADD THE-BONUS TO MY-SALARY
DISPLAY 'Your employer is happy now'



ADD THE-BONUS TO MY-SALARY
   ON OVERFLOW
      DISPLAY 'Help - With a Salary like this I am going to starve'
      MOVE 12 TO RETURN-CODE
      GOBACK
END-ADD

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraph Recursion with IBM Cobol for OS/390?

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-12-09T15:46:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<at2dsu$495$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<fcd09c56.0211201327.1b0cc14@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0211260800.58c92360@posting.google.com> <as07hf$gkv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0211262016.1ae9945f@posting.google.com> <as2mvk$552$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <fcd09c56.0211291122.5b97ce5b@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0212041112.4d7fe3c9@posting.google.com> <aslp6h$smo$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <dcedb8d0.0212050701.8c0f7dc@posting.google.com> <asnt81$b52$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <60h4vusrcacfvngmkri0pqcrorc3nd2rcc@4ax.com>`

```

On  7-Dec-2002, Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com> wrote:

> >I don't think I have EVER seen an END-COMPUTE, or an END-ADD.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> END-ADD/END-COMPUTE

Very rarely - written by me, not by the younger programmers who always had
END-COMPUTE available.

> Or, if you are not coding ON OVERFLOW, how _do_ you cater for the
> OVERFLOw case?
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> DISPLAY 'Your employer is happy now'

In the old days, we did arithmetic overflow checking by hand - as we did table
overflow.   Then we added declaratives, but generally we did our own testing
instead.

Overflow checking is more important than file-status checking as file-status
failure will generally result in an abort either way, while arithmetic overflow
can result in bad data.

But generally, it is possible to make sure we never have arithmetic overflow.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraph Recursion with IBM Cobol for OS/390?

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-12-05T15:44:18-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0212051544.66bd08ea@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<dcedb8d0.0211200724.708c48f2@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0211201327.1b0cc14@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0211260800.58c92360@posting.google.com> <as07hf$gkv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0211262016.1ae9945f@posting.google.com> <as2mvk$552$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <fcd09c56.0211291122.5b97ce5b@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0212041112.4d7fe3c9@posting.google.com> <aslp6h$smo$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <dcedb8d0.0212050701.8c0f7dc@posting.google.com>`

```
jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote

> > My compiler won't alert me to missing scope delimiters.  Occasionally a full
> > stop will enable it to notice that something is wrong.
> 
> It sounds as if your vendor is 17 years late.  That is a very long
> gestation time for any product.

Compilers do not and need not 'alert' missing scope delimiters.  In
many cases they are optional.  eg:

         IF ( a )
             IF ( b )
                perform x
             ELSE
                perform y
         ELSE
             perform z
         END-IF

has correctly terminated scopes.

> Yes.  It makes the indenting look better, 

'Look better' depends entirely on what you are used to.

> and every study I have seen
> indicates people can read indented code faster and with better
> comprehension.

Indenting does not depend on scope delimiters or v.v. 

> IIRC all commands that control flow-of-execution have scope
> terminators.

GO TO doesn't.  Out-of-line PERFORM doesn't.
 
> > > If you limit yourself to a single full stop per paragraph, and you
> > > avoid that horrid perform-thru construct you will not have any
> > > accidental execution.  Ever.  Period.

Not true.  Programmers can be far more inventive than you give them
credit.

> If I had to pick one item to be obsoleted from Cobol and the choice
> was Perform-Thru or full stops I would have a hard time deciding.

As previously indicated it is not possible to eliminate full stops
entirely, besides it would break a lot of existing code.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ More on scope terminators (was: Paragraph Recursion with IBM Cobol for OS/390?

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-05T19:53:50-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<asovvg$anh$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<dcedb8d0.0211200724.708c48f2@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0211201327.1b0cc14@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0211260800.58c92360@posting.google.com> <as07hf$gkv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0211262016.1ae9945f@posting.google.com> <as2mvk$552$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <fcd09c56.0211291122.5b97ce5b@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0212041112.4d7fe3c9@posting.google.com> <aslp6h$smo$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <dcedb8d0.0212050701.8c0f7dc@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212051544.66bd08ea@posting.google.com>`

```

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0212051544.66bd08ea@posting.google.com...
> jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote
>
 <snip>
>
> > IIRC all commands that control flow-of-execution have scope
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Just so the actual "rule" is understood.  *ALL* statements that have a
conditional form have a scope termintor which can make them imperativer
statements.  (This is not a rule that relates to whether or not they are
"flow-of-control" statements but whether they are conditional or not.)

This ties in with the rule (stated elsewhere) that it is NONCONFORMING
(possibly illegal depending on compiler) to nest a conditional statement
within any other statement unless:

A) it is followed immediately by a period
    or
B) it is nested within either "half" of an IF...THEN/ELSE statement.

    ***

It should also be "noted" that every statement that has both an imperative
and a conditional format *ALLOWS* a scope termintor for the imperative
format.  Therefore, the following is non-conforming source code:

Add +1 to Num1
 On Size
   Read ABC
        At End
             Read XYZ
     end-read
  Not on Size
      Display "Other"
 End-ADD

this is because the "single" end-read goes with the most recent READ
statement (the imperative format) leaving the 1st READ as conditional -
which is nonconforming in the ON SIZE phrase.  This will get even more
"confusing" with implementations of the 2002 Standard where the "ON SIZE
ERROR" phrase and "NOT ON SIZE ERROR" phrase (and all other positive and
negative conditional phrases) may be coded in either order.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: More on scope terminators (was: Paragraph Recursion with IBM Cobol for OS/390?

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-12-06T17:31:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DF0DF02.E3D66930@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<dcedb8d0.0211200724.708c48f2@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0211201327.1b0cc14@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0211260800.58c92360@posting.google.com> <as07hf$gkv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0211262016.1ae9945f@posting.google.com> <as2mvk$552$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <fcd09c56.0211291122.5b97ce5b@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0212041112.4d7fe3c9@posting.google.com> <aslp6h$smo$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <dcedb8d0.0212050701.8c0f7dc@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212051544.66bd08ea@posting.google.com> <asovvg$anh$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:

> It should also be "noted" that every statement that has both an imperative
> and a conditional format *ALLOWS* a scope termintor for the imperative
> format.  

Note that not every scope terminator is in this category. An END-PERFORM
is required on an in-line PERFORM and is disallowed on an out-of-line
PERFORM.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: More on scope terminators (was: Paragraph Recursion with IBM Cobol for OS/390?

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-05T23:52:12-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aspdue$74o$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<dcedb8d0.0211200724.708c48f2@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0211201327.1b0cc14@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0211260800.58c92360@posting.google.com> <as07hf$gkv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0211262016.1ae9945f@posting.google.com> <as2mvk$552$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <fcd09c56.0211291122.5b97ce5b@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0212041112.4d7fe3c9@posting.google.com> <aslp6h$smo$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <dcedb8d0.0212050701.8c0f7dc@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212051544.66bd08ea@posting.google.com> <asovvg$anh$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3DF0DF02.E3D66930@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
True and not true.

It is true that in-line PERFORM requires an END-PERFORM and out-of-line
prohibits it.

HOWEVER, read the part of my sentence  that you quoted which says.
   "that every statement that has both an imperative  and a conditional
format"

there is NO "conditional format" of either the inline or out-of-line PERFORM
statement (as defined in the COBOL definition of such statements).  If this
is what you meant by your
  "that not every scope terminator is in this category"

you are correct, but it does alter the truth of the statement that I made,
i.e.

"every statement that has both an imperative  and a conditional format
*ALLOWS* a scope terminator for the imperative  format."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: More on scope terminators (was: Paragraph Recursion with IBM Cobol for OS/390?

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-06T13:35:39-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<asquf7$htb$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<dcedb8d0.0211200724.708c48f2@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0211262016.1ae9945f@posting.google.com> <as2mvk$552$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <fcd09c56.0211291122.5b97ce5b@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0212041112.4d7fe3c9@posting.google.com> <aslp6h$smo$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <dcedb8d0.0212050701.8c0f7dc@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212051544.66bd08ea@posting.google.com> <asovvg$anh$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3DF0DF02.E3D66930@Azonic.co.nz> <aspdue$74o$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <217e491a.0212061124.70f33375@posting.google.com>`

```
Tell me the "referent" for the word "this" in your post that includes
  "that not every scope terminator is in this category"

and I'll tell you what part was "not true" (if any).

If your "this category" refers to the fact that not all scope terminators
are associated with statements that have both conditional and imperative
formats, then your post was not in error - just not relevant to my original
post.

If your "this category" refers to some (or even one) statement that DOES
include both an imperative and conditional format - but does NOT allow a
scope terminator with the imperative format, then the statement is simply
not true (as I far as I know for ANSI/ISO conforming compilers)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: More on scope terminators (was: Paragraph Recursion with IBM Cobol for OS/390?

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-12-06T18:05:04-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0212061805.578b0bcc@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<dcedb8d0.0211200724.708c48f2@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0211291122.5b97ce5b@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0212041112.4d7fe3c9@posting.google.com> <aslp6h$smo$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <dcedb8d0.0212050701.8c0f7dc@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212051544.66bd08ea@posting.google.com> <asovvg$anh$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3DF0DF02.E3D66930@Azonic.co.nz> <aspdue$74o$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <217e491a.0212061124.70f33375@posting.google.com> <asquf7$htb$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote

> Tell me the "referent" for the word "this" in your post that includes
>   "that not every scope terminator is in this category"
> and I'll tell you what part was "not true" (if any).

-------------------//-------------------------------------------------------
> It should also be "noted" that every statement that has both an imperative
> and a conditional format *ALLOWS* a scope termintor for the imperative
> format.  

Note that not every scope terminator is in this category. An
END-PERFORM
                                           ^^^^^^^^^^^^^
is required on an in-line PERFORM and is disallowed on an out-of-line
PERFORM.
-------------------//-------------------------------------------------------

It should be obvious from normal rules of english that:

this category = "every statement that has both an imperative and a
conditional format".
 
> If your "this category" refers to the fact that not all scope terminators
> are associated with statements that have both conditional and imperative
> formats, then your post was not in error - just not relevant to my original
> post.

It was an additional notation (as indicated by the "Note").

Its relevance is that some may make the mistake that all scope
terminators may be *allowed* following their statement.  Code has been
posted here by a student that had an END-PERFORM following an
out-of-line PERFORM.

> If your "this category" refers to some (or even one) statement that DOES
> include both an imperative and conditional format - but does NOT allow a
> scope terminator with the imperative format, then the statement is simply
> not true (as I far as I know for ANSI/ISO conforming compilers)

Are you often confused by negatives ?

   this category = "every statement that has ..."

   not .. in this category = not .. in "every statement that has ..."

My "this category" refers not just to one or to some, but to 'every'. 
The 'not' inverts this to 'none'. The sets of 'this category' and
'every scope terminated statement' are not congruent.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Power .Net

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@nospam.microfocus.com>

```
Later next year Micro Focus will be releasing a .NET version of Net Express.
You will be able code and debug within Visual Studio if you want to. While
it's not the first .NET COBOL product to market, it will have a less painful
migration than that of the product that was first. Currently Net Express
compiled code can work alongside .NET as 'unmanaged code' and I would expect
that PowerCOBOL to be able to do so also.

millions

to

If you a doing web programming try http://www.cobolscript.com/ . It
intergrates with RDBMS well (like Mysql). Or you might buy
Fujitsu's-Linux http://adtools.com , which is commercial but does not
support as much RDBMS(no MySql). Your time, investment in software
compilers and finished products will come back to you in users eye
appeal, ease of installation etc. So don't thing just free because
that is all you will get back. You might try http://Borland.com or
http://www.trolltech.com/ languages and compilers for desktops with a
small learning curve that works well on Linux Desktops? Good luck
Mike.

> intergrates with RDBMS well (like Mysql). 

CobolScript is 'Cobol like' but shouldn't be confused with a real
Cobol.  It is, however, quite useful.

> Or you might buy
> Fujitsu's-Linux http://adtools.com , which is commercial but does not
> support as much RDBMS(no MySql). 

It works fine with MySQL, though you may need to get the latest
version (of MySQL) and ensure that it is set up to support
transactions.

In fact both CobolScript and Fujitsu NetCobol for Linux use the same
ODBC connections, _and_ I can access the Linux RDBMS systems from
Windows Fujitsu Cobol 6.1 too.
