<a id="t-397b1cd633"></a>
## Thread: PIC P — 2 this month / 11 total

_Thread started [2002-11](2002-11.md#t-397b1cd633)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PIC P

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-02T12:05:19-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<asgee0$mel$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3de23fb3$1_8@news.onlynews.com> <lqk4uu8d5gip1dr5c0b6igofjsb1u6rk6k@4ax.com> <3DE2A7E8.9D8C5375@attglobal.net> <35c6uu84c99vvgcun82nk9sgedh54lf49v@4ax.com>`

```
Binyamin Dissen opined:

<< I thought a V would be required for the implied decimal point.>>

The "V" characters in PICTURE VPPP9999 and in PICTURE 999PPPPV are
redundant.   And in a PICTURE with both "P" and "V" characters, these are
the only locations in which the "V" characters are meaningful (before "P"
characters that precede digits, or after "P" characters that follow digits).

From the last three standards:

ISO/IEC 1989:2002

13.16.38, PICTURE clause:

Syntax rule 15:  When the symbol 'V' and one or more symbols 'P' are used in
character-string-1, the symbol 'V' shall either immediately precede the
first symbol 'P' or immediately follow the last symbol 'P'.

General Rule 13:  P  The symbol 'P' specifies the location of an assumed
decimal point when that point is not within the number that appears in the
data item. ... [The] assumed decimal point is either to the left of the
string of 'P's if they indicate the leftmost digit positions ... or to the
right of the string of 'P's if they indicate the rightmost ...

General Rule 13:  V"  The symbol 'V' indicates the position of the assumed
decimal point for data alignment purposes. ...


ANSI X3.23-1985 (ISO 1989:2002)

5.9, THE PICTURE CLAUSE

General Rule 8:  ... The scaling position character 'P' can appear only as a
continuous string of 'P's in the leftmost or rightmost digit positions
within a PICTURE character-string; since the scaling position character 'P'
implies an assumed decimal point (to the left of 'P's if 'P's are leftmost
PICTURE symbols and to the right if 'P's are rightmost PICTURE symbols), the
assumed decimal point symbol 'V" is redundant as either the leftmost or
rightmost character within such a PICTURE description.  ...

ANSI X3.23-1974 (ISO 1989 -- 1978)

4.7, THE PICTURE CLAUSE, wording is identical to the '85 standard.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: PIC P

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-03T09:52:09-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<asir09$2l5r$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3de23fb3$1_8@news.onlynews.com> <3de34e75$2_6@news.onlynews.com>`

```
I would expect COBOL compilers compliant with the standards to produce
zeroes in both cases.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

<a id="t-90665517bd"></a>
## Thread: Find the length of a character field — 1 this month / 12 total

_Thread started [2002-11](2002-11.md#t-90665517bd)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Find the length of a character field

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-02T14:48:22-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<asgnvm$u8q$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3ddac473$0$203$ba620e4c@news.skynet.be> <arerpr$i4b$1@nntp-m01.news.aol.com> <arf04m$o4$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein opined:

<<But I believe that in most systems where CLC people have run tests, a
PERFORM VARYING *performs* better.>>

Arguably in some implementations, maybe even most, but definitely not in
all.   It depends on the underlying platform and the language
implementation.  And I would contend that this is an area where both
hardware support and smarter code generation might be useful.

On Unisys MCP/AS systems, I find that the performance of the "hard-coded"
PERFORM variant varuies dramatically with the number of trailing blanks
encountered in the field at execution time, and the fewer the trailing
blanks, the faster the results are obtained; the more, the slower.   Without
some pretty hairy compile-time optimizations on the part of the compiler
implementor, I'd expect that to be a general truism regardless of compiler
or platform.

The implementor of INSPECT as well as FUNCTION REVERSE may have a greater
repertoire of resources on a given system than would be reflected in the
"hard-coded" approach -- while ONE way to implement INSPECT FUNCTION REVERSE
would be to do a PERFORM VARYING BY -1 and compare each character, it's not
necessarily the BEST way, or the ONLY way.

For example, INSPECT FUNCTION REVERSE approach in MCP/AS COBOL85 takes
advantage of hardware support for character scans, and thus for each such
INSPECT a MAXIMUM of two compares against spaces -- one single-character
compare, one scan if the first character of the FUNCTION REVERSE result is
blank -- is done.   The INSPECT FUNCTION REVERSE takes almost exactly the
same amount of time whether a 2000-character field contains spaces, a single
leading nonblank, all nonblanks, or a single trailing blank on that system,
and it's actually the FUNCTION REVERSE that eats the time.

On the particular system on which I do my work, if say only the first 10
characters of that 2000-character field are non-blank, the INSPECT will be
nearly twice as efficient as the hard-coded PERFORM approach at determining
how many trailing blanks there are.  Even for a 30-character field with 20
trailing blanks, the INSPECT tells me noticeably more quickly how many
trailing blanks the field cointains than the "hard-coded" approach does.

   -Chuck Stevens
```

```
Dear community.

Sorry for disturbing but I would like to offer you our free tool to
develop reports: Rpv Printing System.

Rpv is an easy to learn, freeware Windows based application oriented
to those professional developers that need to produce high quality
reports (including barcodes and images if needed) using a Windows
programming language, a DOS programming language or any language.

To obtain a report, Rpv reads a simple ascii file, then Rpv transforms
it into a professional report.If you know how to create an ascii file
using your programming language, then you know how to program an Rpv
report.

The tool is available in FREE, Plus and PRO editions in english,
spanish and portuguese. Italian and Greek soon in
http://www.rpvreport.com.

Feel free to contact me if you need more information or some help
about Rpv.

Thanks for your time.

Lito
```

## Thread: Concatenate in Easytrieve — 2 this month / 5 total

_Thread started [2002-11](2002-11.md#t-58ca6e3440)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Concatenate in Easytrieve

- **From:** brianc <splashNOSPAM@rainpuddle.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-02T22:44:51-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<splashNOSPAM-71CCE6.22445002122002@news.drizzle.com>`
- **References:** `<5bd25fd1.0211250913.a4e93b1@posting.google.com> <b7k8uug55q1aj69ia4dctt65otunqe9ms7@4ax.com> <5bd25fd1.0211270634.79a5a880@posting.google.com>`

```
In article <5bd25fd1.0211270634.79a5a880@posting.google.com>,
 EmrolGould@YAHOO.COM (Emrol Gould) wrote:

> Thanks for responding,
> 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Emrol

Chapter 16 (Advanced Techniques) of the CA-Easytrieve/Plus Application 
Guide (v6.2) has the following:

This is an example of a macro which concatenates two fields into one, 
with variable spacing between fields. Following is the macro definition:

* EXAMPLE 16.13A
*
MACRO RECEIVE SPACE PART2
*
* CONCAT MACRO
* FORMAT:
* %CONCAT RECEIVE N PART2
*
* IT PERFORMS AS:
* RECEIVE = RECEIVE +SPC(N) + PART2
*
DEFINE CONCAT-HOLD W 254 A
DEFINE CONCAT-SCAN CONCAT-HOLD 1 A INDEX CONCAT-NDX
DEFINE CONCAT-LENGTH W 2 P 0
CONCAT-NDX = 253                         . * START AT END
CONCAT-HOLD = &RECEIVE                   . * COPY PART1
DO WHILE CONCAT-SCAN EQ ' ' AND CONCAT-NDX GE 0
  CONCAT-NDX = CONCAT-NDX - 1            . * FIND 1ST #BLANK
END-DO
CONCAT-NDX = CONCAT-NDX + 1 + &SPACE     . * DO SPACES FACTOR
CONCAT-LENGTH = 253 - CONCAT-NDX
MOVE &PART2 TO CONCAT-SCAN CONCAT-LENGTH . * MOVE PART 2 AFTER
&RECEIVE = CONCAT-HOLD                   . * GIVE BACK TO USER

The CONCAT macro is exercised by the following CA-Easytrieve/Plus job:

  1 *
  2 * EXAMPLE 16.13B
  3 *
  4 *
  5 * THIS EXAMPLE WILL CONCATENATE TWO FIELDS TOGETHER
  6 * THE FORMAT OF THE MACRO IS:
  7 *
  8 * %CONCAT PART1 N PART2
  9 *
 10 * WHERE N IS THE NUMBER OF SPACES TO BE INSERTED BETWEEN
 11 * THE TWO PARTS
 12 *
 13 * THE FIRST PARAMETER (PART1) IS USED AS THE RECEIVING FIELD
 14 * THE THIRD PARAMETER (PART2) MAY BE A LITERAL BUT
 15 * ENSURE THAT THE PROPER SYNTAX RULES ARE FOLLOWED FOR PASSING
 16 * QUOTES (IE TO PASS A COMMA, ''',''' WOULD BE USED)
 17 *
 18 JOB INPUT NULL
 19 DEFINE WHOLE-THING W 40 A
 20 DEFINE SECOND-PART W 40 A
 21 DEFINE PART1       W 10 A VALUE 'SEE HOW'
 22 DEFINE PART2       W 10 A VALUE 'IT PUTS'
 23 DEFINE PART3       W 10 A VALUE 'THE PARTS'
 24 DEFINE PART4       W 10 A VALUE 'TOGETH'
 25 DEFINE PART5       W 10 A VALUE 'ER'
 26   WHOLE-THING = PART1
 27   SECOND-PART = PART2
 28   DISPLAY NEWPAGE WHOLE-THING
 29   PERFORM CONCAT-SPACE
 30   DISPLAY WHOLE-THING
 31   SECOND-PART = PART3
 32   PERFORM CONCAT-SPACE
 33   DISPLAY WHOLE-THING
 34   SECOND-PART = PART4
 35   PERFORM CONCAT-SPACE
 36   DISPLAY WHOLE-THING
 37   SECOND-PART = PART5
 38   PERFORM CONCAT-NOSPACE
 39   DISPLAY WHOLE-THING
 40   STOP
 41 CONCAT-SPACE. PROC
 43 *
 44   %CONCAT WHOLE-THING 1 SECOND-PART
 71 *
 72 END-PROC
 73 *
 74 CONCAT-NOSPACE. PROC
 76 *
 77   %CONCAT WHOLE-THING 0 SECOND-PART
104 *
105 END-PROC

------------------------------------------------------------------------
SEE HOW
SEE HOW IT PUTS
SEE HOW IT PUTS THE PARTS
SEE HOW IT PUTS THE PARTS TOGETH
SEE HOW IT PUTS THE PARTS TOGETHER


Hope this helps!

//brian
```

```
We would like to announce txt2pdf 6.2 version. 
http://www.sanface.com/txt2pdf.html
txt2pdf is shareware; it is a very flexible and powerful PERL5 script
that converts text files to PDF format files, so you can use it in every
operating systems supported by PERL5, including MPE/iX, OpenVMS and
EPOC.
It's simple to design background like invoices, orders etc.
If you prefer we also distribute executables for Windows, Linux,
Solaris, AIX, HP-UX, and Mac OS X. Inside the Windows version is Visual
txt2pdf, a VB GUI.
It's simple to use txt2pdf from your cobol applications to convert the
final textual reports into nice PDFs e.g.
http://www.sanface.com/pdf/Purchase_Order.pdf
http://www.sanface.com/pdf/invoice.pdf
http://www.sanface.com/pdf/hfmus.pdf
http://www.sanface.com/pdf/heraldbill.pdf


What's new in this version

Chinese font support.

Test txt2pdf 6.2!
You can find it at http://www.sanface.com/txt2pdf.html
```

## Thread: web interface — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### web interface

- **From:** matt <info@rudmann.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-03T13:33:24-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3decfa43$0$17645$bbae4d71@news.net-link.net>`

```
Hi group,

I am looking for ideas regarding how to convert our COBOL program screens
into web browser forms.

Can anyone provide me with lead(s)?

Thanks!
-Matt
```

#### ↳ Re: web interface

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-12-03T17:49:36-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0212031749.18c20482@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3decfa43$0$17645$bbae4d71@news.net-link.net>`

```
matt <info@rudmann.com> wrote 

> I am looking for ideas regarding how to convert our COBOL program screens
> into web browser forms.

If these are text mode ACCEPT/DISPLAY and you want to move to Cobol
CGI then there will be some radical changes required.

CGI in Cobol is quite easy to do, even without using proprietry
libraries, but the interaction is stateless.  Each GET or POST from a
form starts a new iteration of the program and may be from different
users.  It is usually necessary to store some state reference in a
HIDDEN field and/or in a cookie so that the appropriate state can be
recovered and the transaction continued.

You should probably use templates for the pages and merge the data
into these rather than directly outputting html, it makes it so much
easier to change the layout without changing the program(s).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: web interface

- **From:** Joseph Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2002-12-04T19:57:07+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<041220021957079929%josephk@iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<3decfa43$0$17645$bbae4d71@news.net-link.net> <217e491a.0212031749.18c20482@posting.google.com>`

```
In article <217e491a.0212031749.18c20482@posting.google.com>, Richard
<riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

> CGI in Cobol is quite easy to do, even without using proprietry
> libraries, but the interaction is stateless. 

Of course, anyone who has programmed CICs transactions, will find this
a snap. Except that HTTP servers offer NONE of the tools that CICS
offers to help in the recovery of state information.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: web interface

- **From:** "Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@nospam.microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-04T15:59:23
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<asl8l0$h2j$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<3decfa43$0$17645$bbae4d71@news.net-link.net> <217e491a.0212031749.18c20482@posting.google.com> <041220021957079929%josephk@iinet.net.au>`

```
"Joseph Katnic" <josephk@iinet.net.au> wrote in message
news:041220021957079929%josephk@iinet.net.au...
> In article <217e491a.0212031749.18c20482@posting.google.com>, Richard
> <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Joe.

That's not strictly true, HTTP provides cookies as a means of maintaining
state. If you don't like cookies then hidden fields can also be used. If you
need to maintain a lot of state information, put a key in your hidden field
and use that with an indexed file to store your temporary results.

It's not rocket science. As you do say though, it's a snap - particularly
when working with Net Express.
```

#### ↳ Re: web interface

- **From:** "Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@nospam.microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-04T12:37:08
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<askspm$fuu$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<3decfa43$0$17645$bbae4d71@news.net-link.net>`

```
Look at Net Express from Micro Focus. It contains the tools to allow you to
do what you require.

www.microfocus.com

news:3decfa43$0$17645$bbae4d71@news.net-link.net...

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
Under Micro Focus Net Express (my compiler), the following errors are
generated:

Rebuilding         c:\tmp\ASSIG4.cbl
COBCH0012S Operand WS-AVERAGE-INCREASE is not declared :
C:\Tmp\ASSIG4.cbl(274,58)
COBCH0012S Operand WS-AVERAGE-INCREASE is not declared :
C:\Tmp\ASSIG4.cbl(303,48)
COBCH0348S Procedure name 1241-WROTE-HEADINGS undeclared, line 282 (first
usage) : C:\Tmp\ASSIG4.cbl(282,24)
Rebuild complete with errors

From looking at the source, 1241-WROTE-HEADINGS should be
1240-WRITE-HEADINGS.

WS-AVERAGE-INCREASE is, as the error states, is not declared as a dataitem.

Gazaloo.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help Please!

- **From:** lcjarvis@sbic.co.za (Lee)
- **Date:** 2002-12-05T06:08:49-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2c4867c8.0212050608.2e0a64fb@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi 
       01  RATE-TABLES.
           05  TAB-RATE-DATA.
               10 FILLER           PIC 9(5) VALUE 15000.
               10 FILLER           PIC 9V99 VALUE 2.50.
               10 FILLER           PIC 9(5) VALUE 10000.
               10 FILLER           PIC 9V99 VALUE 1.75.
      >        10 FILLER           PIC 9(5) VALUE 1.00.
      >        10 FILLER           PIC 9(5) VALUE 00000.
               10 FILLER           PIC 9V99 VALUE 0.50.
               10 FILLER           PIC 9(5) VALUE 00000.
               10 FILLER           PIC 9V99 VALUE 0.00.
           05  TAB-RATE-TABLE REDEFINES TAB-RATE-DATA OCCURS 5 TIMES.
               10 TAB-SALES        PIC 9(5).
               10 TAB-RATE         PIC 9V99.

Are you sure the table above is correct - the redefines would be a problem.

WS-AVERAGE-INCREASE  isnt defined as a data name
1241-wrote-headings also not defined

Hope this helps you
CHeers
Lee
```

```
           VALUE "*** TOTAL OLD COMMISSION PAID WAS".
           05  FILLER      PIC X(2)    VALUE SPACES.
           05  TOC-PAID    PIC $,$$$,$$$.99.

       01  TOTAL-LINE2.
           05  FILLER      PIC X(27)   VALUE SPACES.
           05  FILLER      PIC X(33)
           VALUE "*** TOTAL NEW COMMISSION PAID WAS".
           05  FILLER      PIC X(2)    VALUE SPACES.
           05  TNC-PAID    PIC $,$$$,$$$.99.

       01 TOTAL-LINE3.
           05  FILLER      PIC X(31)   VALUE SPACES.
           05  FILLER      PIC X(30)
           VALUE "AVERAGE PERCENTAGE INCREASE OF".
           05  FILLER      PIC X(7)    VALUE SPACES.
           05  AVERAGE     PIC ZZ9.99.
           05  FILLER      PIC X       VALUE SPACES.
           05  FILLER      PIC X(3)    VALUE "%".

       01  RATE-TABLES.
           05  TAB-RATE-DATA.
               10 FILLER           PIC 9(5) VALUE 15000.
               10 FILLER           PIC 9V99 VALUE 2.50.
               10 FILLER           PIC 9(5) VALUE 10000.
               10 FILLER           PIC 9V99 VALUE 1.75.
               10 FILLER           PIC 9(5) VALUE 1.00.
               10 FILLER           PIC 9(5) VALUE 00000.
               10 FILLER           PIC 9V99 VALUE 0.50.
               10 FILLER           PIC 9(5) VALUE 00000.
               10 FILLER           PIC 9V99 VALUE 0.00.
           05  TAB-RATE-TABLE REDEFINES TAB-RATE-DATA OCCURS 5 TIMES.
               10 TAB-SALES        PIC 9(5).
               10 TAB-RATE         PIC 9V99.

       01  SUB-COMMISSION.
           05  SUB-COMM            PIC 9.
           05  SUB-RATE            PIC 9.

       01  DEPARTMENT-TABLE.
           05  DEPARTMENT-TABLE-WS.
               10      PIC X(17)   VALUE "01WOMENS DEPART".
               10      PIC X(17)   VALUE "01HOSEHOLD DEP".
               10      PIC X(17)   VALUE "03SPORTING GOODS".
               10      PIC X(17)   VALUE "04AUTOM. GOODS".
               10      PIC X(17)   VALUE "05HARDWARE".
               10      PIC X(17)   VALUE "06SIGHT & SOUND".
               10      PIC X(17)   VALUE "07MENS DEPART".
               10      PIC X(17)   VALUE "08PETS".
               10      PIC X(17)   VALUE "09TOYLAND".
           05  DEPT-TABLE-RED  REDEFINES DEPARTMENT-TABLE-WS
                   OCCURS 9 TIMES.
                   10 DEPT-NUMBER      PIC XX.
                   10 DEPT-NAME        PIC X(15).

       01  SUB-DEPARTMENT-NAME.
           05  SUB-DEPT-NAME           PIC 99  VALUE 1.

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.

       1000-MAIN.
           PERFORM 1100-START-UP.
           PERFORM 1200-PROCESS
                           UNTIL WS-MORE-RECORDS = "NO".
           PERFORM 1300-FINISH-UP.
           STOP RUN.

       1100-START-UP.
           OPEN INPUT  SALES-FILE.
           OPEN OUTPUT PRINT-FILE.

           ACCEPT H1-DATE FROM DATE.

           PERFORM 1110-READ.
           IF WS-MORE-RECORDS = "YES"
           NEXT SENTENCE.

       1200-PROCESS.

           MOVE "NO" TO WS-FOUND.
           PERFORM 1210-DEPARTMENT-NAME
               VARYING SUB-DEPT-NAME
                   FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL
                       SUB-DEPT-NAME > 9
                       OR
                   WS-FOUND = "YES"

           MOVE "NO" TO WS-FOUND.
           PERFORM 1220-COMMISSION-PERCENTAGE
               VARYING SUB-COMM
                   FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL
                       SUB-COMM > 4
                   OR
                       WS-FOUND = "YES".

           PERFORM 1230-COMPUTE-SECTION.
           PERFORM 1240-WRITE-SALES-LINE.
           PERFORM 1110-READ.

       1300-FINISH-UP.
           PERFORM 1310-TOTALS.
           CLOSE SALES-FILE.
           CLOSE PRINT-FILE.

       1110-READ.
           READ SALES-FILE
           AT END
           MOVE "NO" TO WS-MORE-RECORDS.

       1210-DEPARTMENT-NAME.
           IF SR-DEPT-NUMBER = DEPT-NUMBER (SUB-DEPT-NAME)
           MOVE DEPT-NAME (SUB-DEPT-NAME) TO DTL-DEPT-NAME
           MOVE 10 TO SUB-DEPT-NAME
           MOVE "YES" TO WS-FOUND
           ELSE
           NEXT SENTENCE.

       1220-COMMISSION-PERCENTAGE.
           IF SR-YTD-SALES > TAB-SALES (SUB-COMM)

           MOVE TAB-RATE (SUB-COMM) TO WS-NEW-COMM-INCREASE
           MOVE 5 TO SUB-COMM
           MOVE "YES" TO WS-FOUND
           ELSE
           NEXT SENTENCE.

       1230-COMPUTE-SECTION.

           COMPUTE WS-NEW-COMM-RATE = SR-COMM + WS-NEW-COMM-INCREASE.
           COMPUTE
               WS-NEW-COMM = (SR-YTD-SALES * WS-NEW-COMM-RATE) / 100.
           COMPUTE WS-OLD-COMM = ( SR-YTD-SALES * SR-COMM) / 100.

           ADD WS-NEW-COMM-INCREASE TO WS-AVERAGE-INCREASE.
           ADD 1 TO WS-COUNTER.
           ADD WS-OLD-COMM TO WS-OLD-COMMISSION.
           ADD WS-NEW-COMM TO WS-NEW-COMMISSION.

       1240-WRITE-SALES-LINE.

           IF WS-LINE-COUNT > 40
               PERFORM 1241-WROTE-HEADINGS
           ELSE
           NEXT SENTENCE.

           MOVE SR-STORE-NUMBER        TO DTL-STORE-NUMBER
           MOVE SR-EMPL-NAME           TO DTL-EMPL-NAME
           MOVE SR-YTD-SALES           TO DTL-YTD-SALES
           MOVE SR-COMM                TO DTL-COMM
           MOVE WS-NEW-COMM-RATE       TO DTL-NEW-COMM-RATE
           MOVE WS-NEW-COMM            TO DTL-NEW-COMM
           MOVE WS-OLD-COMM            TO DTL-OLD-COMM

           WRITE PRINT-REC FROM DETAIL-LINE AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINES

           ADD 1                       TO WS-LINE-COUNT.

       1310-TOTALS.

           MOVE WS-OLD-COMMISSION  TO TOC-PAID
           MOVE WS-NEW-COMMISSION  TO TNC-PAID

           COMPUTE AVERAGE = WS-AVERAGE-INCREASE / WS-COUNTER
           WRITE PRINT-REC     FROM TOTAL-LINE1 AFTER ADVANCING 3 LINE.

           WRITE PRINT-REC     FROM TOTAL-LINE2 AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINE.

           WRITE PRINT-REC     FROM TOTAL-LINE3 AFTER ADVANCING 3 LINE.

       1241-WRITE-HEADINGS.

           ADD 1               TO WS-PAGE-COUNT.
           MOVE WS-PAGE-COUNT  TO H1-PAGE

           WRITE PRINT-REC FROM HDG1 AFTER ADVANCING PAGE
           WRITE PRINT-REC FROM HDG2 AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINE.
           WRITE PRINT-REC FROM HDG3 AFTER ADVANCING 2 LINE.
           WRITE PRINT-REC FROM HDG4 AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINES.
           MOVE 5                  TO WS-LINE-COUNT.
```

## Thread: Problem - Printing foreign characters on Customer Statement — 4 msgs

### Problem - Printing foreign characters on Customer Statement

- **From:** lcjarvis@sbic.co.za (Lee)
- **Date:** 2002-12-04T02:32:44-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2c4867c8.0212040232.126b796@posting.google.com>`

```
I would like to know if and how it's possible to print special
characters for example: ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½.

I have written a Cobol program to print statements for customers and
these characters do not print. I am using a Mannesmann Tally MT661
printer.

However if I edit the output before printing the data is clearly there
as it is intended. If I browse (not editing mode) the characters
cannot be seen.
 
I have also downloaded the printfile to my PC and opened it in MS
Excel - and the data can be seen!!

Any ideas/solutions to my problem?

I Am using MVS Cobol on a OS 390 Mainframe.
```

#### ↳ Re: Problem - Printing foreign characters on Customer Statement

- **From:** Alistair Maclean <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2002-12-04T15:46:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<FMAC2SAXNi79EwUF@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<2c4867c8.0212040232.126b796@posting.google.com>`

```
It is a long time since I had to mess with pc based printers that were
not compatible with mainframe languages but my recollection is that
printers have a predefined character set which may not be compatible
with your mainframe's defined character set. In the past this has
required either a new printer chain (did I get that right?), a new font
cartridge, a new downloadable font or the insertion of character code
sequences to invoke another font. Check your printer's manual and you
will probably find that the default character set does not contain the
correct printing character for the hexadecimal representation that you
are sending. 


In article <2c4867c8.0212040232.126b796@posting.google.com>, Lee
<lcjarvis@sbic.co.za> writes
>I would like to know if and how it's possible to print special
>characters for example: ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½.
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>I Am using MVS Cobol on a OS 390 Mainframe.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Problem - Printing foreign characters on Customer Statement

- **From:** Bob Rasmussen <ras@anzio.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-05T10:02:35-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Pine.LNX.4.42.0212050948050.29725-100000@nimbus.anzio.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<FMAC2SAXNi79EwUF@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<2c4867c8.0212040232.126b796@posting.google.com> <FMAC2SAXNi79EwUF@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```
> In article <2c4867c8.0212040232.126b796@posting.google.com>, Lee
> <lcjarvis@sbic.co.za> writes
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> >I Am using MVS Cobol on a OS 390 Mainframe.

In what character set (codepage) have you encoded the special characters?
Big-5? GB2312? Unicode?

The first step in this process is understanding character sets. Just like
we all know that in ASCII a hex 41 represents an "A", we have to have some
assumption about what a hex C0 represents, for instance. There are various
sets of these, called codepages. For Far East characters, there are
multi-byte codepages, because it takes more than one byte to represent all
the possible characters. Then there's Unicode, that can represent all of
these characters (Cyrillic, Japanese, Greek, etc.) in one scheme.

A printer operates in a particular character set. You can often change its
current character set with an escape sequence. But if you're trying to
print a character that isn't built into the printer, then the printer
needs some help -- some software needs to either preload a soft font into
the printer, or print those characters as bitmaps. This is what the
printer driver in Windows does.

One way to solve your problem is to use the capabilities in Windows, by
routing your print job through a Windows PC. We have a couple of products
that can do this. AnzioWin, our Windows telnet client, has passthrough
print that supports various encodings. Our freestanding Print Wizard
product can also handle special characters. Both do automatic font
selection. More information is available on our web site below, or you can
contact me privately.

Regards,
....Bob Rasmussen,   President,   Rasmussen Software, Inc.

personal e-mail: ras@anzio.com
 company e-mail: rsi@anzio.com
          voice: (US) 503-624-0360 (9:00-6:00 Pacific Time)
            fax: (US) 503-624-0760
            web: http://www.anzio.com
```

- **Date:** 2002-12-05T21:53:10-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2c4867c8.0212052153.10ade4b1@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<2c4867c8.0212040232.126b796@posting.google.com> <FMAC2SAXNi79EwUF@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <Pine.LNX.4.42.0212050948050.29725-100000@nimbus.anzio.com>`

```
Tks for your input.
You know the problem happens before I even print the statement.
After submitting my JCL that produces the report (on the mainframe), I
can view the output before it prints.
When I browse that output the characters are not displayed hence the
printing will be the same in results. When I edit the output I can see

I have spoken to a colleague of mine about this - but I am not sure if
there is a MVSCobol/Mainframe setting concerning these character sets
that restricts the printing of accented characters. This is what my
colleague said he would find out.
Anyway, I hope that somehow this information will be realised soon.
Once again tks 4 all ur input
ciao
Lee
```

## Thread: Java vs. COBOL - Another View — 4 msgs

### Java vs. COBOL - Another View

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-04T10:14:03-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2oecnU3crt1QtHOgXTWc3Q@giganews.com>`

```
There is a court case going on right now; Sun is demanding that Microsoft
include the latest Sun-approved version of Java in Microsoft's operating
system distributions (XP, etc.).

If Sun loses, and Microsoft declines to distribute Java, it would seem Java
use would decline.

Anyway, the future of Java may hinge on something completely unrelated to
Java's intrinsic worth.

As an aside, I can't see by what legal method Sun could, with a straight
face, demand inclusion in a competitor's distribution.
```

#### ↳ Re: Java vs. COBOL - Another View

- **From:** Donald Tees <Donald_Tees@Sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-12-04T11:27:59-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DEE573F.7020205@Sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<2oecnU3crt1QtHOgXTWc3Q@giganews.com>`

```
JerryMouse wrote:

>There is a court case going on right now; Sun is demanding that Microsoft
>include the latest Sun-approved version of Java in Microsoft's operating
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
 The entire evolution of Java, and the making of it public, was based on 
there being a common language.  MS agreed to that when they used Sun's 
copyright. It they do not, they are in Copyright Infringement.

Donald
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Java vs. COBOL - Another View

- **From:** Alistair Maclean <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<DtmTeDANPj79EwHK@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```
<Donald_Tees@Sympatico.ca> writes

What Jerry Mouse was referring to is that MS have chosen to remove
support for the Java VM from Internet Explorer necessitating a separate
download and install, at your own inconvenience. MS are not infringing
on Sun's rights but may be indulging in some restrictive/non-competitive
practice.

As a poisoned dwarf (see my signature) I await the more immediately
imminent death of Java, before Cobol, with a big smirk across my
plastered physiognomy.
```

```

"Dan J" <dali@cogeco.ca> wrote in message news:...
> I'm a student. I've got 6 comliation errors that I can not solve for the
> life of me. Can anybody help? Please, Please!!!
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. SALES.
      **********************************************
      * BY:DALIBOR JOVASEVIC 313578                *
      * ASSIGNMENT 4                               *
      * INPUT - READ CM2002S                       *
      * SALES COMMISSION REPORT                    *
      **********************************************

       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.

       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.

           SELECT  SALES-FILE  ASSIGN TO DISK "D:\COBOL\CM2002S.DAT"
                               ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.

           SELECT  PRINT-FILE  ASSIGN TO PRINTER "D:\COBOL\A4.RPT"
                               ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.

       DATA DIVISION.

       FILE SECTION.

       FD SALES-FILE
           RECORD  CONTAINS    80 CHARACTERS
           DATA    RECORD      IS SALES-REC.

       01  SALES-REC.
           05  SR-STORE-NUMBER     PIC XX.
           05  SR-DEPT-NUMBER      PIC XX.
           05  SR-EMPL-NUMBER      PIC XXX.
           05  SR-EMPL-NAME        PIC X(20).
           05  SR-YTD-SALES        PIC 9(5)V99.
           05  SR-CUR-SALES        PIC 9(5)V99.
           05  SR-COMM             PIC 99.
           05  SR-EMPL-CODE        PIC XX.
           05  SR-YTD-RETURNS      PIC 9(4)V99.
           05  SR-CUR-RECORDS      PIC 9(4)V99.
           05  FILLER              PIC X(23).

       FD  PRINT-FILE
           RECORD  CONTAINS    132 CHARACTERS
           DATA    RECORDS     IS  PRINT-REC.

       01  PRINT-REC               PIC X(132).

       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  WS-SWITCHERS.
           05  WS-MORE-RECORDS     PIC XXX             VALUE "YES".
           05  WS-FOUND            PIC XXX             VALUE "NO".
           05  WS-NEW-COMM-RATE    PIC 99V99           VALUE 0.
           05  WS-OLD-COMM         PIC 9999V99         VALUE 0.
           05  WS-NEW-COMM         PIC 9999V99         VALUE 0.
           05  WS-NEW-COMM-INCREASE PIC 9V99           VALUE 0.

       01  WS-COUNTERS             COMP-3.
           05  WS-RECORD-COUNT     PIC S99             VALUE +0.
           05  WS-PAGE-COUNT       PIC S99             VALUE +0.
           05  WS-LINE-COUNT       PIC S99             VALUE +60.

           05  WS-NEW-COMMISSION   PIC S9(6)V99        VALUE 0.

           05  WS-TOT-YTD-SALES    PIC S9(7)V99   COMP-3   VALUE +0.

           05  FILLER      PIC X(62)   VALUE "COMMISSION SUMMARY".

       01  HDG4.

           05  FILLER      PIC X(4)    VALUE SPACES.
           05  FILLER      PIC X(4)    VALUE "NAME".

       01  DETAIL-LINE.
           05  FILLER           PIC X(7)   VALUE SPACES.
           05  DTL-STORE-NUMBER PIC XX.
           05  FILLER           PIC X(6)   VALUE SPACES.
           05  DTL-DEPT-NAME    PIC X(15).
           05  FILLER           PIC X(4)   VALUE SPACES.
           05  DTL-EMPL-NAME    PIC X(20).
           05  FILLER           PIC X(5)   VALUE SPACES.
           05  DTL-YTD-SALES    PIC $Z,ZZZ.99.
           05  FILLER           PIC X      VALUE SPACES.
           05  DTL-COMM         PIC $$,$$$.99.
           05  FILLER           PIC X      VALUE SPACES.
           05  DTL-NEW-COMM-RATE PIC $$,$$$.99.
           05  FILLER           PIC X(6)   VALUE SPACES.
           05  DTL-NEW-COMM     PIC $$,$$$.99.
           05  FILLER           PIC X(5).
           05  DTL-OLD-COMM     PIC $$,$$$.99.

       01  TOTAL-LINE1.
           05  FILLER      PIC X(27)   VALUE SPACES.
           05  FILLER      PIC X(33)
           VALUE "*** TOTAL OLD COMMISSION PAID WAS".
           05  FILLER      PIC X(2)    VALUE SPACES.
           05  TOC-PAID    PIC $,$$$,$$$.99.

       01  TOTAL-LINE2.
           05  FILLER      PIC X(27)   VALUE SPACES.
           05  FILLER      PIC X(33)
           VALUE "*** TOTAL NEW COMMISSION PAID WAS".
           05  FILLER      PIC X(2)    VALUE SPACES.
           05  TNC-PAID    PIC $,$$$,$$$.99.

       01 TOTAL-LINE3.
           05  FILLER      PIC X(31)   VALUE SPACES.
           05  FILLER      PIC X(30)
           VALUE "AVERAGE PERCENTAGE INCREASE OF".
           05  FILLER      PIC X(7)    VALUE SPACES.
           05  AVERAGE     PIC ZZ9.99.
           05  FILLER      PIC X       VALUE SPACES.
           05  FILLER      PIC X(3)    VALUE "%".

       01  RATE-TABLES.
           05  TAB-RATE-DATA.
               10 FILLER           PIC 9(5) VALUE 15000.
               10 FILLER           PIC 9V99 VALUE 2.50.
               10 FILLER           PIC 9(5) VALUE 10000.
               10 FILLER           PIC 9V99 VALUE 1.75.
               10 FILLER           PIC 9(5) VALUE 1.00.
               10 FILLER           PIC 9(5) VALUE 00000.
               10 FILLER           PIC 9V99 VALUE 0.50.
               10 FILLER           PIC 9(5) VALUE 00000.
               10 FILLER           PIC 9V99 VALUE 0.00.
           05  TAB-RATE-TABLE REDEFINES TAB-RATE-DATA OCCURS 5 TIMES.
               10 TAB-SALES        PIC 9(5).
               10 TAB-RATE         PIC 9V99.

       01  SUB-COMMISSION.
           05  SUB-COMM            PIC 9.
           05  SUB-RATE            PIC 9.

       01  DEPARTMENT-TABLE.
           05  DEPARTMENT-TABLE-WS.
               10      PIC X(17)   VALUE "01WOMENS DEPART".
               10      PIC X(17)   VALUE "01HOSEHOLD DEP".
               10      PIC X(17)   VALUE "03SPORTING GOODS".
               10      PIC X(17)   VALUE "04AUTOM. GOODS".
               10      PIC X(17)   VALUE "05HARDWARE".
               10      PIC X(17)   VALUE "06SIGHT & SOUND".
               10      PIC X(17)   VALUE "07MENS DEPART".
               10      PIC X(17)   VALUE "08PETS".
               10      PIC X(17)   VALUE "09TOYLAND".
           05  DEPT-TABLE-RED  REDEFINES DEPARTMENT-TABLE-WS
                   OCCURS 9 TIMES.
                   10 DEPT-NUMBER      PIC XX.
                   10 DEPT-NAME        PIC X(15).

       01  SUB-DEPARTMENT-NAME.
           05  SUB-DEPT-NAME           PIC 99  VALUE 1.

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.

       1000-MAIN.
           PERFORM 1100-START-UP.
           PERFORM 1200-PROCESS
                           UNTIL WS-MORE-RECORDS = "NO".
           PERFORM 1300-FINISH-UP.
           STOP RUN.

       1100-START-UP.
           OPEN INPUT  SALES-FILE.
           OPEN OUTPUT PRINT-FILE.

           ACCEPT H1-DATE FROM DATE.

           PERFORM 1110-READ.
           IF WS-MORE-RECORDS = "YES"
           NEXT SENTENCE.

       1200-PROCESS.

           MOVE "NO" TO WS-FOUND.
           PERFORM 1210-DEPARTMENT-NAME
               VARYING SUB-DEPT-NAME
                   FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL
                       SUB-DEPT-NAME > 9
                       OR
                   WS-FOUND = "YES"

           MOVE "NO" TO WS-FOUND.
           PERFORM 1220-COMMISSION-PERCENTAGE
               VARYING SUB-COMM
                   FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL
                       SUB-COMM > 4
                   OR
                       WS-FOUND = "YES".

           PERFORM 1230-COMPUTE-SECTION.
           PERFORM 1240-WRITE-SALES-LINE.
           PERFORM 1110-READ.

       1300-FINISH-UP.
           PERFORM 1310-TOTALS.
           CLOSE SALES-FILE.
           CLOSE PRINT-FILE.

       1110-READ.
           READ SALES-FILE
           AT END
           MOVE "NO" TO WS-MORE-RECORDS.

       1210-DEPARTMENT-NAME.
           IF SR-DEPT-NUMBER = DEPT-NUMBER (SUB-DEPT-NAME)
           MOVE DEPT-NAME (SUB-DEPT-NAME) TO DTL-DEPT-NAME
           MOVE 10 TO SUB-DEPT-NAME
           MOVE "YES" TO WS-FOUND
           ELSE
           NEXT SENTENCE.

       1220-COMMISSION-PERCENTAGE.
           IF SR-YTD-SALES > TAB-SALES (SUB-COMM)

           MOVE TAB-RATE (SUB-COMM) TO WS-NEW-COMM-INCREASE
           MOVE 5 TO SUB-COMM
           MOVE "YES" TO WS-FOUND
           ELSE
           NEXT SENTENCE.

       1230-COMPUTE-SECTION.

           COMPUTE WS-NEW-COMM-RATE = SR-COMM + WS-NEW-COMM-INCREASE.
           COMPUTE
               WS-NEW-COMM = (SR-YTD-SALES * WS-NEW-COMM-RATE) / 100.
           COMPUTE WS-OLD-COMM = ( SR-YTD-SALES * SR-COMM) / 100.

           ADD WS-NEW-COMM-INCREASE TO WS-AVERAGE-INCREASE.
           ADD 1 TO WS-COUNTER.
           ADD WS-OLD-COMM TO WS-OLD-COMMISSION.
           ADD WS-NEW-COMM TO WS-NEW-COMMISSION.

       1240-WRITE-SALES-LINE.

           IF WS-LINE-COUNT > 40
               PERFORM 1241-WROTE-HEADINGS
           ELSE
           NEXT SENTENCE.

           MOVE SR-STORE-NUMBER        TO DTL-STORE-NUMBER
           MOVE SR-EMPL-NAME           TO DTL-EMPL-NAME
           MOVE SR-YTD-SALES           TO DTL-YTD-SALES
           MOVE SR-COMM                TO DTL-COMM
           MOVE WS-NEW-COMM-RATE       TO DTL-NEW-COMM-RATE
           MOVE WS-NEW-COMM            TO DTL-NEW-COMM
           MOVE WS-OLD-COMM            TO DTL-OLD-COMM

           WRITE PRINT-REC FROM DETAIL-LINE AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINES

           ADD 1                       TO WS-LINE-COUNT.

       1310-TOTALS.

           MOVE WS-OLD-COMMISSION  TO TOC-PAID
           MOVE WS-NEW-COMMISSION  TO TNC-PAID

           COMPUTE AVERAGE = WS-AVERAGE-INCREASE / WS-COUNTER
           WRITE PRINT-REC     FROM TOTAL-LINE1 AFTER ADVANCING 3 LINE.

           WRITE PRINT-REC     FROM TOTAL-LINE2 AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINE.

           WRITE PRINT-REC     FROM TOTAL-LINE3 AFTER ADVANCING 3 LINE.

       1241-WRITE-HEADINGS.

           ADD 1               TO WS-PAGE-COUNT.
           MOVE WS-PAGE-COUNT  TO H1-PAGE

           WRITE PRINT-REC FROM HDG1 AFTER ADVANCING PAGE
           WRITE PRINT-REC FROM HDG2 AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINE.
           WRITE PRINT-REC FROM HDG3 AFTER ADVANCING 2 LINE.
           WRITE PRINT-REC FROM HDG4 AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINES.
           MOVE 5                  TO WS-LINE-COUNT.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help!

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-05T08:12:01-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<25Ccneb6Wekxw3KgXTWcpw@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<GvqH9.32785$L47.3775200@read2.cgocable.net>`

```

"Dan J" <dali@cogeco.ca> wrote in message
news:GvqH9.32785$L47.3775200@read2.cgocable.net...
>
> "Dan J" <dali@cogeco.ca> wrote in message news:...
…[346 more quoted lines elided]…
>            MOVE 5                  TO WS-LINE-COUNT.

It would help if you told us what these compiler errors were!

I bet if you fix the first two errors - having to do with redefinition of
your tables - the rest would go away.

Here's a hint: Add up the bytes in a table. Add up the bytes in the
redefinition of that table. They should be (except for very advanced
techniques) the same. Yours are not.

Also, you can lose the RECORD CONTAINS and DATA RECORD IS clauses. They're
comments.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help!

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-06T12:47:02-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<asqrb9$9g2$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<GvqH9.32785$L47.3775200@read2.cgocable.net> <25Ccneb6Wekxw3KgXTWcpw@giganews.com> <217e491a.0212051602.2770b083@posting.google.com> <MmmdnQePH9wygm2gXTWcrg@giganews.com> <3DF0EA72.BD177AA8@Azonic.co.nz> <69-cnYZGsJZRem2gXTWcoQ@giganews.com>`

```
Jerry,
  I don't quite agree with you on this one.

I think that it is entirely "reasonable" for a compiler to REQUIRE (as the
ANSI/ISO Standards do, have, and probably always will) that the LARGEST
"variation" of redefined storage be defined first.

There is no problem (in any ANSI/ISO conforming compiler) with putting the
largest variation first and redefining it as SMALLER variations - but if you
put a smaller one first, you aren't really "redefining" existing storage -
but allocating MORE storage and redefining it.  Certainly a compiler COULD
be "forced to" (and I know some do) "read ahead" to find the largest
redefinition in a series of redefinitions, but the COBOL "standard" language
definition is explicitly set up to insure that this isn't REQUIRED.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help!

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-12-07T09:08:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DF1BAA6.6089B86@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<GvqH9.32785$L47.3775200@read2.cgocable.net> <25Ccneb6Wekxw3KgXTWcpw@giganews.com> <217e491a.0212051602.2770b083@posting.google.com> <MmmdnQePH9wygm2gXTWcrg@giganews.com> <3DF0EA72.BD177AA8@Azonic.co.nz> <69-cnYZGsJZRem2gXTWcoQ@giganews.com> <asqrb9$9g2$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> Certainly a compiler COULD
> be "forced to" (and I know some do) "read ahead" to find the largest
> redefinition in a series of redefinitions, 

It doesn't need to 'read ahead'.  It only needs to keep a total size
which is updated if required as it proceeds.  It only needs to commit
this size when it meets a new area that is not a redefinition, at which
point there cannot be a further redefines that could change it.

Whether it is sensible or not to allow larger sizes to redefine depends
on what the areas are used for.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help!

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-06T14:18:56-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<asr0nj$u1q$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<GvqH9.32785$L47.3775200@read2.cgocable.net> <25Ccneb6Wekxw3KgXTWcpw@giganews.com> <217e491a.0212051602.2770b083@posting.google.com> <MmmdnQePH9wygm2gXTWcrg@giganews.com> <3DF0EA72.BD177AA8@Azonic.co.nz> <69-cnYZGsJZRem2gXTWcoQ@giganews.com> <asqrb9$9g2$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3DF1BAA6.6089B86@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Can you give me an example of where it is "better" to put a smaller
definition before a larger one?  I am not saying that there aren't some, but
it seems to me (but that may because I am USED to this restriction) that
there is never a time that it is BETTER to put the smaller item first.

I think that everyone is agreed that you always CAN structure your data
definition with the largest first (as REDEFINES don't really "matter" what
order they appear in) - but I do think that there MAY be reasons why putting
one definition "first" is easier to maintain (without errors).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help!

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-12-07T14:58:44-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0212071458.3d2f8b38@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<GvqH9.32785$L47.3775200@read2.cgocable.net> <25Ccneb6Wekxw3KgXTWcpw@giganews.com> <217e491a.0212051602.2770b083@posting.google.com> <MmmdnQePH9wygm2gXTWcrg@giganews.com> <3DF0EA72.BD177AA8@Azonic.co.nz> <69-cnYZGsJZRem2gXTWcoQ@giganews.com> <asqrb9$9g2$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3DF1BAA6.6089B86@Azonic.co.nz> <asr0nj$u1q$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote 

> Can you give me an example of where it is "better" 

I don't think that I ever claimed that it was 'better' to do it any
specific way (in fact I am certain that I did not make any such
statement), I only said that it need not give an error and may, or may
not, be sensible.

> I think that everyone is agreed that you always CAN structure your data
> definition with the largest first (as REDEFINES don't really "matter" what
> order they appear in) - but I do think that there MAY be reasons why putting
> one definition "first" is easier to maintain (without errors).

Well, 'maintain' is the operative word.

In changing a program it may be that one particular redefinition may
become larger (where this is irrelevant to the other definitions). 
Would it then be 'better' to reorder the definitions just to make the
largest first ?  Or would this just be 'if you don't know what you are
doing, do it neatly' ?

Is it 'better' to define different record types under a variable
record FD in descending order of size ? or increasing record type ? or
in some order they may be expected in the file ?
```

#### ↳ Re: Help!

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-12-05T16:11:13-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0212051611.57466627@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<GvqH9.32785$L47.3775200@read2.cgocable.net>`

```
"Dan J" <dali@cogeco.ca> wrote

>            IF WS-MORE-RECORDS = "YES"
>            NEXT SENTENCE.

You are being taught to use an obsolete version of the language that
was superceeded [probably] before you were born.

Apart from 'NEXT SENTENCE' being incompatible with modern techniques
it is also pointless in this particular case.

>            IF SR-DEPT-NUMBER = DEPT-NUMBER (SUB-DEPT-NAME)
>            MOVE DEPT-NAME (SUB-DEPT-NAME) TO DTL-DEPT-NAME
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>            NEXT SENTENCE.

You should learn to indent, it makes the code easier to see what is
supposed to happen.  The 'ELSE NEXT SENTENCE' is redundant (as well as
being archaic usage).
```

## Thread: IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM V2R2 - withdrawal from marketing — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM V2R2 - withdrawal from marketing

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-04T23:33:26-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<asmof7$lre$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Just in case anyone reading this has forgotten (or missed it), IBM has
announced the withdrawal for later this month from marketing of
  IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM V2R2 (for the OS/390 & z/OS environments - not
VM).

The service withdrawal date is not until 2004.

This means that you will ONLY be able to "purchase" Enterprise COBOL as of
next month.  See:

 http://www-1.ibm.com/servers/eserver/zseries/zos/le/history/cobmvs.html

for the full IBM COBOL Release history for the "z/OS, OS/390�, MVS, and VM
Platforms"

FYI - if you have any programs that still rely on the CMPR2 compiler option
(or the seldom used SOM OO interface) this may be highly significant to you.
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM V2R2 - withdrawal from marketing

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>

- **Message-ID:** `<asmpd8$5j5$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<asmof7$lre$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I should have added (and this may be even more significant for SOME shops),
that LE for OS/390 V2R10 *also* goes out of marketing the end of this month.
See:

     http://www-1.ibm.com/servers/eserver/zseries/zos/le/history/le390.html

Obviously, this ties in with the general "end of marketing" of OS/390
itself. See:

http://www2.ibmlink.ibm.com/cgi-bin/master?request=salesmanual&parms=SMS&xh=
8wh6DkzRIqCTZG2USenGnN9332&xhi=+%BC%7Csalesmanual%5E&type=D&search=&title=T&
product=5647-A01
```

```
           IF  XXXX1

010460             MOVE 1 TO SW-UPP
"                  COMPUTE KOLL-ANTAL = KOLL-ANTAL * 1.5
"                  COMPUTE KOLL-ANTAL = KOLL-ANTAL * ILKAT-MULTIPLICERA
"              ELSE
```

- **From:** "Mr. Black" <blackholedk@hotmail.com>

```
"Erik" <erik06@hotmail.com> skrev i en meddelelse
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Cobol-behaviour

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-12-05T22:27:57-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DF0274C.AD49343A@mb.sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <3def2efb$0$205$edfadb0f@dread14.news.tele.dk>`

```
"Mr. Black" wrote:

> Hello Erik
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

It really isn't very constructive to suggest to somebody that has asked a
question that he try some other technique entirely.  He's asked a question
about one particular thing because he can't understand why it won't work:  it
doesn't help him at all to mention something else as a panacea.

PL
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Cobol-behaviour

- **From:** jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2002-12-06T15:01:23-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dcedb8d0.0212061501.56b56a53@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <3def2efb$0$205$edfadb0f@dread14.news.tele.dk> <3DF0274C.AD49343A@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote in message news:<3DF0274C.AD49343A@mb.sympatico.ca>...
> "Mr. Black" wrote:
> 
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> PL

Well, the problem is in the style the code is written in.  It makes
the scope of the IFs awfully unclear.  Proper indentation and
formatting would go a long way to increasing programmer comprehension,
however, the EVALUATE was designed to replace and simplify several
nested IFs,  Why not suggest it?

Knowing the right tool/technique is 95% of the way to solving any
problem.
```

#### ↳ Re: Strange Cobol-behaviour

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-05T14:15:17+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<roguuuovm0jtgraggr9r6mesbtlhaftb17@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com>`

```
On 5 Dec 2002 02:11:27 -0800 erik06@hotmail.com (Erik) wrote:

:>Can anyone tell me why this one donï¿½t work?

Match each END-IF with the previous IF and I think it will be quite clear.

A period closes all previous IF's.

:>           IF  XXXX1
:>               MOVE 0 TO SW-UPP
:>               IF  ILK  = '10' OR '11' OR '24' OR '29'
:>                   MOVE 1 TO SW-UPP
:>"                  COMPUTE KOLL-ANTAL = KOLL-ANTAL * ILKAT-MULTIPLICERA
:>"              ELSE
:>               IF  ILK = '05' OR '36'
:>010460             MOVE 1 TO SW-UPP
:>"                  COMPUTE KOLL-ANTAL = KOLL-ANTAL * 1.5
:>"                  COMPUTE KOLL-ANTAL = KOLL-ANTAL * ILKAT-MULTIPLICERA
:>"              ELSE
:>"              IF  ILK = '06' OR '38' OR '19'
:>010510             MOVE 1 TO SW-UPP
:>"                  COMPUTE KOLL-ANTAL = KOLL-ANTAL * 2
:>"                  COMPUTE KOLL-ANTAL = KOLL-ANTAL * ILKAT-MULTIPLICERA
:>"              ELSE
:>"              IF  ILK = '16'
:>010560             MOVE 1 TO SW-UPPDATERA
:>"                  COMPUTE KOLL-ANTAL = KOLL-ANTAL * 0.5
:>"              END-IF
:>"
:>"              IF  ILK = '12' OR '15' OR '18' OR '23'
:>010610             MOVE 1 TO SW-UPPD
:>"                  COMPUTE TIDO = TIDO - KOLL-ANTAL
:>"              ELSE
:>010650             IF  SW-UPPD = 1
:>"                    COMPUTE TIDO = TIDO + KOLL-ANTAL
:>010680             END-IF
:>"              END-IF
:>"          END-IF.
:>            
:>           IF XXX.............
:>
:>My problem is, if ILK = '05', then the sentence:
:>                   MOVE 1 TO SW-UPP
:>"                  COMPUTE KOLL-ANTAL = KOLL-ANTAL * 1.5
:>"                  COMPUTE KOLL-ANTAL = KOLL-ANTAL * ILKAT-MULTIPLICERA
:>
:>will be executed, BUT the line: IF  ILK = '12' OR '15' OR '18' OR '23' will not,

:>the line IF XXX........... will be executed next instead.

:>Why is that? I got the END-IF, that should end the first IF statement.
```

#### ↳ Re: Strange Cobol-behaviour

- **From:** Robert Graham <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-05T12:31:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DEF4953.4050207@nycap.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com>`

```
If you want "IF  ILK = '12' OR '15' OR '18' OR '23'" to be a 
separate "IF" statement, then you will need to add a few 
more "END-IF's" ...

Erik wrote:
> Hi.
> Can anyone tell me why this one donï¿½t work?
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> "              END-IF

You need to add: END-IF
                  END-IF
                  END-IF
then "IF  ILK = '12' OR '15' OR '18' OR '23'" statement will 
always be performed within the "IF XXXX1" block.

> "
> "              IF  ILK = '12' OR '15' OR '18' OR '23'
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> "          END-IF.
```

#### ↳ Re: Strange Cobol-behaviour

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-12-05T13:34:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<SBIH9.5257$Kw6.3412085@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com>`

```
"Erik" <erik06@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com...
> Hi.
> Can anyone tell me why this one don�t work?
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> will be executed, BUT the line: IF  ILK = '12' OR '15' OR '18' OR '23'
will not,
>
> the line IF XXX........... will be executed next instead.
>
> Why is that? I got the END-IF, that should end the first IF statement.

When I indent *EVERY* IF.. that's what you wrote. The ONLY lines in the
whole snippet which should execute when ILK='05' are the three you show.

The reason the code behaves as it does is because it's only doing what it's
told.

Try cutting and pasting and indenting and it will be obvious.

MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-05T08:04:53-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<whmcnUJ1Eb-dwHKgXTWcqg@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com>`

```

"Erik" <erik06@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com...
> Hi.
> Can anyone tell me why this one don�t work?
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
> Why is that? I got the END-IF, that should end the first IF statement.

You've got six IFs and four END-IFs - a recipe for confusion. Consider:

IF XXXX1
   MOVE 0 TO SW-UPP
  EVALUATE ILK
      WHEN '10' '11' '24' '25'
         MOVE 1 TO SW-UPP
         COMPUTE ...
      WHEN '05'  '36'
         MOVE 1 TO SW-UPP
         COMPUTE...
...
   END-EVALUATE
END-IF
```

```
> You've got six IFs and four END-IFs - a recipe for confusion. Consider:
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

- **From:** erik06@hotmail.com (Erik)
- **Date:** 2002-12-06T00:20:21-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b973b52c.0212060020.680c3b53@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <whmcnUJ1Eb-dwHKgXTWcqg@giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message news:<whmcnUJ1Eb-dwHKgXTWcqg@giganews.com>...
> "Erik" <erik06@hotmail.com> wrote in message
> news:b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com...
…[39 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

The number of END-IF:s donï¿½t not matter.
Look at this:
IF  NYCKEL IN IKUA111 (1:5) = 'IKONO'   GO TO F-120 ELSE
IF  NYCKEL IN IKUA111 (1:5) = 'IKADR'   GO TO F-120 ELSE
IF  NYCKEL IN IKUA111 (1:5) = 'IPRNT'   GO TO F-120 ELSE
IF  NYCKEL IN IKUA111 (1:4) = 'ANST'    GO TO F-120 ELSE
IF  NYCKEL IN IKUA111 (1:4) = 'IUTL'    GO TO F-130 ELSE
IF  NYCKEL IN IKUA111 (1:4) = 'FSEK'    GO TO F-130 ELSE
IF  NYCKEL IN IKUA111 (1:4) = 'USEK'    GO TO F-130 ELSE
IF  NYCKEL IN IKUA111 (1:4) = 'IK53'    GO TO F-000 ELSE
IF  NYCKEL IN IKUA111 (1:4) = 'IK54'    GO TO F-130 ELSE
IF  NYCKEL IN IKUA111 (1:4) = 'IK57'    GO TO F-130.

Here I got 10 IF:s and not any END-IF:s.
This is correct programming, because the ELSE taka care of the IF.
My problem is not to get my code to work, but WHY it donï¿½t work as written.

/Erik
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

- **From:** Robert Graham <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-06T12:43:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DF09D7C.8010508@nycap.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <whmcnUJ1Eb-dwHKgXTWcqg@giganews.com> <b973b52c.0212060020.680c3b53@posting.google.com>`

```


Erik wrote:
> 
> 
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> /Erik

Erik,
The original poster wanted to know why a subsequent IF 
statement was not always being executed within the major IF. 
  It was because he was lacking some END-IF's.

Using your example:

IF  NYCKEL IN IKUA111 (1:5) = 'IKONO'   GO TO F-120 ELSE
IF  NYCKEL IN IKUA111 (1:5) = 'IKADR'

IF NEW-IF IN IKUA111 (1:5) = "ERIKS' GO TO F121 ELSE

IF  NYCKEL IN IKUA111 (1:5) = 'IPRNT'   GO TO F-120 ELSE
IF  NYCKEL IN IKUA111 (1:4) = 'ANST'    GO TO F-120 ELSE
IF  NYCKEL IN IKUA111 (1:4) = 'IUTL'    GO TO F-130 ELSE
IF  NYCKEL IN IKUA111 (1:4) = 'FSEK'    GO TO F-130 ELSE
IF  NYCKEL IN IKUA111 (1:4) = 'USEK'    GO TO F-130 ELSE
IF  NYCKEL IN IKUA111 (1:4) = 'IK53'    GO TO F-000 ELSE
IF  NYCKEL IN IKUA111 (1:4) = 'IK54'    GO TO F-130 ELSE
IF  NYCKEL IN IKUA111 (1:4) = 'IK57'    GO TO F-130.

In this case what happens if the test in the second IF for 
'IKADR' fails?
```

```
> > IF  NYCKEL IN IKUA111 (1:5) = 'IKADR'   GO TO F-120 ELSE

It is perfectly correct Cobol and if the values and labels are correct

write it) dows not make it incorrect.
```

```
F-130.
```

```
Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote

> :>It is perfectly correct Cobol and if the values and labels are correct
> :>(ie if it works) then it is perfectly correct programming.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Might I then correctly assume that you would support

No. You may not assume anything.

I was not 'supporting' the style, in fact I quite clearly stated that
it was not how I would write it.

It just was _not_ incorrect Cobol.

ALTER is obsolete and so your rather contrived example is in a
different category.  As the behaviour of you example may differ from
the original then it may be an incorrect translation.
```

```
It was never claimed that 'programming equalled COBOL'; it was claimed, by

first and then 'good' might be looked at.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-09T07:04:44-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<IsOdnWk7vtl8CWmgXTWcow@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <whmcnUJ1Eb-dwHKgXTWcqg@giganews.com> <b973b52c.0212060020.680c3b53@posting.google.com> <cPacnULY09mc-2-gXTWcpg@giganews.com> <3DF33032.AD59D621@Azonic.co.nz> <_yqdnfmpyqYPX2-gXTWcrg@giganews.com> <03BEBBFAFE74C816.7B7913A8CC8DAF19.DD6E22ED04882F7B@lp.airnews.net>`

```

"SkippyPB" <swiegand@NOSPAM.neo.rr.com>
> >> >
> >> > The above example is not correct programming even if it works.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> clean and would execute.  That makes the "code" correct.

OK, I think I see the source of confusion between what I wrote and what you
read. I agree the "code" is correct; I assert the programming is bad. Good
programming is not simply the ability to write code that will cleanly
compile (probably a necessary, but not sufficient condition); good
programming includes efficiency, maintainability, as readily understandable
as possible, conformance to generally accepted standards, and many other
chacteristics.

"Correct code" (i.e., code that will compile cleanly and perform perfectly)
does not, alone, make good programming. In fact, as the above illustrates,
"correct code" can demonstrate really bad programming. I'll go so far as to
propose that good programming can be demonstrated by code that won't
compile, by code for which a compiler doesn't even exist (such as
flow-charting or prototyping)!

Clean code IS NOT EQUAL good programming, and to those whose standards and
definitions stop at "clean compile, program works," I can only say:
"Tsk-tsk."
```

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** erik06@hotmail.com (Erik)

- **Message-ID:** `<b973b52c.0212170702.269f390c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <whmcnUJ1Eb-dwHKgXTWcqg@giganews.com> <b973b52c.0212060020.680c3b53@posting.google.com> <cPacnULY09mc-2-gXTWcpg@giganews.com> <3DF33032.AD59D621@Azonic.co.nz> <_yqdnfmpyqYPX2-gXTWcrg@giganews.com> <3DF383C9.C0C45E88@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Hi everyone.
This conversation is taking strange ways. My issue was NOT to get my
code to work, and the GO TO:s is comlpetely irrelevant. It just
happened to be in the code snippet, it could just as well being a MOVE
instead of a GO TO. The thing is that many people here is just
focusing on the GO TO, instead of the original question, and just a
few of you has tried to explain why the code is behave like it does in
the:
IF X=Y
   MOVE 4 TO Z1 
   IF Z2 = Z3
      MOVE 6 TO Z4
   ELSE
   IF Z5 = 6
      MOVE 4 TO Z6
   ELSE
   IF Z7 = 7
      MOVE 8 TO Z8
   END IF
END IF

And still has no one give an answer that seems OK to me. I know that
the logic ca be solved with EVALUTE or something else, but still, that
was NOT the issue from the beginning.

/Erik
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-12-17T15:38:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<atngdj$33s$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <whmcnUJ1Eb-dwHKgXTWcqg@giganews.com> <b973b52c.0212060020.680c3b53@posting.google.com> <cPacnULY09mc-2-gXTWcpg@giganews.com> <3DF33032.AD59D621@Azonic.co.nz> <_yqdnfmpyqYPX2-gXTWcrg@giganews.com> <3DF383C9.C0C45E88@Azonic.co.nz> <b973b52c.0212170702.269f390c@posting.google.com>`

```

On 17-Dec-2002, erik06@hotmail.com (Erik) wrote:

> and just a
> few of you has tried to explain why the code is behave like it does in
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> END IF

I thought people answered that at the beginning, by suggesting you place END-IF
where ever they are appropriate and seeing what you get.
The above code is the same as:

IF X=Y
    MOVE 4 TO Z1
    IF Z2 = Z3
       MOVE 6 TO Z4
    ELSE
       IF Z5 = 6
          MOVE 4 TO Z6
       ELSE
          IF Z7 = 7
             MOVE 8 TO Z8
          END IF
       END-IF
    END-IF	
 END IF

Plug in the values and you should get the results you got.
I suspect you may have been trying for:

 IF X=Y
    MOVE 4 TO Z1
    IF Z2 = Z3
       MOVE 6 TO Z4
    END-IF
    IF Z5 = 6
       MOVE 4 TO Z6
    END-IF
    IF Z7 = 7
       MOVE 8 TO Z8
    END IF
 END IF

But it isn't obvious what you wanted.  The reason you aren't getting specific
answers of "why the code works the way it is written" is because it is obvious
to us what it is doing - but it is not obvious to us what you want it to be
doing.  Was my guess correct?

Without knowing what you want, all we can do is suggest ways to clarify your
code.   The first suggestion was to put in END-IFs, and make sure you indent.  
This should make the current flow obvious.   Other suggestions were to re-write
the logic in ways less likely to have mistakes.

Here's a parallel to your question:
Why does the following code do what it does?
        X= -16
        Y = 3
        ADD X to Y.

We need to know why you are asking the question.  What were you expecting.   We
might discuss that maybe you could understand the COMPUTE command more, or we
could discuss the signs and sizes in the pictures of X and Y, but we can only
guess why you don't understand your results.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-12-17T18:32:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<atnqjj$9aj$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <whmcnUJ1Eb-dwHKgXTWcqg@giganews.com> <b973b52c.0212060020.680c3b53@posting.google.com> <cPacnULY09mc-2-gXTWcpg@giganews.com> <3DF33032.AD59D621@Azonic.co.nz> <_yqdnfmpyqYPX2-gXTWcrg@giganews.com> <3DF383C9.C0C45E88@Azonic.co.nz> <b973b52c.0212170702.269f390c@posting.google.com> <atngdj$33s$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

On 17-Dec-2002, "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote:

> > IF X=Y
> >    MOVE 4 TO Z1
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> > END IF

Or maybe you meant it to work like this:

 IF X=Y
    MOVE 4 TO Z1
    IF Z2 = Z3
       MOVE 6 TO Z4
    ELSE
    IF Z5 = 6
       MOVE 4 TO Z6
    ELSE
    IF Z7 = 7
       MOVE 8 TO Z8
    END IF
 END IF

END-IF
END-IF
DISPLAY "This should be displayed no matter what the values of X and Y".

The extra END-IFs at the end can all be replaced by a period, but having the
END-IFs is much more clear, and having both the END-IFs and a full stop period
is the most clear.

Walk through your code and see what your next statement executed is if X=Y or if
X^=Y.   Remove the two END-IF's that you didn't include and try again.   Once
you see that the END-IF's change your logic, put them where you want them, and
indent appropriately.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2002-12-09T10:19:43-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dcedb8d0.0212091019.1ad325a3@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <whmcnUJ1Eb-dwHKgXTWcqg@giganews.com> <b973b52c.0212060020.680c3b53@posting.google.com> <cPacnULY09mc-2-gXTWcpg@giganews.com> <3DF33032.AD59D621@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message news:<3DF33032.AD59D621@Azonic.co.nz>...
> JerryMouse wrote:
> 
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> write it) dows not make it incorrect.

<rant>

The phrase 'perfectly correct Cobol' disturbs me a bit.  Cobol was
designed well before any thought had gone into good programming style.
 As such it will allow things that are structually dangerous to
readability, understandability and maintainability.  Combine that with
a group of programmers that tend not to study proper program
construction methods and you can get some incredibly twisted spegitti
code.

What may be 'perfectly correct Cobol' should never get past a halfway
interested code review.  I think the only other langauge that can
create such a mess would be assembler.

I have always thought that the primary reason that non-Cobol
programmers look upon Cobol and its users with a certain lack of
respect is their unwillingness to drag their standards and style away
from the Cobol-66 era.

The above code fragment raises several red flags.

 -- It uses reference modification and 'magic' literals embeded in the
code.  I would suggest defining 88-levels for each of these values,
the code will get cleaner and easier to deal with.  Or if that is
inconvienient, then at least some constant definations for those
literals.

 -- The code uses GO TO.  This means the programmer is manageing their
own flow of control.  Properly structured code removes this
requirement from the programmer and removes a common source of bugs.

 -- There is no indentation.  It is not a personal preference, the
overwhelming evidance is that using indention to mirror program logic
enhances comprehension.

 -- It has horrible and meaningless names like F-000 and F-130.  
 
I suggest this code would be a far better read if written like:

     Evaluate true

       when NYCKEL-IN-IKUA111-IKONO
       when NYCKEL-IN-IKUA111-IKADR
       when NYCKEL-IN-IKUA111-IPRNT
       when NYCKEL-IN-IKUA111-ANST
          Perform F-120 

       when NYCKEL-IN-IKUA111-IUTL
       when NYCKEL-IN-IKUA111-FSEK
       when NYCKEL-IN-IKUA111-USEK
       when NYCKEL-IN-IKUA111-IK54
       when NYCKEL-IN-IKUA111-IK57
          Perform F-130

       when NYCKEL-IN-IKUA111-IK53
          Perform F-000

     End-Evaluate

</rant>
```
