###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-12-09T15:54:33-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0212091554.7b8aa76f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <whmcnUJ1Eb-dwHKgXTWcqg@giganews.com> <b973b52c.0212060020.680c3b53@posting.google.com> <cPacnULY09mc-2-gXTWcpg@giganews.com> <3DF33032.AD59D621@Azonic.co.nz> <dcedb8d0.0212091019.1ad325a3@posting.google.com>`

```
jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote 

> The phrase 'perfectly correct Cobol' disturbs me a bit.  Cobol was
> designed well before any thought had gone into good programming style.

The original Cobol was designed for the machines of the time. It was
'good programming style' to ensure that the compiler and the program
could run efficiently.

It may well be that in 30 years time that Java/C++/C# may be said to
have been 'designed before any thought went into good programming
style' where 'good' is something unthought of today.

>  As such it will allow things that are structually dangerous to
> readability, understandability and maintainability. 

These are completely subjective and based on what one is 'used to'. 
It may well be true that you may make a complete balls up of a program
that used GO TOs, but that does not mean that everyone will.

> Combine that with
> a group of programmers that tend not to study proper program
> construction methods and you can get some incredibly twisted spegitti
> code.

There is, in fact, nothing inherent in any specific style that
mandates an output that is spegetti, nor mandates an avoidance of
this.
 
> What may be 'perfectly correct Cobol' should never get past a halfway
> interested code review.  I think the only other langauge that can
> create such a mess would be assembler.

You condem whole programs based on a dozen line snippet ?
 
"Real programmers can write Fortran programs in any language."

> I have always thought that the primary reason that non-Cobol
> programmers look upon Cobol and its users with a certain lack of
> respect is their unwillingness to drag their standards and style away
> from the Cobol-66 era.

Or, perhaps, their pre-conceived ideas that Cobol is still stuck in
the 60s based entirely on a what they read 30 years ago.
 
> The above code fragment raises several red flags.
> 
>  -- It uses reference modification 

What is wrong with reference modification ?

> and 'magic' literals embeded in the code.
> I would suggest defining 88-levels for each of these values,

88 levels still bury the literals in the program, just in a different
place.  Actually I dislike 88 levels and never use them because they
make text searching problematical.

> the code will get cleaner and easier to deal with.  Or if that is
> inconvienient, then at least some constant definations for those
> literals.

That is just a preference you have.
 
The rest I agree with.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2002-12-10T14:21:21-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dcedb8d0.0212101421.445e3f29@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <whmcnUJ1Eb-dwHKgXTWcqg@giganews.com> <b973b52c.0212060020.680c3b53@posting.google.com> <cPacnULY09mc-2-gXTWcpg@giganews.com> <3DF33032.AD59D621@Azonic.co.nz> <dcedb8d0.0212091019.1ad325a3@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212091554.7b8aa76f@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote in message news:<217e491a.0212091554.7b8aa76f@posting.google.com>...
> jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote 
> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> style' where 'good' is something unthought of today.

When I refer to "good programming style" please read "industry
accepted standards of structured programming and object oriented
programming".  They both have a common base of well accepted
practices.  One of those practices is that programmers should not muck
around with their program counter on a whim.

This is reflected in the trend of ALL high level languages toward more
automatic flow of control and less programmer intervention in flow of
control.  Even languages that were created before structured
programming was a buzzword (like Fortran and Cobol) have added support
for these widely accepted standards and practices.  The current state
of these two allow entire programs to be written without the use of a
single goto.

Java/C++/D-flat have several decades of structured programming study
and object oriented programming study built into their designs.  Cobol
(and Fortran) did not.  I am sure that some new school of thought will
arise in the coming decades that will change things 30 years from now,
but it will build on the previous 30 years of prior art, not start
from scratch like Fortran and Cobol.

It doesn't help for Cobol (or Fortran) to enhance support for
structured programming and/or object oriented programming if
programmers insist on ignoring those enhancements.


> >  As such it will allow things that are structurally dangerous to
> > readability, understandability and maintainability. 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> that used GO TOs, but that does not mean that everyone will.

I do not make a 'complete balls up' when using GO TO, it is an
absolute necessity when coding in assembler to use it everywhere. 
However, responsible use of goto tends to create well formed selection
(IF-like) and iteration (PERFORM-like) structures that are easily
understandable and maintainable.  It also allows nightmarish
constructs that allow programmers to jump wherever, whenever.

Cobol allows both styles, but it provides safe and easy to use
selection and iteration constructs.  As such, they should be used. 
Cobol has prepackaged constructs with well defined rules that anyone
familiar with the language can follow.  The roll-your-own constructs
requires full study of the original programmers structure before
accepting it as valid.  It is extra work to roll-your-own and an extra
opportunity to introduce bugs.  It also has the added disadvantage
that the optimizing part of the compiler will default to 'no
optimization' if it cannot understand and guarantee accurate
reproduction of the home-rolled construct.  As such, I can see no
valid reason to create your own constructs using GO TO when the
compiler offers an equally good prepackaged construct.

And the original posting had made a complete balls up of those dozen
or so lines.  If the original poster could not clearly understand
where the scope of is various conditionals was going how the hell was
he going to us those GO TO's in a responsible way?  That snippet was a
great argument for learning the fundamentals.


> > Combine that with
> > a group of programmers that tend not to study proper program
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> this.

Structured programming tells use the widespread use of GO TO is as
distasteful as kitten abuse.  Object oriented programming has mostly
avoided the use completely, and in some cases totally prohibited it.

I don't want to start the often fought religious war of GOTO -v-
NOGOTO again.  But the roll-your-own constructs that Cobol allows (but
does not require) can be quite dangerous.  In the hands of a person
that is not disciplined in their use it can quickly generate a
maintenance headache.  Since the compiler can provide the necessary
constructs with out recourse to GO TO, why would you take the risk
that the programmer is going to be responsible?

In the case of the snippet in question, if the programmer can't use IF
properly why would you think he/she can use GO TO properly?


> > What may be 'perfectly correct Cobol' should never get past a halfway
> > interested code review.  I think the only other language that can
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> "Real programmers can write Fortran programs in any language."

Only the dozen line snippet, 3 ambiguous label names, and the
willy-nilly use of GO TO to access those names.  Those problems should
be corrected.

I haven't seen the rest of the program, but if it is ignoring all
those great features added to Cobol in the last three standards to
support structured programming and object oriented programming, then
yes, I do.

You are quite correct that Fortran could also create such a mess.

> > I have always thought that the primary reason that non-Cobol
> > programmers look upon Cobol and its users with a certain lack of
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> the 60s based entirely on a what they read 30 years ago.

My elementary school only taught PDP assembler, so I didn't get to
view any Cobol 30 years ago.  I have, however, spent the last decade
maintaining Cobol written by a variety of programmers that have
learned at various times over the last 40 years and followed standards
written by people that haven't written a line of code in 40 years.

I never said Cobol was stuck in the 60's, I said a lot of Cobol
programmers were.


> > The above code fragment raises several red flags.
> > 
> >  -- It uses reference modification 
> 
> What is wrong with reference modification ?

Nothing is wrong with reference modification.  I love it.  It may well
be one of my favorite Cobol features.

That statement read "reference modification AND 'magic' literals".  In
this case you have one totally nameless area being compared to another
totally nameless area.  A receipt for headaches.


> > and 'magic' literals embedded in the code.
> > I would suggest defining 88-levels for each of these values,
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> > literals.

It isn't literals in the load module that are the problem, it is the
literal string in the source that is the problem.  So called magic
numbers or magic literals are a notorious source of bugs.  Making them
88-level conditionals allows you to change it in a single place.  If
88's are not good for you, the constant works as well to remove the
'magicness' of the value.

What happens when we get a new widget and the magic code changes from
"IK57" to "IK57.1".  If you have scattered literals throughout your
code you must change it in each place, in each program, knowing that
each place you change in another possible point of failure.  If you
have used a conditional or a constant you change it in a single place
and recompile all the modules that use that.  Less points of failure.


> That is just a preference you have.

Well, in the sense that I prefer the generally accepted software
construction practices, yes that is true. There are many people
involved in software construction that deplore the use of magic
numbers, but allow me to cite the jargon file:

<quote cite="jargon file" name="magic number">
magic number ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ n. ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ [Unix/C; common] 1.ï¿½In source code, some
non-obvious constant whose value is significant to the operation of a
program and that is inserted inconspicuously in-line (hardcoded),
rather than expanded in by a symbol set by a commented #define. Magic
numbers in this sense are bad style. 2.ï¿½A number that encodes critical
information used in an algorithm in some opaque way. The classic
examples of these are the numbers used in hash or CRC functions, or
the coefficients in a linear congruential generator for pseudo-random
numbers. This sense actually predates and was ancestral to the more
commonsense 1...ï¿½
</quote>

Regards, 

Joe Zitzelberger
jzitzelb@tsys.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-12-10T19:00:26-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0212101900.11aabffc@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <whmcnUJ1Eb-dwHKgXTWcqg@giganews.com> <b973b52c.0212060020.680c3b53@posting.google.com> <cPacnULY09mc-2-gXTWcpg@giganews.com> <3DF33032.AD59D621@Azonic.co.nz> <dcedb8d0.0212091019.1ad325a3@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212091554.7b8aa76f@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0212101421.445e3f29@posting.google.com>`

```
jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote

> Java/C++/D-flat have several decades of structured programming study
> and object oriented programming study built into their designs.  

Actually C++ did not have 'several decades .. built into its design'.
20 years ago there wasn't much prior OO study.

> but it will build on the previous 30 years of prior art, not start
> from scratch like Fortran and Cobol.

Cobol and Fortran were based on the previous decades of prior art in
program design.  This was partly a strength (in the 60s) in that
programmers could adopt it without complete disorientation.
 
> And the original posting had made a complete balls up of those dozen
> or so lines.  If the original poster could not clearly understand
> where the scope of is various conditionals was going how the hell was
> he going to us those GO TO's in a responsible way?  That snippet was a
> great argument for learning the fundamentals.

You may be making a mistake of assuming that the code snippets were
written by the poster (who had shown that he did not understand them).
 The first (END-IF) appeared to be supplied code where the test
(student exercise) was to explain why it didn't work.  The second (GO
TO) appeared to be course material too, or perhaps taken from working
code.

Yes, the student hadn't yet learned the fundementals.
 
> In the case of the snippet in question, if the programmer can't use IF
> properly why would you think he/she can use GO TO properly?

Where did you get that from ?  When did I say that the poster was
capable of any particular task ?
 
> What happens when we get a new widget and the magic code changes from
> "IK57" to "IK57.1".  

In the specific case the reference modification will only look at 4
characters for that code.  Perhaps the code is currently handling
"IK57.2" and "IK57.3" correctly by this mechanism.

> If you have scattered literals throughout your code 

You have no information that this is the case.  The code may be unique
and not require duplicating anywhere.

> you must change it in each place, in each program, 

Granted that is this _actually_ were the case then there may be a
reasonable case for having selection in a _copy_book_.  Merely moving
the codes to 88 levels or constants in the program does not, of
itself, make it propagate to other programs.

The way that I often avoid codes in the program source is by having
them in a file that the user can maintain, my 'decodes' file.  That
way many of the functions of the program can be tailored to specific
needs without having to recompile.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2002-12-11T09:28:42-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dcedb8d0.0212110928.3309998f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <whmcnUJ1Eb-dwHKgXTWcqg@giganews.com> <b973b52c.0212060020.680c3b53@posting.google.com> <cPacnULY09mc-2-gXTWcpg@giganews.com> <3DF33032.AD59D621@Azonic.co.nz> <dcedb8d0.0212091019.1ad325a3@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212091554.7b8aa76f@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0212101421.445e3f29@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212101900.11aabffc@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote in message news:<217e491a.0212101900.11aabffc@posting.google.com>...
> jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 20 years ago there wasn't much prior OO study.

The first incarnation of C++ was 1983 as a macro library, but it
wasn't formalized until the 90's.  It had at least two decades of
Simula, Smalltalk, Snobol and others to build on.  I know it looks
similar, but it really isn't the same thing as C.

> > And the original posting had made a complete balls up of those dozen
> > or so lines.  If the original poster could not clearly understand
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Yes, the student hadn't yet learned the fundementals.

I didn't see anything about homework in the original post.  Only a
question of why a snippet of poorly-nested IFs did not work.  I
assumed someone was trying to maintain a poorly written set of IF's.

My favorite sage advice from my local cookie jar is:  Don't comment
bad code, rewrite it.

> > In the case of the snippet in question, if the programmer can't use IF
> > properly why would you think he/she can use GO TO properly?
> 
> Where did you get that from ?  When did I say that the poster was
> capable of any particular task ?

You didn't and I didn't mean to imply that you did.  I was only
commenting on the revised code that changed the nested IFs into:

  IF 'A' GOTO X-1 ELSE
  IF 'B' GOTO X-2 ELSE
  IF 'C' GOTO X-3 ELSE......

Which you defended as a perfectly reasonable style that would not
inherently cause speghetti.

Suggesting that GO TO would improve the problem the original snippet
is like prescribing a bullet in the head as a cure for illness. 
Technically correct, but not very useful.

A programming style that makes non-trivial use of GO TO, in the hands
of the uncareful, is a receipt for speghitti.

If the original poster needs a new tool to fix their problem then the
sensable one is EVALUATE, not GO TO.

> > What happens when we get a new widget and the magic code changes from
> > "IK57" to "IK57.1".  
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> and not require duplicating anywhere.

Even without them scattered through the code, a single instance of two
nameless fields being compared is open to the following error:

   IF A-FLD (1:4) = 'IK57'

can often be updated to the quite incorrect:

   IF A-FLD (1:4) = 'IK57.1'  (*error)

when the magic literal is changed.  The compiler can help to catch
this common problem if you name your fields.  Its success will depend
on how diligent you are, but the form:

   IF A-FLD (1:6) = 'IK57.1'   (*correct)

is the least safe style.  Whereas:

   IF A-FLD (1:length if K-IK57) = K-IK57

or 

   IF A-FLD-IK57

will prevent such things.
```

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message news:<3DF839D0.89C7C2B8@Azonic.co.nz>...
> Joe Zitzelberger wrote:
>  
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> 'At least two decades' is not true according to simple arithmetic.

I'm not sure what cflow has to do with C++, it is a C
graphing/flowcharting tool or a website construction tool depending on
the vendor.

IIRC it was the late-80's that saw actual C++ compilers in widespread
usage.  Prior to that we had to use macro extensions with regular C
compilers to create and manipulate objects.  This is all PC-world
recollection, I didn't do much mainframe stuff then.

Granted, 1983 is 16 years from Simula-67, but 30 years from the
formal, stable, C++ we have today.  Between those two there were
plenty of things that changed C++.  However, I should have said
"almost two decades".

> > I didn't see anything about homework in the original post.  Only a
> > question of why a snippet of poorly-nested IFs did not work.  I
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> defend free speech either.

Not my claim.  You confuse me with JerryMouse.

I do however agree that the snippet was quite 'incorrect', regardless
of compiler output.

> Code can be written well or badly in any style.
>  
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> garbled misunderstanding of what everyone said.

You actually said in defense of the
If/goto/else/if/goto/else...snippet:
|->There is, in fact, nothing inherent in any specific
|->style that mandates an output that is spegetti, 
|->nor mandates an avoidance of this.

I suggest that there is everything in that style that will lead to
speghetti code.  (At this point I will shamelessly drop some heavy
weight name like the late Edager Dijkstra who agrees that GO TO is a
bad thing.)

C and Pascal both have labels and goto statements, but do not nearly
have the reputation for hard to maintain speghetti that Cobol has. 
Having maintained all three my personal observations are that C and
Pascal programmers mostly use the constructs
(if/else,while/for(perform)) and avoid goto, while many Cobol
programmers try to avoid constructs and use goto instead.

A surprising number of Cobol programmers seem not to know that
goto-style programming makes bug ridden and error prone programs.  The
also seem not to know that its use is completely avoidable in most
cases.

I will happily assert that the style of programming that makes more
than non-trivial use of goto is bad programming style, regardless of
correctness.  It is a harmful style.  It should be avoided when
writing and condemned when seen.


> > If the original poster needs a new tool to fix their problem then the
> > sensable one is EVALUATE, not GO TO.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Yeah right, you obviously have the magic formula for perfection.

I have no magic formula for software construction -- though I do seem
to have a magic ability to cause people to take umberage at my posts. 
Just some ideas that I have learned the hard way.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

```
On 12-Dec-2002, jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote:

> C and Pascal both have labels and goto statements, but do not nearly
> have the reputation for hard to maintain speghetti that Cobol has.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> programmers try to avoid constructs and use goto instead.

That's because there aren't many C programs that have been modified 20 times
over 30 years.   The reason there is so much old CoBOL code around is that it is
relatively easy to maintain.    C has a reputation for being a "write only"
language, a reputation that is well deserved, IMHO.   There never were competing
camps telling us to change our spaghetti code for structured, it wasn't a
maintenance friendly enough of a language to be worth that - and people weren't
willing to trade performance for maintainability.    You don't even pick C as
your language of choice if maintainability is your major requirement.

When maintenance became an issue (for huge systems, primarily), they didn't
write structured C, they switched to C++.

In the cases where you do have old, large, oft-maintained code - C is more
likely to be a maintenance nightmare than CoBOL.   (I don't have experience with
Pascal in such an environment - someone else will have to compare it).
```

```
Joe,

while I have considerable sympathy for your position in this, I just
HAVE to comment on some of it <G>.

see below...

Pete

jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote in message news:<dcedb8d0.0212120853.2fb2a0d0@posting.google.com>...
> Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message news:<3DF839D0.89C7C2B8@Azonic.co.nz>...
> > Joe Zitzelberger wrote:

<snipped interesting stuff on the history of CFlow> 

> Granted, 1983 is 16 years from Simula-67, but 30 years from the
> formal, stable, C++ we have today.  Between those two there were
> plenty of things that changed C++.  However, I should have said
> "almost two decades".
>

I love date arithmetic, don't you? <G>

<more snippage> 
> >  
> > > Which you defended as a perfectly reasonable style that would not
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Richard's point here is very dear to my heart. I believe that many in
this forum are too rigid in applying their own opinion on style to
everything.

REAL programmers deal with style...<G>. And I agree with Richard that
anyone using the language should be free to use whatever style he is
OK with. Having said that, I would criticise style I felt was bad...
(and have done, countless times...<G>) but I still respect the
individual programmer's right to code any way he sees fit...

I also have to agree with Richard, Joe, that style in and of itself
does NOT cause "spaghetti" (please note spelling...<G>) It is tangled
mental processes that give rise to spaghetti code. In my early days as
a young COBOL programmer, I had to deal with tons of this and always
managed to do so...(We even had "ALTER" in frequent use in those
days...).

Despite all the wailing and gnashing of teeth about GO TO, in the old
days, we dealt with it... (I am NOT advocating that we should regress
to the "old days", but I don't believe bad style is SO terrible when
it comes to programming that we should have "Religious Wars" about it
<G>)


> Not my claim.  You confuse me with JerryMouse.
>

Arrghhh! A fate worse than death! Jerry is such a one-off I am sure it
is extremely unusual for anyone to be confused with him...<G>
 
> I do however agree that the snippet was quite 'incorrect', regardless
> of compiler output.
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>
This is a fair statement. I support it.

> I suggest that there is everything in that style that will lead to
> speghetti code.  (At this point I will shamelessly drop some heavy
> weight name like the late Edager Dijkstra who agrees that GO TO is a
> bad thing.)
>

Please do not ascribe words to Djikstra that he never actually said.

I read his original paper on "Structured programming" when he first
published it. Nowhere did it make moral judgements ("good" and "bad"
are moral terms...) about anything. What it did was give an abstruse
mathematical proof that the ONLY constructs actually required for
(procedural) computer programming were SEQUENCE, CONDITION, ITERATE.
The term "GOTOless programming" as far as I remember was NOT coined by
Djikstra. (It was Michael Jackson who marketed the concept and
inflicted Jackson Structured Programming on an unsuspecting
World...but he DID use Djikstra as an "Authority" for his nonsense...)
 
> C and Pascal both have labels and goto statements, but do not nearly
> have the reputation for hard to maintain speghetti that Cobol has. 
> Having maintained all three my personal observations are that C and
> Pascal programmers mostly use the constructs

...based on a sample of How Many? C and Pascal programmers?


> (if/else,while/for(perform)) and avoid goto, while many 

(how many? I know "lots", and none of them use GO TO from
preference...)

>Cobol
> programmers try to avoid constructs and use goto instead.
> 
> A surprising number of Cobol programmers seem not to know that
> goto-style programming makes bug ridden and error prone programs. 

I would be surprised if you could give an indication of this
surprising number...<G>


> The
> also seem not to know that its use is completely avoidable in most
> cases.
>

This is groundless and unfair. Put up or shut up! You cannot exoect to
win this particular argument by sweeping statements without
statistical backing... How many COBOL programmers, from your own
personal experience, fall in the categories you describe? (Please
provide Names, Addresses, phone numbers so that all the "Educators"
who frequent this forum can sell them courses in structured
COBOL...<G>)

> I will happily assert that the style of programming that makes more
> than non-trivial use of goto is bad programming style, regardless of
> correctness.  It is a harmful style.  It should be avoided when
> writing and condemned when seen.
>

And most people here would agree with you, so what exactly is your
point?
 
> 
> > > If the original poster needs a new tool to fix their problem then the
> > > sensable one is EVALUATE, not GO TO.
> >

Yes, I believe that most people who have posted to this thread have
alredy endorsed that approach. The argument is not about what COULD
have been used, it is about a SPECIFIC construct that WAS used. I
believe that was Richard's point also...

> > All that is needed is the ability to count IFs and END-IFs for the
> > original problem.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Just some ideas that I have learned the hard way.

Don't worry about people taking umbrage... It is a public forum; there
will be a cross section of views.  (So, some of them will get
cross..<G>)

I respect that you are trying to pass on experience you have acquired
and that is commendable. The trouble is that you are seeing
disagreement where there really isn't any. Richard knows as well as
you do that using GO TO can lead to programs that are difficult to
maintain. (Ironically, Richard's style of programming (gleaned from
posts he has made here) is even purer than the style used by many,
self included - I would be more likely to use GO TO than Richard
would...).

The argument arose because of some code that we all agree is bollocks.
But the point the poster made, was that it compiled OK and he wanted
to know why it didn't work.

Don't take it to heart <G>.

Pete.
```

- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <3DF33032.AD59D621@Azonic.co.nz> <dcedb8d0.0212091019.1ad325a3@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212091554.7b8aa76f@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0212101421.445e3f29@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212101900.11aabffc@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0212110928.3309998f@posting.google.com> <3DF839D0.89C7C2B8@Azonic.co.nz> <dcedb8d0.0212120853.2fb2a0d0@posting.google.com> <b3638c46.0212130859.7ab4c99e@posting.google.com>`

```
dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Peter E. C. Dashwood) wrote in message news:<b3638c46.0212130859.7ab4c99e@posting.google.com>...
>
> <lots of quotes snipped>

> > I do however agree that the snippet was quite 'incorrect', regardless
> > of compiler output.
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> Please do not ascribe words to Djikstra that he never actually said.

Please see his "Goto Considered Harmful" (1968) where he says things
like:

 "More recently I discovered why the use of the go to statement has
such
 disastrous effects, and I became convinced that the go to statement
 should be abolished from all "higher level" programming languages
(i.e.
 everything except, perhaps, plain machine code)."

  and

 "The unbridled use of the go to statement has an immediate
consequence
 that it becomes terribly hard to find a meaningful set of coordinates
 in which to describe the process progress"

  and

 "The go to statement as it stands is just too primitive; it is too
much
 an invitation to make a mess of one's program"

Which are the general thoughts I have been echoing on this thread.

That last one, "[go to] is too much an invitation to make a mess of
one's program" is really the core of my argument.  With the added
thought that even if you resist the temptation to make a complete
bullocks of your program with GOTO you cannot guarantee that the
person
you hand it over to for maintenance will equally resist that
temptation.



> > C and Pascal both have labels and goto statements, but do not nearly
> > have the reputation for hard to maintain speghetti that Cobol has. 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> ...based on a sample of How Many? C and Pascal programmers?

I cannot quantify an exact number.  I can however say that I'm a
die-hard computer geek that has spent most of the last 20 years with
much of my free time spent looking at or creating source code.  With
the
exception of a few years in the army I've also spent my professional
time doing the same. (Isn't it great that people will pay you to
indulge
in your hobby?)

I have seen, written and maintained C/C++ for the Amiga, DOS, the
various Windows and Linuxen, Be, BSD, MacOS and SCO.

I have also seen a great deal of Pascal for MacOS, GS/OS,
DOS, Windows and MVS/XA.

If I had to guess it would be more than a hundred and less than
a thousand of each.


> > (if/else,while/for(perform)) and avoid goto, while many 
> 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> surprising number...<G>

I cannot give an exact number.  I can say I currently work in a shop
of
about 2000ish programmers, many use GOTO with great frequency.  The
shop
code standards actually states "Perform/thru/goto/exit promotes
structured programming".  How is that for out of date?

Of those 2000 there are perhaps 10% assembler and 10%
more Java/C/++/#/VB.  The remaining Cobol programmers show a
propensity
to (ab)use GOTO.  Perhaps half the programs I look at (ab)use GOTO.  A
little more maybe.  Does that say half the programmers? Not
necessarily.  

> > The
> > also seem not to know that its use is completely avoidable in most
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> COBOL...<G>)

Having been on the receiving end of countless "class schedule" spams I
could not do that to another person.

Again, that estimation is only on what I see coming through this shop.
 But when compared with other languages the goto abuse in Cobol is
epidemic, when it is almost non-existant in others.  I directly
attribute this to people who knew Cobol-66 or Cobol-74 never bothering
to update their skills.  In a recent anecdotal case I had the joy of
explaining to a 47-year veteran of programming (mostly Cobol) what the
'Evaluate' statement was.  I sometimes go through the same thing with
other Cobol-85 additions.

It is worth notating that I don't see people writing K&R style C
anymore -- that subgroup seems, more or less, to be keeping up.

You can get a number by posting two equal-but-opposite fragments here
on
comp.lang.cobol and asking for the opinions of the subscribers.  I'll
offer the following:

Do you prefer:

   1000-MAIN.
       PERFORM 1010-CALCULATE-ANN-BAL THRU
       1012-CALCULATE-ANN-BAL-EXIT.

   1010-CALCULATE-ANN-BAL.
       MOVE 1 TO WS-MONTHS.
       MOVE 0 TO WS-ANN-BAL.

   1011-ACCUM-MON-BAL.
       ADD WS-MON-BAL (WS-MONTHS) TO WS-ANN-BAL
       ADD 1 TO WS-MONTHS
       IF WS-MONTHS = 12 GO TO 1010-CALCULATE-ANN-BAL-EXIT.
	 GO TO 1011-ACCUM-MON-BAL.
	  
   1012-CALCULATE-ANN-BAL-EXIT.
       EXIT.
	   
or:

   Calculate-Ann-Bal.

       Move 0 to WS-ANN-BAL

       Perform varying WS-MONTHS from 1 by 1
         until WS-MONTHS > 12

           Compute WS-ANN-BAL = WS-ANN-BAL + WS-MON-BAL (WS-MONTHS)

       End-Perform

       Exit. 	
			

													 
If you want statistical information there are some studies that I
know of on the helpfulness of indentation (Miaria 1983) and whitespace
(Gorla & Benander 1990).

I cannot cite studies on the quality of GOTO code -vs- non-GOTO code,
but I can cite people that believe that GOTO is a dangerous thing (and
I
did) and I know it to be true.  I will look for studies to confirm or
deny that belief.

<flamebait>
*** As a cute aside on my theory that Cobol programmers tend not to
update themselves as frequently as other subgroups of programmers I
would offer the current edition of _Stern_&_Stern_ 'Structured Cobol
Programming' which not only shows some examples on code sheets, but
offers blanks in the back of the book for photocopying.  I've also
seen
this in other post-1995 Cobol texts, but I only have the Stern^2
available to cite right now.

This was common for all kinds of texts over two decades ago, but
quickly
faded as terminals became common.  Cobol texts are the only places I
know that one can find such historical artifacts. 
</flamebait>


> The argument arose because of some code that we all agree is bullocks.
> But the point the poster made, was that it compiled OK and he wanted
> to know why it didn't work.

Well, not exactly.  The original code was GOTOless, the suggested fix
was GOTOful.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-12-13T12:23:17-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0212131223.40116ee2@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <cPacnULY09mc-2-gXTWcpg@giganews.com> <3DF33032.AD59D621@Azonic.co.nz> <dcedb8d0.0212091019.1ad325a3@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212091554.7b8aa76f@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0212101421.445e3f29@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212101900.11aabffc@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0212110928.3309998f@posting.google.com> <3DF839D0.89C7C2B8@Azonic.co.nz> <dcedb8d0.0212120853.2fb2a0d0@posting.google.com>`

```
jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote 

> I'm not sure what cflow has to do with C++, it is a C
> graphing/flowcharting tool or a website construction tool depending on
> the vendor.

When 'C with Classes' was first implemented in 1980 (later renamed
C++) it was implemented with tools that converted the OO code into C
for the compiler, part of this process involved applying a version of
cflow to the oo source before preprocessing.
 
> IIRC it was the late-80's that saw actual C++ compilers in widespread
> usage.  Prior to that we had to use macro extensions with regular C
> compilers to create and manipulate objects.  This is all PC-world
> recollection, I didn't do much mainframe stuff then.

While it is true that the C++ was converted to C, macro-expansion was
only a part of the process.  Zortech was the first direct C++ compiler
that I tried on a PC - 1987 ?
 
> > I defended his right to use such code against claims that it was
> > 'incorrect', criminal even. I don't have to agree with what is said to
> > defend free speech either.
> 
> Not my claim.  You confuse me with JerryMouse.

I didn't say they were your claims.

> I suggest that there is everything in that style that will lead to
> speghetti code. 

It _may_ lead to spagetti code, or it may not.  Some may even say that
any code with a GO TO in it _is_ 'spagetti code'.

> (At this point I will shamelessly drop some heavy
> weight name like the late Edager Dijkstra who agrees that GO TO is a
> bad thing.)

Actually it is not the 'GO TO' that is a bad thing, that is a
perfectly clear operation with no ambiguity and is readily understood.
 What _is_ a bad thing is at the target of the go to there is a label
that has no readily understood relationship to the flow that arrives
there.

When isnspecting a piece of code that has a label in it and go to has
been used anywhere in the program it becomes necessary to analyse the
whole program in order to understand the flow at that small part.
 
> C and Pascal both have labels and goto statements, but do not nearly
> have the reputation for hard to maintain speghetti that Cobol has. 

The reason was this is that C and Pascal have _different_ types of
labels for different functions: you cannot goto a procedure name, or
drop into it, or call a label.  When a name is seen it is obvious how
it can be used for its single purpose.

In Cobol it is possible to use a label as target for a perform, as the
implicit end of a perform, as a drop-through or as the target of a GO
TO.  In order to make code more easily understood it is most effective
to make sure that labels only have _one_ usage.  The easiest way to
ensure this is to disallow GO TO and PERFORM THRU, and to only PERFORM
paragraphs.  Other mechanisms may also work (but not as well IMHO).

However, while GO TO often leads to spagetti code, there is no
inherent rule that makes this compulsory (eg some programs that I
wrote 30 years ago  ;-).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <cPacnULY09mc-2-gXTWcpg@giganews.com> <3DF33032.AD59D621@Azonic.co.nz> <dcedb8d0.0212091019.1ad325a3@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212091554.7b8aa76f@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0212101421.445e3f29@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212101900.11aabffc@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0212110928.3309998f@posting.google.com> <3DF839D0.89C7C2B8@Azonic.co.nz> <dcedb8d0.0212120853.2fb2a0d0@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212131223.40116ee2@posting.google.com>`

```

On 13-Dec-2002, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

> Actually it is not the 'GO TO' that is a bad thing, that is a
> perfectly clear operation with no ambiguity and is readily understood.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> whole program in order to understand the flow at that small part.

Which means allowing drop thru to reach a label has these same problems.  And in
my experience I have seen those same problems by drop through paragraphs. 
Unfortunately, it isn't always obvious that drop through code exists.

> The reason was this is that C and Pascal have _different_ types of
> labels for different functions: you cannot goto a procedure name, or
> drop into it, or call a label.  When a name is seen it is obvious how
> it can be used for its single purpose.

That is a big advantage.   But even with this advantage there is lots of C out
there that is harder to maintain than the worst CoBOL out there.
```

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-12-13T19:28:11-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uvku6jgpudb52b@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <cPacnULY09mc-2-gXTWcpg@giganews.com> <3DF33032.AD59D621@Azonic.co.nz> <dcedb8d0.0212091019.1ad325a3@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212091554.7b8aa76f@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0212101421.445e3f29@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212101900.11aabffc@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0212110928.3309998f@posting.google.com> <3DF839D0.89C7C2B8@Azonic.co.nz> <dcedb8d0.0212120853.2fb2a0d0@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212131223.40116ee2@posting.google.com> <atdhoq$pdv$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
WARNING! Sections versus Paragraphs.

Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:atdhoq$pdv$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On 13-Dec-2002, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> > whole program in order to understand the flow at that small part.

I use performed sections for procedures and paragraph names
for labels. It is never necessary to look beyond the current section
to see the relationship between a GO TO and its destination. Often,
the GO TO and the label appear on the same screen! <G>

> Which means allowing drop thru to reach a label has these same problems.
And in
> my experience I have seen those same problems by drop through paragraphs.
> Unfortunately, it isn't always obvious that drop through code exists.

With performed sections and paragraph names for labels, there
are no "drop thru" paragraphs; because, in practice, there are no
paragraphs; and I still get to use GO TO where it makes sense! <G>

> > The reason was this is that C and Pascal have _different_ types of
> > labels for different functions: you cannot goto a procedure name, or
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> That is a big advantage.   But even with this advantage there is lots of C
out
> there that is harder to maintain than the worst CoBOL out there.

That has not been my experience!

System 1: Design language was unstructured assembly; but written
in COBOL-74.

System 2: Design language was FORTRAN-66; but written in C.

The language in which one thinks has more influence than the
language in which one writes. The COBOL system was harder
to maintain because unstructured assembly is harder to maintain
than FORTRAN-66; not because of the attributes of either COBOL
or C.
```

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>

- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <cPacnULY09mc-2-gXTWcpg@giganews.com> <3DF33032.AD59D621@Azonic.co.nz> <dcedb8d0.0212091019.1ad325a3@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212091554.7b8aa76f@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0212101421.445e3f29@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212101900.11aabffc@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0212110928.3309998f@posting.google.com> <3DF839D0.89C7C2B8@Azonic.co.nz> <dcedb8d0.0212120853.2fb2a0d0@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212131223.40116ee2@posting.google.com> <atdhoq$pdv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <uvku6jgpudb52b@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:
> 
> With performed sections and paragraph names for labels, there
> are no "drop thru" paragraphs; because, in practice, there are no
> paragraphs; 

It seems that you have (or similar):

    Procedure-1 SECTION.
        code.
        IF ( ... )
            GO TO Procedure-1-exit.
        more code.
    Procedure-1-Exit.
        EXIT.

Technically, this would 'drop thru' into the ~exit paragraph.

> and I still get to use GO TO where it makes sense! <G>

I only ever use performed paragraphs, and I get to use GO TO 'where it
makes sense' too, it just happens that (for my code) this is never.
```

- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <cPacnULY09mc-2-gXTWcpg@giganews.com> <3DF33032.AD59D621@Azonic.co.nz> <dcedb8d0.0212091019.1ad325a3@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212091554.7b8aa76f@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0212101421.445e3f29@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212101900.11aabffc@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0212110928.3309998f@posting.google.com> <3DF839D0.89C7C2B8@Azonic.co.nz> <dcedb8d0.0212120853.2fb2a0d0@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212131223.40116ee2@posting.google.com> <atdhoq$pdv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <uvku6jgpudb52b@corp.supernews.com> <3DFB69D1.22255579@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3DFB69D1.22255579@Azonic.co.nz...
> Rick Smith wrote:
> >
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>         EXIT.

No, I use EXIT SECTION when I want to exit a section. <G>

It is most often in the form:

procedure-1 section.
    initialization
loop-label.
    some code
    if (...)
        go to loop-label
    end-if
    more code
    .

I do this when PERFORM with EXIT PERFORM CYCLE is
not appropriate.

An hour ago, I removed a GO TO because PERFORM UNTIL
EXIT with EXIT PERFORM was appropriate.

[...]
> > and I still get to use GO TO where it makes sense! <G>
>
> I only ever use performed paragraphs, and I get to use GO TO 'where it
> makes sense' too, it just happens that (for my code) this is never.

But, I also use GO TO ... DEPENDING ON when EVALUATE
will not work; such as, variable length loop unrolling for
optimization. We discussed this, maybe, two years ago.
The test program was 200% faster. The final program was
80% faster.

GO TO ... DEPENDING ON requires SECTION or PERFORM
THRU. I don't use PERFORM THRU.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uvn3l63jc6fe52@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <cPacnULY09mc-2-gXTWcpg@giganews.com> <3DF33032.AD59D621@Azonic.co.nz> <dcedb8d0.0212091019.1ad325a3@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212091554.7b8aa76f@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0212101421.445e3f29@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212101900.11aabffc@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0212110928.3309998f@posting.google.com> <3DF839D0.89C7C2B8@Azonic.co.nz> <dcedb8d0.0212120853.2fb2a0d0@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212131223.40116ee2@posting.google.com> <atdhoq$pdv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <uvku6jgpudb52b@corp.supernews.com> <3DFB69D1.22255579@Azonic.co.nz> <uvliikdoia360e@corp.supernews.com> <3DFC2B43.A667E057@Azonic.co.nz>`

```

Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3DFC2B43.A667E057@Azonic.co.nz...
> Rick Smith wrote:
> >
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Then you do have a "drop thru" into loop-label.

You are assuming, perhaps, from your knowledge and practice,
that "in [my] practice" loop-label is a paragraph. It is not; even
if the language describes it as such. There is no "paragraph"
code; only "section" code.

One cannot "drop thru" into a label, because a label does not
contain executable code; it only marks a point at which a
transfer to executable code may occur.

This style is an accommodation that I use to allow me to more
easily switch between COBOL and C. Every COBOL section
is equivalent, in C, to the form, for example:

void function_name (void) {
    initialization
loop_label:
    some code
    if (...)
        goto loop_label;
    more code
}

In practice in C, I do pass parameters and return values.
For general purpose procedures, in COBOL, I set up
parameter and result fields for a performed section.

Thus, when switching between COBOL and C, I need not
think much differently about how to compose a solution.
It allows me to use concepts from either language, as needed.
I can write C in COBOL and COBOL in C and offend both
COBOL and C programmers by doing so. <G>
```

_(thread depth: 20)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uvo57hime6tm89@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0212110928.3309998f@posting.google.com> <3DF839D0.89C7C2B8@Azonic.co.nz> <dcedb8d0.0212120853.2fb2a0d0@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212131223.40116ee2@posting.google.com> <atdhoq$pdv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <uvku6jgpudb52b@corp.supernews.com> <3DFB69D1.22255579@Azonic.co.nz> <uvliikdoia360e@corp.supernews.com> <3DFC2B43.A667E057@Azonic.co.nz> <uvn3l63jc6fe52@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212141959.7fb5b299@posting.google.com>`

```

Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0212141959.7fb5b299@posting.google.com...
[...]
> What part of "paragraph" do you not understand ?

Para (beside) + graph (drawn or written). A paragraph is
the mark ( � ) beside someting drawn or written. Why is the
text, itself, not called a para-paragraph? After all, the text
is beside the mark that is beside the text!

But there is no mark beside the text in COBOL programs;
therefore, no paragraphs!

> What part of "drop thru" do you not understand ?

Why "drop thru" paragraphs never "drop"; that is, I have
never had to pick one up and put it back where it belongs.

Why is it not "fall from"; so I when I see it I could call it a
"Fall, from Grace"? <G>

> Merely inventing new terms for it (or claiming that the term doesn't
> apply) does not mean that your code isn't doing it.

What new terms?

What is wrong with having a different way of seeing
something?
```

```
Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0212151554.428b4aaf@posting.google.com...
> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> the language works.

I never claimed that it changes the way the language works; only
that it changes the way I think about how the language works.
I simply do not think about paragraph names as the first element
of a paragraph--to me they are labels.

The COBOL standard defines a procedure as one or more
paragraphs or sections in the procedure division. If I use sections
as procedures, I am not using the contained paragraphs as
procedures. If the paragraphs are not procedures, the paragraph
names serve only as labels for transfer of control.

But let's not get carried away. Most of my programs do not have
any paragraph names; because they have no GO TO statements.

> > What is wrong with having a different way of seeing
> > something?
>
> Because 'the next programmer' will see it for what it really is:
> Programs with a design language of Cobol-74 written in Cobol and C.

And, that is a problem because ...?
```

```
I have seen cut and paste errors, where a program has a GO TO WRONG-EXIT.

One advantage of structure theory is that without GO TO EXIT, you won't have GO
TO WRONG-EXIT.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 24)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-12-16T11:51:30-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0212161151.195468ec@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <atdhoq$pdv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <uvku6jgpudb52b@corp.supernews.com> <3DFB69D1.22255579@Azonic.co.nz> <uvliikdoia360e@corp.supernews.com> <3DFC2B43.A667E057@Azonic.co.nz> <uvn3l63jc6fe52@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212141959.7fb5b299@posting.google.com> <uvo57hime6tm89@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212151554.428b4aaf@posting.google.com> <uvqjsshfqc0436@corp.supernews.com> <atks8p$9m5$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote 

> I have seen cut and paste errors, where a program has a GO TO WRONG-EXIT.
> 
> One advantage of structure theory is that without GO TO EXIT, you won't have GO
> TO WRONG-EXIT.

While being 100% in agreement with you I am interested if anyone has
solved this problem by only ever using a single name for the exit
paragraph.

Paragraph labels only need be unique within a section and from within
that section qualification need not be used.

         A SECTION.
            IF () GO TO This-Exit.
         This-Exit.
         B SECTION.
            IF () GO TO This-Exit.
         This-Exit.
         C SECTION.

If the This-Exit is not provided in any section then the compiler
should give an error if there is a GO TO as this should require a
qualifier (I would not use or recommend this code).

However, I have seen a program where there were two paragraphs of the
same name and the compiler silently used just one of them for PERFORMs
leading to some confusion.
```

```
> Paragraph labels only need be unique within a section and from within
> that section qualification need not be used.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

Interesting.   Oh, well, if people get compilers with EXIT PARAGRAPH, then they
will stop having any reason to argue that SECTIONS are useful.  (I still have to
maintain programs with PERFORM  this THRU this-exit, which is even worse than
SECTIONs).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 24)_

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2002-12-16T12:36:14-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0212161236.4cc92591@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <atdhoq$pdv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <uvku6jgpudb52b@corp.supernews.com> <3DFB69D1.22255579@Azonic.co.nz> <uvliikdoia360e@corp.supernews.com> <3DFC2B43.A667E057@Azonic.co.nz> <uvn3l63jc6fe52@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212141959.7fb5b299@posting.google.com> <uvo57hime6tm89@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212151554.428b4aaf@posting.google.com> <uvqjsshfqc0436@corp.supernews.com> <atks8p$9m5$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
One interesting way to ALLOW this - is to code sections with the SAME
NAMED exit paragraph at the end of each section.  Then the GO TO EXIT
always goes to teh proper exit.

"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message news:<atks8p$9m5$1@peabody.colorado.edu>...

> One advantage of structure theory is that without GO TO EXIT, you won't have GO
> TO WRONG-EXIT.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 25)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-12-16T20:49:18+00:00

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 26)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-12-29T14:57:50-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v0ukb19qf5qg5e@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<atks8p$9m5$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <20021228222354.01967.00000039@mb-df.aol.com> <bfdfc3e8.0212290742.7dabf75d@posting.google.com>`

```

Thane Hubbell <thaneh@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:bfdfc3e8.0212290742.7dabf75d@posting.google.com...
> One could make the argument that such an "early exit" as it is known
> (whether it be GO TO, EXIT SECTION, EXIT PARAGRAPH, EXIT PERFORM....
> etc, means that your code/process has not been sufficiently
> decomposed.

Sufficiently decomposed? Could it be that you meant
sufficiently structured?

Early exit affects indentation; when EXIT PARAGRAPH,
EXIT SECTION, or EXIT PERFORM CYCLE is used as
an alternative to reversal of condition testing.

Early exit avoids the addition of single-use flags; when EXIT
PARAGRAPH, EXIT SECTION, or EXIT PERFORM ... is
used from within nested code. This also applies to EXIT
PROGRAM, EXIT METHOD, EXIT FUNCTION, GOBACK,
and STOP RUN; when used at a point other than the end
of the "structured" flow of execution.

Early exit avoids overloading or complicating condition testing;
when EXIT PERFORM is used within PERFORM ... UNTIL.

Each of these early exits may be associated with either
the early exit from a structure (structured programming or
a non-SP structure) or the early exit from a module
(modular programming).

EXIT PERFORM ... is always an early exit from a structure.

EXIT PARAGRAPH and EXIT SECTION are early exits
from a structure when they occur within a module (procedure)
that is executed using PERFORM procedure-name-1 THRU
procedure-name-2.

All others uses are early exits from a module (procedure).

Some uses of GO TO may be seen as a substitute for, or
as a adjunct to, these early exits.

Each of these early exits may be seen to eliminate some
of the complications that are introduced by strict
conformance to "structured programming." Each does so
by transferring control to a "single-exit" that would have
been reached by any "structured" means.

If the early exit is from a module, how would additional
levels of decomposition (more modules) eliminate the
need for the exit? I think it is clear that it cannot!

If the early exit is from a structure, how does additional
decomposition prevent the insertion of flags to control
the flow upon return from the module?



> ronglaz6@aol.comnojunk (Ron Glazier) wrote in message
news:<20021228222354.01967.00000039@mb-df.aol.com>...
> > I found that you can get rid of go to but you will still need it in the
> > situation where you want to branch to exit-para.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >       Ron
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 27)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-12-29T16:00:18-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0212291600.1bceffbe@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<atks8p$9m5$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <20021228222354.01967.00000039@mb-df.aol.com> <bfdfc3e8.0212290742.7dabf75d@posting.google.com> <v0ukb19qf5qg5e@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote

> > One could make the argument that such an "early exit" as it is known
> > (whether it be GO TO, EXIT SECTION, EXIT PARAGRAPH, EXIT PERFORM....
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> sufficiently structured?

This may illustrate a difference in appraoch between top-down and
bottom-up advocates.  Top-down (eg Jackson) approach is to design the
structure into more and more levels of refinement (this is probably
what you mean by not 'sufficiently structured' - it needs another
level), while bottom-up (which is used in OO) decomposes the problem
into its essentials which are directly implemantable and then
assembles these into various structures finally arriving at the top
level.

Early Exit might be a signal that there are two or more conditions
being processed in a block of code and that some of the code is only
used for some of the conditions.  Further decomposition may break the
code into smaller blocks which are then executed easily within a
simple IF .. ELSE or EVALUATE and the need for the 'early exit'
disappears.

Some coding styles require much red-tape to be done for each new level
of breakdown (level numbers on labels, section headers, positioning
within source code, -exit labels) and thus see 'early exit' as a way
of avoiding further decomposition. Other coding styles seek
decomposition as a means of code reuse that avoids 'early exit'.
 
> Early exit affects indentation; when EXIT PARAGRAPH,
> EXIT SECTION, or EXIT PERFORM CYCLE is used as
> an alternative to reversal of condition testing.

Frightened of ELSE ?
 
> Early exit avoids the addition of single-use flags; 

There are other ways of avoiding flags - such as decomposition for
example.

> Each of these early exits may be seen to eliminate some
> of the complications that are introduced by strict
> conformance to "structured programming." 

I take it here you equate 'structured programming' to top-down,
perform-section style programming.

> If the early exit is from a module, how would additional
> levels of decomposition (more modules) eliminate the
> need for the exit? I think it is clear that it cannot!

That may be very clear to you, but your clarity does not restrict my
eliminating the need for 'early exit'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 28)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-12-30T03:48:57-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v101gqe67rsk54@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<atks8p$9m5$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <20021228222354.01967.00000039@mb-df.aol.com> <bfdfc3e8.0212290742.7dabf75d@posting.google.com> <v0ukb19qf5qg5e@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212291600.1bceffbe@posting.google.com>`

```

Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0212291600.1bceffbe@posting.google.com...
> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> bottom-up advocates.

It may influence thinking, somewhat; but I do not see that
it applies to this case.

>  Top-down (eg Jackson) approach is to design the
> structure into more and more levels of refinement (this is probably
> what you mean by not 'sufficiently structured' - it needs another
> level),

What I mean is another level of nesting (indentation) to
show two branches of a condition when one no longer
applies or the addition of flags (and nesting) to control
the flow of execution.

> while bottom-up (which is used in OO) decomposes the problem
> into its essentials which are directly implemantable and then
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> disappears.

Here is a template(?) for a program that selects records,
sorts them, and prints a report. It also supplies some
record counts. It uses four early exits.

I know how to massage the code to eliminate the four
early exits. I can do so by adding flags, changing
condition testing, and by adding additional levels of
nesting. I have no idea how decomposition, alone,
(which seemed to be the earlier point,) may be used to
eliminate the exits.

The earlier point, "... means that your code/process has
not been sufficiently decomposed." Hence, I questioned
"sufficiently decomposed" versus "sufficiently structured."

procedure division.
    sort sort-file
        ascending this descending that
        input procedure sort-release-proc
        output procedure sort-return-proc
    stop run
    .
sort-release-proc.
    open input in-file
    perform until 1 = 0
        read in-file
            at end exit perform
        end-read
        add 1 to records-read
        if in-rec-code > "B"
            exit perform cycle
        end-if
        evaluate in-rec-code
          when "A"
            perform a-reformat
          when "B"
            perform b-reformat
          when other
            add 1 to error-records
            exit perform cycle
        end-evaluate
        release sort-rec
        add 1 to records-written
    end-perform
    close in-file
    display "records read" records-read
    display "records written" records-written
    display "error records" error-records
    .
sort-return-proc.
    open output print-file
    initiate report-1
    perform until 1 = 0
        return sort-file
            at end exit perform
        end-return
        add 1 to sorted-records
        generate detail-1
    end-perform
    terminate report-1
    close print-file
    display "sorted records" sorted-records
    .

The more interesting question is, "Does the use of these
early exits affect the clarity of the code?"

[...]
> > Early exit affects indentation; when EXIT PARAGRAPH,
> > EXIT SECTION, or EXIT PERFORM CYCLE is used as
> > an alternative to reversal of condition testing.
>
> Frightened of ELSE ?

No, I sometimes don't find it useful.

> > Early exit avoids the addition of single-use flags;
>
> There are other ways of avoiding flags - such as decomposition for
> example.

Your opportunity to explain how was given above.

> > Each of these early exits may be seen to eliminate some
> > of the complications that are introduced by strict
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> perform-section style programming.

No, sequence, iteration, and choice.
```

```
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…

My method:

      move space to done-flag
      perform until all-done
          read in-file
              at end set all-done to true
          not at end
              perform record-selected
          end-read
      end-perform

Then the record-selected paragraph contains all the code you have
below..
(With a modification to the EVALUATE to eliminate this early exit for
rec-code > "B" (Seems thrown in there for no good reason, does strange
things but this is an example).

>         if in-rec-code > "B"
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 30)_

- **From:** Donald Tees <Donald_Tees@Sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-12-30T10:27:48-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E109023.8000900@Sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<atks8p$9m5$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <20021228222354.01967.00000039@mb-df.aol.com> <bfdfc3e8.0212290742.7dabf75d@posting.google.com> <v0ukb19qf5qg5e@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212291600.1bceffbe@posting.google.com> <v101gqe67rsk54@corp.supernews.com> <bfdfc3e8.0212300700.1b03dcbb@posting.google.com>`

```
I'd make the same arugement, actually.  I never use either an early 
exit, or a go to.  I do use periods, but think the real key to both 
readability and maintenance is simply chopping the code down into enough 
paragraphs with decent names that say what they are doing.  I see code 
in here with in-line code and scope delimiters that goes on for pages, 
and is as hard to read as any unstructured code I read twenty years back.

I really do not see the advantage of putting everything into one 
sentence, regardless of "structure".  Any decent compiler should be able 
to do the exact same thing at the binary level from nice neat little 
paragraphs, and they are designed for *humans* to read.

As for the "topdown vs bottom up" arguement, thus it has ever been.  You 
design bottom up, code top down.  OOP does not change that.

Donald

Thane Hubbell wrote:

>Rick - Comments on specific code constructs below.
>
…[90 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 30)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-12-30T14:02:32-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v115fub1dpstd6@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<atks8p$9m5$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <20021228222354.01967.00000039@mb-df.aol.com> <bfdfc3e8.0212290742.7dabf75d@posting.google.com> <v0ukb19qf5qg5e@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212291600.1bceffbe@posting.google.com> <v101gqe67rsk54@corp.supernews.com> <bfdfc3e8.0212300700.1b03dcbb@posting.google.com>`

```

Thane Hubbell <thaneh@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:bfdfc3e8.0212300700.1b03dcbb@posting.google.com...
> Rick - Comments on specific code constructs below.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> me arguing this but..."

This is a discussion group. It occurs often that when "one
could argue," one is called upon to do so. <g>

The use of EXIT ..., in its various forms, may be considered
a matter of style. Matters of style are often taken seriously. <g>

One could argue that few actually change their styles as a
result of these discussions; because the same issues are
raised by the same people. <g>

>
> Please read below...
>
> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
news:<v101gqe67rsk54@corp.supernews.com>...
>
> .. Snip to code ..
>
[...]
> > sort-release-proc.
> >     open input in-file
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>       end-perform

I have a preference for not using NOT AT END as a catch-all
to do processing that is not part of the READ. I did it for a
while, then stopped because it felt wrong.

To me, there are two separate steps that must be shown,
within a loop: read and process. I will use NOT AT END (and
NOT INVALID KEY) to do reformating, transformation, and
extension (virtual fields) of a record before the process step.
When control reaches the END-READ, whatever data is
required by the process step is present.

Recently, I noticed that, conceptually, this is no different than
reading an object from persistant storage; and, inside a loop,
iterating through a collection of objects. Who needs OO? <G>

> Then the record-selected paragraph contains all the code you have
> below..
> (With a modification to the EVALUATE to eliminate this early exit for
> rec-code > "B" (Seems thrown in there for no good reason, does strange
> things but this is an example).

There is a reason; but the test is out of place and I failed to
account for one possibility.

I did, finally, work out how the procedure could be decomposed
to remove the EXIT PERFORM CYCLE statements without
adding flags. It added two levels and replicated code (actually,
common code was performed from two places.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 29)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-12-30T11:38:47-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0212301138.5d8271ed@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<atks8p$9m5$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <20021228222354.01967.00000039@mb-df.aol.com> <bfdfc3e8.0212290742.7dabf75d@posting.google.com> <v0ukb19qf5qg5e@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212291600.1bceffbe@posting.google.com> <v101gqe67rsk54@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote

> sort-release-proc.
>     open input in-file
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>     end-perform

You give the impresion that you would really like to use go to, but
resist this and use 'modern' substitutes.  They are still 'go to's,
but anonymous ones.

I dislike the 'until hell-freezes-over' approach as this implies that
there is no actual end to the perform while it is actually easy to
supply an end point. Using the record code (or key on an indexed file)
I would use:

>     open input in-file
      move spaces    to in-record
>     perform until in-rec-code = high-value
>         read in-file
>             at end 
                 move high-value to in-record
              not at end
>                add 1 to records-read
                 ...
          end-read
      end-perform

Yes, it is used as a flag, but one that is already in use.

In the case of processing part of an indexed file this has the
advantage that the start can move high-values on invalid key and the
perform does not need any other condition check as the 'until' is
already satisfied.

>         if in-rec-code > "B"
>             exit perform cycle
>         end-if

This seems to be a completely spurious addition that should be
included in the evaluate:

>         evaluate in-rec-code
>           when "A"
>             perform a-reformat
>           when "B"
>             perform b-reformat
            when "C" THRU "z"
               continue
>           when other
>             add 1 to error-records
>             exit perform cycle
>         end-evaluate

>         release sort-rec
>         add 1 to records-written

Basically you have structured the code as:

        until end-of-world
           read-record
           evaluate type
             reformat-record
           release-record

The 'early exits' make a lie of this because the release is only done
for some records.
 
> The more interesting question is, "Does the use of these
> early exits affect the clarity of the code?"

Absolutely.  It is necessary to read _all_ the code to understand what
happens because any of it might 'early exit' which affects what
happens later. My prefernce is to deal with each case and not revisit
conditions.

>     open input in-file
      move spaces    to in-record
>     perform until in-rec-code = high-value
>         read in-file
>             at end 
                 move high-value to in-record
              not at end
>                add 1 to records-read
                 ...
>               evaluate in-rec-code
>               when "A"
>                   perform a-reformat
                    perform release-record
>               when "B"
>                   perform b-reformat
                    perform release-record
                when "C" THRU high-value
                    continue
>               when other
>                   add 1 to error-records
>               end-evaluate
          end-read
      end-perform
      ....
      .

   release-record.
>     release sort-rec
>     add 1 to records-written
       .
Here it is completely explicit what happens when a particular record
is read.  It is also very easy to determine from a _single_ structure
which records take part in the sort.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 30)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-12-30T20:06:24-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v11skdri4mre18@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<atks8p$9m5$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <20021228222354.01967.00000039@mb-df.aol.com> <bfdfc3e8.0212290742.7dabf75d@posting.google.com> <v0ukb19qf5qg5e@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212291600.1bceffbe@posting.google.com> <v101gqe67rsk54@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212301138.5d8271ed@posting.google.com>`

```

Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0212301138.5d8271ed@posting.google.com...
> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote
>
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> but anonymous ones.

Hmmm! You may be close. I think I may be fighting with
the language. When I visualize a procedure, routine, or block.
I outline the steps required. In this case, read, filter, release.
I place each of these steps at the same level of indentation.
Finally, I make the code fit my model of the layout, using
whatever means are available. Flags are excluded because
they, essentially, force indentation. Decomposition for simple
cases is excluded, because it reminds me of chasing the flow
of GO TO statements. This leaves EXIT PERFORM statements
to maintain the indentation I want.

> I dislike the 'until hell-freezes-over' approach as this implies that
> there is no actual end to the perform while it is actually easy to
> supply an end point.

The intended implication is that the exit is determined inside
the loop by conditions that occur inside the loop.

> Using the record code (or key on an indexed file)
> I would use:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Yes, it is used as a flag, but one that is already in use.

Years ago, I moved high-values to the record, because
that is what I learned from other programmers. Then I
moved "Y" to a flag, because that is what I learned from
structured programming. Then I used exit perform, because
I decided that it was best for me. <g>

> In the case of processing part of an indexed file this has the
> advantage that the start can move high-values on invalid key and the
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> included in the evaluate:

Quite right!

> >         evaluate in-rec-code
> >           when "A"
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> for some records.

If the evaluate is seen as a filter, there is no lie.

> > The more interesting question is, "Does the use of these
> > early exits affect the clarity of the code?"
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> happens later.

On the other hand, one need not read all the code to know
that, under certain conditions, the balance of the code does
not apply.

> My prefernce is to deal with each case and not revisit
> conditions.
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> which records take part in the sort.

I discussed, elsewhere, my dislike for using NOT AT END
as a catch-all. I also recognized that moving the EVALUATE
to another procedure and performing that procedure after
the END-READ would accomplish the same.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 31)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-12-31T21:16:21-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0212312116.3a31141c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<atks8p$9m5$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <20021228222354.01967.00000039@mb-df.aol.com> <bfdfc3e8.0212290742.7dabf75d@posting.google.com> <v0ukb19qf5qg5e@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212291600.1bceffbe@posting.google.com> <v101gqe67rsk54@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212301138.5d8271ed@posting.google.com> <v11skdri4mre18@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote 

> Years ago, I moved high-values to the record, because
> that is what I learned from other programmers. Then I
> moved "Y" to a flag, because that is what I learned from
> structured programming. Then I used exit perform, because
> I decided that it was best for me. <g>

Interestingly the Cobol books that I have show the exact oposite idiom
change.  The earliest from the 70s (and most archaic) use an early
exit in the AT END (GO TO README-EXIT), slightly later ones from the
80s use an EoF flag.  The most recent (mid 90s) uses NOT AT END, but
also uses setting record area to high-values and tests on this.

High-values to record areas is the most useful mechanism in such
things as merging and selecting part of the file, in many cases this
is done whether it is used as a EoF indicator or not in order to
satisfy the key matching code. This has been done since the 60s and is
still done today.

ie it was not replaced when the early exit (GO TO) was superceeded by
EoF Flag setting and testing.

If you note my code you will see that the NOT AT END is not required,
the END-READ could be moved there and the 'WHEN "C" THRU HIGH-VALUES'
will safely ignore the [non] record (this _was_ deliberate) except
that the count will be the number of READs executed instead of number
of records read.
```

```
> One could make the argument that such an "early exit" as it is known

> decomposed.

However when doing maintenance, sometimes it is important to have KISS changes.
Don't change a loop structure, just get out of the loop with a new condition.

I don't see that EXIT PARAGRAPH will be that useful for new programs I write. 
It will be very useful for old programs that I maintain.
```
