###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-12-15T10:05:07-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0212151005.5e0015f0@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0212110928.3309998f@posting.google.com> <3DF839D0.89C7C2B8@Azonic.co.nz> <dcedb8d0.0212120853.2fb2a0d0@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212131223.40116ee2@posting.google.com> <atdhoq$pdv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <uvku6jgpudb52b@corp.supernews.com> <3DFB69D1.22255579@Azonic.co.nz> <uvliikdoia360e@corp.supernews.com> <3DFC2B43.A667E057@Azonic.co.nz> <uvn3l63jc6fe52@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote

> One cannot "drop thru" into a label, because a label does not
> contain executable code; it only marks a point at which a
> transfer to executable code may occur.

"Drop thru" is the term used when the executing path of a program
progresses from above a label to below a label.  If the label is also
used as a point to which transfer is made then there are two ways of
arriving at that point.  This increases the complexity of analysing
the program.

Obviously it is a style you are used to and so the 'overhead' of
resolving the complexity is insignificant _to_you_.  In fact you
probably consider it to be far less complex than other styles you have
seen, but that is not the issue, it is what the next programmer must
do to ensure that he understands the program.
 
> This style is an accommodation that I use to allow me to more
> easily switch between COBOL and C. Every COBOL section
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> COBOL and C programmers by doing so. <G>

Well, no, you are not writing 'C programs in Cobol', you are only
writing Cobol programs in Cobol and C.  No self-respecting C
programmer would iterate a loop using a label and a goto, they would
call it 'spagetti code'.  Apart from the fact that there are adequate
C structures for all loop needs (including while(1) and break), there
are dangers in a block structured, stack based language in doing
arbitrary transfer of control. And, of course, it completely buggers
up any optimisation that might occur.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 20)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-12-15T16:16:33-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uvprnhf4s9v6bd@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0212110928.3309998f@posting.google.com> <3DF839D0.89C7C2B8@Azonic.co.nz> <dcedb8d0.0212120853.2fb2a0d0@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212131223.40116ee2@posting.google.com> <atdhoq$pdv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <uvku6jgpudb52b@corp.supernews.com> <3DFB69D1.22255579@Azonic.co.nz> <uvliikdoia360e@corp.supernews.com> <3DFC2B43.A667E057@Azonic.co.nz> <uvn3l63jc6fe52@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212151005.5e0015f0@posting.google.com>`

```

Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0212151005.5e0015f0@posting.google.com...
[...]
> Well, no, you are not writing 'C programs in Cobol', you are only
> writing Cobol programs in Cobol and C.  No self-respecting C
> programmer would iterate a loop using a label and a goto, they would
> call it 'spagetti code'.

Rodney Dangerfield can't get respect. I can't even get
self-respect!

>  Apart from the fact that there are adequate
> C structures for all loop needs (including while(1) and break), there
> are dangers in a block structured, stack based language in doing
> arbitrary transfer of control.

Where I use goto, there is no danger.

I looked over some places where I used GO TO (goto) and
found that I use it when the loop is incidental to the task; that
is, where I want to emphasize the steps to be accomplished.
Essentially, the combination of the label and the GO TO form
a "Repeat as necessary" instruction.

In C, I suppose could use:

do {
    statements;
    if (condition)
        continue; /* repeat as necessary */
    statements;
} while (0); /* never loop */

some of the time; but the COBOL equivalent, if I got it right:

perform with test after
  until 0 = 0 *> never loop
    statements
    if condition
        exit perform cycle *> repeat as necessary
    end-if
    statements
end-perform

emphasizes the loop. In any case; both use "tricky" code.

> And, of course, it completely buggers
> up any optimisation that might occur.

And, sometimes that is not an important consideration.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 21)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-12-15T16:37:45-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uvpsvbljvbhs47@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0212110928.3309998f@posting.google.com> <3DF839D0.89C7C2B8@Azonic.co.nz> <dcedb8d0.0212120853.2fb2a0d0@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212131223.40116ee2@posting.google.com> <atdhoq$pdv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <uvku6jgpudb52b@corp.supernews.com> <3DFB69D1.22255579@Azonic.co.nz> <uvliikdoia360e@corp.supernews.com> <3DFC2B43.A667E057@Azonic.co.nz> <uvn3l63jc6fe52@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212151005.5e0015f0@posting.google.com> <uvprnhf4s9v6bd@corp.supernews.com>`

```

Rick Smith <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
news:uvprnhf4s9v6bd@corp.supernews.com...
[...]
> [...] the COBOL equivalent, if I got it right:
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> end-perform

Well, I didn't get it right. The 'exit perform cycle' transfers
control to a point just before the 'end-perform'; then because
'0 = 0' is true, the 'perform' will terminate; which means it will
never "repeat as necessary".

There is no COBOL equivalent to the C code.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 21)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-12-15T19:13:09-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0212151913.3a23d45a@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <3DF839D0.89C7C2B8@Azonic.co.nz> <dcedb8d0.0212120853.2fb2a0d0@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212131223.40116ee2@posting.google.com> <atdhoq$pdv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <uvku6jgpudb52b@corp.supernews.com> <3DFB69D1.22255579@Azonic.co.nz> <uvliikdoia360e@corp.supernews.com> <3DFC2B43.A667E057@Azonic.co.nz> <uvn3l63jc6fe52@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212151005.5e0015f0@posting.google.com> <uvprnhf4s9v6bd@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote 

> >  Apart from the fact that there are adequate
> > C structures for all loop needs (including while(1) and break), there
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Where I use goto, there is no danger.

Maybe not, but you are probably laying traps for the next programmer
to fall into.  C is an idiomatic language, if you use a completely
different idiom (such as the Cobol-74 idiom that you are using) then
you cannot expect the code to be readily understood by the next coder.

> I looked over some places where I used GO TO (goto) and
> found that I use it when the loop is incidental to the task; that
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> } while (0); /* never loop */

This is telling lies to the next programmer. Does it "never loop" or
does it "repeat" ?  In any case it is inconsistent in its comments.

The 2nd 'statements' is only ever done once, so why is it within the
do .. while anyway ?  Why not the much simpler (and C idiom):

     do {
         statements;
        }
     while ( condition );
     statements;

Which a) is less code
      b) does not give confusing and contradictory signals

> some of the time; but the COBOL equivalent, if I got it right:
> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> emphasizes the loop. In any case; both use "tricky" code.

(I know you noticed it didn't work).

It _DOESN'T_ 'emphasize' the loop, your comment quite clearly states
"never loop".  If one discovers the lie and that this will loop it is
necessary to search for when and why.

Again the 2nd 'statements' is only ever done once, yet it is within
the scope of what is normally a loop structure.

     PERFORM
         WITH TEST AFTER
         UNTIL NOT ( condition )

         statements
     END-PERFORM
     stements

Clearly identifies that it does loop, the correct extent of the code
that does loop and puts the exit condition where it belongs and where
it will be noticed.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 22)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-12-16T18:53:20-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uvsp9llq05kg68@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <3DF839D0.89C7C2B8@Azonic.co.nz> <dcedb8d0.0212120853.2fb2a0d0@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212131223.40116ee2@posting.google.com> <atdhoq$pdv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <uvku6jgpudb52b@corp.supernews.com> <3DFB69D1.22255579@Azonic.co.nz> <uvliikdoia360e@corp.supernews.com> <3DFC2B43.A667E057@Azonic.co.nz> <uvn3l63jc6fe52@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212151005.5e0015f0@posting.google.com> <uvprnhf4s9v6bd@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212151913.3a23d45a@posting.google.com>`

```

Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0212151913.3a23d45a@posting.google.com...
> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote
[...]

This is what happens when I think out loud--while some of my
thoughts are still fuzzy. <g>

> > In C, I suppose could use:
> >
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> does it "repeat" ?  In any case it is inconsistent in its comments.

What "next programmer"? I was trying to be informative to COBOL
programmers in comp.lang.cobol. 'continue', in C, does not do the
same thing as CONTINUE, in COBOL. Its effect, in C, would be to
/* repeat [the above statements from the do] as necessary */.
The same with 'while (0)', it will never loop (cause control to return
to the 'do'.

> The 2nd 'statements' is only ever done once, so why is it within the
> do .. while anyway ?  Why not the much simpler (and C idiom):

The model was an over-simplification. Try this:

    do {
        statements;
        if (condition_1) {
            statements;
            if (condition_2) {
                statements;
                continue; /* repeat the above from the do */
            }
            statements;
        }
    }
    while (0); /* if control reaches here, do not loop */

This is what you would see if the earlier model had an unterminated
'if' in its first 'statements'.

>      do {
>          statements;
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>       b) does not give confusing and contradictory signals

Moving the 'condition' to loop control is exactly what I do not
want. It emphasizes the loop. Here, you might want to consider
recursive algorithms. The conditions leading to recursion are
not part of loop control.

> > some of the time; but the COBOL equivalent, if I got it right:
> >
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> necessary to search for when and why.

The only purpose for the COBOL comments was to extablish
the relationship between the C and COBOL models.

> Again the 2nd 'statements' is only ever done once, yet it is within
> the scope of what is normally a loop structure.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> it will be noticed.

And, that is "respectable" COBOL.

We have been here before! There are times when I am more
comfortable with exiting from or repeating from the middle of
a loop.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 23)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-12-16T22:39:13-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uvt6hcivvoek70@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <3DF839D0.89C7C2B8@Azonic.co.nz> <dcedb8d0.0212120853.2fb2a0d0@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212131223.40116ee2@posting.google.com> <atdhoq$pdv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <uvku6jgpudb52b@corp.supernews.com> <3DFB69D1.22255579@Azonic.co.nz> <uvliikdoia360e@corp.supernews.com> <3DFC2B43.A667E057@Azonic.co.nz> <uvn3l63jc6fe52@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212151005.5e0015f0@posting.google.com> <uvprnhf4s9v6bd@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212151913.3a23d45a@posting.google.com> <uvsp9llq05kg68@corp.supernews.com>`

```

Rick Smith <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
news:uvsp9llq05kg68@corp.supernews.com...
>
> Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> to the 'do'.

While every C programmer knows that 'continue' starts
the next iteration of a loop, I just found out that it does so
by first transferring control to the end of the loop. This
means that 'continue', in C, behaves the same as
EXIT PERFORM CYCLE, in COBOL. My comments, above,
concerning the use of 'continue', in C, were in error.
```

Joe Zitzelberger <jzitzelb@tsys.com> wrote in message
news:dcedb8d0.0212170840.70cd7eb6@posting.google.com...
> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
news:<uvsp9llq05kg68@corp.supernews.com>...
> > Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
> > news:217e491a.0212151913.3a23d45a@posting.google.com...
…[47 more quoted lines elided]…
> and two conditions?

It is part of an embedded program that controls a terminal.
The micro is an 8051 with 2K for variables and 128K of
flash for data storage. It has an LCD, keypad, and other
input devices. It communicates through a serial port and
has options for modem and LAN connections. The device
runs 24/7 and many thousands have been running for
more than five years without a hiccup.

Some older units, with a different design, have appeared
on "Touched by an Angel" where it served as an active prop,
"Friends" where it appeared in the background, and, in the
distance ... way in the distance ... down at the end of the
hall ... if I wasn't its mother, I wouldn't have recognized it, one
of the "Bloopers" TV programs.

The routine begins the processing of commands received
through the serial port. The code is proprietary.

The first 'statements' gets a character, massages it, and
may place it in a buffer. 'condition_1' determines whether
the command buffer is in use. The second 'statements',
an if-else case structure calls one of five functions depending
upon the command mode. 'condition_2' detects a potential
FIFO input buffer overrun. The third 'statements' resets
variables and transfers to the first 'statements'. The fourth
'statements' does clean-up.

The routine is entered every 5ms, or 200 times per second.
Certain long commands will overrun the FIFO when
operating at 9600 baud, or 960 characters per second.
Display updates, keyboard processing, and several other
functions are also entered every 5ms; therefore, the code
for command processing must be minimally intrusive on
the other functions. A 'jerky' display or missed keystrokes
is unacceptable.

Functional decomposition is problematic due to limited
stack space. Using flags to control loops is problematic
due to limited memory. Having said that, I have looked
at the code, again, and accept that it may be possible
to eliminate the 'goto'. Obviously, I am not going to
change a program that has been working fine for more
than six years.

I use two other labels in the program. One transfers
control from a deeply nested 'if' into the else of the
lowest level 'if'. I do this to take advantage of common
processing under one unique circumstance. The other is
'AbortTransaction' which may be entered from a deeply
nested 'if'. I use a 'goto' in some cases to bypass other
statements at the lower levels; otherwise, it is entered
through normal flow of the program.

I have added this additional information, hopefully, to
satisfy your request from another thread where you wrote:
"I've never actually seen code that was made simplier [sic] by
the use of a go to when the other constructs were available.
Could you provide some examples?"

In the first of these examples, I might have been able to
detect the unique condition earlier and used a flag to
control the flow; but I see that as more complicated. For the
second example, I see no reasonable way to avoid aborting
the transaction, with 'goto', in the places where I did.

Embedded C is not the same as C and is far removed from
business programming in COBOL. Yet, I have seen cases
where 'goto' makes sense. Perhaps, because I have seen
such cases, I feel more relaxed about using GO TO where
it makes sense. Still, I only use it, rarely.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 25)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-12-18T14:35:18-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v01iueivin9i9a@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212131223.40116ee2@posting.google.com> <atdhoq$pdv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <uvku6jgpudb52b@corp.supernews.com> <3DFB69D1.22255579@Azonic.co.nz> <uvliikdoia360e@corp.supernews.com> <3DFC2B43.A667E057@Azonic.co.nz> <uvn3l63jc6fe52@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212151005.5e0015f0@posting.google.com> <uvprnhf4s9v6bd@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212151913.3a23d45a@posting.google.com> <uvsp9llq05kg68@corp.supernews.com> <dcedb8d0.0212170840.70cd7eb6@posting.google.com> <uvv19a7lcr1f84@corp.supernews.com>`

```

Rick Smith <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
news:uvv19a7lcr1f84@corp.supernews.com...
>
> Joe Zitzelberger <jzitzelb@tsys.com> wrote in message
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > thoughts are still fuzzy. <g>
[...]
> > > The model was an over-simplification. Try this:
> > >
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> > and two conditions?
[...]
> Functional decomposition is problematic due to limited
> stack space. Using flags to control loops is problematic
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> the transaction, with 'goto', in the places where I did.
[...]

The backward branching 'goto' can be removed by code
in the following form:

     do {
         statements;
         if (condition_1) {
             statements;
             if (condition_2) {
                 statements;
                 continue; /* repeat the above from the do */
             }
             statements;
         }
        break; /* if control reaches here, do not loop */>
     } while (1);

The other cases were forward branching 'goto' statements.
By reversing one condition and by removing the labeled
code into sepearte functions, it is possible to replace each
'goto' with a function call and 'return', as follows:

Before:
    goto AbortTransaction;

After:
    AbortTransaction();
    return;

In standard COBOL, the equivalents are like:

perform until 1 = 0
    statements
    if condition-1
        statements
        if condition
            statements
            exit perform cycle *> repeat the above from the perform
        end-if
        statements
    end-if
    exit perform *> if control reaches here, do not loop
end-perform

and, for GO TO, in most places

Before:
    go to procedure-name

After:
    perform procedure-name
    exit paragraph *> or section (not required at end of paragraph or
section)

or, for conditional GO TO, at the end of a paragraph or section

Before:
    if condition
        go to procedure-name
    end-if

After:
    if condition
        perform procedure-name
        exit paragraph *> or section
    end-if
    perform next-paragraph *> or next-section

The new implicit GO TO statements make it much easier to
avoid explicit GO TO, even in deeply-nested code.

This still leaves the implementation of finite state machines,
translations from non-imperative languages, and code
generators as means for introducing GO TO statements
into COBOL source code.

The day that I cannot find some justification for using GO TO
will be the day my pulse drops to zero and all brain wave
activity ceases. <g>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 26)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-12-18T19:57:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<atqk0f$lmo$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212131223.40116ee2@posting.google.com> <atdhoq$pdv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <uvku6jgpudb52b@corp.supernews.com> <3DFB69D1.22255579@Azonic.co.nz> <uvliikdoia360e@corp.supernews.com> <3DFC2B43.A667E057@Azonic.co.nz> <uvn3l63jc6fe52@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212151005.5e0015f0@posting.google.com> <uvprnhf4s9v6bd@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212151913.3a23d45a@posting.google.com> <uvsp9llq05kg68@corp.supernews.com> <dcedb8d0.0212170840.70cd7eb6@posting.google.com> <uvv19a7lcr1f84@corp.supernews.com> <v01iueivin9i9a@corp.supernews.com>`

```

On 18-Dec-2002, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:

> The day that I cannot find some justification for using GO TO
> will be the day my pulse drops to zero and all brain wave
> activity ceases. <g>

With enough searching, we can find justification for anything.

But the reason to get rid of GO TO is that such a policy results in clearer,
more maintainable code than not having that policy.   We all have chaffed
against standards, but standards are about the general good - not about what a
particular piece of code wants.

Most places will let you try to justify breaking a programming standard, but you
will generally need a bigger advantage than the one you gave to win that
argument.
```

simplify the completion of these forms; even marginally so?

also accept that some programs may be made clearer by
the use of GO TO?

- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212131223.40116ee2@posting.google.com> <atdhoq$pdv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <uvku6jgpudb52b@corp.supernews.com> <3DFB69D1.22255579@Azonic.co.nz> <uvliikdoia360e@corp.supernews.com> <3DFC2B43.A667E057@Azonic.co.nz> <uvn3l63jc6fe52@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212151005.5e0015f0@posting.google.com> <uvprnhf4s9v6bd@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212151913.3a23d45a@posting.google.com> <uvsp9llq05kg68@corp.supernews.com> <dcedb8d0.0212170840.70cd7eb6@posting.google.com> <uvv19a7lcr1f84@corp.supernews.com> <v01iueivin9i9a@corp.supernews.com> <atqk0f$lmo$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <v01npn55gtq7b3@corp.supernews.com>`

On 18-Dec-2002, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:

> > But the reason to get rid of GO TO is that such a policy results in
> clearer,
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> the use of GO TO?

That income tax form is a very simple program that doesn't require maintenance.
 Neither it, nor Assembler language, nor directions to a party are CoBOL
programs in shops with lots of complex CoBOL programs that require maintenance.

CoBOL shops that have standards that don't normally allow GOTO have fewer coding
& maintenance problems than shops that allow them.   People can argue theory all
they want, but real life experience trumps theory every time.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 30)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-12-19T14:52:42+00:00

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 31)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-12-19T16:04:36-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v04cg4kqljqg56@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212151005.5e0015f0@posting.google.com> <uvprnhf4s9v6bd@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212151913.3a23d45a@posting.google.com> <uvsp9llq05kg68@corp.supernews.com> <dcedb8d0.0212170840.70cd7eb6@posting.google.com> <uvv19a7lcr1f84@corp.supernews.com> <v01iueivin9i9a@corp.supernews.com> <atqk0f$lmo$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <v01npn55gtq7b3@corp.supernews.com> <atqoaf$odt$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <v01uuv4btcom25@corp.supernews.com> <dcedb8d0.0212190855.1adec6bb@posting.google.com>`

```

Joe Zitzelberger <jzitzelb@tsys.com> wrote in message
news:dcedb8d0.0212190855.1adec6bb@posting.google.com...
> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
news:<v01uuv4btcom25@corp.supernews.com>...
[...]
> > Hmm! My experience with state machines trumps "structured"
> > theory, every time. <G>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Evaluate.

Many, likely most, state machines have structured solutions
because a human structured the allowable inputs to the
machine.

If a problem is not structured, why should we expect a
structured solution?

Some state machines have unstructured solutions because
the inputs were not "designed". Some of these unstructured
problems may be made structured by the use state flags
(an alternative to GO TO). Others may be solved by
non-structured techniques, such as directly implemented
state machines, using GO TO for all state transitions.

Interestingly, one COBOL paragraph for each state works
well. The first part deals with required processing. The
second part with conditions for state transitions. The state
transition diagram is reflected, directly, in the code. This
means that a list of changes to a state transition diagram
may by applied directly to the code. This also means that
the code should never be changed, by itself; nor, should
anyone attempt to understand the state machine by reading
the code! Failure to follow these directions would result in
"spaghetti" !
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 32)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-12-20T15:37:08-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v06v9249vu7613@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212151005.5e0015f0@posting.google.com> <uvprnhf4s9v6bd@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212151913.3a23d45a@posting.google.com> <uvsp9llq05kg68@corp.supernews.com> <dcedb8d0.0212170840.70cd7eb6@posting.google.com> <uvv19a7lcr1f84@corp.supernews.com> <v01iueivin9i9a@corp.supernews.com> <atqk0f$lmo$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <v01npn55gtq7b3@corp.supernews.com> <atqoaf$odt$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <v01uuv4btcom25@corp.supernews.com> <dcedb8d0.0212190855.1adec6bb@posting.google.com> <v04cg4kqljqg56@corp.supernews.com>`

```

Rick Smith <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
news:v04cg4kqljqg56@corp.supernews.com...
>
> Joe Zitzelberger <jzitzelb@tsys.com> wrote in message
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
[...]
>
> Interestingly, one COBOL paragraph for each state works
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> "spaghetti" !

It occurs to me that I did not adequately address the issue
of PERFORM and EVALUATE. It is possible to replace
every GO TO statement with MOVE <next state> to
STATE-NUMBER and have the entire process driven by
a state machine driver, in the form:

state-machine-driver.
    move 1 to state-number
    perform until state-number = 0
        evaluate state-number
          when 1
              perform state-1
          ...
          when X
            perform state-X
          when other
            perform post-state-machine-failure
        end-evaluate
    end-perform
    .

This is dangerous because it provides the illusion that
the paragraphs implementing the various states of the
state machine may be modified, independently of the
documentation. They must not!

In a state machine, all states occur at the same level.
Some programming "genius" might decide that a
new state may be performed from within an existing
state, thus avoiding changes to the state machine driver.
The "genius" might also decide to perform states directly.
The result of several such changes is "ST spaghetti";
that is, the simple control structure of the state machine
driver is no longer correct because some states are not
reachable from the driver and some states are entered
by two methods.

Furthermore, this same "genius" may fail to update the
state transition diagram because "anyone can understand
the change, I used structured programming". As a result,
the state machine can no longer be understood from the
documentation.

Thus a state machine, that may be well-understood by
its documentation and easily coded and maintained from
that documentation, using GO TO; becomes a confusing
and difficult mess without the use of a single GO TO!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 33)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-12-20T20:49:47-08:00

```
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote 

> This is dangerous because ....

> Some programming "genius" might decide ...
> The "genius" might also decide ...
> Furthermore, this same "genius" may fail to ...

> Thus a state machine, that may be well-understood by
> its documentation and easily coded and maintained from
> that documentation, using GO TO; becomes a confusing
> and difficult mess without the use of a single GO TO!

That is a really crap argument:

 "Clueless programmer can stuff up a decent program, therefore goto is
better".

Surprise: clueless programmers can stuff up a program that is based on
gotos as well, in fact probably much more easily.

As an aside your state machine driver looks just like a Windows
callback. Surprisingly, programmers tend to be able to cope with these
even though they rarely use a goto.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 34)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-12-21T01:07:59-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v080n6q3s4j386@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <uvsp9llq05kg68@corp.supernews.com> <dcedb8d0.0212170840.70cd7eb6@posting.google.com> <uvv19a7lcr1f84@corp.supernews.com> <v01iueivin9i9a@corp.supernews.com> <atqk0f$lmo$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <v01npn55gtq7b3@corp.supernews.com> <atqoaf$odt$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <v01uuv4btcom25@corp.supernews.com> <dcedb8d0.0212190855.1adec6bb@posting.google.com> <v04cg4kqljqg56@corp.supernews.com> <v06v9249vu7613@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212202049.6fc7dcde@posting.google.com>`

```

Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0212202049.6fc7dcde@posting.google.com...
> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> better".

You seemed to have missed the point. "Clueless programmer
can stuff up a program that depends upon maintaining a
particular structure; therefore program modifications that
damage that structure are 'bad'".

Using GO TO to violate single-entry/single-exit in structured
programming or creating a state heirarchy in an
implicit-GOTO-based state machine are both "bad" because
they damage the structure.

> Surprise: clueless programmers can stuff up a program that is based on
> gotos as well, in fact probably much more easily.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> even though they rarely use a goto.

It's different because callbacks execute isolated procedures;
the state procedures are purposefully connected. Any who
cope with state procedures, as if they were isolated, would
stuff up the machine; hence it is dangerous to use such a
familiar idiom.
```

Well I have seen it used in a number of different scenarios particularly by
one programmer.  As you mentioned PERFORMing a different paragraph to edit
each field can make the code easier to read and modify.  The paragraphs may
then be reused when applicable.  To me the concept of modularity is key to
structured programming.  Using constucts with one entry and one exit seem
to naturally lead to procedures or functions with the same property. I
often make a structure chart of a new program to get familiar with how it
does its job. This helps me zero in on the best places to make any required
changes.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 33)_

- **From:** jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2002-12-30T12:40:22-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dcedb8d0.0212301240.710d8078@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <uvsp9llq05kg68@corp.supernews.com> <dcedb8d0.0212170840.70cd7eb6@posting.google.com> <uvv19a7lcr1f84@corp.supernews.com> <v01iueivin9i9a@corp.supernews.com> <atqk0f$lmo$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <v01npn55gtq7b3@corp.supernews.com> <atqoaf$odt$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <v01uuv4btcom25@corp.supernews.com> <dcedb8d0.0212190855.1adec6bb@posting.google.com> <v04cg4kqljqg56@corp.supernews.com> <v06v9249vu7613@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message news:<v06v9249vu7613@corp.supernews.com>...
[snip]
> It occurs to me that I did not adequately address the issue
> of PERFORM and EVALUATE. It is possible to replace
…[44 more quoted lines elided]…
> and difficult mess without the use of a single GO TO!


So your claim here is that someone who does not understand state
machines will have a harder time screwing up a goto style FSM than
they would a perform and evaluate style FSM.

Did I read that correctly?

FTR, all of my Cobol based FSMs have a driver that mostly looks like:

       Set Initial-State to true
       Perform until Terminal-State
         Perform Locate-Current-State
         Evaluate true
           when Some-State
             Perform Do-Some-State
           when Some-Other-State
             Perform Do-Some-Other-State
           when ...
             Perform Do-...
           when other
             Perform Do-Unknown-State
         End-Evaluate
       End-Perform
       
If someone does not understand the transition diagram and naritive at
the top of the source module their hopes of successfully changing said
program are pretty much null anyway.

One serious, significent and common bug that the perform and evaluate
style is free of is the dreaded Copy-&-Paste goto with mis-changed
label error.  Not much a problem in the trivial examples cited here,
but in programs with hundreds or thousands of states it can become
tedious to match your gotos with your labels and keep your labels in
the right order.  With the above form, the right order becomes
unimportant, cutting the number of bug-introducing opportinities in
half.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 34)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-12-30T20:37:39-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v11skfcqph5o19@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <uvsp9llq05kg68@corp.supernews.com> <dcedb8d0.0212170840.70cd7eb6@posting.google.com> <uvv19a7lcr1f84@corp.supernews.com> <v01iueivin9i9a@corp.supernews.com> <atqk0f$lmo$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <v01npn55gtq7b3@corp.supernews.com> <atqoaf$odt$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <v01uuv4btcom25@corp.supernews.com> <dcedb8d0.0212190855.1adec6bb@posting.google.com> <v04cg4kqljqg56@corp.supernews.com> <v06v9249vu7613@corp.supernews.com> <dcedb8d0.0212301240.710d8078@posting.google.com>`

```

Joe Zitzelberger <jzitzelb@tsys.com> wrote in message
news:dcedb8d0.0212301240.710d8078@posting.google.com...
> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
news:<v06v9249vu7613@corp.supernews.com>...
[...]
> > Thus a state machine, that may be well-understood by
> > its documentation and easily coded and maintained from
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Did I read that correctly?

Not quite. The problem is in the use of PERFORM to
execute, what is for all purposes, an explicit GO TO
structure. Essentially, the use of PERFORM removes
the clues that are needed to keep the clueless
programmer from becoming a "genius."

The programmer may understand FSMs and may
have worked with some. An experienced swimmer
will not dive into a lake without knowing what is
under the water. If the programmer see PERFORM
under the WHEN, they may consider it safe to use
PERFORM inside the machine.

> FTR, all of my Cobol based FSMs have a driver that mostly looks like:
>
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> half.

I have seen:
        evaluate current-state also true
          when 1 also condition
            code for any action
            move <next state> to current-state
   etc.

for a code driven approach.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 35)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-12-31T21:29:49-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0212312129.72475c14@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <uvv19a7lcr1f84@corp.supernews.com> <v01iueivin9i9a@corp.supernews.com> <atqk0f$lmo$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <v01npn55gtq7b3@corp.supernews.com> <atqoaf$odt$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <v01uuv4btcom25@corp.supernews.com> <dcedb8d0.0212190855.1adec6bb@posting.google.com> <v04cg4kqljqg56@corp.supernews.com> <v06v9249vu7613@corp.supernews.com> <dcedb8d0.0212301240.710d8078@posting.google.com> <v11skfcqph5o19@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote

> Not quite. The problem is in the use of PERFORM to
> execute, what is for all purposes, an explicit GO TO
> structure. 

Something is only "an explicit GO TO structure" if it actually uses GO
TO, that is what explicit means.

You probably see all state machines as if they were a GO TO ...
DEPENDING ON .. regardless of how they are actually constructed, it is
just what you think a state machine _is_.

> Essentially, the use of PERFORM removes
> the clues that are needed to keep the clueless
> programmer from becoming a "genius."

Get over it.  A state machine does not have to fit your [lack of]
imagination. Certainly a mix of PERFORM and GO TO may well a cause of
disaster, but a mix of PERFORM and the GO TO structure that you
imagine around, say, an in-line perform  and evaluate, only exists in
your mind.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 30)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-12-19T10:25:36-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0212191025.5f34565@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212151005.5e0015f0@posting.google.com> <uvprnhf4s9v6bd@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212151913.3a23d45a@posting.google.com> <uvsp9llq05kg68@corp.supernews.com> <dcedb8d0.0212170840.70cd7eb6@posting.google.com> <uvv19a7lcr1f84@corp.supernews.com> <v01iueivin9i9a@corp.supernews.com> <atqk0f$lmo$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <v01npn55gtq7b3@corp.supernews.com> <atqoaf$odt$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <v01uuv4btcom25@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote

> Hmm! My experience with state machines trumps "structured"
> theory, every time. <G>
> 
> The implementation of state machines, by using GO TO, have
> fewer coding and maintenance problems than without GO TO!

Programmers who have developed their styles using goto have fewer
coding and maintenance problems when working on programs that are
built using goto than when working on programs using structed
techniques.

Programmers who use structured techniques have fewer problems when
working on programs that use structured techniques than when working
on programs that break this with gotos.

State machines can be developed using un-structured mechanisms or
using structured mechanisms.

It may well be true that you (who uses goto in preference to other
mechanisms) will have less trouble with a goto based state machine
than you would have with a structured one, but that is _not_ the fault
of the structured techniques.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 31)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-12-19T19:24:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<att6e2$bs7$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212151005.5e0015f0@posting.google.com> <uvprnhf4s9v6bd@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212151913.3a23d45a@posting.google.com> <uvsp9llq05kg68@corp.supernews.com> <dcedb8d0.0212170840.70cd7eb6@posting.google.com> <uvv19a7lcr1f84@corp.supernews.com> <v01iueivin9i9a@corp.supernews.com> <atqk0f$lmo$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <v01npn55gtq7b3@corp.supernews.com> <atqoaf$odt$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <v01uuv4btcom25@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212191025.5f34565@posting.google.com>`

```

On 19-Dec-2002, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

> Programmers who have developed their styles using goto have fewer
> coding and maintenance problems when working on programs that are
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> on programs that break this with gotos.

I developed my original style using ALTER goto's.  But I have changed through
the years and I have worked with users of various styles through the years. 
Maybe programmers using goto's were doing so because they were poorer
programmers, maybe the goto's contributed the the problems - but they wrote
programs with more errors and programs that were harder to maintain (by both
styles of programmers), than did programmers who changed their styles.

Like I said - I don't have evidence to support causality.  It could be that
poorer programmers were more resistant to change, and that the better
programmers would have programmed better even if the styles were reversed.   But
they weren't reversed - there was an obvious difference in the quality of the
code between people who used perform logic and those who used goto logic.   This
was observed in quite a few companies across quite a few years.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 32)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-12-19T18:13:36-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v04k1t6l4m211a@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212151005.5e0015f0@posting.google.com> <uvprnhf4s9v6bd@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212151913.3a23d45a@posting.google.com> <uvsp9llq05kg68@corp.supernews.com> <dcedb8d0.0212170840.70cd7eb6@posting.google.com> <uvv19a7lcr1f84@corp.supernews.com> <v01iueivin9i9a@corp.supernews.com> <atqk0f$lmo$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <v01npn55gtq7b3@corp.supernews.com> <atqoaf$odt$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <v01uuv4btcom25@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212191025.5f34565@posting.google.com> <att6e2$bs7$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:att6e2$bs7$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
[...]
> Maybe programmers using goto's were doing so because they were poorer
> programmers,

This insults a few million well-respected programmers. Maybe
they were using goto because that was what was available.

> maybe the goto's contributed the the problems

Not likely! See Donald Knuths paper on structured programming
using goto.

> - but they wrote
> programs with more errors

This has nothing to do with goto. Some programmers may not
have had enough experience or knowledge in design theory
which was also changing during the "structured revolution." Some
may not have adequately tested their programs due to lack of
tools or knowledge.

> and programs that were harder to maintain (by both
> styles of programmers),

"[H]arder to maintain" is a relative concept. "The program is
s***, no one can maintain it." versus "The program uses
advanced concepts to solve a complex problem; those lacking
knowlege of the concepts and experience with the problem
area cannot maintain it." If you must assess blame, blame the
programmer for the first case; blame yourself for the second. <g>

> than did programmers who changed their styles.

Maybe the truth is that structured programming allowed
some marginal programmers to become good programmers;
because they were less burdened by the need to properly
organize and document their non-structured programming
solutions.

> Like I said - I don't have evidence to support causality.

No one does. The use of preponderance to prove anything
is unreliable; because there are things we do not know and
may never know.

>  It could be that
> poorer programmers were more resistant to change,

But, you implied, earlier, that those who use goto are poorer
progammers.

> and that the better
> programmers would have programmed better even if the styles were reversed.

Here, you imply that programmer capability is independent of goto!

>But
> they weren't reversed - there was an obvious difference in the quality of
the
> code between people who used perform logic and those who used goto logic.
This
> was observed in quite a few companies across quite a few years.

Structured techniques, including programming and design, and
improved testing have together raised the quality of computer
programs; but, I still don't understand what all this has to do with
goto!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 33)_

- **From:** jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2002-12-30T09:23:59-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dcedb8d0.0212300923.132f4911@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <uvsp9llq05kg68@corp.supernews.com> <dcedb8d0.0212170840.70cd7eb6@posting.google.com> <uvv19a7lcr1f84@corp.supernews.com> <v01iueivin9i9a@corp.supernews.com> <atqk0f$lmo$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <v01npn55gtq7b3@corp.supernews.com> <atqoaf$odt$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <v01uuv4btcom25@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212191025.5f34565@posting.google.com> <att6e2$bs7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <v04k1t6l4m211a@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message news:<v04k1t6l4m211a@corp.supernews.com>...

> > maybe the goto's contributed the the problems
> 
> Not likely! See Donald Knuths paper on structured programming
> using goto.

Which is a good and interesting paper.  However, something of a
mis-cite.  The first half of the paper discusses techniques for
removal of GoTos from code.

The second half discusses places where a GoTos should be added. 
However, several of those places no longer apply -- 1974 efficiency
very different from 2002 efficiency.  For example:

  -- 'recursion elimination' and 'recursion vs iteration' is usually
handled today by optimizing compilers.  You get the benefit of the
clear, easy to understand recursive source code with the faster,
compiler tuned object.  Granted, his tuning approach still works, but
it will break todays optimizers (as most GoTos will).  I would say
this no longer applies.

 -- 'Boolean variable elimination' has the same arguments as above. 
Any optimizer will do this for you (In part because of Knuths ideas on
optimization).  You get the easier to read source code with the
boolean variable and the fast object.  This case also has been made
moot by the passage of time.

You are taking too much from the title of the paper and not enough
from the content.  Lets not forget his concluding statement:

  "I personally wouldn't mind having go to in the highest level
[language], just in case I really need it; but I probably would never
use it, if the general iteration and event constructs suggested in
this paper were present."

Well, it is 30 years later, and Knuths ideas about control structures
have long since arrived, even in Cobol.

The problem was, and still is, that most use of GoTo is not for
creating sturcture, but speghetti.

> > Like I said - I don't have evidence to support causality.
> 
> No one does. The use of preponderance to prove anything
> is unreliable; because there are things we do not know and
> may never know.

There are several studies that have provided some evidence. 
Primarily, that code that is indented to follow the logic has a much
higher understandability (see previous posts).  Since it is
practically impossible to indent goto code to follow the program
logic, it follows that goto code will suffer the lack of
understandability of unindented code.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 34)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-12-30T18:48:33-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v11m7mteu4o1dc@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <uvsp9llq05kg68@corp.supernews.com> <dcedb8d0.0212170840.70cd7eb6@posting.google.com> <uvv19a7lcr1f84@corp.supernews.com> <v01iueivin9i9a@corp.supernews.com> <atqk0f$lmo$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <v01npn55gtq7b3@corp.supernews.com> <atqoaf$odt$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <v01uuv4btcom25@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212191025.5f34565@posting.google.com> <att6e2$bs7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <v04k1t6l4m211a@corp.supernews.com> <dcedb8d0.0212300923.132f4911@posting.google.com>`

```

Joe Zitzelberger <jzitzelb@tsys.com> wrote in message
news:dcedb8d0.0212300923.132f4911@posting.google.com...
> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
news:<v04k1t6l4m211a@corp.supernews.com>...
>
> > > maybe the goto's contributed the the problems
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> very different from 2002 efficiency.

First, I will apologize for what may seem a harsh response.
It comes from taking the role of a Devil's Advocate. Goto
is often characterized as evil. <G>

The statement, to which I responded, referred to the past
and suggestted an assignment of blame to goto. One
could also assign the blame to the absence of structured
programming knowledge and practice; rather than the
presence of goto. It would be wrong; because things
cannot contribute to problems, nor can the absence of a
thing.

Every problem comes about from a relationship between
a human and a thing; and, often, other humans. Hence,
the problem of difficult to maintain source code, for example,
did not come from goto; but, rather, the uncontrolled use
of goto and the absence of adequate documentation by
humans.

The existence of Knuth's paper means that it was always
possible to use goto in a manner consistent with structured
programming theory. It is possible, even likely, that some
programmers were using goto in such a manner, even prior
to Dijkstra's first paper on goto, in 1965. For example, a
COBOL pioneer, after being given the explanation of a
technique, was quoted as saying, "We have always done
it that way. We just never had a name for it." Maybe some
programmers told the truth when presented the concept
of structured programming.

One early structured (non-SP) use of goto was in finite state
automata. The first paper, relating this field to computer
programming, appeared in 1956 (Kleene, referenced in Aho
and Ullman, "Principles of Compiler Design"). Early, or classic,
state machines used goto as the method for structuring flow
of control. Hence, the controlled use of goto actually occurred
prior to Knuth's paper and Dijkstra's observations. It just
wasn't structured programming.

Finally, we may be easily confused by our own observations.
Structured programming, using a good set of statements,
makes every use of goto appear horrible, by comparison.
This is because, structured programming provides a
common form for communicating ideas with other
programmers; hence these other programmers more easily
identify the common structures than the same or similar
structures implemented with goto.

The observant (and impartial) reader might conclude, from
the above, that the absence of structured programming was
the problem and its availability the solution. As I said earlier,
that conclusion would be wrong. <g>

Goto did not contribute to the problems, goto was not a
problem, and the occasional use of goto in some situations
should never cause any programmer to respond with
projectile vomiting. <G>

[...]
>
> You are taking too much from the title of the paper and not enough
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> creating sturcture, but speghetti.

I am not sure what most progammers do; but some
programmers, who use goto, might characterize their use
as exiting an intractable situation created by adherrence
to structured programming. While the intent is to violate
the structure, it is not to create spaghetti. We are
programmers; not chefs. <g>

> > > Like I said - I don't have evidence to support causality.
> >
> > No one does. The use of preponderance to prove anything
> > is unreliable; because there are things we do not know and
> > may never know.

I should have used "decide" instead of "prove", above. Proof
requires belief in assertions that have not yet been falsified.
Preponderance allows for opinion (beliefs) without regard to
its truth.

> There are several studies that have provided some evidence.
> Primarily, that code that is indented to follow the logic has a much
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> understandability of unindented code.

Of course, this goes right to preponderance. Several studies
report on programming factors, all intended to help
programmers to decide how to program, generally. No
study, no matter how confident the result, can ever show
that goto must not be used in "this" program at "this" point.
No study can ever show that "this" program would be made
clearer by adhering to "that" set of rules.
```

```

Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0212191025.5f34565@posting.google.com...
> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> using structured mechanisms.

"[U]n-structured? Just because a program does not use
structures as defined in "structured programming" and
procedural decomposition does not mean it has no structure.
Maybe we should call such methods non-ST, as opposed to
ST (Structured Techniques). Then, the ability of any programmer
to work with such non-ST programs is determined, in part, by
how well they learn the specific non-ST structures. (And, yes,
in some programs there is no discernible structure.)

> It may well be true that you (who uses goto in preference to other
> mechanisms) will have less trouble with a goto based state machine
> than you would have with a structured one, but that is _not_ the fault
> of the structured techniques.

I find no fault with defined ST, except that it may not define
some useful structures. Just because three structures is
all that is required to create any program; does not mean
that other structures, including non-ST structures, are not
useful. Nor, do I accept that ST is, or should be, the only
way to create COBOL programs.
```

_(thread depth: 32)_

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>

```
Just because a program uses only structured programming constructs and no
GO TOs does NOT mean it has a good structure. I have seen programs where
one huge nested IF was used to edit all the fields on a screen. By huge I
mean that this single nested IF continued on for over 20 pages.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 33)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-12-20T22:27:38-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v07nahis6m7m9f@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212151005.5e0015f0@posting.google.com> <uvprnhf4s9v6bd@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212151913.3a23d45a@posting.google.com> <uvsp9llq05kg68@corp.supernews.com> <dcedb8d0.0212170840.70cd7eb6@posting.google.com> <uvv19a7lcr1f84@corp.supernews.com> <v01iueivin9i9a@corp.supernews.com> <atqk0f$lmo$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <v01npn55gtq7b3@corp.supernews.com> <atqoaf$odt$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <v01uuv4btcom25@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212191025.5f34565@posting.google.com> <v05brag0qsjb3b@corp.supernews.com> <01c2a88e$62a99ae0$fbccf943@chottel>`

```

Charles Hottel <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote in message
news:01c2a88e$62a99ae0$fbccf943@chottel...
> Just because a program uses only structured programming constructs and no
> GO TOs does NOT mean it has a good structure. I have seen programs where
> one huge nested IF was used to edit all the fields on a screen. By huge I
> mean that this single nested IF continued on for over 20 pages.

I seem to recall you mentioning this some time ago. You had
mentioned having to use a ruler to line up the IF and ELSE
words to make certain you were working with the correct part
of the statement; but I do not recall your mentioning 'edit'.

If you mean 'edit' in the sense of validate, then it might be
possible, under the new standard, to use VALIDATE to
replace this 20 page nested IF. The companion clauses for
the data division will slightly complicate the creation of the
data definitions; but it greatly simplifies the procedure
division.

Many seem to forget that structured programming is
associated with proving program correctness, not for
improved readability. That it improves readability most
of the time is a benefit. That readability deteriorates
rapidly with deep nesting is a drawback; hence many
resort to procedural decomposition to maintain some
level of readability.

I would suggest that VALIDATE and its companion clauses
are non-ST; because the clauses are not procedures or
structures within the sense of ST. Sometimes non-ST is
better than ST. <g>
```

One of the nice features of the IBM complier is it keeps track of the
nesting level and prints it on the listing.  This helps you see what is
related to what or whether an END-if has been left out.  I also like it
that it prints the line number reference for data fields and PERFORMed
procedures.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 33)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-12-23T15:14:59+00:00

```

On 20-Dec-2002, "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote:

> Just because a program uses only structured programming constructs and no
> GO TOs does NOT mean it has a good structure. I have seen programs where
> one huge nested IF was used to edit all the fields on a screen. By huge I
> mean that this single nested IF continued on for over 20 pages.

Certainly structure alone is insufficient.  But generally when we set standards
for structure the standards also encourage small, manageable paragraphs, and
discourage nested IF statements.

The goal isn't to get rid of GO TO.  The goal is to have clear, maintainable
code.   Having a function that fits on the screen is a way of doing this - and
this happens most easily with PERFORMs.

A standard that doesn't work towards this goal is clueless.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Programmer Behaviour

_(thread depth: 25)_

- **From:** jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2002-12-19T08:45:36-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dcedb8d0.0212190845.362df355@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <uvku6jgpudb52b@corp.supernews.com> <3DFB69D1.22255579@Azonic.co.nz> <uvliikdoia360e@corp.supernews.com> <3DFC2B43.A667E057@Azonic.co.nz> <uvn3l63jc6fe52@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212151005.5e0015f0@posting.google.com> <uvprnhf4s9v6bd@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212151913.3a23d45a@posting.google.com> <uvsp9llq05kg68@corp.supernews.com> <dcedb8d0.0212170840.70cd7eb6@posting.google.com> <uvv19a7lcr1f84@corp.supernews.com>`

```
In this case a GO TO, real or implied, is wholly appropriate when the
alternatives consist if adding frames to the activation stack,
expanding the size of the executable and causing a low-powered
processor to perform extra instructions.

I had no idea we were talking about embedded code.  I was thinking in
terms of a largeish Dell with half a gig of ram and an idle task that
runs 90% of the time, or a mainframe that wouldn't mind an extra few
dozen instructions or a few bytes here or there.

For most applications Moores Law has relieved the need to be overly
concerned with these issues.  Not that I'm advocating bloatware, but
there is a reasonable balance.  I once wrote a hard disk driver for an
Apple II in under 100 bytes.  I had to juggle and align op codes to
make them do double duty as data, but it worked.  It was completely
unmaintainable, but it lived in an area of memory that would not be
overwritten by any application.  I would never advocate such an
approach today, but in those tight memory days it worked.

You have to do what you have to do to live within your hardware
limits.  I surrender.

Regards,

Joe Zitzelberger

"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message news:<uvv19a7lcr1f84@corp.supernews.com>...
> It is part of an embedded program that controls a terminal.
> The micro is an 8051 with 2K for variables and 128K of
…[67 more quoted lines elided]…
> it makes sense. Still, I only use it, rarely.
```

_(thread depth: 21)_

- **From:** jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2002-12-16T08:51:05-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dcedb8d0.0212160851.1928c1d5@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <3DF839D0.89C7C2B8@Azonic.co.nz> <dcedb8d0.0212120853.2fb2a0d0@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212131223.40116ee2@posting.google.com> <atdhoq$pdv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <uvku6jgpudb52b@corp.supernews.com> <3DFB69D1.22255579@Azonic.co.nz> <uvliikdoia360e@corp.supernews.com> <3DFC2B43.A667E057@Azonic.co.nz> <uvn3l63jc6fe52@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0212151005.5e0015f0@posting.google.com> <uvprnhf4s9v6bd@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message news:<uvprnhf4s9v6bd@corp.supernews.com>...
><snippage>
> In C, I suppose could use:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> emphasizes the loop. In any case; both use "tricky" code.

The question on my mind is why you are over-complicating things.  Both
of these constructs offer you exactly what you are looking for without
the "trickyness".  Why not write them thus:

 do {
     statements;
 } while (condition);
 statements;
 
and

 perform with test after
   until not condition
     statements
 end-perform
 statements

Clear, concise and free of excess noise.
```

#### ↳ Re: Strange Cobol-behaviour

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-12-05T11:42:37-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0212051142.4ff1d68@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com>`

```
erik06@hotmail.com (Erik) wrote

> Can anyone tell me why this one donï¿½t work?
> 
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> "              END-IF
                 END-IF
                 END-IF
                 END-IF
> "
> "              IF  ILK = '12' OR '15' OR '18' OR '23'
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> "          END-IF.

> Why is that? 

The behaviour you described is correct for the code as written.

> I got the END-IF, that should end the first IF statement.

No. Your first END-IF terminated the "IF ILK = '16'", you need an
END-IF for each IF that you want to terminate.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Cobol-behaviour

- **From:** erik06@hotmail.com (Erik)
- **Date:** 2002-12-06T00:21:19-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b973b52c.0212060021.4a24dbab@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212051142.4ff1d68@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote in message news:<217e491a.0212051142.4ff1d68@posting.google.com>...
> erik06@hotmail.com (Erik) wrote
> 
…[43 more quoted lines elided]…
> END-IF for each IF that you want to terminate.


The number of END-IF:s donï¿½t not matter.
Look at this:
IF  NYCKEL IN IKUA111 (1:5) = 'IKONO'   GO TO F-120 ELSE
IF  NYCKEL IN IKUA111 (1:5) = 'IKADR'   GO TO F-120 ELSE
IF  NYCKEL IN IKUA111 (1:5) = 'IPRNT'   GO TO F-120 ELSE
IF  NYCKEL IN IKUA111 (1:4) = 'ANST'    GO TO F-120 ELSE
IF  NYCKEL IN IKUA111 (1:4) = 'IUTL'    GO TO F-130 ELSE
IF  NYCKEL IN IKUA111 (1:4) = 'FSEK'    GO TO F-130 ELSE
IF  NYCKEL IN IKUA111 (1:4) = 'USEK'    GO TO F-130 ELSE
IF  NYCKEL IN IKUA111 (1:4) = 'IK53'    GO TO F-000 ELSE
IF  NYCKEL IN IKUA111 (1:4) = 'IK54'    GO TO F-130 ELSE
IF  NYCKEL IN IKUA111 (1:4) = 'IK57'    GO TO F-130.

Here I got 10 IF:s and not any END-IF:s.
This is correct programming, because the ELSE taka care of the IF.
My problem is not to get my code to work, but WHY it donï¿½t work as written.

/Erik
```

Never mind the If's or number of End-If's, your code is sloppy.  How
many levels of if's do you want?  You are evaluating the same
variable, therefore, use an EVALUATE, it's simpler to understand and

chiTownVet wrote:

> Never mind the If's or number of End-If's, your code is sloppy.  How
> many levels of if's do you want?  You are evaluating the same

Sloppy code is usually a matter of inexperience:  (USUALLY!!  Keep your flamethrowers off; I have
encountered code by people with years of experience that I literally couldn't understand and had to
rewrite!).  A series of IF's, >> properly written <<, is perfectly understandable, at least as much so as a
properly writtten EVALUATE.  Once this fellow catches on, his coding will improve.

BTW: Djikstra's dictum was that all programs could be written using "sequential code (not the right phrase,
I know)", IF's, & PERFORM's.  So who dared to invent the EVALUATE & why????

PL

only logical constucts needed to describe any algorithm.  EVALUATE is just a

have a problem with it.  (Though he had some unflattering things to say

Cobol language has on programmers' imaginations)  :-)

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Cobol-behaviour

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-17T13:27:20-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<atntqq$cto$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212051142.4ff1d68@posting.google.com> <5cc91e47.0212071825.780157c9@posting.google.com>`

```
Eric,
  In reply to your latest note, the following is just one of about 5 or 6
notes that directly answered your question.

The simple fact is that the way you "indented" your code made you THINK that
your END-IF's matched IF's that they did not match.  The suggestion to
"indent better" was simply to show you exactly which END-IF goes with which
IF.

In case you missed the end ot this message it explictly answers your
question with the statement,

" No. Your first END-IF terminated the "IF ILK = '16'", you need an  END-IF
for each IF that you want to terminate."
```

#### ↳ Re: Strange Cobol-behaviour

- **From:** charles.godwin@ca.com (Charles Godwin)
- **Date:** 2002-12-07T01:47:48-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c8035ce7.0212070147.be6d998@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com>`

```
erik06@hotmail.com (Erik) wrote in message news:<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com>...
> Hi.
> Can anyone tell me why this one donï¿½t work?

<< A lot cut >>
An alternate solution is to use the in-line PERFORM ... END-PERFORM
construction. The END-PERFORM acts as a scope terminator so all 
"hanging" IF's are terminated. Advatages include:
 - You don't need a period
 - You can insert another ELSE IF series without worrying about END-IF's

Here's my suggestion.
IF  XXXX1
    MOVE 0 TO SW-UPP
    PERFORM *> Start a PERFORM "block"
    IF  ILK  = '10' OR '11' OR '24' OR '29'
        MOVE 1 TO SW-UPP
        COMPUTE KOLL-ANTAL = KOLL-ANTAL * ILKAT-MULTIPLICERA
    ELSE
    IF  ILK = '05' OR '36'
        MOVE 1 TO SW-UPP
        COMPUTE KOLL-ANTAL = KOLL-ANTAL * 1.5
        COMPUTE KOLL-ANTAL = KOLL-ANTAL * ILKAT-MULTIPLICERA
    ELSE
    IF  ILK = '06' OR '38' OR '19'
        MOVE 1 TO SW-UPP
        COMPUTE KOLL-ANTAL = KOLL-ANTAL * 2
        COMPUTE KOLL-ANTAL = KOLL-ANTAL * ILKAT-MULTIPLICERA
    ELSE
    IF  ILK = '16'
        MOVE 1 TO SW-UPPDATERA
        COMPUTE KOLL-ANTAL = KOLL-ANTAL * 0.5
    END-PERFORM *> This the terminator of all the IF's
    PERFORM *> do the same again for clarity
    IF  ILK = '12' OR '15' OR '18' OR '23'
        MOVE 1 TO SW-UPPD
        COMPUTE TIDO = TIDO - KOLL-ANTAL
    ELSE
    IF  SW-UPPD = 1
        COMPUTE TIDO = TIDO + KOLL-ANTAL
    END-PERFORM
END-IF.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Cobol-behaviour

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-07T05:59:28-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<assnr3$sp1$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <c8035ce7.0212070147.be6d998@posting.google.com>`

```
Not true in an ANSI/ISO conforming compiler.

The nested "construct" within a conforming PERFORM ... END-PERFORM *must* be
an imperative statement, not a conditional one, so a "dangling IF" (or else)
would be non-conforming and would get a compiler error on some/many
compilers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Strange Cobol-behaviour

- **From:** charles.godwin@ca.com (Charles Godwin)
- **Date:** 2002-12-07T06:23:24-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c8035ce7.0212070623.6a29178@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com> <c8035ce7.0212070147.be6d998@posting.google.com> <assnr3$sp1$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message news:<assnr3$sp1$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>...
> Not true in an ANSI/ISO conforming compiler.
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>  wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com
That's too bad, Bill. It was an simple solution. On the other hand,
using that enforced rule on the END-PERFORM an argument could be made
that it would still be a good construct as the compiler would enforce
the implied logic and ensure the corect number of END-IFs were
inserted.
```

```
Erik,

I see that no one yet has really answered the question.  I'll show you
why it does't work.  I have inserted *[* around the parts of the IF's
that go together and are causing PART of the problem.

erik06@hotmail.com (Erik) wrote in message news:<b973b52c.0212050211.316c13ba@posting.google.com>...
> Hi.
> Can anyone tell me why this one donï¿½t work?
> 
        IF  XXXX1
            MOVE 0 TO SW-UPP
            IF  ILK  = '10' OR '11' OR '24' OR '29'
                MOVE 1 TO SW-UPP
                COMPUTE KOLL-ANTAL = KOLL-ANTAL * ILKAT-MULTIPLICERA
            ELSE
            IF  ILK = '05' OR '36'
                MOVE 1 TO SW-UPP
                COMPUTE KOLL-ANTAL = KOLL-ANTAL * 1.5
                COMPUTE KOLL-ANTAL = KOLL-ANTAL * ILKAT-MULTIPLICERA
            ELSE
            *[*
            IF  ILK = '06' OR '38' OR '19'
                MOVE 1 TO SW-UPP
                COMPUTE KOLL-ANTAL = KOLL-ANTAL * 2
                COMPUTE KOLL-ANTAL = KOLL-ANTAL * ILKAT-MULTIPLICERA
            ELSE

            IF  ILK = '16'
                MOVE 1 TO SW-UPPDATERA
                COMPUTE KOLL-ANTAL = KOLL-ANTAL * 0.5
            END-IF
**>>> Note the above IF, END-IF pair don't impact the logic flow.  
***>>> You might be able to visualize this easier with it removed.
            IF  ILK = '12' OR '15' OR '18' OR '23'
                MOVE 1 TO SW-UPPD
                COMPUTE TIDO = TIDO - KOLL-ANTAL
            ELSE
                IF  SW-UPPD = 1
                    COMPUTE TIDO = TIDO + KOLL-ANTAL
                END-IF
            END-IF *]*
        END-IF.

Thane Still - When ILK is "05" it executed the code after that IF - up
to ELSE - but then quits in this IF - doing nothing more because the
NEXT IF is false -- Everything inside my notation is part of the ELSE
condition - the "Otherwise" portion of IF ILK = "05".

>            IF XXX.............
> 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> /Erik

#### ↳ Re: Article outlining Micro Focus plans for .NET

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-05T10:45:42-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<asnvrp$98t$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<asnb4c$o3h$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`

```
Posted at comp.lang.cobol with CC's to various Micro Focus and Fujitsu
people.

Given the statement in the article,

"... while there may be some esoteric things, such as a few obsolete things
in the COBOL standards, we are taking forward almost all of the language.
..."

I will (personally) be VERY interested in a comparison chart of what COBOL
language supported in Fujitsu COBOL V6.0 and NetExpress 4.0 (including
extensions, ANSI'85 *and* *ISO 2002* syntax) will be supported in each of
the two .NET products.  (It will be particularly interesting to see which of
the "dialect" things that Micro Focus currently supports are retained -
whehter TRANSFORM statment or COMP-6 or Argument-Value or whatever)

Certainly, I hope (expect?) Micro Focus won't have the "basic" problems of
not supporting writing to printers and disallowing internal SORT's and
nested programs, that Fujitsu has - while NOT introducing any "new"
ommissions that Fujitsu already supports.

Suggestion to those in Micro Focus - when using the phrase "obsolete things
in the COBOL standards" - you may want to make it clear whether you are
talking about those items designated obsolete in the '85 Standard or in the
2002 Standard.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Article outlining Micro Focus plans for .NET

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2002-12-05T20:26:03-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0212052026.24fd6440@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<asnb4c$o3h$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <asnvrp$98t$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Speaking as an individual and a representative of Fujitsu....

The Fujitsu implementation seems to be geared toward taking advantage
of the interlanguage object inheritence of the .NET platform.  With
Fujitsu COBOL .NET you can inherit from objects of other languages,
and other languages can inherit from COBOL objects.  This is one of
the most interesting aspects of .NET.

The MicroFocus press releases I have read - I have not seen or
reviewed the product - seem to have a mapping aspect where you can
compile your COBOL under .NET, but with no mention of the cross
language inheritence.  As I understand it - one can create ILC from a
single language.  This is similar to the creation of Java from COBOL
that PerCOBOL does.  A full COBOL implementation that runs in the JVM
via conversion to Java.

What I am interested in learning is whether or not MicroFocus is
sacrificing the Interlanguage inheritence offered by .NET in order to
implement their compiler for .NET.



"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message news:<asnvrp$98t$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>...
> Posted at comp.lang.cobol with CC's to various Micro Focus and Fujitsu
> people.
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

<a id="t-9164f25e90"></a>
## Thread: Conditional "checking" — 3 msgs

### Conditional "checking"

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-05T15:30:33-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<asoghr$bed$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
This is a new thread - but a follow-up on the scope terminator discussion.

As someone who is NOT currently supporting production COBOL code, I just
thought that I would throw out a "personal" opinion on two coding techniques
(that are MEDIUM controversial).  I have worked in shops where these were
strongly recommended and others where they are almost never used.

1) *ALWAYS* check for the success of file I/O operations.  In '85 Standard
compilers there are 3 ways of doing this - each has PROs and CONs.
  - Use <not> AT END/INVALID KEY  (not available on OPEN/CLOSE - and may
require further "investigation")
 - Status Key checking after each I/O (remember that only the first digit
being "0" means successful,  02, 05, and the new 0x codes may require
additional checking).  This may be a problem as the Standard and
implementors add new codes
 - "Traditional" I/O Declaratives (Let's you check file status code and
"group" types of I/O - but is certainly not structured)

  With the 2002 Standard, new "common exception handling" declaratives are
also available - and provide for significant new "Standard" error checking.

2) *ALWAYS* use a <not> ON SIZE phrases with arithmetic statements UNLESS
they are nested within IF statements that have already checked for "valid"
possible results (which is NOT a particularly efficient way to do such
things).

    ***

Coming from (long ago and far away) an IBM mainframe environment where it
was relatively common to let a program "ABEND" if there were (QSAM) file
problems or unexpected arithmetic "results" - I know that there is much code
out there without such "condition handling" - but especially as the world is
going more and more to online systems, long running batch programs (with
tight production windows) and 24x7 systems,  I just don't (personally) think
that new (or significantly modified) code should be relying on abnormal
program termination as the best thing to do when the "unexpected" happens.
```

#### ↳ Re: Conditional "checking"

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-12-05T22:06:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<asoilc$srs$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<asoghr$bed$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

On  5-Dec-2002, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:

> 2) *ALWAYS* use a <not> ON SIZE phrases with arithmetic statements UNLESS
> they are nested within IF statements that have already checked for "valid"
> possible results (which is NOT a particularly efficient way to do such
> things).

What about other cases where it is impossible to have a size error?  I just
don't see those errors in real life.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Conditional "checking"

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-05T16:28:34-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<asojul$9ls$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<asoghr$bed$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <asoilc$srs$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
I guess that I have been hit too often with the famous user/analyst saying
  "Such-and-such a field can NEVER get bigger than ..."

and then talking to them after the 3am ABEND and hearing,

  "Well, it was never SUPPOSED to be bigger than ... but we added a new
product/department/thingy and I guess we forgot to tell you ..."
```

<a id="t-ea3d1a5e76"></a>
## Thread: COBSW — 16 msgs

### COBSW

- **From:** dcantua@co.la.ca.us (Daniel G. Cantua)
- **Date:** 2002-12-05T14:38:18-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6cca870b.0212051438.630564cc@posting.google.com>`

```
Can anyone help me? I have Some OS/2 workstations using MicroFocus
Cobol v3.4 and Windows2000 workstations using MicroFocus NetExpress
3.1.  The program open one of the files with lock.  Sometimes this
cause the system to hang.  We think this is a locking problem and we
are going to tried to set the COBSW to '+R+L1+B'.  We are hoping that
this will solve our problem.  if this does not work can anyone suggest
what else we can try?
```

#### ↳ Re: COBSW

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2002-12-06T01:28:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-4BRfF73Qi4sb@h24-82-204-17.wp.shawcable.net>`
- **References:** `<6cca870b.0212051438.630564cc@posting.google.com>`

```
On Thu, 5 Dec 2002 22:38:18 UTC, dcantua@co.la.ca.us (Daniel G. 
Cantua) wrote:

> Can anyone help me? I have Some OS/2 workstations using MicroFocus
> Cobol v3.4 and Windows2000 workstations using MicroFocus NetExpress
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> what else we can try?

Are you using a "no timeout wait" for a lock, that will cause a hang 
if the lock is not released. You might also want to check to see if 
the Windows "escalated locking" is causing problems between the two 
types of machines.

Are the files resident on the OS/2 box or the Windows box?
```

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@worldnet.att.net>

…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
