<a id="t-4d24d612f8"></a>
## Thread: How to make string representation of an arbitrary type — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How to make string representation of an arbitrary type

- **From:** dsha <member@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-14T08:40:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2281425.1039855210@dbforums.com>`

```

Hi all,

I've encountered the following problem using Cobol and need help from
Cobol experts. I am developing a tool that instruments a Cobol program
by inserting calls
before selected statements of the program. Those calls send the values
of variables occuring in the statements
to the message-queue middleware. The values of variables should be in a
human-readable form, so I need to
convert variables of an arbitrary type into string representation before
sending them. Some USAGES (like
DISPLAY) do not need conversion, while others (BINARY, COMP-1 ....)
should be converted.

It seems that all I need is to redirect somehow the output of the
DISPLAY statements into a memory buffer
(under which I mean a variable with PIC X(...)). So,
I wonder whether can I DISPLAY to the memory and, if yes, how this
could be done?

I use IBM Visual Age 2.2 Cobol and IBM MQ Series. If someone could
suggest any other solution to the problem,
it will be greately appreciated.

Thanks in advance.
```

#### ↳ Re: How to make string representation of an arbitrary type

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-12-14T13:27:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OkGK9.1152$qU5.576114@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<2281425.1039855210@dbforums.com>`

```
"dsha" <member@dbforums.com> wrote in message
news:2281425.1039855210@dbforums.com...
>
>. I am developing a tool that instruments a Cobol program
> by inserting calls before selected statements of the program.....
>.. I need to  convert variables of an arbitrary type into string
representation before
> sending them.....It seems that all I need is to redirect somehow the
output of the
> DISPLAY statements into a memory buffer (under which I mean a variable
with PIC X(...))

A variable IS a memory buffer, so just insert a MOVE to a variable you
define with USAGE IS DISPLAY PIC X(n) for alpha, PIC  9(9)V9(4) SIGN IS
SEPARATE for any numeric. (you may need to define alternate scale and
decimals depending on the dataname, but that's no big deal). Let the
compiler handle any conversions. This also lets the compiler handle any
conversions of 'implementor-defined' numeric datatypes (e.g.,
 COMP-1,-2 -3).

If you need it, you can get the address of that variable with the ADDRESS OF
function, but if you insert a CALL you can just add this variable to the
parameter list and the compiler will handle that for you, too.

You may have some problems dealing with in-line literals because you'll have
to parse out the portion which is the literal, but as long as you are
'inserting' code, this seems pretty reasonable.
```

#### ↳ Re: How to make string representation of an arbitrary type

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2002-12-14T09:00:30-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0212140900.237a2c4e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<2281425.1039855210@dbforums.com>`

```
Your main issue is with Floats.  COMP-1.

I'm not sure which portions of this IBM has implemented - are there
scientific notation representations (an intrinsic function perhaps) of
floats available?

A Display I don't think is the right thing to do - you simply need to
find usage display equivalents for each.

Would a hex conversion work?  Meaning - you might have a COMP-1 field
with a hex value of X"00073498Df47" - could a conversion to that
notation - the X" notation - work for you?


dsha <member@dbforums.com> wrote in message news:<2281425.1039855210@dbforums.com>...
> Hi all,
> 
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks in advance.
```

```
Hey guys,

I'm working with function keys for the first time and I have a little
problem.

I wanted to make a menu where I can choose between:
- pushing on the arrow buttons to choose an option, and press enter to
confirm
- just enter a number

I was wondering if anybody has a list with all function keys (i'm using MF
Cobol, didn't manage to find one on Google), so that I can include an
if-statement that says:
IF arrow-up OR arrow-down OR ent OR esc THEN run-code-1
IF numberbutton1 OR numberbutton2 OR... THEN run-code-2

(I only know the value of the function keys "escape", "enter", "arrow-up"
and "arrow-down", so this part of my code works perfect, the problem is the
numberbuttons)

Thanks in advance,

Vincent
```

```
Chris
```

## Thread: Interest in COBOL — 28 msgs

### Interest in COBOL

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-16T13:00:35-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<atl7sk$mgs$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
The following note was sent out recently by the Chair of J4 COBOL Committee,

"In Japan, the COBOL Consortium - made up of Micro Focus, NEC, Fujitsu,
Hitachi and IBM amongst others - held a COBOL IT Seminar on the subject of
"COBOL & JAVA". The seminar attracted 410 participants ...

And in the U.S., I'm hoping we will have 50 participants in our futures
workshop.  What am I doing wrong?

Don
Don Schricker
Micro Focus
Product Manager, Language Standards
Chairman J4, the COBOL technical committee of INCITS
   Don.Schricker <at> microfocus.com
 http://www.cobolstandards.com
 http://www.microfocus.com "

I thought this might be relevant to several recent threads in both
comp.lang.cobol and IBM-MAIN.

Those with "constructive" responses MIGHT want to contact Don and give him
your ideas.

(I will hold off on my "possibly too negative" responses until I see what
others say.)
```

#### ↳ Re: Interest in COBOL

- **From:** "Ron" <NoSoy@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2002-12-16T15:29:14-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<atlgjb$5k0$1@ngspool-d02.news.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<atl7sk$mgs$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I would love to go to a seminar like this but:

a) I can't afford the time or money to go on my own.

b) It is not a good time economically for companies to
   send their people on long, expensive, far-away
   seminars.

c) It is not a good time economically for companies to
   experiment with new technology. They are laying
   off people and those that are left are just keeping
   the remaining stuff going.

d) COBOL and Java is good thing but it will take time to
   catch on in this economic environment. Regular
   on-line seminars and downloadable documents which
   focus on the technology and not sales would help.

My 2 cents.
```

```
In WebSphere, for example, the real cost includes CICS, DB/2, and MQ Series software, all of which
can run without the Java server just fine.
-Paul
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Interest in COBOL

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Alister Munro" <alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2002-12-20T02:00:26

```
I expect a compiler to produce full machine code for the target chip /
op-sys. I feel a little cheated when corners are cut to produce cross
platform support by implementing an additional cost per sale of my
code known as a run-time. When I buy a specific chipset / op-sys
compiler I expect to get that. When I buy a non specific chipset / op-
sys(e.g.. JAVA) interpreter I expect to pay for run-times.

Eye's are getting sleepy, hope that made some sence.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Runtimes with compiled code was Re: Interest in COBOL

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-12-20T10:10:05-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E0324BD.71EABEF5@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<atl7sk$mgs$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <H78H5p.Az7@news.boeing.com> <atlnhr$ru7$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk> <mYcM9.272202$GR5.92038@rwcrnsc51.ops.asp.att.net> <atttdr$jaq$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk> <JCwM9.278814$GR5.94163@rwcrnsc51.ops.asp.att.net>`

```
The IBM mainframe compiler depends on the Language Environment run-time
for common subroutines and services.  It depends on the operating system
for indexed file support (note that this difference between mainframe
from traditional vendors and UNIX/PC).  Thus requiring a run-time avoids
having to generate code for common services and reduces disk space
requirements.

Gazaloo wrote:
> 
> "Alister Munro" wrote:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Gazaloo.
```

```
It sounds like an interesting project, Alister.

But I am puzzled as to why you would do this?

Do you believe that C is more transportable across platforms than COBOL?

If you like Borland as you say, then why not "COBOL to Delphi"?

I guess my question comes down to this:

Rather than the inefficiencies inherent in writing in one procedural
language, then translating it to another and compiling it, why not just
learn the other language and write in that?

Genuinely curious,

Pete.
```

```
OOPS! I was thinking along the lines of chip familly rather than
set. There again I have coded for chipsets in the past to cater
for variable text screens of upto 132x66 for viewing on screen
reports but that was around eight years ago.

Competion is constanly growing and so the final cost to the client
often becoms the deciding factor for the client. Runtime charges
dont do so well here.

One client (a univercity) has over 25000 PC's. How many run-
times would I need to purchase to allow any one of the 50 or
so employees that need to use the software on anyone of these
PC's ?

You seem to like Java others VB etc. So maybe it is time to
move on. I have been working on my own Cobol > C translator.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Interest in COBOL

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2002-12-20T15:41:28-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0212201541.312f1894@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<atl7sk$mgs$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <H78H5p.Az7@news.boeing.com> <atlnhr$ru7$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk> <mYcM9.272202$GR5.92038@rwcrnsc51.ops.asp.att.net> <atttdr$jaq$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk> <1nEM9.2446$qU5.1575523@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <atvrem$uj9$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
There are *AT LEAST FOUR* very capable COBOL compilers for sale that
have no (Generally) runtime fee requirements.

1 - Fujitsu (Fee for some supporting ad ons, and in Japan, but the
WINDOWS native compiler has no runtime fees generally).  Very good
optimization for Intel chipsets.

2 - CA-Realia COBOL

3 - IBM Visual Age for COBOL (Interesting they want you to rename the
IBM named DLL's to something else before distribution, but there are
no fees)

4 - PerCOBOL from LegacyJ - A very modern compiler supporting many new
features in the 2002 standard which generates Java then compiles it
and runs anywhere there is a JVM.

There may very well be others --- especially if you look at KOBOL or
Objective COBOL from Brazil.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Interest in COBOL

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-17T11:21:21-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<atntfi$ii5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<atl7sk$mgs$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <H78H5p.Az7@news.boeing.com>`

```
It's my understanding that the *workshop* (not a seminar) that Don mentions
is being seriously considered as part of a much larger COBOL-related event.
Don Schricker's private message to the J4 committee (as a member and as
chair of that committee, not as an employee of Micro Focus) was published in
this forum without any indication of prior permission from the author (a
surprising act, in my opinion, as I have been told it is Extremely Rude to
post a private mail message, or a message received from a limited mailing
list, in a public forum without express permission and without any
indication that that express permission had been granted!).

Moreover, if the plans for this workshop do gel, it is intended as a forum
in which the COBOL user community has an opportunity to discuss their wishes
for new features in the next COBOL standard with members of J4 and WG4, face
to face.  I for one think that's a Really Good Idea.

COBOL Expo 2003 is tentatively scheduled for June 23-25, 2003 in Las Vegas
Nevada, and Don has been in communication with the organizers of that event
on the possiblity of the workshop in question.

Moreover, Don has offered, on behalf of Micro Focus, to host the next WG4
meeting in Las Vegas, Nevada, on June 26-28, 2003.  This date has been
chosen precisely to allow both J4 and WG4 members to attend this workshop --
if the COBOL Expo organizers agree to host it -- so that these members can
discuss the suggestions from the user community given at this workshop
directly into the WG4 meeting.  The decision as to whether Micro Focus'
offer to the United States to host this meeting at this location on these
dates is accepted is, I believe, in the hands of ANSI, and the decision as
to whether to accept that offer in the hands of ISO/IEC JTC1 SC22.

Note that there is much that is tentative about this.  It is not clear to me
that COBOL Expo 2003 has formally agreed to hold the workshop, it is not
clear that the process of asking permission to host the WG4 meeting has gone
through all the right channels, or that the offer has formally been made to
ISO/IEC JTC1 SC22, or that that offer has been accepted.

I also don't know where Don's estimate of fifty participants in the workshop
came from, but my best guess is that it comes in part from the experience of
COBOL Expo 2002 and in part from estimates from the organizers of COBOL Expo
2003.

I believe Don's planned participation in this workshop is as both chair and
member of J4, and as convener of WG4, not as an employee of Micro Focus.  I
expect to participate in this workshop by *listening* to the user community,
and I expect to do so as a member of the US delegation to WG4, not as an
employee of Unisys.  I strongly suspect the same is true for the other
members of these two committees, whether they are sponsored by corporations
that happen to produce COBOL compilers for sale or not.

Thus, it strikes me that boycotting this workshop whose purpose is to help
guide the future of Standard COBOL, on the grounds that Micro Focus' pricing
policy is displeasing, could be considered a bit short-sighted.

Don expressed his concern to J4 that the interest in this workshop in a
country that has a population of nearly 300,000,000 indicates a potential
for fifty or so participants, and interest in a workshop with a much
narrower focus in a country with a population of some 130,000,000 resulted
in over four hundred participants.  I tend to agree with Don, that this is a
surprising and frustrating estimate.

What can Don do to get the user community to participate more in determining
the future feature content of standard COBOL?   This workshop is an effort
in that direction; does boycotting it help the future of COBOL?

Most (not all, I'm sorry to say) participants in comp.lang.cobol participate
because they're interested in the future of Standard COBOL and wish to see
it improve.

I hope the readership takes this discussion as an opportunity to consider
feature content that will assist both in the functionality and the longevity
of Standard COBOL, rather than as an opportunity to bash the standards
process or the people who have done their best to make that process work.

    -Chuck Stevens

"sdb3309 boeing.com>" <"sdb3309<"@> wrote in message
news:H78H5p.Az7@news.boeing.com...
> It possible that the response to Don is the fact that he works for Micro
> Focus.  Micro Focus has a beautiful products and I would love to use
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…

Committee,

of
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Interest in COBOL

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-12-17T23:49:54
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e00fa59_6@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<atl7sk$mgs$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <H78H5p.Az7@news.boeing.com> <atntfi$ii5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
My position on this is very well known and I have no intention of re-raking
all the reasons why I think Don and his committee are a waste of time.

If I had the track record that he has with this, I'd resign immediately and
apologise to the COBOL Community.

Instead he has accepted honour for his outstanding work... Is it any wonder
this committe has no credibility?

I know there has been a lot of hard work by many people (probably including
Don Schricker) but it is results that count.

Why wouldn't I attend such a Workshop?

Well, maybe it is because it is being run by a non-profit organization, that
charges people a substantial amount to be on a committee, that takes 17
years to do 3 years work, and charges $200 a copy when something is finally
produced...

No, that isn't it.

I wouldn't attend because it is about as interesting as deciding whether a
frail old lady should have breast implants or a face lift as her best chance
on a new lease of life...and then arguing interminably about the size and
shape of the pointless, painful, surgery.

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: Interest in COBOL

- **Date:** 2002-12-18T19:28:00-06:00

- **Message-ID:** `<atr7b1$m9j$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<atl7sk$mgs$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
As a (partial) background on this "proposed" forum, the following
information is from the *draft* minutes of the last J4 meeting posted at:

 http://www.cobolportal.com/j4/files/02-0239.doc

"The next WG4 meeting is 26 - 28 June 2003 <sic> at Las Vegas, Nevada.
COBOL EXPO is also in Las Vegas 23 - 25 June 2003.  The last day of COBOL
Expo will include a workshop on the future of COBOL.  J4 and WG4 members are
encouraged to participate in that workshop."

I hope people are already looking at possibility of attending CobolExpo, see

 http://www.cobolexpo.com/homepage.html

which says,

"Heads-up Alert !
COBOL Expo 2003! is scheduled for June 23-25 in Las Vegas.
Impossible to improve over COBOL Expo 2002!? Don't believe it. Among the
additions to the focused sessions and outstanding hands-on workshops will be
a "mainframe track."
You'll find details here soon. Set aside those dates."

  ***

If you are interested in attending the WG4 meeting following the CobolExpo
date, make certain that you look into the requirements for *YOUR* national
standards body for jointing their delegation to a WG4 meeting.  These
meetings are *not* open "to the public" but are open to officially
recognized delegations from appropriate national standards bodies.
```

<a id="t-f7d586e6e1"></a>
## Thread: Avoiding the "wrong" Go To XYZ-EXIT problem — 42 this month / 45 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Avoiding the "wrong" Go To XYZ-EXIT problem

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-16T14:06:30-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<atlbo7$922$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
This is my usual "suggestion" (follow-up on another thread) on how to
"guarantee" that you never cut-and-paste or otherwise cause a "wrong" Go To
XXX-EXIT code problem.  (This assumes that you have the new "EXIT"
enhancements from the 2002 COBOL Standard - available as extensions in some
'85 Standard compilers)

1) NEVER code a "GO TO" any where in your program

2) Never code a "PERFORM ... THRU" in your program

3) Always use ONLY paragraphs or only sections (no combinations of the two)

4) Use EXIT PARAGRAPH (or EXIT SECTION - depending on what you select for
item 3) to "escape" from within a procedure.  (Although it is true that this
is an "implicit Go To" - it never uses a Label and can never lead to "fall
thru" logic")

    ***

If you follow those 4 rules (obviously easier in NEW code than in
maintenance), then you will NEVER run into a problem with incorrect
"exit/escape" logic labels - but will still be able to "escape" from within
a procedure without deeply nesting IF's or using switches.
```

```
17 years and have never, ever found a situation where I had to code a GOTO
unless the program had been filled with them already by a predecessor.  For
new programs - NEVER.  JMEO.

Walter
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding the "wrong" Go To XYZ-EXIT problem

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Danny Maijer" <info@liveartists.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-17T16:32:25+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<atngc0$j58$1@reader08.wxs.nl>`
- **References:** `<atlbo7$922$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <atle65$nv2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3dfe431f$0$176$45beb828@newscene.com> <atlkt5$bac$1@reader08.wxs.nl> <atncp9$uf$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> schreef in bericht
news:atncp9$uf$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> I take it you haven't done maintenance on a wide variety of styles created
by
> other programmers.

Ive seen it all.....From beautiful structured code to the most horrible
spaghetti, from mainframe to PC you name it. And yes, one can screw up a
COBOL program by jumping back and forth (even saw jumping to labels in other
sections). But usually a simple GO TO is way simpler then nesting performs
and screwing around with flags.

16 years ago I wrote a, pretty complex, system for processing real estate
transactions. In COBOL and with GO TO's on a NCR minicomputer (ITX).  Today
that same system is still in use (W2K) and easy to maintain (by others then
myself). We're "guinizing" it right now with Fujitsu COBOL. Even that is not
hard to do....

I've seen GO TO-less programs written using the Michael Jackson structured
development method which ended up having a life cycle of less then 1
year.....

The point i'm trying to make is that GO TO -less programming is not a
guarantee for delivery of structured and maintainable code (ever saw the
Assembly code of a GO TO-less program ? You should take a peek)....

By the way, in C I never needed the goto statement......But that is quite a
differrent world in my opinion.

Danny.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding the "wrong" Go To XYZ-EXIT problem

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-17T12:00:50-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<atnooj$sc9$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<atlbo7$922$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <atle65$nv2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3dfe431f$0$176$45beb828@newscene.com> <atlkt5$bac$1@reader08.wxs.nl> <dcedb8d0.0212170740.5d215e01@posting.google.com>`

```
"Joe Zitzelberger" <jzitzelb@tsys.com> wrote in message
news:dcedb8d0.0212170740.5d215e01@posting.google.com...
> "Danny Maijer" <info@liveartists.com> wrote in message
news:<atlkt5$bac$1@reader08.wxs.nl>...
<snip>
> > One should not jump back and forward in a program but using a GO TO in
order
> > to skip some code is OK
> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> some examples?

The "traditional" example of a (semi-?)good use of a Go To is to "escape"
from within a procedure to its exit - without the need of deeply nested IF's
or switches - or more nested performs.

For example (in semi-pseudo-code)

 Mainline.
      Perform  Para1 thru Para1-exit
          Until   Something
     Stop Run.
Para1.
    Do some-logic
    If  Field-1 = X
       Do Something-Else
       If Field-2 = Y
            Go To Para1-Exit
       Else
             Do Some-other-thing
       End-If
    Else
      Do Still-Something-else
   End-If
  Do All-but-both-X-and-Y
     .
Para1-Exit.
   Exit.

****

It is POSSIBLE to get around the "GO TO" statement - but it isn't easy - and
probably (depending on what you are used to) is more error prone.

If you try and "restructure" this code to avoid the "GO TO" - you will see
how it MAY actually end-up more "complex".  (FYI, this situation is the
rationale behind the "EXIT PARAGRAPH" feature of the 2002 Standard).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding the "wrong" Go To XYZ-EXIT problem

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-12-17T18:23:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<atnq2t$8ut$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<atlbo7$922$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <atle65$nv2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3dfe431f$0$176$45beb828@newscene.com> <atlkt5$bac$1@reader08.wxs.nl> <dcedb8d0.0212170740.5d215e01@posting.google.com> <atnooj$sc9$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

On 17-Dec-2002, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:

> The "traditional" example of a (semi-?)good use of a Go To is to "escape"
> from within a procedure to its exit - without the need of deeply nested IF's
> or switches - or more nested performs.
>
...
> It is POSSIBLE to get around the "GO TO" statement - but it isn't easy - and
> probably (depending on what you are used to) is more error prone.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> rationale behind the "EXIT PARAGRAPH" feature of the 2002 Standard).

In new code, this doesn't need to be complex - but doing maintenance,
restructuring code is something we try to avoid.  The more we touch, the more
likely we are creating bugs.   So GO TO EXIT stays around until EXIT PARAGRAPH
is available.  (and even longer if we have multiple paragraph PERFORM THRU logic
we have to put up with).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding the "wrong" Go To XYZ-EXIT problem

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Walter Willis" <wwillis1@qwest.net>
- **Date:** 2002-12-17T14:32:02-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3dff8934$0$250$45beb828@newscene.com>`
- **References:** `<atlbo7$922$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <atle65$nv2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3dfe431f$0$176$45beb828@newscene.com> <atlkt5$bac$1@reader08.wxs.nl> <dcedb8d0.0212170740.5d215e01@posting.google.com> <atnooj$sc9$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
A lot also depends on coding standards in your shop.  For example, our shop
standard is to use a period at the end of a paragraph only.  It is not used
otherwise.  In an if construct if you need to exit the paragraph you state
NEXT SENTENCE which finds the next period and continues from there.
Otherwise you use CONTINUE which simply drops you to the end-if you are
currently in and continues from there.  You thus avoid having to write GOTO
some-exit-point.

Thus:
Para1.
     Do some-logic
     If  Field-1 = X
        Do Something-Else
        If Field-2 = Y
             Next Sentence
        Else
              Do Some-other-thing
        End-If
     Else
       Do Still-Something-else
    End-If
   Do All-but-both-X-and-Y
    .

Since the only period is at the end of the paragraph, the next sentence will
cause the paragraph to be exited.

Walter

> The "traditional" example of a (semi-?)good use of a Go To is to "escape"
> from within a procedure to its exit - without the need of deeply nested
IF's
> or switches - or more nested performs.
>
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> It is POSSIBLE to get around the "GO TO" statement - but it isn't easy -
and
> probably (depending on what you are used to) is more error prone.
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding the "wrong" Go To XYZ-EXIT problem

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-17T14:49:53-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ato2li$ns4$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<atlbo7$922$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <atle65$nv2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3dfe431f$0$176$45beb828@newscene.com> <atlkt5$bac$1@reader08.wxs.nl> <dcedb8d0.0212170740.5d215e01@posting.google.com> <atnooj$sc9$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3dff8934$0$250$45beb828@newscene.com>`

```
Which compiler are you using?  The ANSI/ISO Standard (both '85 and 2002)
PROHIBITS the use of NEXT SENTENCE in the same IF that has an END-IF with
it.

There are a NUMBER of compilers that allow this (as an extension), but it is
NOT portable to a "strictly" ANSI/ISO conforming compiler.

FYI,
  the "NEXT SENTENCE" construct has been labeled "archaic" (not yet
OBSOLETE) in the 2002 COBOL Standard.
```

- **References:** `<atlbo7$922$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <atle65$nv2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3dfe431f$0$176$45beb828@newscene.com> <atlkt5$bac$1@reader08.wxs.nl> <dcedb8d0.0212170740.5d215e01@posting.google.com> <atnooj$sc9$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3dff8934$0$250$45beb828@newscene.com> <ato2li$ns4$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <ato59l$fp7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3E01AA38.5080900@optonline.NOSPAM.net>`

```
On 19-Dec-2002, Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net> wrote:

> >>Which compiler are you using?  The ANSI/ISO Standard (both '85 and 2002)
> >>PROHIBITS the use of NEXT SENTENCE in the same IF that has an END-IF with
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Time to update the pre-compiler.

Tell that to CA.

But that's not an easy thing to do with a large existing code base.   The
pre-compiler can't simply start writing CONTINUE instead of NEXT SENTENCE.   We
have just seen (in this newsgroup) an example of a shop using NEXT SENTENCE as a
replacement for EXIT PARAGRAPH.  Upgrading a pre-compiler to change the CoBOL it
produces WILL cause some existing programs to break.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<atlbo7$922$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <atle65$nv2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3dfe431f$0$176$45beb828@newscene.com> <atlkt5$bac$1@reader08.wxs.nl> <dcedb8d0.0212170740.5d215e01@posting.google.com> <atnooj$sc9$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3dff8934$0$250$45beb828@newscene.com> <ato2li$ns4$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <ato59l$fp7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3E01AA38.5080900@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <atslj2$2ce$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3E02B061.7050201@optonline.NOSPAM.net>`

```
On 19-Dec-2002, Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net> wrote:

> > Tell that to CA.
>
> Waste of breath.

Agreed.

> > But that's not an easy thing to do with a large existing code base.   The
> > pre-compiler can't simply start writing CONTINUE instead of NEXT SENTENCE.
>
> I didn't claim it would be an easy update, Howard:)

It is difficult enough that there would be lots of angry customers, and few
satisfied customers.

> Doesn't seem as
> though the original creators of the pre-compiler gave much thought to
> their brute force implementation.

They didn't anticipate which changes there would be in the CoBOL language.   I
think they thought it through.  If they use a pre-compiler we could see what
gets done, and they don't need a new version every time IBM comes up with a new
compiler.

Oh, another thing that doesn't work right is that the pre-compiler doesn't
recognize "D" in column 7.  Now that could be fixed and wouldn't make customers
angry.  But the demand is pretty low for this change.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding the "wrong" Go To XYZ-EXIT problem

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Tom McFarland" <nobodyshome@home.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-20T19:48:22-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6-idncLS_dIdJJ6jXTWcqA@comcast.com>`
- **References:** `<atlbo7$922$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <atle65$nv2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3dfe431f$0$176$45beb828@newscene.com> <atlkt5$bac$1@reader08.wxs.nl> <dcedb8d0.0212170740.5d215e01@posting.google.com> <atnooj$sc9$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3dff8934$0$250$45beb828@newscene.com> <ato2li$ns4$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <ato59l$fp7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3E01AA38.5080900@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <atslj2$2ce$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3E02B061.7050201@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <atvc1b$41k$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard,

CA would ignore you because you're incorrect.  I've corrected you here
before when you've claimed that the IDMS pre-compiler generates NEXT
SENTENCE statements.  It does not do any such thing.  You've even
acknowledged that the NEXT SENTENCE statements were coming from home-grown
code that the IDMS pre-compiler was merely copying from the data dictionary.
I could probably dig out that post if I had the time.

Tom McFarland
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding the "wrong" Go To XYZ-EXIT problem

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-12-23T15:01:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<au78g6$jg2$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<atlbo7$922$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <atle65$nv2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3dfe431f$0$176$45beb828@newscene.com> <atlkt5$bac$1@reader08.wxs.nl> <dcedb8d0.0212170740.5d215e01@posting.google.com> <atnooj$sc9$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3dff8934$0$250$45beb828@newscene.com> <ato2li$ns4$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <ato59l$fp7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3E01AA38.5080900@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <atslj2$2ce$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3E02B061.7050201@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <atvc1b$41k$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <6-idncLS_dIdJJ6jXTWcqA@comcast.com>`

```

On 20-Dec-2002, "Tom McFarland" <nobodyshome@home.com> wrote:

> CA would ignore you because you're incorrect.  I've corrected you here
> before when you've claimed that the IDMS pre-compiler generates NEXT
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> I could probably dig out that post if I had the time.

Sorry.   But it does not match up END-IFs, and because it expects our code to be
included within the ELSE, it wouldn't be easy to match END-IFs.  Because of
this, the safest thing to do is have full stop periods.  That way I KNOW the the
IF has been terminated.

I really don't see the downside of full stop periods, and in my case I see a
distinct downside of a standard that eliminated these except where syntax
dictates.
```

```
> As Y2K showed so abundantly, pretty much no one did.
>
> What is this pre-compiler for?

Accessing IDMS database.   But any pre-compiler that inserts its own CoBOL code
is subject to risk that is best eliminated with full stop periods.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding the "wrong" Go To XYZ-EXIT problem

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Tom McFarland" <nobodyshome@home.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-24T01:19:48-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MeCcnaBhGaI7ZpqjXTWcqw@comcast.com>`
- **References:** `<atlbo7$922$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <atle65$nv2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3dfe431f$0$176$45beb828@newscene.com> <atlkt5$bac$1@reader08.wxs.nl> <dcedb8d0.0212170740.5d215e01@posting.google.com> <atnooj$sc9$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3dff8934$0$250$45beb828@newscene.com> <ato2li$ns4$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <ato59l$fp7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3E01AA38.5080900@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <atslj2$2ce$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3E02B061.7050201@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <atvc1b$41k$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3E05F68D.3090402@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <au78be$j9p$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:au78be$j9p$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On 22-Dec-2002, Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net> wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Accessing IDMS database.   But any pre-compiler that inserts its own CoBOL
code
> is subject to risk that is best eliminated with full stop periods.

Sorry, Howard.  You obviously don't understand what the IDMS pre-compiler,
and the AUTOSTATUS protocol specifically, does.  It's not brain surgery.  If
you, the programmer, know what you're doing, the use, or non-use, of full
stop periods is not dependent on what the pre-compiler does.

If you disagree, please provide specific examples that relate to the
original issue in this thread.

Tom McFarland
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding the "wrong" Go To XYZ-EXIT problem

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-12-27T15:38:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<auhs61$r8f$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<atlbo7$922$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <atle65$nv2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3dfe431f$0$176$45beb828@newscene.com> <atlkt5$bac$1@reader08.wxs.nl> <dcedb8d0.0212170740.5d215e01@posting.google.com> <atnooj$sc9$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3dff8934$0$250$45beb828@newscene.com> <ato2li$ns4$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <ato59l$fp7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3E01AA38.5080900@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <atslj2$2ce$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3E02B061.7050201@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <atvc1b$41k$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3E05F68D.3090402@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <au78be$j9p$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <MeCcnaBhGaI7ZpqjXTWcqw@comcast.com>`

```

On 23-Dec-2002, "Tom McFarland" <nobodyshome@home.com> wrote:

> Sorry, Howard.  You obviously don't understand what the IDMS pre-compiler,
> and the AUTOSTATUS protocol specifically, does.  It's not brain surgery.  If
> you, the programmer, know what you're doing, the use, or non-use, of full
> stop periods is not dependent on what the pre-compiler does.

I don't want to depend upon everybody doing maintenance knowing what the
pre-compiler does.   A standard that disallows periods before the end of the
paragraph requires that anybody reading the source code knows what really gets
inserted where that IDMS statement is.    But a standard that allows or even
encourages periods puts a lesser burden upon the new programmers who can see for
sure that that implied IF statement is finished.

We can say with any style "if you know what you're doing it is clear".   But
much of our style choices are to make it easy for other programmers to know what
you're doing.    With a pre-compiler, failure to put in full stop periods does
obfuscate.   I have seen no corresponding advantage to leaving periods out.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding the "wrong" Go To XYZ-EXIT problem

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-12-28T11:39:33
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e0d8aa4_6@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<atlbo7$922$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <atle65$nv2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3dfe431f$0$176$45beb828@newscene.com> <atlkt5$bac$1@reader08.wxs.nl> <dcedb8d0.0212170740.5d215e01@posting.google.com> <atnooj$sc9$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3dff8934$0$250$45beb828@newscene.com> <ato2li$ns4$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <ato59l$fp7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3E01AA38.5080900@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <atslj2$2ce$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3E02B061.7050201@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <atvc1b$41k$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3E05F68D.3090402@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <au78be$j9p$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <MeCcnaBhGaI7ZpqjXTWcqw@comcast.com> <auhs61$r8f$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard,

I've watched this thread with mild bewilderment <G>.

Personally, I don't like using periods in COBOL  procedures and haven't done
so since scope delimiters were introduced (apart from where actually
required, and then I favour having them on a separate line...).

However, I see you feel very strongly about this and I have enough respect
for your opinion to sit down and consider what you are saying.

I haven't seen any sample code as to how you would invoke the IDMS
pre-compiler, but it is usual in COBOL to flag statements for
pre-compilation with an EXEC statement. Is this NOT the case for IDMS?

If it is, I would've thought the use of END-EXEC would make it very clear.
(I remember using IDMS with ICL COBOL 20 years ago, but I can't remember how
we invoked it. (I was much more interested (fascinated!) by the concepts of
the Database Management and use of Owner/Member relationships <G>)).

If it isn't, and the pre-compiler simply inserts source code into your
program, then it seems to me that the onus is on the pre-compiler to ensure
that it maintains the correct control flow. The extension of this would be
that the pre-compiler should place scope terminators in the right place.

In other words, I believe if the pre-compiler does its job properly, there
should be no need for the programmer to insert a period OR a scope
delimiter...

Am I missing something here?

Pete.

news:auhs61$r8f$1@peabody.colorado.edu...

full

gets

even

see for
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding the "wrong" Go To XYZ-EXIT problem

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** "Tom McFarland" <nobodyshome@home.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-29T21:12:28-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kZudnSmansc8N5KjXTWcoA@comcast.com>`
- **References:** `<atlbo7$922$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <atle65$nv2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3dfe431f$0$176$45beb828@newscene.com> <atlkt5$bac$1@reader08.wxs.nl> <dcedb8d0.0212170740.5d215e01@posting.google.com> <atnooj$sc9$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3dff8934$0$250$45beb828@newscene.com> <ato2li$ns4$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <ato59l$fp7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3E01AA38.5080900@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <atslj2$2ce$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3E02B061.7050201@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <atvc1b$41k$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3E05F68D.3090402@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <au78be$j9p$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <MeCcnaBhGaI7ZpqjXTWcqw@comcast.com> <auhs61$r8f$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e0d8aa4_6@Usenet.com>`

```
Peter,

The IDMS coding you wrote 20 years ago would not look much different today.
IDMS calls do not take the form of EXEC/END-EXEC, but use their own set of
verbs that the pre-compiler recognizes, and converts to CALLs to IDMS.  It's
also possible to store code in the IDMS data dictionary that can be copied
anywhere in your program by use of 'COPY IDMS entity-name' statements.

I think that the situations that Howard is referring to come about due to
the use of a protocol option called AUTOSTATUS which will generate code
after every IDMS DML statement in order to insure that the return code from
the DML statement is checked.  The default code that is always generated is
simply a PERFORM of a section or paragraph named IDMS-STATUS.  The source
code for IDMS-STATUS is stored in the data dictionary and is included in the
source code via a COPY IDMS statement.  The code in IDMS-STATUS is protocol
dependent, i.e. different code is used for batch vs on-line programs.  It
can also be shop customized.  But it's primary function is to abort the
program on any non-zero return code from the IDMS call.

Obviously, there are some error status conditions that are expected and
acceptable, like 'not on file' or 'end of set', so its possible to allow
processing to continue in those circumstances by coding an IDMS 'ON'
statement.  The most common acceptable status codes have level 88 condition
names associated with them in the IDMS control block which must be copied
into every IDMS program.  It's those level 88 names that must be used in the
IDMS 'ON' statement.

With the AUTOSTATUS option, if you code:
MOVE '12345' to CUSTOMER-NUMBER
OBTAIN CALC CUSTOMER-RECORD

The precompiler will generate, from the OBTAIN statement:
* OBTAIN CALC CUSTOMER-RECORD   ---comment your DML
    CALL 'IDMS' USING etc         ---generated (also some MOVE statements
preceding the CALL)
    PERFORM IDMS-STATUS     ---generated

If you want to allow for the 'record not found' condition, you would code:
MOVE '12345' to CUSTOMER-NUMBER
OBTAIN CALC CUSTOMER-RECORD
ON DB-REC-NOT-FOUND
     your code to handle the condition
END-IF

and you will get:
* OBTAIN CALC CUSTOMER-RECORD    ---commented
* ON DB-REC-NOT-FOUND                     ---commented
   CALL 'IDMS' USING etc                ----generated
    IF NOT DB-REC-NOT-FOUND   ----generated
          PERFORM IDMS-STATUS     ---generated
    ELSE                                               ---generated
         your code to handle the condition    ---your code
    END-IF                                              ---your code

If you were to replace the END-IF in your code with a period, then the
resultant IF-ELSE will end with
a period.

The pre-compiler does not generate any scope terminators; they are up to the
programmer.  I would guess the reason that no scope terminator is generated
is because this IDMS feature pre-dates them.  (And how would it decide
whether to use a scope terminator or a period).  I also wouldn't want to see
the pre-compiler generate a period for me, as that would preclude the use of
a DML statement in a nested structure.  Note that there are no restrictions
on what you can code in your own error handling process.  Additional IF
statements can be nested, and would, of course, require their own scope
terminators.  But that's all under the control of the programmer.  It's only
necessary to code one scope terminator (or period if that's your preference)
to match with the 'ON' statement.  The need for any others is created by the
programmer.

Although my preference in (not) using periods is the same as yours, others
obviously feel differently.  But claiming that the IDMS compiler requires
their use because it generates NEXT SENTENCE statements is simply incorrect.
When I challenged that claim last year, Howard's response was:

> > I just checked - all of my supplied NEXT SENTENCE are in IDMS COPY
> > statements.  These could easily be fixed.

As I stated at that time, I've worked in IDMS shops for more than 20 years,
and have not found it necessary to code a period since scope terminators
became available, other than to end a paragraph.

Tom McFarland
```

```
Tom,

Thank you very much. I appreciate your taking the trouble to post this and I
found it very enlightening.

I'm sure IDMS is at the mercy of its ancient past, but what you described
seems very reasonable.

For me, it will still be a scope delimiter <G> but I respect everybody
else's right to do whatever they are comfortable with.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding the "wrong" Go To XYZ-EXIT problem

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-12-30T14:58:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aupmuo$jsm$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<atlbo7$922$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <atle65$nv2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3dfe431f$0$176$45beb828@newscene.com> <atlkt5$bac$1@reader08.wxs.nl> <dcedb8d0.0212170740.5d215e01@posting.google.com> <atnooj$sc9$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3dff8934$0$250$45beb828@newscene.com> <ato2li$ns4$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <ato59l$fp7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3E01AA38.5080900@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <atslj2$2ce$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3E02B061.7050201@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <atvc1b$41k$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3E05F68D.3090402@optonline.NOSPAM.net> <au78be$j9p$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <MeCcnaBhGaI7ZpqjXTWcqw@comcast.com> <auhs61$r8f$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e0d8aa4_6@Usenet.com>`

```

On 28-Dec-2002, "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

> I haven't seen any sample code as to how you would invoke the IDMS
> pre-compiler, but it is usual in COBOL to flag statements for
> pre-compilation with an EXEC statement. Is this NOT the case for IDMS?

Nope.   In IDMS, we have source code that looks like this  (I just pulled out a
random piece of code that I didn't write):

      MOVE CC-INST            TO FIN-INSTIT-KEY.
      OBTAIN CALC IASRCIN
         ON ANY-ERROR-STATUS
             MOVE BRS08       TO ERROR-MSG
             STRING "COULDN'T FIND INST=", CC-INST
               " ON DATABASE ERROR ",  ERROR-STATUS
                 DELIMITED BY SIZE
                              INTO ERROR-BODY
             GO TO ABORT-ROUTINE.

The pre-compiler replaces it with:
     MOVE CC-INST            TO FIN-INSTIT-KEY.
*    OBTAIN CALC IASRCIN
*       ON ANY-ERROR-STATUS
           MOVE 30 TO DML-SEQUENCE
           CALL 'IDMS' USING SUBSCHEMA-CTRL
                   IDBMSCOM (32)
                   SR1402
                   IDBMSCOM (43)
           IF NOT ANY-ERROR-STATUS PERFORM IDMS-STATUS;
           ELSE
            MOVE BRS08       TO ERROR-MSG
            STRING "COULDN'T FIND INST=", CC-INST
              " ON DATABASE ERROR ",  ERROR-STATUS
                DELIMITED BY SIZE
                             INTO ERROR-BODY
            GO TO ABORT-ROUTINE.


> If it isn't, and the pre-compiler simply inserts source code into your
> program, then it seems to me that the onus is on the pre-compiler to ensure
> that it maintains the correct control flow. The extension of this would be
> that the pre-compiler should place scope terminators in the right place.

Maybe so.  This wouldn't be an easy task, and I don't think CA sees that it
would be financially rewarded by doing this.

> In other words, I believe if the pre-compiler does its job properly, there
> should be no need for the programmer to insert a period OR a scope
> delimiter...
>
> Am I missing something here?

We have code where the scope delimiters aren't obvious to all programmers. 
Computer Associates has not provided your suggested solution.  One solutions is
to make sure all programmers are very knowledgeable about the code that they
can't see in the source.   Another solution is to provide full stop periods.  
This seems to be the easiest and most reliable solution, and I don't see any
downside to it.

The desire to get rid of periods seems to be a cosmetic.  Some people think it
looks better.   But I have not seen any evidence that it creates more
maintainable code.    A standard of requiring indentation and end delimiters is
very valuable - but when you have this standard, the only thing that eliminating
periods does is eliminate a few warnings that the compiler catches with
miss-matched end-if's.

I want to see that a change in standards produces better and more easily
maintained code.  I haven't seen that eliminating periods does that.  In fact in
my environment, the opposite is true.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding the "wrong" Go To XYZ-EXIT problem

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Walter Willis" <wwillis1@qwest.net>
- **Date:** 2002-12-17T16:54:12-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3dffaa89$0$166$45beb828@newscene.com>`
- **References:** `<atlbo7$922$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <atle65$nv2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3dfe431f$0$176$45beb828@newscene.com> <atlkt5$bac$1@reader08.wxs.nl> <dcedb8d0.0212170740.5d215e01@posting.google.com> <atnooj$sc9$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3dff8934$0$250$45beb828@newscene.com> <ato576$fp3$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
> Which can work as long as you don't purchase code that follows a different
> standard.   Counting on a standard to make your code work correctly has
its
> dangers.

I am aware of that, standards may make the program look pretty, but pretty
code does not alway work right.  You still have to code correctly.  But
standards can certainly help create more maintanable programs, and it lends
itself to the concept of ego-less programming.  It lends itself to a
commonality across the system, and reduces the plethora of program
differences from one person to the next.  There will always remain an
element of individuality amongst the programs, but that incidence is
reduced.

I worked in a shop that had the following standards:

1) End the paragraph with a period - not each sentence
2) Code something on each side of an if-else
3) Do not use continue - use next sentence in if statements

When I attempted to explain how this combination was rather difficult to
work with and why, their response was 'So what, that's the standard and we
want no input'.  Oh, and as it turns out, not a single one of them was a
COBOL programmer.  And the standards were 'strictly enforced'.  Since I
never wrote code in the shop it never became much of a problem (I left after
three months for other reasons - they had me going through several hundred
programs doing 'change all x y' to rename their DB2 table names, and I had 9
years of experience at that time).

I also have not worked in a shop that purchased a COBOL system, the system
has been developed in-house.  We currently have some fairly rigerous
standards, and they have been fairly well adhered to and have worked so far.
Our system has been running for 3 years now creating utility bills for 1.5
million customers per month, maintaining customer information, and data
related to electric/gas metered usage.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding the "wrong" Go To XYZ-EXIT problem

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-12-17T18:41:43-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0212171841.71e747a3@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<atlbo7$922$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <atle65$nv2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3dfe431f$0$176$45beb828@newscene.com> <atlkt5$bac$1@reader08.wxs.nl> <dcedb8d0.0212170740.5d215e01@posting.google.com> <atnooj$sc9$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3dff8934$0$250$45beb828@newscene.com>`

```
"Walter Willis" <wwillis1@qwest.net> wrote

> A lot also depends on coding standards in your shop.  For example, our shop
> standard is to use a period at the end of a paragraph only.  It is not used
> otherwise.  In an if construct if you need to exit the paragraph you state
> NEXT SENTENCE which finds the next period and continues from there.

NEXT SENTENCE is not allowed in any IF that contains an END-IF.

> Otherwise you use CONTINUE which simply drops you to the end-if you are
> currently in and continues from there.  You thus avoid having to write GOTO
> some-exit-point.

A misused NEXT SENTENCE (as you have done) is _worse_ than a GO TO in
that it is a GO TO to an anonymous point: the next full stop.

It also causes the worst type of bugs where changing code in one place
affects what happens at a completely different place. If one added a
full stop in the latter part of a paragraph then it will change what
happens in the earlier part - ie the destination of your anonymous GO
TO.

> Since the only period is at the end of the paragraph, the next sentence will
> cause the paragraph to be exited.

Until some programmer forgets and adds a full stop somewhere.
```

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> "Joe Zitzelberger" <jzitzelb@tsys.com> wrote in message
> news:dcedb8d0.0212170740.5d215e01@posting.google.com...

(snip)

>>>Heh...fire up the discussion ;)
>>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> or switches - or more nested performs.

If you're checking a switch in multiple places, especially at the bottom 
of your loop, you're not coding it right. A switch should be tested at 
an upper level to drive lower lever processing, e.g., PERFORM UNTIL, 
etc. I can't remember writing any programs in which I maintained the 
indexing variable (using indexing generically). It's sometimes necessary 
to calculate/maintain a secondary index to reflect records selected or 
the current offset into a data structure such as a map. It's just a 
different mindset.
```

- **References:** `<atlbo7$922$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <atle65$nv2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3dfe431f$0$176$45beb828@newscene.com> <atln2e$dsb$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
"William Klein" wrote...

>The "traditional" example of a (semi-?)good use of a Go To is to "escape"
>from within a procedure to its exit - without the need of deeply nested IF's
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
>rationale behind the "EXIT PARAGRAPH" feature of the 2002 Standard).


Ok, I'll try:

Mainline.
     Perform Para1
       Until Something
     Stop Run.

Para1.
    Do some-logic
    If  Field-1 = X
       Do Something-Else
       If Field-2 Not = Y
          Do Some-other-thing
          Do All-but-both-X-and-Y
       End-If
    Else
      Do Still-Something-else
      Do All-but-both-X-and-Y
   End-If
   .
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding the "wrong" Go To XYZ-EXIT problem

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-17T13:34:37-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<atnu8e$3gb$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<atlbo7$922$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <atle65$nv2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3dfe431f$0$176$45beb828@newscene.com> <atln2e$dsb$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <dcedb8d0.0212171039.6a217fd8@posting.google.com>`

```
I am NOT saying that you can't do it your way (or several other ways),
however, the reasons that "your re-write" *MIGHT* be considered potentially
error-prone for maintenance are:

1) The test for
    "If Field-2 Not = Y"
is a negative test (instead of the original positive test).   If another
"good value" besides Y is added later, this can get more complicated.

2) Your code has two occurrences of
   "Do All-but-both-X-and-Y"
If this is actually a series of statements - rather than an out-of-line
PERFORM, then it becomes QUITE easy to add some bit of logic (in maintenance
mode) in one place and forget about it in another.

  ***

Again, I personally have NEVER liked the "GO TO xxx-EXIT" escape method, but
it is the one semi-common justification for GO TO.  Furthermore, there are
SOME (certainly not all) definitions of "structured" coding which allow for
a "downward go to" to a single exit-point for a procedure.  (This NEVER
leads to "spaghetti code" as GO TO's can never "cross" and logic cannot
"fall thru".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding the "wrong" Go To XYZ-EXIT problem

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-12-17T14:35:50-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0212171435.557d3b9a@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<atlbo7$922$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <atle65$nv2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3dfe431f$0$176$45beb828@newscene.com> <atln2e$dsb$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <dcedb8d0.0212171039.6a217fd8@posting.google.com> <atnu8e$3gb$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote

> 1) The test for
>     "If Field-2 Not = Y"
> is a negative test (instead of the original positive test).   If another
> "good value" besides Y is added later, this can get more complicated.

Are you frightened of negative tests ?

       IF ( Field-2 NOT = "Y" AND NOT = "y" AND NOT = "X" )

or
       IF NOT ( Field-2 = "Y" OR "y" OR "X" )

or
       IF ( Field-2 = "Y" OR "y" OR "X" )
           CONTINUE
       ELSE       

Personally I prefer the 2nd.  The rule is that if you take NOT out of
a compare group you must change the ANDs to ORs or v.v.  Once this is
remembered then all fear of NOT should disappear.
> 2) Your code has two occurrences of
>   "Do All-but-both-X-and-Y"
> If this is actually a series of statements - rather than an out-of-line
> PERFORM, then it becomes QUITE easy to add some bit of logic (in maintenance
> mode) in one place and forget about it in another.

One of the differences between 'heavyweight' and 'lightweight' styles
is the perceive cost of creating a new procedure.  In a 'heavyweight'
style the code must be enclosed in section header, exit paragraph,
given a number and put in the source in some place suitable for its
level.  Thus 'heavyweight' programmers tend to go to great lengths to
avoid this (such as using GO TO for loops, or fiddling nested loops).

'Lightweight' style just breaks a paragraph in two where it is and
sticks in a paragraph label and a PERFORM.  This means that they don't
need duplicated code (just two performs) and don't need to convolute
code to avoid this.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding the "wrong" Go To XYZ-EXIT problem

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2002-12-18T12:18:49-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dcedb8d0.0212181218.160b5f90@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<atlbo7$922$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <atle65$nv2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3dfe431f$0$176$45beb828@newscene.com> <atln2e$dsb$1@newsg1.svr.pol.co.uk> <dcedb8d0.0212171039.6a217fd8@posting.google.com> <atnu8e$3gb$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message news:<atnu8e$3gb$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>...
> I am NOT saying that you can't do it your way (or several other ways),
> however, the reasons that "your re-write" *MIGHT* be considered potentially
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> "good value" besides Y is added later, this can get more complicated.

My local shop standard actually once said that we should do:

   IF a = b
      continue
   ELSE
      do stuff
   END-IF

When I look at this it always strikes me as strange.  If someone can
understand that A=B then they must understand is compliment A<>B.

This is one of the places where '<>' is a bit nicer than 'NOT ='. 
People can indeed write some extremely complicated negative tests, but
'=' or 'not =' don't really qualify as too confusing.

If it were something like:

   IF NOT (A = B) 

I would agree the complication factor goes up slightly, but still,
fewer lines means less reading and more meaningful lines per screen. 
I'm a big fan of the reduced noise of:

   IF A NOT = B
     do stuff
   END-IF

In my case there was a happy ending -- my boss, a long time assembler
programmer, looked at that standard and revised it.
```

<a id="t-17deafab8f"></a>
## Thread: zip individual pds members winthin a cobol program — 1 msgs

### zip individual pds members winthin a cobol program

- **From:** suman.malapati@usa.xerox.com (sum)
- **Date:** 2002-12-16T13:14:27-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3de7cadb.0212161314.8b3f043@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi, 

I have a requirement to zip files on the mainframe and ftp to unix
server for some processing. On the mainframe, we have a PDS dataset
which we zip using PKZIP utility.

Is there a way to call the PKZIP utility from within the cobol program
and zip each individual PDS member before transfering the entire file
to the server. The PDS file is created by calling the IEBGENER from
within a cobol instead of outside as a utility. Can I do the same with
PKZIP.

Appreciate your time.
Thankyou.
Sum
```

```
here, what i recevied  by roger jhonson from fujtisu

Carlos lages

NetCOBOL for Windows Version 7 - Enhancement List
General
�       The look and feel of the Documentation Viewer is now more like
Web help systems with the list of documents in the left-hand pane and
the contents of the selected document displayed in the right-hand pane.
�       The style of most of the PDF manuals has been enhanced to look
better, fit more information on a page, and use an 8.5" x 11" page
format so that user printing of pages or complete manuals will be more
convenient.
�       Common graphics have been used in different parts of the product
giving a more integrated product image. For example, the autorun screen,
Project Manager start-up banner, manual cover pages, and product boxes
all use the same artwork.
COBOL
Enhancements:
�       BSAM file system has been extended to support large files (sizes
being confirmed - probably 3.4GB for indexed, 2.0GB for other file
organizations)
�       Solaris applications can be developed remotely using Project
Manager in NetCOBOL for Windows.
�       A project property setting feature has been added for setting
project attributes.
�       Projects can be viewed in a List View in addition to the
standard Tree View. The list view can show all items in the project or
just the editable items.
�       Our performance in using ODBC has been improved (may be viewed
as a bug fix by some)
�       The compiler will check calling and called program parameter
conformance for internal programs.
�       A special register SORT-CORE-SIZE has been added so that
programs can specify the amount of memory to be used by PowerBSORT in
sort or merge operations.
The following bugs have been fixed (i.e. the following will no
longer occur):
�       JMN0102I-U message is issued if you compile your programs with
compiler option PRINT, SOURCE, and TEST.
�       Compiler abends if AS modification is specified in in-line
invocation of METHOD.
�       Compiler may get into an infinite loop if incorrect conditions
are written in the "EVALUATE WHEN" condition.
�       Message JMN3482I-S is issued if a program for AIM-NDB without
the READY statement is compiled. (AIM is a Fujitsu mainframe product
that is similar to CICS)
�       Open error occurs if the top value of the target sequential file
is 0xFFFE or 0xEFBBBF.

PowerCOBOL
The following controls have been added:
�       Edit Control (a lightweight version of the TextBox control that
also supports data binding)
�       Label Control (a lightweight version of the StaticText control)
Also there are a few additions and adjustments to properties and
parameters.
PowerFORM
No enhancements.
PowerFORM Run-time
�       Print preview ability added.
�       PNG format is available as one of the supported picture types.
Collection Class Library
No enhancements.
J adaptor Class Generator
�       JDK1.4 is supported.
DB Access Class Library
No enhancements.
Data Editor
�       Data can be imported to or exported from XML data files.
�       Supports Unicode character data.
�       Replace function supports using character data as well as
hexadecimal values.
�       Layout Definition Files (*.lay) can be used with the Layout
Definition Function.
�       Handles binary data files.
�       Can set initial values when automatically generating data.
Data Converter
�       Supports conversion between XML files and the other supported
file formats.
�       Can create DTD (Document Type Definition - used with XML) files
and matching COPY libraries from the Layout Definition file.
PowerGEM Plus
No enhancements.
PowerBSORT
�       bsortex command added. The bsortex command can perform separate
processes in the one command whereas the earlier bsort command could
only perform one function per command.
�       Record skip feature added allowing the user to specify
conditions for omitting records in the sort or merge operation.
�       The event log output feature is supported on the NT kernel
OS. When serious errors occur, error information is written to the
event log.
�       Standard input and standard output can be specified to the bsort
command as input and output files.
�       Error messages that were issued to the standard output are now
being issued to standard error.
�       PowerBSORT can access files that exceed 2 GB.
�       PowerBSORT supports the BSAM access mode. So, PowerBSORT can
access NetCOBOL files larger than 1 GB using the BSAM access mode.
```

<a id="t-191a2ea489"></a>
## Thread: GNT's versus EXE's — 14 msgs

### GNT's versus EXE's

- **From:** renfrewthejust@yahoo.com (Renfrew)
- **Date:** 2002-12-17T03:28:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<atm5kq$psh$1@bob.news.rcn.net>`

```
My company is in the process of converting from old MF 16-bit over to Net 
Express.  We only used GNT files in our original system, but we are now faced 
with a choice of these versus EXE's.  Could someone be so kind as to describe 
the practical and/or strategical differences between the usage of GNT's versus 
EXE's?  The manual is not very clear about this.

I appreciate any help or even if simply pointed in the direction of a resource 
that will help me figure this stuff out for myself.  

Thanks,
DaveM

(Remove "SPAMOUT" if replying to my email address)
```

#### ↳ Re: GNT's versus EXE's

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-12-17T18:04:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3DFF671C.7C5815F7@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<atm5kq$psh$1@bob.news.rcn.net>`

```
Renfrew wrote:
> 
> My company is in the process of converting from old MF 16-bit over to Net
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> EXE's?  The manual is not very clear about this.

I've used .EXEs with MF 16-bit Cobol for many years.  .INT is
intermediate code (byte coded) and can be executed using the run-time
system on any machine (in theory) regardless of processor type or OS, as
long as a run-time is available.

.GNT is 'generated' with much of the code converted to machine code for
the specific processor, but it still requires the run-time which may be
for any OS on that processor type.

.EXE makes the program only for Intel and DOS or Windows or OS/2
(depending on link options) but it doesn't require the run-time system
(cobrun), it may need a support module such as COBLIB.DLE or similar, or
this may be statically linked in.

The _content_ of an .EXE may be byte coded (equivalent to .INT), or
generated machine code and optimised (equivalent to .GNT).

The advantage of not using the run-time system (in MF 3.x and 4.x) is
that you don't need to deploy it.  .EXEs (with some additional
restrictions) may be distributed free.  This is not available in the
latest Net Express as I understand it.

The disadvantage of not using the run-time system is that you may need
to do some simple memory management, and some esoteric facilities may
not be available.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: GNT's versus EXE's

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-12-17T13:54:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<G0GL9.1886$qU5.1102792@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<atm5kq$psh$1@bob.news.rcn.net> <3DFF671C.7C5815F7@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
"Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3DFF671C.7C5815F7@Azonic.co.nz...
> Renfrew wrote:
> >
> > My company is in the process of converting from old MF 16-bit over to
Net
> > Express.  We only used GNT files in our original system, but we are now
faced
> > with a choice of these versus EXE's.  Could someone be so kind as to
describe
> > the practical and/or strategical differences between the usage of GNT's
versus
> > EXE's?  The manual is not very clear about this.

> The advantage of not using the run-time system (in MF 3.x and 4.x) is
> that you don't need to deploy it.  .EXEs (with some additional
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> not be available.

Two other advantages of using a runtime

1. The common (library) code need not be included in each program, saving
disk space. (Which today, of course, means nowhere near what it usedto
mean).

2. If a bug is discovered in a common routine, you fix it and change the
runtime, install it and you are done. There is no need to recompile/relink
every program in the system which uses that routine. (Or for that matter,
*identify* every program in the system which uses that routine).

"Runtime" here is not limited to the runtime support module(s) provided by
the compiler vendor: it can include common I-O or validation routines
developed by the user and used in multiple programs within the system.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GNT's versus EXE's

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-12-17T11:41:24-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0212171141.380a6f4f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<atm5kq$psh$1@bob.news.rcn.net> <3DFF671C.7C5815F7@Azonic.co.nz> <G0GL9.1886$qU5.1102792@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```
"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote

> Two other advantages of using a runtime

In MicroFocus there are several 'runtime's.  

The "Run Time Environment" (cobrun) or "Application Server" will
execute .int and .gnt and provides portability.  This is what is
usually meant by 'runtime'. This provides memory management,
libraries, application level control and several other things that I
don't care about.  It is chargable per user.

The 'Static linked run time support' where the program is linked to
form one .EXE. This is 'free' in MF 2.x thru 4.1 (with certain
restrictions) and may be in older versions of NE as long as some rules
are followed.

The 'Shared run time support' where an application can be many .EXEs
(DOS) or .DLLs (OS/2 or Windows) that are CALLed and CANCELed. This is
'free' as above, but each user should check their actual licences and
documentation.

The Microsoft versions of MF Cobol did not have the "Run Time
Environment" and all programs had to be built static or shared, but
there were no runtime fees either.
 
> 1. The common (library) code need not be included in each program, saving
> disk space. (Which today, of course, means nowhere near what it usedto
> mean).

No. Using the shared support module achieves this.  In MF 2.x to 4.x
the support code can be in separate loadable files (eg COBLIB.DLE,
EXTFH.EXE, ADIS.EXE, SP2.EXE).  There is a small amount of code linked
in to each EXE/DLL but this is not significant.
 
> 2. If a bug is discovered in a common routine, you fix it and change the
> runtime, install it and you are done. There is no need to recompile/relink
> every program in the system which uses that routine. (Or for that matter,
> *identify* every program in the system which uses that routine).

No. Programs can be completely dynamically loadable from separately
CALLed and CANCELed .EXE or .DLL files exactly as if they were .int or
.gnt.

Of course you _can_ statically link everything into one large .EXE if
this is required.  Even then there is no need to recompile everything,
only those source s that have changed and then relink all the .obj.

> "Runtime" here is not limited to the runtime support module(s) provided by
> the compiler vendor: it can include common I-O or validation routines
> developed by the user and used in multiple programs within the system.

Yes.  Each separately compiled source code can become a .EXE or .DLL
that is loaded and discarded on CALL/CANCEL.  Common routines can be
replaced individually without needing to recompile/relink anything
else (unless there are interface changes).  Only the newly changed
files need to be redistributed.
```

```
[Snipped]


Please ignore the mess on the walls.  Is just my brain exploding...

I was initially given to the belief that we could compile multiple 
programs into one EXE.  I was later advised by my co-workers that I 
was mistaken -- that there can only be one EXE per CBL.  But now, at 
long last, if I am reading this right, your statement suggests that we 
CAN combine multiple programs into one EXE after all.  Please confirm 
or tell me I'm wrong. 

Thanks,
DaveM

(Remove "SPAMOUT" if replying to my email address)
```
