###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GNT's versus EXE's in Net Express

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2002-12-23T04:05:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-zjRHWOosdnjj@h24-82-204-17.wp.shawcable.net>`
- **References:** `<atm5kq$psh$1@bob.news.rcn.net> <3DFF671C.7C5815F7@Azonic.co.nz> <G0GL9.1886$qU5.1102792@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <217e491a.0212171141.380a6f4f@posting.google.com> <au5plq$ndq$1@bob.news.rcn.net>`

```
On Mon, 23 Dec 2002 01:42:18 UTC, renfrewthejust@yahoo.com (Renfrew) 
wrote:

> In article <217e491a.0212171141.380a6f4f@posting.google.com>, riplin@Azonic.co.nz says...
> >
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

When you use "dynamic" calling of programs the MF documentation seems 
to indicate that you cannot have more than a single program linked 
into an EXE or DLL. This is not true.

You can link as many objects as you want into a DLL. In the 
initialization logic of the program that will use the routines in the 
DLL you should use "SET xyz-dll-pointer to "xyz.dll" where 
xyz-dll-pointer is a procedure pointer. This will load the module 
names so that dynamic calls will locate the subroutines.

WARNING - if you CANCEL the first subroutine called from the DLL the 
DLL will be unloaded from memory and further calls to the subroutines 
in the DLL will fail.

You can also link as many objects as you want into an EXE
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GNT's versus EXE's in Net Express

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Harald.Cordes@microfocus.com (Harald Cordes)
- **Date:** 2002-12-23T01:12:41-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1fabdc0d.0212230112.21840b65@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<atm5kq$psh$1@bob.news.rcn.net> <3DFF671C.7C5815F7@Azonic.co.nz> <G0GL9.1886$qU5.1102792@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <217e491a.0212171141.380a6f4f@posting.google.com> <au5plq$ndq$1@bob.news.rcn.net>`

```
If P1.cbl, P2.cbl, P3.cbl, P4.cbl are COBOL programs you can put them
all in one EXE, e.g. by compiling and linking from the Net Express
Command Prompt CBLLINK P1.cbl P2.cbl P3.cbl P4.cbl.

Or better from the IDE. Just drag-and-drop all CBLs from the right
site of the projects view to the left site. Select "Object code".
Right-click the main obj on the left project site and chose "Package
selected files", chose "Executable file (EXE)". Now drag-and-drop all
other OBJs into this EXE.

You can also link OBJs from other programming languages like C into
this EXE but you cannot combine OBJs from C with GNTs in this easy
way.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GNT's versus EXE's in Net Express

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-12-23T13:53:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fzEN9.2891$qU5.2119919@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<atm5kq$psh$1@bob.news.rcn.net> <3DFF671C.7C5815F7@Azonic.co.nz> <G0GL9.1886$qU5.1102792@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <217e491a.0212171141.380a6f4f@posting.google.com> <au5plq$ndq$1@bob.news.rcn.net>`

```
"Renfrew" <renfrewthejust@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:au5plq$ndq$1@bob.news.rcn.net...
> I was initially given to the belief that we could compile multiple
> programs into one EXE.  I was later advised by my co-workers that I
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> or tell me I'm wrong.

You can place multiple programs in a single source code file. See the
entries in your compiler's manual under:

PROGRAM-ID. PROG1.
..
END PROGRAM PROG1.
PROGRAM-ID. PROG2.
..
END PROGRAM PROG2.
PROGRAM-ID. PROG3.
..
END PROGRAM PROG3.

This will (should!) put all three programs into a single executable. You can
then CALL 'literal' any of the programs from within the resulting
executable.

You can also play with the nesting...

PROGRAM-ID. PROG1.
..
PROGRAM-ID. PROG2.
..
END PROGRAM PROG2.
PROGRAM-ID. PROG3.
..
END PROGRAM PROG3.
PROGRAM-ID. PROG1.
..
END PROGRAM PROG1.
PROGRAM-ID. PROG2.
..
END PROGRAM PROG2.
PROGRAM-ID. PROG3.
..
END PROGRAM PROG1.


When you nest programs like this, there are some limits on what can be
called from where, as well as some data-sharing considerations when GLOBAL
and EXTERNAL datanames are used.

Someone gave you the a sequence to to this with the Microfocus compiler;
with the IBM mainframe compiler the the linker is also used, except for the
most part the required linker commands are created by the compiler.

But using "multiple programs per load module" is not quite the same as
creating dynamically-called modules. I have used multiple programs per
source code module for forms-printing applications when the input file may
be one of two or three seperate versions. Instead of two separate programs,
one program reads the input and decides what the format is, then CALLs the
appropriate output routine using an EXTERNAL file connector. These
applications do not have any 'reuseability' requirements, in that the code
only does one thing and no other programs will ever need access to the code,
so for ease of maintenance I just put everything in one place.

That is, the exact 'architecture' you choose will be application-dependent.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GNT's versus EXE's in Net Express

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-12-23T15:45:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E072F99.E2BB499F@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<atm5kq$psh$1@bob.news.rcn.net> <3DFF671C.7C5815F7@Azonic.co.nz> <G0GL9.1886$qU5.1102792@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <217e491a.0212171141.380a6f4f@posting.google.com> <au5plq$ndq$1@bob.news.rcn.net>`

```
Renfrew wrote:

> I was initially given to the belief that we could compile multiple
> programs into one EXE.  I was later advised by my co-workers that I
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> or tell me I'm wrong.

I don't use Net Express, just MF Cobol for various systems including
DOS, Windows, OS/2 SCO and Linux.

Under Windows (and DOS and OS/2) the output from a compile is a .OBJ,
these can be linked individually to be a dynamically called module
(which may be .EXE for DOS, .DLL or .DLW for Windows) or they can be
linked together to form one large statically linked .EXE. 

Note that I never use the MF IDE (I hate every IDE that I have ever seen
and use tools that _I_ have selected as appropriate) and it may be that
the Net Express development environment may be restrictive and not allow
one to specify that .EXEs are to be built from several separate compile
units.  or it may be a local site restriction to ensure full modularity
and ease of distribution.
```

#### ↳ Re: GNT's versus EXE's

- **From:** "Gazaloo" <gaz@a.loo>
- **Date:** 2002-12-19T05:32:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aScM9.392986$%m4.123943@rwcrnsc52.ops.asp.att.net>`
- **References:** `<atm5kq$psh$1@bob.news.rcn.net>`

```
"Renfrew" wrote:
> My company is in the process of converting from old MF 16-bit over to Net
> Express.  We only used GNT files in our original system, but we are now
faced
> with a choice of these versus EXE's.  Could someone be so kind as to
describe
> the practical and/or strategical differences between the usage of GNT's
versus
> EXE's?  The manual is not very clear about this.

Use an EXE with DLL's -- this is likely to be the most efficient footprint
you will have for executing your application. GNT's perform well (they are
processor code), but are not shareable -- i.e. if you have multiple
instances of A.GNT you will have duplicate copies of the code in memory.

Gazaloo.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: GNT's versus EXE's

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-12-19T10:13:53-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0212191013.5f004dd0@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<atm5kq$psh$1@bob.news.rcn.net> <aScM9.392986$%m4.123943@rwcrnsc52.ops.asp.att.net>`

```
"Gazaloo" <gaz@a.loo> wrote
> 
> Use an EXE with DLL's -- this is likely to be the most efficient footprint
> you will have for executing your application. GNT's perform well (they are
> processor code), but are not shareable -- i.e. if you have multiple
> instances of A.GNT you will have duplicate copies of the code in memory.

I think that you are confusing system DLLs with MF Cobol ones.  Within
one Cobol run unit each .GNT or each .DLL will occur exactly once at
most.  These .GNTs (or .INT) or .DLLs will not be shared between
different run units.

These dynamic loaded programs will hold state information (in
Working-storage and file sections) and it would not be useful to
duplicate within a run unit or shared between run units.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GNT's versus EXE's

- **From:** "Gazaloo" <gaz@a.loo>
- **Date:** 2002-12-19T19:14:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_ToM9.398606$P31.144678@rwcrnsc53>`
- **References:** `<atm5kq$psh$1@bob.news.rcn.net> <aScM9.392986$%m4.123943@rwcrnsc52.ops.asp.att.net> <217e491a.0212191013.5f004dd0@posting.google.com>`

```
"Richard" wrote:
> I think that you are confusing system DLLs with MF Cobol ones.  Within
> one Cobol run unit each .GNT or each .DLL will occur exactly once at
> most.  These .GNTs (or .INT) or .DLLs will not be shared between
> different run units.

Within a run-unit, yes you get 1 copy. If multiple run-units are invoked,
GNTs (code & data) will be duplicated. In this scenario, I thought that if
the code is in DLL's then it would be shared? On UNIX systems, with callable
shared objects (.so) this would be the case.

So, with run-time overhead aside, if you have 100K of code and 100K of data
and 10 instances of the application, it would occupy 2000K. With shared
objects (but not DLL's?) it would occupy 1100K.

> These dynamic loaded programs will hold state information (in
> Working-storage and file sections) and it would not be useful to
> duplicate within a run unit or shared between run units.

Agreed that data will not be shared.

Data sharing between run-units is useful, but the mechanisms are typically
files or shared memory.

Sharing working-storage data is also possible via multi-threading. A lot
more work needed to the application to handle that type of execution model,
but the resulting footprint would typically be a lot smaller (code AND data
sharing across multiple instances).

Gazaloo.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GNT's versus EXE's

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-12-19T15:49:04-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0212191549.77f2eafe@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<atm5kq$psh$1@bob.news.rcn.net> <aScM9.392986$%m4.123943@rwcrnsc52.ops.asp.att.net> <217e491a.0212191013.5f004dd0@posting.google.com> <_ToM9.398606$P31.144678@rwcrnsc53>`

```
"Gazaloo" <gaz@a.loo> wrote

> So, with run-time overhead aside, if you have 100K of code and 100K of data
> and 10 instances of the application, it would occupy 2000K. With shared
> objects (but not DLL's?) it would occupy 1100K.

This is MS Windows. Why would one user be running 10 instances of the
application ?  Given that I usually have an application divided into
between 20 and 100 loadable (call/cancel) modules, the chances of each
instance concurrently using the same modules is quite low.

I know that there is TSE but this invokes a somewhat different model
where each user has his own environment.  For example where an
application is restricted to a single instance TSE can run a single
instance per user.  I suspect that sharing is not done between TSE
users.

> Sharing working-storage data is also possible via multi-threading. A lot
> more work needed to the application to handle that type of execution model,

Not necessarily, Java does quite well without 'more work'.  Actually
Windows API programs can be a pain because of callbacks even without
being multi-threaded - one has to cater for non-sequential execution
of code.

> but the resulting footprint would typically be a lot smaller (code AND data
> sharing across multiple instances).

Multi-threaded programs are _not_ 'multiple instances', they are one
instance with multiple execution threads.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GNT's versus EXE's

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Gazaloo" <gaz@a.loo>
- **Date:** 2002-12-20T03:40:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fjwM9.400191$WL3.117720@rwcrnsc54>`
- **References:** `<atm5kq$psh$1@bob.news.rcn.net> <aScM9.392986$%m4.123943@rwcrnsc52.ops.asp.att.net> <217e491a.0212191013.5f004dd0@posting.google.com> <_ToM9.398606$P31.144678@rwcrnsc53> <217e491a.0212191549.77f2eafe@posting.google.com>`

```
"Richard" wrote:
> This is MS Windows. Why would one user be running 10 instances of the
> application ?  Given that I usually have an application divided into
> between 20 and 100 loadable (call/cancel) modules, the chances of each
> instance concurrently using the same modules is quite low.

I responded originally with multi-user in mind (my mistake), but I think you
know that already.

> Not necessarily, Java does quite well without 'more work'.

It's a COBOL application, but I think you know that already.

> Multi-threaded programs are _not_ 'multiple instances', they are one
> instance with multiple execution threads.

Fair enough, poor terminology on my part. I meant 'multiple threads', but I
think you know that already.

Gazaloo.
```

## Thread: COBOL FAQ — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### COBOL FAQ

- **From:** wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
- **Date:** 2002-12-17T09:22:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,news.answers,comp.answers
- **Message-ID:** `<computer-lang/cobol-faq_1040116911@rtfm.mit.edu>`

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2002/05/06
Version: 3.03
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Spring 2002

The full COBOL FAQ can be viewed at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send
private email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

Toward the top of the FAQ is a pointer to the appendix listing the most
recent significant updates and changes.

This FAQ answers such questions as (and addresses topics such as):

 - Where can I get a COBOL compiler? 
 - Is there a free COBOL compiler? 
 - Where can I contact... ?
 - What is happening with the draft of the next COBOL Standard and what
is in it? 
 - What about OO COBOL? 
 - Books about COBOL
 - COBOL Tools
 - What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?
 - What about USAGE? COMP? Storage for data in xyz format? etc?
 - How do I get started with COBOL? Where can I get education?
Tutorials? Etc

and much, MUCH more
```

## Thread: Programming AI for beginner — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Re: Programming AI for beginner

- **From:** uj797@victoria.tc.ca (Arthur T. Murray)
- **Date:** 2002-12-17T07:43:09-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.ai.genetic,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.lisp
- **Message-ID:** `<3dff460d@news.victoria.tc.ca>`
- **References:** `<atmj41$i05$1@news.krline.net>`

```
"nanda" <nanda@hotmail.com> writes on Tue, 17 Dec 2002:
>
> How to start programming AI and what language usually use?

You may use theoretically _any_ language for GOFAI, even 
http://mind.sourceforge.net/cobol.html -- old-fashioned COBOL.

What you need for AI is a good, introductory AI textbook, such as
http://www.cs.berkeley.edu/~russell/aima.html -- "Modern Approach."

If you really want to "start programming" an AI Mind, you may need
http://www.iuniverse.com/bookstore/book_detail.asp?isbn=0595259227 or
http://search.barnesandnoble.com/bookSearch/isbnInquiry.asp?ISBN=0595259227 .

> Is Lisp only use for academic purpose?

http://mind.sourceforge.net/lisp.html has _many_ purposes, not just AI.

> What about C or C++, are there AI implementation use these langauges?

http://mind.sourceforge.net/c.html is for AI Minds in C.
http://mind.sourceforge.net/cpp.html is about C++ AI Minds.

Hopes this helps, and leads to AI4U books left around via BookCrossing.

Arthur T. Murray
```

## Thread: CICS Receive — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### CICS Receive

- **From:** demosbrucker@yahoo.com (Devin)
- **Date:** 2002-12-17T08:57:57-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<28cc3bdd.0212170857.75078ff9@posting.google.com>`

```
Please keep in mind I am new to CICS/COBOL... Though I have not
activated the FSET attribute in my map, I am receiving the sent values
even though there have been no changes to the map.  I am using an
FRSET for the map which, according to my CICS manuals (coupled with my
interpretation) shouldn't cause values to be sent back, unless a
changes has occurred.  Of course, when I say values I mean something
other than LOW-VALUES.  Thanks for your help!
```

#### ↳ Re: CICS Receive

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2002-12-18T00:18:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c2a62a$e1016f00$eb95f243@chottel>`
- **References:** `<28cc3bdd.0212170857.75078ff9@posting.google.com>`

```
First of all the attribute values set by the map can be overridden from
with the program. Say the map has a field named RATE.  The symbolic map
will have a RATEA field that you can use to set the attribute. 

Also the MDT (modified data tag) is set automatically when the user keys
data into an unprotected field.  AAFAIK CICS never transmits (send/receive)
LOW-VALUES.

Devin <demosbrucker@yahoo.com> wrote in article
<28cc3bdd.0212170857.75078ff9@posting.google.com>...
> Please keep in mind I am new to CICS/COBOL... Though I have not
> activated the FSET attribute in my map, I am receiving the sent values
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: CICS Receive

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.NOSPAM.net>
- **Date:** 2002-12-19T11:19:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E01AB33.5040200@optonline.NOSPAM.net>`
- **References:** `<28cc3bdd.0212170857.75078ff9@posting.google.com>`

```
Devin wrote:
> Please keep in mind I am new to CICS/COBOL... Though I have not
> activated the FSET attribute in my map, I am receiving the sent values
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> other than LOW-VALUES.  Thanks for your help!

Is the program modifying any attribute bytes, e.g., field_name.A? As 
Charles pointed out, setting MDT ON will cause the data fields to be 
transmitted whether they're touched or not by the terminal user. Is 
literally every data field being received?
```

- **Date:** 2002-12-18T13:31:22-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c8dbad00.0212181331.4f38b1c7@posting.google.com>`

```
We are looking for a individual to provide full life cycle development
with COBOL, CICS, and DB2 Coding programming on a Unix platform. This
person
will have 3-5 years of experience in both skills. This person will
also have the opportunity to learn new skills and technologies as our
fortune 500 clients grow. They will have an opportunity to client
server technologies as mainframe applications are converted to GUI
technologies.

HTML, C++ or JAVA are a plus.


     CONTACT INFORMATION:

     Ryan Mac Donald
     7450 West 130th Street / Suite 300
     Kansas City, Kansas 66213
     1-800-676-6625 Ext.309
     913-402-0800 #309
     913-402-8454 - Fax
     http://www.megaforceusa.com
     rmac@megaforceusa.com
```

<a id="t-d9bc54960f"></a>
## Thread: My company wants to develop a new COBOL Programming Standard — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### My company wants to develop a new COBOL Programming Standard

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2002-12-18T14:57:07-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E00FD43.7F684024@attglobal.net>`

```
Suddenly, I might have a need for the "official" ISO Standard, which is
priced at a stunning $215, as Bill Klein pointed out a couple of weeks
ago.  Thane Hubbell wrote that he thought the total market for the
standard document would essentially be the companies developing COBOL
compilers to comply with it.  I hope it would be somewhat wider,
including other people like me, who are concerned with good usage of the
compilers.

I did download the Final Committee Draft a couple of months back.  Does
anyone think I need the "official" document, or could I work from that
Final Draft?

Also, I wondered if any of you would care to share your organization's
COBOL Standard?  I would be happy to share what we use now, and what we
come up with.  My organization implements our standard in a program that
is attached to the IBM mainframe COBOL compiler, so that diagnosis is
carried out on every compile (including when programs are put into
production).  Perhaps I could provide at least general information about
doing that, if anyone is interested.

Please reply to the Newsgroup, or feel free to e-mail me.  It's my home
e-mail address, but I'll see it, and be able to get back to you.
Thanks,
Colin Campbell
```

#### ↳ Re: My company wants to develop a new COBOL Programming Standard

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2002-12-22T13:13:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E05BAE9.7E1DA5A8@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3E00FD43.7F684024@attglobal.net>`

```
Colin Campbell wrote:
> 
> Suddenly, I might have a need for the "official" ISO Standard, which is
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> Colin Campbell

Have you looked at the Kasten COBOL page?  It discusses some aspects
of application coding standards.

http://home.swbell.net/mck9/cobol/cobol.html
```

## Thread: CICS on PC ? — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### CICS on PC ?

- **From:** "bassman" <no@spam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-19T15:23:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5wlM9.3557$HD1.703505621@hestia.telenet-ops.be>`

```
Hi there,

As a freelance IT consultant with several years of cobol experience on
Unisys and Wang, i just started with some CICS selfstudy.
I am already a bit experienced on IBM MVS with TSO, APS (cobol generator) en
DB2 so I have just started with the book "Murah's CICS for the cobol
programmer" which I find very usefull.

I would enjoy it a lot if could do some CICS testing on my w2K laptop. Any
suggestions which inexpensive CICS / COBOL software is available for pc ?
Any other ideas that might be usefull ?

Thanks for the help.
```

#### ↳ Re: CICS on PC ?

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-20T04:52:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nmxM9.15667$KW2.686873@twister.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<5wlM9.3557$HD1.703505621@hestia.telenet-ops.be>`

```
Real CICS is available with, and in fact, delivered wth Visual Age COBOL. Occasionally, copies are sold on E-Bay, usually older
copies but that would not make any difference with that book. If you are member of
IBM PWD, you can pick up a copy for under $50 directly from IBM.

-Paul


"bassman" <no@spam.com> wrote in message news:5wlM9.3557$HD1.703505621@hestia.telenet-ops.be...
> Hi there,
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: CICS on PC ?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-19T21:33:36-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<atu32h$adj$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<5wlM9.3557$HD1.703505621@hestia.telenet-ops.be> <nmxM9.15667$KW2.686873@twister.austin.rr.com>`

```
When one says "Real CICS" - it is important that this be distinguished from
"RealCICS" (no space) which was the CICS emualator that used to be available
with the Realia COBOL compiler (pre-CA) - and which is now known as
"Advantage CA-Realia II Workbench Option for CICS" see

 http://www3.ca.com/Solutions/ProductOption.asp?ID=3816

The IBM PC offering is discussed in
  "Chapter 16. Developing COBOL programs for CICS"

in the document at:
 http://publibfp.boulder.ibm.com/epubs/pdf/c2708121.pdf
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CICS on PC ?

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-20T06:32:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Thank you Bill - I did not know that, appreciate the clarification
-Paul

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CICS on PC ?

- **From:** charles.godwin@ca.com (Charles Godwin)
- **Date:** 2002-12-20T03:29:10-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c8035ce7.0212200329.23419cc9@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<5wlM9.3557$HD1.703505621@hestia.telenet-ops.be> <nmxM9.15667$KW2.686873@twister.austin.rr.com> <atu32h$adj$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Bill

Thanks for the mention of CA-Realia version of CICS. However I must
clarify your information.

The Advantage CA-Realia II Workbench includes the CICS development and
runtime environment for a PC based CICS emulator. This is all you need
to develop, test and run CICS applications on a PC.

See this URL for details.
http://www3.ca.com/Solutions/Product.asp?ID=1475

The Option for CICS you mentioned is an add on extra for users who
also want to use our Workbench to debug CICS programs running on the
mainframe. This uses an interface to Intertest running on a mainframe
CICS region.

I will say that the text on our website could be misleading so I'll
see about getting it clarified.

Charles Godwin
Development Manager
Advatage CA-Realia II Workbench

> "RealCICS" (no space) which was the CICS emualator that used to be available

- **From:** "Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@nospam.microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-20T14:51:10

- **References:** `<5wlM9.3557$HD1.703505621@hestia.telenet-ops.be>`

```
You might want to consider Meinframe Express for the PC available from Micro
Focus.

It's an IDE that allows development and debiugging of mainframe COBOL (and
PL/I) applications on the PC. It emulates all of the relevant mainframe
features on the PC.

www.microfocus.com

<a id="t-ff0bfddfc2"></a>
## Thread: Visual Age COBOL and MS SQL Server — 17 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Visual Age COBOL and MS SQL Server

- **From:** Al Rudderham <xal.rudderhamx@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-12-20T00:12:00-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5a850vsupgebk7ph32aa0ftadjvcba8995@4ax.com>`

```
I'm looking at porting a small COBOL application to VA COBOL.  The
client is committed to VA COBOL specifically because it is being used
for another MUCH larger project.  The client also uses SQL Server
quite extensively.

The most desirable approach would be to use embedded SQL in the COBOL
code.  So far everything I've found in the VA COBOL docmentation only
points to using DB2 with VA COBOL.  Is there a preprocessor available
for VA COBOL that works with MS SQL Server, and if so where can I find
more info.

I've spent hours searching with Google, and on the IBM and Microsoft
web sites without any success so far.

Thanks!
```

#### ↳ Re: Visual Age COBOL and MS SQL Server

- **From:** "Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@nospam.microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-20T11:43:38
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<atuv80$1m5$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<5a850vsupgebk7ph32aa0ftadjvcba8995@4ax.com>`

```
You might want to look at an alternative, Net Express from Micro Focus for
example. Using embedded SQL it can access any SQL database that has ODBC
support.

www.microfocus.com

> client is committed to VA COBOL specifically because it is being used

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Visual Age COBOL and MS SQL Server

- **From:** "Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@nospam.microfocus.com>

```
You might look at Transoft - they can map flat files to ODBC - it may be
possible to do it the other way round. They can be found at www.transoft.com

Other than that it might well have to be direct calls to the ODBC API.

www.microfocus.com

#### ↳ Re: Visual Age COBOL and MS SQL Server

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-21T03:58:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NFRM9.22208$KW2.868799@twister.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<5a850vsupgebk7ph32aa0ftadjvcba8995@4ax.com>`

```
Couldn't be much easier, though it is - ah - non intuitive.

First, no, the preprocessor that is included is for DB/2 only. But guess what? Microsoft has their own preprocessor for SQL server,
and you can use that. You would have to contact Microsoft's SQL group about it or find someone with a full MSDN subscription though.
I dropped all support for Microsoft about 2 years ago.

Or you can use ODBC, which is probably a whole lot easier. Just link to the OBDC libraries and make the appropriate calls. It looks
complex, but it really is simple.

By the way, if the big project is using VA COBOL, they are probably talking to DB/2 anyway. Why not just use DB/2 instead of SQL
Server, even under Windows?


-Paul

"Al Rudderham" <xal.rudderhamx@sympatico.ca> wrote in message news:5a850vsupgebk7ph32aa0ftadjvcba8995@4ax.com...

> client is committed to VA COBOL specifically because it is being used

```

Given that DB2 would be a better choice of database engine, I 
whole-heartedly agree

(someone who works with both SQL server and DB2 - both with COBOL)

And if you have stuff that resides in SQL server, use the federated 
database stuff in DB2 to present the tables to a DB2 client as if they
are present in DB2
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Visual Age COBOL and MS SQL Server

- **From:** Al Rudderham <xal.rudderhamx@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-12-22T21:59:34-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<29uc0vo5kmqtr81fc6n20i36u9t0ba5qug@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<5a850vsupgebk7ph32aa0ftadjvcba8995@4ax.com> <NFRM9.22208$KW2.868799@twister.austin.rr.com>`

```
On Sat, 21 Dec 2002 03:58:37 GMT, "Paul Raulerson"
<praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com> wrote:

>Couldn't be much easier, though it is - ah - non intuitive.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>I dropped all support for Microsoft about 2 years ago.

Thanks, I'll check that out.

>Or you can use ODBC, which is probably a whole lot easier. Just link 
>to the OBDC libraries and make the appropriate calls. It looks
>complex, but it really is simple.

That seems to be where I'm headed.

>By the way, if the big project is using VA COBOL, they are probably 
>talking to DB/2 anyway. Why not just use DB/2 instead of SQL
>Server, even under Windows?

There are a couple of reasons.  The primary reason is that an outside
vendor is driving the "big project" (and defined the toolset).  They
have control of the DBA function for DB2, whereas there are already
plenty of SQL Server databases in house.  It would not be permitted to
interfere in any way with the "big project", and use of DB2 would
probably wrest control of the DB2 admin function from them.  The small
project I'm interested in can only go ahead if it doesn't impact the
big one.  Using SQL Server keeps out of their way.

The second reason is the toolset.  I've worked with SQL Server
databases for the last 5 years, and the toolset (for developers and
for admin) is slick and well integrated with Windows.  DB2 reminds me
of Oracle - somebody did a quick port to Windows and to hell with
following Windows conventions.  Not to state a religious war - no
matter how good or bad the tools are, the real issue is not stepping
on the big project vendor's toes...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Visual Age COBOL and MS SQL Server

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-23T05:10:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<JUwN9.42374$Nz5.1076220@twister.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<5a850vsupgebk7ph32aa0ftadjvcba8995@4ax.com> <NFRM9.22208$KW2.868799@twister.austin.rr.com> <29uc0vo5kmqtr81fc6n20i36u9t0ba5qug@4ax.com>`

```

"Al Rudderham" <xal.rudderhamx@sympatico.ca> wrote in message news:29uc0vo5kmqtr81fc6n20i36u9t0ba5qug@4ax.com...
> On Sat, 21 Dec 2002 03:58:37 GMT, "Paul Raulerson"
> <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com> wrote:
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>

There would only be anissue if you integrated the database with the "big project."
If in fact, they are totally separate databases, then I am unsure why there would be
any conflict. DB/2 under Windows does not have to have any interaction with
the mainframe or UNIX systems.


> The second reason is the toolset.  I've worked with SQL Server
> databases for the last 5 years, and the toolset (for developers and
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I'm not really clear on this one either, except on the differences between T-SQL and DB/2 SQL.
Hands down, DB./2 is much better. Other than that, DB/2 integrates a lot better with Java,
less well with ASP. (Well, what did you expect? You almost have to use ODBC to use ASP.)
It costs less, and outperforms SQL Server in almost every area. Development toolsets
include extremely good integration with more than one COBOL dialect, Java, C, C++,
ODBC, and XML, all of which are mature at this point, having been introduced at least
2 years ago.

Perhaps you should take another look at DB/2, without the Window's blinders on. :)

-Paul

> --
> Remove preceding and trailing X from username for replies
> (Sorry, but I'm SICK of spam...)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Visual Age COBOL and MS SQL Server

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Al Rudderham <xal.rudderhamx@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-12-23T01:10:12-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<119d0v0naga144djib7b7d3n0aduu1uaru@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<5a850vsupgebk7ph32aa0ftadjvcba8995@4ax.com> <NFRM9.22208$KW2.868799@twister.austin.rr.com> <29uc0vo5kmqtr81fc6n20i36u9t0ba5qug@4ax.com> <JUwN9.42374$Nz5.1076220@twister.austin.rr.com>`

```
On Mon, 23 Dec 2002 05:10:01 GMT, "Paul Raulerson"
<praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com> wrote:

>There would only be anissue if you integrated the database with the "big project."
>If in fact, they are totally separate databases, then I am unsure why there would be
>any conflict. DB/2 under Windows does not have to have any interaction with
>the mainframe or UNIX systems.

The big project is using DB/2 on Win2K Server.  The project I'm
interested in isn't big enough to justify any more hardware, or
software licences.  I just want to piggy-back on pre-existing
infrastructure.  What I'd like to prevent is the outsourcing of the
business function, which would mean a permanent loss of jobs.

While I'm sure you could *technically* do things so there were 2
independent DB/2 databases on the same server, *politically* it would
be a can of worms, and probably open up the opportunity for a lot of
finger pointing.  I don't believe they would buy the idea, and I don't
want to try to sell it.

>I'm not really clear on this one either, except on the differences between T-SQL and DB/2 SQL.
>Hands down, DB./2 is much better. Other than that, DB/2 integrates a lot better with Java,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>Perhaps you should take another look at DB/2, without the Window's blinders on. :)

No blinders.  I don't get to pick the toolset - I get to write code.
My client is a large company with their own staff.  They made a
commitment to SQL Server several years ago (ripping out Oracle mid-way
through a large project when the vendor ran into problems making
Oracle work with MTS), and are using SQL Server everywhere.  They now
have one project that is more legacy based, which will use DB/2.

I'd just like to find a low cost and relatively painless way to port
an old COBOL application without incurring any software licencing
costs, and without jumping through too many hoops.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Visual Age COBOL and MS SQL Server

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-12-23T23:53:53
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e07a0e9_1@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<5a850vsupgebk7ph32aa0ftadjvcba8995@4ax.com> <NFRM9.22208$KW2.868799@twister.austin.rr.com> <29uc0vo5kmqtr81fc6n20i36u9t0ba5qug@4ax.com> <JUwN9.42374$Nz5.1076220@twister.austin.rr.com> <119d0v0naga144djib7b7d3n0aduu1uaru@4ax.com>`

```
Al,

I totally understand your reasoning and agree that SQL Server is the way to
go.

(I have a similar environment in some respects...)

You can use SQL Server from COBOL without problem. You need the ODBC driver
and away you go.

If ODBC doesn't appeal you can actually use OLE to SQL Server if you are
into OO COBOL (and you are using the Fujitsu toolset and compiler), and that
enables running stored procedures and triggers, or using ADO, but that's a
bit cutting edge for most people here.

Using SQL Server with ODBC and embedded SQL from COBOL is a doddle.

Pete.

- **References:** `<5a850vsupgebk7ph32aa0ftadjvcba8995@4ax.com> <NFRM9.22208$KW2.868799@twister.austin.rr.com> <29uc0vo5kmqtr81fc6n20i36u9t0ba5qug@4ax.com> <JUwN9.42374$Nz5.1076220@twister.austin.rr.com> <119d0v0naga144djib7b7d3n0aduu1uaru@4ax.com> <3e07a0e9_1@Usenet.com> <2geh0v02dr3d8002lsto0bn4gnc3neih1d@4ax.com>`

```
On Tue, 24 Dec 2002 19:50:11 UTC, Al Rudderham 
<xal.rudderhamx@sympatico.ca> wrote:

> On Mon, 23 Dec 2002 23:53:53 -0000, "Peter E. C. Dashwood"
> <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

The ODBC driver for SQL server is part of the MDAC package (Microsoft 
Data Access Component). Do a search at the microsoft website and you 
should turn up an installation package...

Visualage COBOL uses whatever ODBC drivers are present. The ODBC calls
are actually to the ODBC32.DLL and are forwarded from that DLL to the 
appropriate database specific driver that is tied to the DSN that you 
are attaching to.

When you link the program you have to link to the ODBC32.LIB.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Visual Age COBOL and MS SQL Server

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-12-26T12:33:24
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e0af6c6_2@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<5a850vsupgebk7ph32aa0ftadjvcba8995@4ax.com> <NFRM9.22208$KW2.868799@twister.austin.rr.com> <29uc0vo5kmqtr81fc6n20i36u9t0ba5qug@4ax.com> <JUwN9.42374$Nz5.1076220@twister.austin.rr.com> <119d0v0naga144djib7b7d3n0aduu1uaru@4ax.com> <3e07a0e9_1@Usenet.com> <2geh0v02dr3d8002lsto0bn4gnc3neih1d@4ax.com>`

```

Al Rudderham <xal.rudderhamx@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:2geh0v02dr3d8002lsto0bn4gnc3neih1d@4ax.com...
> On Mon, 23 Dec 2002 23:53:53 -0000, "Peter E. C. Dashwood"
> <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
> >You can use SQL Server from COBOL without problem. You need the ODBC
driver
> >and away you go.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
If you have SQL Server installed you will have automatically installed the
SQL Server ODBC driver. As Lorne pointed out, it is part of the MDAC package
that SQL Server requires in order to run.

I won't repeat Lorne's very apposite and accurate post (except to observe
that the VA environment should automatically include linking with ODBC32.LIB
when it encounters embedded SQL in the pre-compile.).

This post was simply to say that if you need help mail me privately. I have
a lot of stuff on using Relational Database from COBOL in a Client Server
environment.

I have been advocating this approach for years in the face of stiff
opposition from the ISAM/VSAM lovers who seem unable to grasp the fact that
the closed file system has been killing COBOL for a long time now.

I used to get abusive mail from people telling me that RDB was a fad and
that ISAM/VSAM was a proven and tested way to implement systems. (I never
disputed that it wasn't. Spent many years of my career (prior to 1983) using
VSAM/KSDS/RRDS/ESDS and ISAM in different flavours and developing Access
Methods and File system "layers" with these tools as they were all that was
available for random access from COBOL.) In those days hierarchic DB (IMS)
was King but it was unwieldy and had some serious limitations in that it
required traversal of the hierarchy, and for a long time it was necessary to
re-establish position by multiple calls after a dequeue boundary. By the
time they gave us the DL/1 call code that "remembered" current position so
you could pick up where you left off with a single call, it was too late...I
had already experimented with concatenating segment keys as the hierarchy
was traversed, but it was unwieldy and you had to store it somewhere across
a dequeue boundary...)

Currently, when I see my staff writing SQL server procedures and look at
what they write, it is a humbling experience. These are young people who
have never known any OTHER way than Relational Database and they manipulate
complex joins and selections with a facility born of years of experience in
doing just that.(I thought I was a pretty dab had at SQL until I saw what
these folks do...<G> However, my education is continuing...)

Nowadays I get private mail from people who can't understand why I still
make this point, as their installations automatically use RDB and the
message is clear. They can't imagine anyone clinging to the idea of serious
development WITHOUT using RDB.

I wish it had happened while there was still time to make COBOL part of it.

The fact is that embedded static SQL in COBOL programs is just not an option
for the new world. However, it serves nicely in the interim <G>.

Pete.



 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

with the statement "open input afile"

       FILE SECTION.

in STATUS-BYTES.

from a SAP-application running on the same AIX machine.

not AFILE. I choose AFILE because this name isn't linked

the AIX filesystem. I believe the file contains several
thousand records with fixed length. The File Description
Entry in the DATA DIVISION follows a data group for the
definition of the record structure in the data file.
Here is correct code snippet:

       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.
           SELECT  AFILE  ASSIGN    TO  SAP
                FILE STATUS IS STATUS-BYTES.
       ...
       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.

           LABEL    RECORD   STANDARD

       01  SAP-SATZ.
           05                    PIC X(001).
           05 SAP-SATZ1.
              10                    PIC X(006).
              10 SAP-KENNUNG        PIC X(004).
              10 SAP-PROGNAME       PIC X(008).
              10 SAP-HDLNR          PIC X(005).
              10 SAP-BELNR          PIC X(006).
       ...

           DISPLAY 'Status-Bytes is: ' STATUS-BYTES

After "OPEN INPUT AFILE" the STATUS-BYTES always shows
an error code 35 or 95. In the IBM documentation for
AIX-Cobol (Cobol Language Reference) these error codes
are defined as:

       35 An OPEN statement with the INPUT, I-O, or
          EXTEND phrase was attempted on a non-optional
          file that was not present.
       95 For all files, except QSAM:
          Invalid or incomplete file information.

Thanks for your help.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: open input afile

- **From:** Donald Tees <Donald_Tees@Sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-12-22T08:26:19-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E05E7AB.9040002@Sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<ba4d0b76.0212200703.2808eda4@posting.google.com> <20021220162604.03151.00000508@mb-mq.aol.com> <ba4d0b76.0212220325.35d15667@posting.google.com>`

```
I do not know the AIX file system, but on most machines, the code below 
uses SAP as a data name.  In effect, you would either re-write the 
select clause as

           SELECT  AFILE  ASSIGN    TO  "/usr/cobol/SAP"
                FILE STATUS IS STATUS-BYTES.

or define the word SAP in working storage

           SELECT  AFILE  ASSIGN    TO  SAP
                FILE STATUS IS STATUS-BYTES.
and in working storage:
	01 SAP			PICTURE X(25) VALUE "/USR/COBOL/SAP".

Since your program is compiling, I suspect that the data name SAP is defined, but defined wrong.  You may have to put a drive name or computer name on the beginning of the literal if the drive your program is on is not the same drive as the drive your data is on (IE: "A:\user ...  or \\machinename\user\ ...).

As I say, I am not familiar with your specific platform, so I may be out to lunch here, but it is what I would check first.

Donald


   
m8java wrote:

>sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky) wrote in message news:<20021220162604.03151.00000508@mb-mq.aol.com>...
>  
…[56 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: open input afile

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-22T16:36:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0SlN9.37254$6H6.1089618@twister.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<ba4d0b76.0212200703.2808eda4@posting.google.com> <20021220162604.03151.00000508@mb-mq.aol.com> <ba4d0b76.0212220325.35d15667@posting.google.com>`

```
Could easily be a coupe of things;

(1) Whatever environment variable you are using to define SAP could be pointing to the wrong place. The way you have "SAP" defined
in your SELECT clause will cause the runtime to find an environment variable named "SAP" and substitute the value for that into the
OPEN. Try hardcoding the file in the select, using something like ... ASSIGN TO "/usr1/SAP/files/afile"  (using the real path and
file name of course.)

(2) The file you are trying to open may not be one of the types that COBOL recognizes, though it looks to be pretty much a flat file
(i.e. Sequential) from your definition. If it *is* a sequential file, it has to have particular headers on it that the COBOL
compiler runtime inserts. If, as is more likely, it is an AIX sequential file, define the file as LINE SEQUENTIAL and I expect it
will start working.

Just a personal idiosyncrasy, but I always fully define files in the SELECT. If you had fully defined it, we would probably be able
to tell you more exactly what the problem is. :)

-Paul

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2002-12-22T18:29:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20021222132929.17913.00000603@mb-mv.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<ba4d0b76.0212220325.35d15667@posting.google.com>`

```
In article <ba4d0b76.0212220325.35d15667@posting.google.com>,
klaus.duttle@online.de (m8java) writes:

>
>After "OPEN INPUT AFILE" the STATUS-BYTES always shows
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

This would then be pointing to the connector in your JCL or whatever
acts as your Job Control Language in AIX.
In JCL you would have a DDname that points to the actual SAP file.
//SAP  DD DSN=usr.cobol.sap, <all the other related attributes>
In PC COBOL you would define in the FD an ATTRIBUTE TITLE IS "/usr/cobol/sap"
or TITLE is WS-FILENAME
and WS-FILENAME PICx(nn) value "/usr/cobol/sap"
or in the ASSIGN statement as ASSIGN TO "/usr/cobol/sap".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: open input afile

- **From:** ctimmer@gci.net (Curt Timmerman)
- **Date:** 2002-12-23T20:01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Xns92ED70123D1Ectimmermanacsalaskac@10.136.100.32>`
- **References:** `<ba4d0b76.0212200703.2808eda4@posting.google.com> <20021220162604.03151.00000508@mb-mq.aol.com> <ba4d0b76.0212220325.35d15667@posting.google.com>`

```
klaus.duttle@online.de (m8java) wrote in
<ba4d0b76.0212220325.35d15667@posting.google.com>: 

>sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky) wrote in message
>news:<20021220162604.03151.00000508@mb-mq.aol.com>... 
…[51 more quoted lines elided]…
>.

IF 'SAP' is a reference to a data field use (note USING key word):
SELECT  AFILE  ASSIGN    USING  SAP

Otherwise, prior to executing you application set the 'SAP' environment 
variable:
export SAP="/usr/cobol/SAP"

These are both AIX Cobol constructs.
```

#### ↳ Re: open input afile

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-12-21T07:21:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E04167B.28996C7F@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<ba4d0b76.0212200703.2808eda4@posting.google.com>`

```
m8java wrote:

> without any problem but under AIX there are some trouble
> with the statement "open input afile"
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> in STATUS-BYTES.

I think that this is not what the original code looks like, the SELECT
is probably:

        SELECT AFILE ASSIGN SAP

then this will match with 'FD AFILE' and 'OPEN INPUT AFILE'.

The file status indicates that it can't find the file.  This is because
you haven't told it what the actual name of the file is.  On the
mainframe there will be a run-card that specifies the actual filename to
use for 'AFILE'.  You need to find out how to specify this in AIX,
either externally in some configuration file or environment entry, or
within the program such as:

       SELECT AFILE ASSIGN "/livedata/host/AFILE.DAT"
```

<a id="t-b8a54baaeb"></a>
## Thread: Interest in COBOL — 1 msgs

### Re: Interest in COBOL

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-12-20T22:56:20-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E03D854.9303769D@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<000001c2a535$86f02510$9865fea9@WMK>`

```
The perception of COBOL in many North American companies is that it is
obsolete and will be replaced.  In addition, the long time that it has
taken to produce a new standard has alienated many of us practitioners.
My firm belief is that the whole process must change.  If I understand
correctly, for an internet standard to become effective, two separate
implementations must be produced and shown to interoperate.  Thus the
most important criteria of any further changes must be that they are
developed AND TESTED in a timely (1-2 year timeframe).  ISO and ANSI
should study the IETF and modify current processes so that we see tested
and testable code.  The various open source COBOL projects may be a
valuable source of effort in this regard. 

"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> The following note was sent out recently by the Chair of J4 COBOL Committee,
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> Search the archives at http://bama.ua.edu/archives/ibm-main.html
```

I spent 10 years working with IMS and know a thing or two about it. I don't
post  on it here because most people don't even say "thanks" and I no longer
have access to a mainframe to test any code I posted.

There are probably a lot of people here who are using IMS every day. Did it
occur to you that some of them may make a living out this and would be loth
to give it away free?

Having said that, there is a wealth of information here that is freely given
and the general tone of the NG is "helpful".

Maybe you should try posting a specific problem rather than asking for
general support on IMS?

Pete.

## Thread: description/explanation of CICS macros — 2 this month / 3 total

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### description/explanation of CICS macros

- **From:** hitesh_kakaiya@hotmail.com (hitesh k)
- **Date:** 2002-12-23T03:15:06-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<105d75a4.0212230315.4c4c5ba6@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi
	Anyone knows where can I find description/explanation of CICS macros.
Newer versions of CICS do not support these macros. There are third
party tools also available to convert CICS macros to corresponding
CICS command But I want to know about these macros.
		Please guide me.

				Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: description/explanation of CICS macros

- **From:** wobconsult@sprynet.com (WOB Consulting)
- **Date:** 2002-12-23T09:21:34-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<173f80ae.0212230921.2dab193@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<105d75a4.0212230315.4c4c5ba6@posting.google.com>`

```
I think you're out of luck. CICS/MVS Version/Release 2.1.2 was the
last CICS that supported Macro Level support for HLL's. CICS/ESA
Version/Release 3.1.1 was the last CICS that supported Macro Level
support for Assembler.

You may want to look through IBM Book Manager On-Line -

http://www.s390.ibm.com/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/LIBRARY

or, locate someone that has these manuals in their possession.

Bill

hitesh_kakaiya@hotmail.com (hitesh k) wrote in message 
news:<105d75a4.0212230315.4c4c5ba6@posting.google.com>...

Hallo,

How can I convert a .DAT file to a DBase5 .dbf file ?

??
or is it not done (even to ask!)

thanks,
paul ( a VB man)

paul van keeken wrote:
>

> paul ( a VB man)

You might want to try a generic parser like ParseRat (http://www.parserat.com/) in its 
generic binary mode.  (A DAT file is unlikely to have separators - most are fixed 
format, fixed length records.)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: convert Cobol - .dat file to dbase ?

- **From:** Robert Graham <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-23T19:27:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E0765DF.8070700@nycap.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<au79cp$9iv$1@reader10.wxs.nl> <3E07413D.7D3@paralynx.com>`

```
Another way to do this if it's a one-shot deal is to import 
it into Excel where it will ask you to mark the field 
divisions.  This assumes that the .DAT file is standarda 
ASCII with nothing odd, like packed fields, etc.

There is also a product called DATA JUNCTION which is 
designed for file conversions.

Ed Guy wrote:
> paul van keeken wrote:
> 
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@paralynx.com>

```
That's a good solution if there is CR/LF to define the end of each record.  Often there 
isn't if the records are fixed length (it would be redundant - which used to matter 
when storage was costly).

#### ↳ Re: convert Cobol - .dat file to dbase ?

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-23T13:57:14-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<k4OcnQ2UxuYQ95qjXTWc3A@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<au79cp$9iv$1@reader10.wxs.nl>`

```

"paul van keeken" <paul@keeken.nl> wrote in message
news:au79cp$9iv$1@reader10.wxs.nl...
> Hallo,
>
> How can I convert a .DAT file to a DBase5 .dbf file ?
>
> I can read the file but what are the saperators Is it be best done in
Excel
> ??
> or is it not done (even to ask!)
>
> thanks,
> paul ( a VB man)

You should understand that "DAT" has no intrinsic meaning unique to COBOL;
the interior format of the file could be anything (including a dbf file).
There is no requirement that ties a file generated by a COBOL program to any
external filename.

A (suitably written) COBOL program can generate text files, undelimited text
files, tab-delimited text files, relative files, dbf files, PDF  files,
Excel or Access files, HTML files, or any other format, and then give the
result any name desired by the programmer. Conventionally, "DAT" means
"data" without regard for the internal format.

So, then, your quest is not so much with converting a "COBOL" file as it is
with converting the format you've got to something else. To do that, you
obviously have to define your present file - the definition of your present
file does not include the word "COBOL."
```

#### ↳ Re: convert Cobol - .dat file to dbase ?

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2002-12-23T12:02:20-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0212231202.577b9bae@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<au79cp$9iv$1@reader10.wxs.nl>`

```
You can write a COBOL program to do this.  I have a rudimentary DBF
conversion routine - email me privately, leaving off the trailing h in
thaneh (Else it goes to spam bucket).  You will need access to the
COBOL compiler used to create the .dat file and the source code used
in it's creation.

"paul van keeken" <paul@keeken.nl> wrote in message news:<au79cp$9iv$1@reader10.wxs.nl>...

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

#### ↳ Re: I need a sample program

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-12-28T02:01:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E0D05CE.136CB075@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<bf59aa40.0212231301.68c5cc07@posting.google.com>`

```


ZATLAS wrote:

> Hi
> I am new to oo COBOL. I learn best by example. Could somebody please
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Z. Atlas

I agree. I too learn best from examples. I show the difference below,
between FACTORY only and Instance methods - but chances are 90% of your
coding will
relate to creating instances. Hope the following helps. Check your
compiler's reference manuals/on-line help for syntax.

Jimmy, Calgary AB

*>------------------------------------------------------------
Program-id. Demo1.

*> This is your 'Trigger' program to get OO started.

*>Repository.
Class-Control.          FactoryDemo   is class "facdemo"
                        InstanceDemo  is class "instdemo"
                        .
Working-storage section.
01 n                            pic 9(03).
   88 GoodValue                 value 1 thru 4.
   88 QuitValue                 value 0.
01 ws1.
  05 pic x(15) value "Good day".
  05 pic x(15) value "Bonjour".
  05 pic x(15) value "Guten Tag".
  05 pic x(15) value "Buonas diaz".
  05 pic x(15) value spaces.
01 ws2 redefines ws1.
  05 ws-Text pic x(15) occurs 5.
01 occurs 4.
   05 os-Instance             object reference.

Procedure Division.
perform varying n from 1 by 1 until n > 4
  invoke FactoryDemo "setTitleOne" using n, ws-Text(n)
End-perform
perform varying n from 1 by 1 until n > 4
  invoke FactoryDemo "displayTitleOne" using n
End-perform

*> Note : While the above works, it is NOT a GOOD IDEA to rely
*> upon FACTORY variables being consistent between invokes.
*> The following two approaches are 'SAFER'

perform varying n from 1 by 1 until n > 4
  invoke FactoryDemo "displayTitleTwo" using n
End-perform
move 5 to n
move "Good afternoon" to ws-Text(n)
invoke FactoryDemo "displayOther" using n, ws-Text(n)

*> Now INSTANCES. Where I have "InstanceDemo" below, you could
*> substitute Dialog, COBOL File, a record, DB Tables accessed
*> with SQL, lists (collections/dictionaries) etc.......
*> The following can be achieved in several different ways.

perform varying n from 1 by 1 until n > 4
  invoke InstanceDemo "new" using n, returning os-Instance(n)
End-perform
perform varying n from 1 by 1 until n > 4
  invoke os-Instance(n) "passTitle" using ws-Text(n)
End-perform
Display "Instances - Enter 1 to 4 or zero to Quit"
move 99 to n
perform GET-INSTANCES until QuitValue.

STOP RUN.

GET-INSTANCES.

accept n
Evaluate true
   when GoodValue
        invoke os-Instance(n) "displayTitle"
*> when ......
*> when ......
End-evaluate.
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
*>-------------------------------------------------------------

*> This class only has methods in FACTORY

Class-id.           FactoryDemo inherits from Base.

Class-Control.      FactoryDemo is class "facdemo"
                    .
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
FACTORY.
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
*>OBJECT-STORAGE SECTION.
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01 T1.
   05 T2 pic x(15) occurs 4.
01 T3.
  05 pic x(15) value "Good day".
  05 pic x(15) value "Bonjour".
  05 pic x(15) value "Guten tag".
  05 pic x(15) value "Buonas diaz".
  05 pic x(15) value spaces.
01 T4 redefines T3.
  05 T5 pic x(15) occurs 5.
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "setTitleOne".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
Linkage section.
01 n                pic 9(03).
01 lnk-Text         pic x(15).
Procedure Division using n, lnk-Text.
 move lnk-Text to T2(n)
End Method "setTitleOne".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "displayTitleOne".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
Linkage section.
01 n                    pic 9(03).
Procedure Division using n.
 display "Factory - The greeting is :- " T2(n)
End Method "displayTitleOne".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "displayTitleTwo".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
Linkage section.
01 n                            pic 9(03).
Procedure Division using n.
  display "Factory - The greeting is :- " T5(n)
End Method "displayTitleTwo".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "displayOther".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
Linkage section.
01 n                            pic 9(03).
01 lnk-Text                     pic x(15).
Procedure Division using n, lnk-Text.
 move lnk-Text to T5(n)
 invoke self "displayTitleTwo" using n
End Method "displayOther".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
END FACTORY.
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
OBJECT.       *> NOT REQUIRED
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
END OBJECT.   *> NOT REQUIRED
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
END CLASS FactoryDemo.
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
*>-------------------------------------------------------------

*>  You create 'n' number of INSTANCES using the FACTORY as a
*> template (cookie cutter)

Class-id.           InstanceDemo inherits from Base.

*> Repository.
Class-Control.      InstanceDemo is class "instdemo"
                    .
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
FACTORY.
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "new".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
Linkage section.
01 n                            pic 9(03).
01 lnk-Self                     object reference.
Procedure Division using n returning lnk-Self.
  invoke super "new" returning lnk-self
  invoke lnk-self "setN" using n
End Method "new".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
END FACTORY.
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
OBJECT.
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
OBJECT-STORAGE SECTION.      *> NetExpress requires this syntax
*>WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.   *> to work correctly on instances
01 n                                pic 9(03).
01 ws-Message                       pic x(15).
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "displayTitle".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
*> Note : Using Micro Focus format - a method can consist of
*> just the code
display "Instance # " n " The greeting is - "  ws-Message
End Method "displayTitle".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "passTitle".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
Linkage section.
01 lnk-Title                        pic x(15).
Procedure Division using lnk-Title.
 move lnk-Title to ws-Message
End Method "passTitle".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "setN".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
Linkage section.
01 lnk-n                            pic 9(03).
Procedure Division using lnk-n.
  move lnk-n to n
End Method "setN".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
END OBJECT.
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
END CLASS InstanceDemo.
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: (NEWBIE) Cobol trace file

- **From:** Barry Steinholtz <Bazza@Adelphia.Net>
- **Date:** 2002-12-24T17:42:28+00:00

```
Hi:

    For COBOL mainframe we actually had a command "Ready Trace", but it 
has since been dropped. To get around this we displayed the paragraph 
names by putting Exhibit Named "Paragraph Name" statements into the 
program. There is a switch setting that can be set at the beginning of 
your code to turn on Test Mode. Then you can code an "IF" statement 
leaving the Paragraph displays in the program for later usage.

sreesmart wrote:

>Hi Andrew...,
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: (NEWBIE) Cobol trace file

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-24T14:52:03-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<auahdn$oir$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<7a276543.0211200838.67687cdd@posting.google.com> <3e080c4f$1_7@news.onlynews.com> <3E089C84.3090501@Adelphia.Net>`

```
EXHIBIT is also removed from programs that have READY TRACE removed.

The "correct" way to do this with COBTEST (now out of service) or the newer
"Debug Tool" is described as well as the way to do this with debugging
procedures is described at:

 http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3mg10/4.1.5

if you search on
  "READY TRACE"
```

It's been a while since I did the "paragraph name" or READY 
TRACE, has DISPLAY been eliminated?  I think you could still 
use that.  Also, what about products like "EXELERATOR" <sp?> 
or something like that, or the INTERTEST product which is 
just like it.  Batch is a pain in these products unless you 
test with a small subset of the file (especially if you want 
to STEP through the program).  They work much better for 
CICS but there are batch versions.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: (NEWBIE) Cobol trace file

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2002-12-25T00:08:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E08F734.E561D332@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<7a276543.0211200838.67687cdd@posting.google.com> <3e080c4f$1_7@news.onlynews.com> <3E089C84.3090501@Adelphia.Net> <auahdn$oir$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3E08DE31.6080901@nycap.rr.com>`

```
Robert Graham wrote:
> 
> It's been a while since I did the "paragraph name" or READY
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> (snip)

I think I used READY TRACE once back in the early 1980's, and found it
not very helpful.

I liked EXHIBIT NAMED, and I used to see it in production programs
where it was used to display summary totals at end-of-job.  Obviously,
EXHIBIT NAMED was intended as a debugging tool, but like "ON 1" it was
an IBM extension that tended to get used in production programs for
non-testing purposes.  

It seems to me that IBM OS/VS COBOL also allowed an EXHIBIT (without
named) that did what DISPLAY could NOT do at that time, which was
convert binary and packed-decimal numerics into displayable format. 
The DISPLAY verb couldn't do that in OS/VS COBOL.  It wasn't until
COBOL II that DISPLAY actually worked the way it should.  

DISPLAY has not been eliminated.  
```

### MVS & CA7 Structure *.CA7.SASJOB

- **From:** "Jp-Gillard" <jp-gillard@wanadoo.fr>
- **Date:** 2002-12-24T14:16:43+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

Here in the Midlands of England Xmas Day is "warm" (8 degrees) patches
of blue sky, and not a snow flake in sight. I am a little disappointed
as I had braced myself for a cold holiday. The e-mails and phone calls
coming in from Down Under tell me that back home in the Bay of Plenty
it is 23 degress and they are all off to the beach... (they could be
lying <G>)

Eileen, where is the Glass City? 

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: Merry Christmas all...

- **From:** dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Peter E. C. Dashwood)
- **Date:** 2002-12-25T04:43:47-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3638c46.0212250443.674c3a8@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<B8aO9.50938$Nz5.1365395@twister.austin.rr.com>`

```
"Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com> wrote in message news:<B8aO9.50938$Nz5.1365395@twister.austin.rr.com>...
> This is without a doubt, the best online group of people I know of since 
> Bix turned out the lights. 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> --

You need to get out more, Paul...<G>

Seriously, thanks for the kind thoughts (and back atcha!). 

It always amazes me how you get to feel like you know people who post
regularly to a News Group.

I have my own mental image of many who post here and ...well, I just
hope I'm wrong <G>...

The good thing about it is, that despite all the noise and the
off-topic nonsense, the very action of interacting with a group from
hugely diverse backgrounds and locations, based around the single
common interest of the COBOL language, helps each of us to grow.

Over the years, interactions in this group have moved me through
almost every  possible emotion, from extreme anger to elation. None of
it has done me any harm and I am wiser for it (for example, I have
learned not to argue with Doc <G>).

The snippets of information, the humour, the insights into different
viewpoints, the cut and thrust of intense debate, winning some, losing
some, is all part of a rich pattern that beats the Hell out of sitting
in a chat room...<G>

I am (in all seriousness, now) thankful for the interactions and the
opportunity to get someone thinking about their future and their
career and maybe moving it in a better direction.

It goes way beyond COBOL Programming and technical advice.

My very warmest wishes to all of you and sincere hopes that 2003 will
be prosperous and fulfilling.

Pete.
```

## Thread: COBOL style guides — 11 msgs

### COBOL style guides

- **From:** bdhobbs18@acm.org (Bill Hobbs)
- **Date:** 2002-12-25T22:06:05-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74ca5032.0212252206.7cbb0887@posting.google.com>`

```
Anyone know of a COBOL style guide on the 'net?  The type that says
stuff like "thou shalt not use the ALTER verb under pain of being
vaporized by thy fellow programmers", "paragraph names should begin
with three digit numbers, with leading zeros, and be in ascending
order", and "indent nested code by two spaces".  Basically how to
write readable COBOL code ... or at least one organization's vision
(or hallucination) of readable.  Something like "Indian Hill C Style
and Coding Standards"
(http://www.chris-lott.org/resources/cstyle/indhill-annot.html), but
for COBOL.

Also, has anyone developed a style formatting program for COBOL like
C's indent program?  (http://www.gnu.org/software/indent/indent.html)
```

-----------------------------------------------------
1. Code must work.
2. Code must work relatively efficiently.
3. Code must be maintainable.

End of Rules.

MCM

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL style guides

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** kanoako@netnet.net (Kano Ako)
- **Date:** 2002-12-30T04:46:17-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<677e6c81.0212300446.7a25b2da@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<74ca5032.0212252206.7cbb0887@posting.google.com> <QtZO9.3510$qU5.2701090@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <I41P9.15148$p_6.1239581@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3E0D859B.37C68CB@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message news:<3E0D859B.37C68CB@Azonic.co.nz>...
> Hugh Candlin wrote:
> 
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> working programs.

Hmmm,

Programs that conform to the spec.s work! Most shops you see create a
slew of spec documents prior to coding. Once those spec docs are ready
the low level specs aer handed off to code monkeys. The code monkeys
code and unit test. The higher ups then system test. If the spec
happens to be wrong or incomplete then it is the job of unit and
system testing to catch and correct.

One of the shops I have been in have usually been sticklers about
conformance to company coding guidelines the other almost non-existant
concern for COBOL coding standards. Since I was a contractor who was
not going to be responsible for maintaining the code, I conformed.

However I agree that rules 1, 2 and 3 do take conformance with specs
and coding standards into account. I disagree that such concerns are
trivial.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL style guides

- **From:** "Ron" <NoSoy@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2002-12-27T10:12:44-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<auhu5t$8nb$1@ngspool-d02.news.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<74ca5032.0212252206.7cbb0887@posting.google.com>`

```
There used to be an option on the mainframe compiler that
performed formatting: paragraph indention, line-up IF and ELSE,
align PIC clauses etc. It existed on OS/VS COBOL for sure but
I don't know if it carried through to later versions and I
don't have access at the moment. This was very useful to
straighten out spaghetti code and at least make it more
easily readable. Anyone remember what this was called and if
it's still available?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL style guides

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2002-12-30T14:04:29-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E10C2ED.F23F6977@attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<74ca5032.0212252206.7cbb0887@posting.google.com> <auhu5t$8nb$1@ngspool-d02.news.aol.com>`

```
That was the "Lister" option, and it didn't carry forward into newer IBM
compilers.  There are many ways outside of the compiler for doing this.
The IBM CCCA and COBOL/SF programs, for example, will clean up code as
they convert and restructure it.  In addition, the ISPF Editor now
offers the HILITE option for helping us effectively view code in many
languages.

These capabilities are part of  VisualAge COBOL, too.

I'm not up to date on other vendor's offerings, but I'd be surprised if
most of them didn't have something along these lines.

Ron wrote:

> There used to be an option on the mainframe compiler that
> performed formatting: paragraph indention, line-up IF and ELSE,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> it's still available?
```

### Linking problem with Fujitsu COBOL85 software...

- **From:** Khan_Sumanta@hotmail.com (Sumanta Khan)
- **Date:** 2002-12-26T09:24:14-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7d7c0707.0212260924.4a49fa00@posting.google.com>`

```
I recently downloaded COBOL85 V30L10, Fujitsu Limited software from
www.download.com and was trying to run a small COBOL program.
Compilation went okay, but, it failed while linking ("LINK : fatal
error LNK1561: entry point must be defined"). Can anybody tell me what
I will have to do ? FYI, I have Windows Millennium. Please Cc your
reply to Khan_Sumanta@hotmail.com
Thanks in advance.
Sumanta Khan
-----------------------------
000020 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000030 PROGRAM-ID. Fickle.
000040 AUTHOR. Sumanta Khan.
000110 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
000120 DATA DIVISION.
000130 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
000140 01 RunningTotal     PIC 9(4) VALUE 150.
000190 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
000210      ADD 1 TO RunningTotal.
000220      DISPLAY "The total so far is " RunningTotal
000240      STOP RUN.
------------------------------
NMAKE : fatal error U1077: 'C:\FSC\PCOBOL32\LINK' : return code '0x68'
Stop.

Microsoft (R) 32-Bit Incremental Linker Version 3.00.5270
Copyright (C) Microsoft Corp 1992-1995. All rights reserved.

/OUT:C:\akhan\EDS\COBOL\Fuj.exe
C:\akhan\EDS\COBOL\Fujitsu.OBJ
C:\FSC\PCOBOL32\PROJECT.RES
C:\FSC\PCOBOL32\F3BICIMP.LIB
C:\FSC\PCOBOL32\LIBC.LIB
C:\FSC\PCOBOL32\KERNEL32.LIB
C:\FSC\PCOBOL32\USER32.LIB
LINK : fatal error LNK1561: entry point must be defined
Linking files ended.
Please close the window.
```

#### ↳ Re: Linking problem with Fujitsu COBOL85 software...

- **From:** Alistair Maclean <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2002-12-26T19:25:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Sr53vAAUe1C+EwB9@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<7d7c0707.0212260924.4a49fa00@posting.google.com>`

```
Would somebody tell me to get a life as I shouldn't be doing this on
Boxing Day...



The manual does not list the U1077 error but does contain the following
explanation of LNK1561:

LNK1561
Explanation:
An attempt was made to create an executable program (EXE) by compiling
the main program without the compiler option MAIN. Operator response:
Specify the compiler option MAIN before compiling the main program.


What this means is that you have not told the compiler whether the
compilation is for a standalone EXE (ie MAIN) or a called program. Stick
@MAIN in as the first line of code, eg:

000100 @OPTIONS MAIN, APOST
000200 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.

In article <7d7c0707.0212260924.4a49fa00@posting.google.com>, Sumanta
Khan <Khan_Sumanta@hotmail.com> writes
>I recently downloaded COBOL85 V30L10, Fujitsu Limited software from
>www.download.com and was trying to run a small COBOL program.
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…

#### ↳ Re: Linking problem with Fujitsu COBOL85 software...

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-12-26T14:42:25-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0212261442.31f73ed2@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<7d7c0707.0212260924.4a49fa00@posting.google.com>`

```
Khan_Sumanta@hotmail.com (Sumanta Khan) wrote 

> "LINK : fatal error LNK1561: entry point must be defined"). 

On the Fujitsu web site under 'Support' the first item highlighted in
red is:

"Most common error when first using Fujitsu COBOL:
Link: fatal error: entry point must be defined - set the compiler
options to MAIN before compiling your program."
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Linking problem with Fujitsu COBOL85 software...

- **From:** "R. Hendriks" <R.Hendriks_1@uvt.nl>
- **Date:** 2002-12-27T10:29:06+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<auh6i6$bla$1@mailnews.kub.nl>`
- **References:** `<7d7c0707.0212260924.4a49fa00@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212261442.31f73ed2@posting.google.com>`

```
Add a line before the first one:

@OPTIONS MAIN(WINMAIN) or @OPTIONS MAIN(MAIN)

that's a way of setting the program as a main program.

From the Fujitsu manuals:

Specify MAIN to use a COBOL source program as a main program;

specify NOMAIN to use a COBOL source program as a subprogram.

MAIN(WINMAIN):

Specify this when you use a console window created by COBOL

for the input/output target of an ACCEPT statement or DISPLAY

statement, and when you use a message box for the output target

of an error message at execution time.

MAIN(MAIN):

Specify this when you use the system console (command prompt

window) for the input/output target of ACCEPT statement,

DISPLAY statement and error message at execution time.

If only MAIN is specified, it is handled as MAIN(WINMAIN).





"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0212261442.31f73ed2@posting.google.com...
> Khan_Sumanta@hotmail.com (Sumanta Khan) wrote
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> options to MAIN before compiling your program."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Linking problem with Fujitsu COBOL85 software...

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-12-27T12:15:19-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0212271215.27d26e58@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<7d7c0707.0212260924.4a49fa00@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0212261442.31f73ed2@posting.google.com> <auh6i6$bla$1@mailnews.kub.nl>`

```
"R. Hendriks" <R.Hendriks_1@uvt.nl> wrote

>> I recently downloaded COBOL85 V30L10, 

> Add a line before the first one:
> 
> @OPTIONS MAIN(WINMAIN) or @OPTIONS MAIN(MAIN)

Note that for Version 3 the parameter is not valid, the only way this
should be specified is as MAIN and not as MAIN(something) which is
only appropriate to later versions.
```
