## Thread: net express and .exe code — 2 msgs

### net express and .exe code

- **From:** ronglaz6@aol.comnojunk (Ron Glazier)
- **Date:** 2002-12-26T19:01:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20021226140118.11044.00000243@mb-mc.aol.com>`

```
     I would like to find out how to get net express university edition 3.0 to
compile into  .exe code ( and .obj code).    It is creating .int code and I
have not found out how to do this.   I also have microfocus personal cobol
which also makes .int code and not so far been able to make .exe code with that
either.
  any ideas ?
   Ron 
RonGlaz6@aol.com
```

#### ↳ Re: net express and .exe code

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-12-26T14:33:34-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0212261433.438ed75@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<20021226140118.11044.00000243@mb-mc.aol.com>`

```
ronglaz6@aol.comnojunk (Ron Glazier) wrote

> I would like to find out how to get net express university edition 3.0 to
> compile into  .exe code ( and .obj code).    It is creating .int code and I
> have not found out how to do this.   I also have microfocus personal cobol
> which also makes .int code and not so far been able to make .exe code with that
> either.

These have academic licences and are designed to allow you to write,
compile and test programs on your own.  The licences specifically
disallow making programs that you can distribute and the product thus
includes no mechanisms to build such programs.

Much the same restrictions apply to Fujitsu version 3 free download
and to other student versions of compilers.
```

```
news:2320431.1040965490@dbforums.com...
>
> Speak of blowing the dust off, well I dug up an old floppy or two and
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> to this outdated sucker?

If you can handle the "no" or even a "you have got to be kidding" you can
contact the publisher:

Microsoft Corporation
One Microsoft Way
Redmond WA 98052-6399
Sales Info: 1-800-426-9400

Microsoft must have been out of the COBOL business for ten years; and even
at that, I believe "Microsoft" COBOL was a private-labelled Microfocus
product.

If you have your heart set on PC COBOL better you should start looking at
current offerings. (Or figure out how to get the job done with the more
limited version of INSPECT).
```

```
news:3E0D8737.77ADCCEA@Azonic.co.nz...

>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> what the answers should be.

Richard, you are so correct.  This sort of request is all too typical of the
"students" (predominantly foreigners) out there today.  I just finished
teaching a graduate course on a technical subject [telecomm/data comm] at a
university in our area.....never again !    Common refrains......"You can't
ask us that, you didn't give us the exact formula to use."......"Nobody said
we had to know math (simple integrals & trig identities)" ........ "Tell me
what other books I can study" (this despite the fact that the textbook had a
'Recommended Reading' section in each chapter, and there was a fine library
in the neighboring building on campus).   Little or no problem-solving
skills.  I pointed out several times that this is **graduate school**  not
trade school (and I apologize for insulting any decent trade schools out
there).

Universities are diploma mills....don't want to enforce
prerequisites....instead let instructor sort out the mess....just get those
tuition $$.

It hasn't been that long (mid 80s) since I got my degrees but I never
would've turned in such pure & unadulterated crap as I received for homework
& exams even in high school much less undergraduate or graduate school.  Not
to mention the cheating.....maybe I was naive but never remember any of that
especially in grad school.....nowadays extremely blatant and
widespread....out in the open, virtually, and when you say anything about
it, students just look at you daring you to cause a confrontation,
etc.....in my case, cheating ramapant among Orientals & Indians.  One or two
decent students, but not worth the hassles.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Interview based question please...

- **From:** Robert Graham <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-28T17:51:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E0DE6FA.800@nycap.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<5352c8fb.0212262353.61f0e037@posting.google.com> <3E0D8737.77ADCCEA@Azonic.co.nz> <Bt4P9.81101$hK4.6665795@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
I remember one year when I was looking for work that I went 
on about 30 interviews.  At each interview I was asked 
almost all the same questions.  I formulated them into what 
I called the SST questions, SORT, SEARCH and TABLES.  I 
concluded that if a person knew those answers, he could 
bluff his way into a COBOL job, not even knowing COBOL. 
(Though the bluff would be seen through soon enough.)

At one of the last interviews, for a company I wouldn't work 
for even at twice my going rate, I was asked if I had any 
questions.  I said "Yes, is there a standard list of 
questions that all interviewers have because you are the 
30th. person to ask me the same questions."  Of course I 
wasn't offered the job there, as I greatly insulted the HR 
person with my sneering tone.

So, if you want a job as a COBOL programmer, know COBOL.  It 
will be obvious that you are entry level and they will 
expect a learning curve.  But if your knowledge of COBOL is 
that "it is some kind of English-like computer language" 
then McDonald's has a place for you.

keep-it-clean wrote:
> "Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
> news:3E0D8737.77ADCCEA@Azonic.co.nz...
…[39 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Interview based question please...

- **From:** epc8@juno.com (E P Chandler)
- **Date:** 2002-12-29T11:48:17-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7f64e2ff.0212291148.2bc14ff8@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<5352c8fb.0212262353.61f0e037@posting.google.com> <3E0D8737.77ADCCEA@Azonic.co.nz> <Bt4P9.81101$hK4.6665795@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
"keep-it-clean" <keep-it-clean@worldnet.att.net> wrote:

> Richard, you are so correct.  This sort of request is all too typical of the
> "students" (predominantly foreigners) out there today.  I just finished
> teaching a graduate course on a technical subject [telecomm/data comm] at a

Unfortunately true. Recent postings to several of the technical
newsgroups in comp.lang.* have borne this out.

1. Read before posting - RTFM then read the FAQ too.
2. Do a Google search first.
3. Post what you've tried, but cut it down to the minimum needed to
explain your problem and test it.
4. Read other newsgroups too, particularly if your question involves
mixed language programming.
5. Do your own homework.
6. Don't expect me to train you to take my job.
7. If it's more than a hobby, be prepared to spend money on books and
programming tools.
8. A pair of Murach beats a pair of Stern or Fernandez.
```

<a id="t-398c57620e"></a>
## Thread: weirdness — 9 msgs

### weirdness

- **From:** CJ <exspecto2000@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-27T16:09:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PW_O9.134104$qF3.10347@sccrnsc04>`

```
im using tiny-cobol on linux.

05 CURRENT-SALES-SR                PIC S9(5)V99.

i define a numeric field there with 7 digits and two decimal places.

on the output side i do:

05 CURRENT-SALES-PR                PIC ZZ,ZZ9.99.


then i do a simple:

MOVE CURRENT-SALES-SR TO CURRENT-SALES-PR.

easy enough right?  but here's what it outputs:

12-12345     Brian Smith                   99,999.9:     9,999.9:     A
34-67890     Samuel Jackson                88,888.88     8,888.8;     B


the 99,999.9: is the field i showed you up top. why in the world would 
i get crazy output like this? the data file its reading from is defined 
as line sequential.
```

#### ↳ Re: weirdness

- **From:** CJ <exspecto2000@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-27T16:23:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<p8%O9.530346$NH2.35401@sccrnsc01>`
- **References:** `<PW_O9.134104$qF3.10347@sccrnsc04>`

```
CJ wrote:

> im using tiny-cobol on linux.
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
i fixed it by making the fields unsigned and defining it 99999V99

this stopped the strange output, but what if i needed to use a signed
field? does tiny-cobol really break on such a simple thing i wonder?

> on the output side i do:
> 
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> as line sequential.
```

- **From:** renfrewthejust@yahoo.com (Renfrew)

```
In article <p8%O9.530346$NH2.35401@sccrnsc01>, exspecto2000@yahoo.com says...
>
>CJ wrote:
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>


CJ,

You didn't say whether the source value is negative, but 
if that source value IS negative, like maybe -99999.99 or -88888.88, then 
the picture in your destination field does not make provision for dealing 
with it.  Compiler bug or not, I would try a couple of things to get around 
this.  First, expand the destination field by just one more digit, i.e., 
zzz,zz9.99 instead of zz,zz9.99 .  Then, if this does not resolve the 
problem, try ensuring that the source field is converted to a positive 
value before moving it.

If neither of these things work, grab me up by the collar and demand your 
money back.

DaveM
```

```
 C> A 34-67890     Samuel Jackson                88,888.88     8,888.8;
```

#### ↳ Re: weirdness

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2002-12-27T18:57:40-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0212271857.58cf6c93@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<PW_O9.134104$qF3.10347@sccrnsc04>`

```
It looks like the Z's are being treated as floating insertion
characters (like a $) instead of zero suppression.  I would report it
as a potential compiler defect.

For Example - if it were $$,$$$.99 - the highest value would be
$9,999.99 - because there is always ONE of the $ visible.

CJ <exspecto2000@yahoo.com> wrote in message news:<PW_O9.134104$qF3.10347@sccrnsc04>...
> im using tiny-cobol on linux.
> 
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> as line sequential.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<3E0D89A8.4F1E22B5@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<PW_O9.134104$qF3.10347@sccrnsc04>`

```
CJ wrote:
> 
> 12-12345     Brian Smith                   99,999.9:     9,999.9:     A
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> as line sequential.

While you have said that the file is line sequential, you haven't shown
us the values that the file contains or the FD for it.  This could be
caused by you mis-aligning data.

Is 99,999.99 what you expected ? or does the file contain some other
value ?  Is the value negative ?  

This is 'Tiny Cobol', what version are you using ? does the
documentation indicate that this should work ? have you reported this to
the developers ?
```

<a id="t-3e9e178194"></a>
## Thread: Help with BPXWDYN and output descriptors. — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help with BPXWDYN and output descriptors.

- **From:** reg_wilkinson@firstbs.com (Reg)
- **Date:** 2002-12-27T09:13:14-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6cad0285.0212270913.416af8b@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,

I'm working on a COBOL program that will have to write 0-n SYSOUTs. 
Each sysout will have a specific form and may require a specific print
class.

Seemed like a job for dynamic allocation!  I've successfully played
around with dynamic allocation of datasets but cannot get the OUTDES
to work.  I keep getting nonsensical return codes like 002M or 002L, I
think I've probably been through every posting on the subject and I've
even downloaded the IBM manual.


This is the sample code I've been playing around with:

        IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.                                     
        PROGRAM-ID. DYNALLOC.                                        
        DATA DIVISION.                                               
        WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.                                     
        01 BPXWDYN         PIC X(8) VALUE 'BPXWDYN'.                 
        01 ALLOC-STRING.                                             
           05 ALLOC-LENGTH PIC S9(4) BINARY VALUE 100.               
           05 ALLOC-TEXT   PIC X(100) VALUE                          
             'OUTDES(CCF001) SYSOUT(N)'.                             
                                                                     
        PROCEDURE DIVISION.                                          
                                                                     
               CALL BPXWDYN USING ALLOC-STRING                       
                                                                     
               IF RETURN-CODE NOT = ZERO                             
                                                                     
                  DISPLAY 'ALLOC FAILED, RETURN-CODE WAS ' RETURN-CODE

               ELSE 

                  DISPLAY 'ALLOC OK'                                
                                                                      
               END-IF                                                 
                                                                      
               GOBACK.                                                

Any suggestions gladly received.
Reg.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help with BPXWDYN and output descriptors.

- **From:** reg_wilkinson@firstbs.com (Reg)
- **Date:** 2002-12-30T02:23:43-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6cad0285.0212300223.5c96fd5@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<6cad0285.0212270913.416af8b@posting.google.com>`

```
reg_wilkinson@firstbs.com (Reg) wrote in message news:<6cad0285.0212270913.416af8b@posting.google.com>...
> Hi,
> 
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
> Reg.


Hi ...

Finally managed to suss this out for myself experimentation winning
out over RTFM, for future reference the following works:

        IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
        PROGRAM-ID. DYNALLOC.
        ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
        INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
        FILE-CONTROL.
            SELECT SYSOUT
                   ASSIGN TO SUB.

        DATA DIVISION.
        FILE SECTION.
        FD SYSOUT
           RECORDING MODE IS F
           BLOCK CONTAINS 0 RECORDS
           LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD.
        01 OUTREC          PIC X(133).

        WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
        01 BPXWDYN         PIC X(8) VALUE 'BPXWDYN'.
        01 ALLOC-STRING.
           05 ALLOC-LENGTH PIC S9(4) BINARY VALUE 100.
           05 ALLOC-TEXT   PIC X(100) VALUE
           'ALLOC DD(SUB) SYSOUT(6) FORMS(1234)'.
                                                                      
        01 PRINT-LINE      PIC X(133)       VALUE
           ' TEST PRINT - CLASS 6 FORMS 1234'.
                                                                      
        PROCEDURE DIVISION.
                                                                      
               CALL BPXWDYN USING ALLOC-STRING

               IF RETURN-CODE NOT = ZERO
                                                                      
                DISPLAY 'ALLOC FAILED, RETURN-CODE WAS ' RETURN-CODE

                ELSE DISPLAY 'ALLOC OK'

                    PERFORM B-PRINT

                END-IF

                GOBACK.

         B-PRINT SECTION.

                OPEN OUTPUT SYSOUT

                MOVE PRINT-LINE TO OUTREC

                WRITE OUTREC
        
                CLOSE SYSOUT

                EXIT.

Cheers,
Reg.
```

<a id="t-f88e7d55ec"></a>
## Thread: weirdness — 3 this month / 6 total

### Re: weirdness

- **From:** David Essex <dessex@arvotek.net>
- **Date:** 2002-12-28T02:15:05-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e0d50ad_2@news.eol.ca>`

```
CJ wrote:

 >> Iï¿½m using TinyCOBOL on Linux.
 >>
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
 >> reading from is defined as line sequential.

The problem here may be how the input data is defined and stored.

For signed numeric data, TC uses the last digit to store the sign. The
symbol '}' is zero, 'A' to 'I' is 1 to 9, and 'J' to 'R' is -1 to -9.

If your input data is stored in an unsigned format and your program 
reads it in as a signed, then you will run into problems.
```

#### ↳ Re: weirdness

- **From:** CJ <exspecto2000@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-28T12:27:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<DMgP9.339477$pN3.24304@sccrnsc03>`
- **References:** `<3e0d50ad_2@news.eol.ca>`

```
that was indeed the problem. could you point me to documentation which 
describes how to enter signed data into an ascii file with a regular text 
editor?

David Essex wrote:

> The problem here may be how the input data is defined and stored.
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> reads it in as a signed, then you will run into problems.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: weirdness

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-28T17:59:37-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<auldte$bv6$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3e0d50ad_2@news.eol.ca> <DMgP9.339477$pN3.24304@sccrnsc03>`

```
The "best" way to enter signed data into an "ASCII" file with a normal
editor is to use + and - signs and define the COBOL data with "sign is
separate" (leading or trailing).  Use of an "internal" sign with COBOL data
tends to be most useful in COBOL-only applications (no "normal editor" or
printer input/output).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: abandoning COBOL !!!

- **From:** "Alister Munro" <alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2002-12-29T03:59:47
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aulrvk$jol$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<TajP9.83255$pe.2956158@news2.east.cox.net> <ML-dnaE1xeCUu5OjXTWcog@giganews.com>`

```
Other than vendor specific/3rd party add-ons; What specific commands
have been added to the COBOL language in the past decade ? How
has the average terminal in the average office changed within the past
decade ? End users now expect a feature rich environment which
COBOL on its own fails to deliver forcing the COBOL programmer
into using another product / language to fill in the gaps. Over time more
and more gaps are filled and you end up asking yourself why are you
still using COBOL ?.

I am sure COBOL will still be used for many years to come especially
within large cooperates but being self-employed and dealing with small
to medium sized companies allowing me to choose the best product for
the job without having to follow some company policy.

Personally, I just out-grew COBOL in favour of C although I suspect
it will be many years before I remove it from the my hard disk. I have
enjoyed 15+ years coding in COBOL and wouldn't mind a few more
if the language were expanded to cater for the modern environments.

"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:ML-dnaE1xeCUu5OjXTWcog@giganews.com...
>
> "John Smith" <no_name@no_domain.no> wrote in message

programming
> > lanaguges?

> Nothing.
>

to
> switch?"

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: abandoning COBOL !!!

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2002-12-29T07:48:37-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0212290748.17c07fb3@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<TajP9.83255$pe.2956158@news2.east.cox.net> <ML-dnaE1xeCUu5OjXTWcog@giganews.com> <aulrvk$jol$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Alister, 

While I admit that between the years 1989 and 2002 there were not
substantial changes to the COBOL standard - the COBOL vendors did not
sit still.  They continued to adapt to the current environment of the
day.  While an instantaneously adapting standard might appear to be a
good thing, it means nothing if it's not implemented.  There is
nothing inherent in C that makes it a better choice.  There's no GUI
in C, there's no indexed files, there's no embedded SQL.... It's a
spurious argument at best.  EXTERNAL forces made C a better choice for
Windows API work.  Why?  Because the API was documented for C!  Were
the API documented for COBOL, then COBOL would have been the better
choice for API work.  Nothing at all to do with any weakness in COBOL!

"Alister Munro" <alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk> wrote in message news:<aulrvk$jol$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk>...
> Other than vendor specific/3rd party add-ons; What specific commands
> have been added to the COBOL language in the past decade ? How
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: abandoning COBOL !!!

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-12-29T18:20:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<r1HP9.3958$qU5.3035715@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<TajP9.83255$pe.2956158@news2.east.cox.net> <ML-dnaE1xeCUu5OjXTWcog@giganews.com> <aulrvk$jol$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk> <bfdfc3e8.0212290748.17c07fb3@posting.google.com>`

```
"Thane Hubbell" <thaneh@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:bfdfc3e8.0212290748.17c07fb3@posting.google.com...
>  EXTERNAL forces made C a better choice for
> Windows API work.  Why?  Because the API was documented for C!  Were
> the API documented for COBOL, then COBOL would have been the better
> choice for API work.  Nothing at all to do with any weakness in COBOL!

The Windows API is documented, period. Maybe they use a lot of 'C' examples,
but MSDN explains all the datatypes and calling conventions quite
thoroughly. Even the OO stuff goes to great pains to explain the properties
and methods available for COM objects.

If one cannot work with the MS Win/32 API documentation for use with COBOL
programs, perhaps one should consider a vocation other than computer
programming.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: abandoning COBOL !!!

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-12-29T18:26:20
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e0f3cdb_5@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<TajP9.83255$pe.2956158@news2.east.cox.net> <ML-dnaE1xeCUu5OjXTWcog@giganews.com> <aulrvk$jol$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk> <bfdfc3e8.0212290748.17c07fb3@posting.google.com> <r1HP9.3958$qU5.3035715@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```

Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message
news:r1HP9.3958$qU5.3035715@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com...
> "Thane Hubbell" <thaneh@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
> news:bfdfc3e8.0212290748.17c07fb3@posting.google.com...
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> The Windows API is documented, period. Maybe they use a lot of 'C'
examples,
> but MSDN explains all the datatypes and calling conventions quite
> thoroughly. Even the OO stuff goes to great pains to explain the
properties
> and methods available for COM objects.
>
As I can't justify subscribing to MSDN, I use the API documentation that is
available on the Web. It is all documented with VB examples. Nowadays, I
have picked up enough VB to be able to use the API if I want to. It is
sometimes frustrating "translating" VB prototypes into COBOL, but if you
want to do something badly enough it is usually achievable (sometimes with a
little help from my friends...<G>).

I think it would be very useful if one of the COBOL vendors offered a Win32
API guide with COBOL examples. I have seen one on the Web but was
disappointed in the standard of it, and found a number of errors that
stopped it working. (NO, I am not going to name it... (I understand the
author is reviewing it) but I would strongly advise you all not to pay money
for such a work unless you have actually tried it first...)

I'd be interested to see the documentation on the COM objects; I use
whatever I can get my hands on in this regard.

> If one cannot work with the MS Win/32 API documentation for use with COBOL
> programs, perhaps one should consider a vocation other than computer
> programming.
>
I endorse this sentiment (if you call yourself a "computer programmer" you
should be able to program computers...<G>)  but I think it is a bit hard
here, Michael. COBOL has been the poor relation in terms of MS documentation
ever since they withdrew support for MS COBOL (re-badged MicroFocus). They
made it abundantly clear that COBOL was not on their list of suitable
languages. It is interesting to see how this has changed a bit since their
working partnerships with both Fujitsu and MicroFocus. I wonder how much of
this is an effort to show how "universal" the .NET product is, and how much
is genuine recognition that COBOL is of value?

I guess time will tell...

Pete.
```

```
news:3e0f3cdb_5@Usenet.com...

is
> available on the Web...

You can get the platform SDK on CD for $9.95 (US), at least from the US you
can. This has all the doc for the API calls, but you don't get all the
Knowledge Base or .NET stuff. (total: about 1.8 Gb). You can also download
it from the MS site, but it's either 160 or 200 MEGAbytes depending on to
whom you listen. The CD option has got to be worth a look for you; poke
around at the MSDN site.

I just installed the "new improved" MSDN library on my local system last
week and have decided I shall not be renewing this subscription.  (The
'Library' subscription is $114.00/year).

The "new, improved library" is slower than molasses in January (July in New
Zealand!), has way too much bias to "COM" and ".NET" (i.e., not all the
'pure' API calls appear in the index, although you 'can' get to them via
other methods), and now if you want to 'bookmark' your favorite reference
pages, those entries go right into Internet Explorer's "favorites," too!
(Just what I need: an I/E link to "listview control extended window style
reference").


MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ COBOL and API's (was: abandoning COBOL !!!

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-29T12:52:35-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aung9o$s94$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<TajP9.83255$pe.2956158@news2.east.cox.net> <ML-dnaE1xeCUu5OjXTWcog@giganews.com> <aulrvk$jol$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk> <bfdfc3e8.0212290748.17c07fb3@posting.google.com> <r1HP9.3958$qU5.3035715@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```
*Actually* one of the major (in my opinion) changes in the 2002 Standard is
"C-ification" i.e. the ability to create COBOL code working with all (most?)
of the documented API's that "assume" a C-type environment.  Although many
(most?) vendors provide some or all of the following extensions to their '85
Standard compilers, I view the following enhancements in the 2002 Standard
as FINALLY allowing ANSI/ISO conforming COBOL to "play" in common "modern"
API environments:

 - CALL by VALUE (as well as CONTENT and REFERENCE)
 - RETURNING phrase (on CALL or Procedure Division)
 - Pointer and procedure pointer data types
 - non-intrinsic function "calls"
 - recursion
 - "true" binary fields (i.e. fields not truncated to mod-10/decimal values)

Without these (and some other)  "language" features, I disagree with Michael
that '85 Standard (or '89 Standard) COBOL can "easily" be used to implement
many/most of the API's that exist for Windows or other API-documented
interfaces.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: abandoning COBOL !!!

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2002-12-29T19:53:46-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0212291953.238716a@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<TajP9.83255$pe.2956158@news2.east.cox.net> <ML-dnaE1xeCUu5OjXTWcog@giganews.com> <aulrvk$jol$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk> <bfdfc3e8.0212290748.17c07fb3@posting.google.com> <r1HP9.3958$qU5.3035715@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```
I have a MSDN subscription and do use it regularly for COBOL calls to
the API.  HOWEVER - it takes C knowledge to make it work.  It really
does.  Many of the "values" documented in the API are only declared as
hex values in the C headers.  In order to find the root value of
something like: WMMinimized (made up name) you ultimately have to find
the C header file.  Were this documented easier for COBOL in the
BEGINNING - it would have meant an easier time for COBOL programmers
using the API.

"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message news:<r1HP9.3958$qU5.3035715@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>...
```

```
> the API.  HOWEVER - it takes C knowledge to make it work.  It really
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> using the API.

You can get a reasonably current set of 'C' header files by downloading the
now-free Borland C++ v 5.5  compiler from the Borland web site.

At least, that's what I did. I don't program in C, but I can understand..
#define WM_RBUTTONDOWN                  0x0204
#define WM_RBUTTONUP                    0x0205
#define WM_RBUTTONDBLCLK                0x0206
#define WM_MBUTTONDOWN                  0x0207
#define WM_MBUTTONUP                    0x0208
#define WM_MBUTTONDBLCLK                0x0209

.. plenty good enough.

The *.h files "Include" directory supplied with that compiler is 966 files,
30.2 Mb. Thank goodness for "find files containing text ...."

Hmmm, I have a program I wrote to build BASIC-syntax include files using
these as input... maybe I should do a COBOL-syntax output version....what
would you suggest...in copylib (no 01 level) format, maybe like...

 * constants for window messages..
    05 WM-RBUTTONDOWN     PIC s9(9) USAGE BINARY VALUE x'0204'.
    05 WM-RBUTTONUP           PIC s9(9) USAGE BINARY VALUE x'0205'.
    05 WM-RBUTTONDBLCLK   PIC s9(9) USAGE BINARY VALUE x'0206'.
   .. etc....

... or maybe convert the 'value' clauses to decimal?

Hmmm.... "No 01-level"  looks like a loser.. hell, I could just use "01
FILLER " I suppose. Also  there's an awful lot of conditional compilation
stuff in there, too I'd have to figure what to do with.

Looking at the license VERY quickly, it appears this is a permitted use as
long as I retain the original Microsoft copyright notice in the resulting
"*.cob" or "*.cbl" or "*.cpy" file...

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: abandoning COBOL !!!

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2002-12-30T11:01:25-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0212301101.68e3d690@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<TajP9.83255$pe.2956158@news2.east.cox.net> <ML-dnaE1xeCUu5OjXTWcog@giganews.com> <aulrvk$jol$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk> <bfdfc3e8.0212290748.17c07fb3@posting.google.com> <r1HP9.3958$qU5.3035715@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <bfdfc3e8.0212291953.238716a@posting.google.com> <0IXP9.4212$qU5.3178963@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```
It's a worthwhile project, but note:

ms - h'xxxxxx' is different from x'xxxxxx'

h'xxxxxx' needs bytes reversed for endian while x'xxxxxx' doesn't.

There are other subtlties - like which should be 9(9) comp-5 and which
should be s9(9) comp-5 - and s9(4) 9(4) comp-5 and some compilers use
comp-1 etc etc etc.... Also some won't accept Pic s9(4) comp-5 value
32764.  Some accept and truncate.

Because of this variety (that's what Usage BINARY-SHORT cures etc) I
have taken to wrapping API calls in an ActiveX control and utilizing
them that way - in a compiler independent fashion.  Not as fast as
direct invocation (OLE overhead and all).

Still the project is worthwhile.
```

#### ↳ Re: abandoning COBOL !!!

- **From:** "D De Villiers" <~ddevilliers999@lando.co.za>
- **Date:** 2002-12-29T13:48:16+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<auq053$h7c$5@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net>`
- **References:** `<TajP9.83255$pe.2956158@news2.east.cox.net>`

```
> What prevent companies from abandoning COBOL in favor of other programming
> lanaguges?

There's still * alot * of companies running existing COBOL programs on there
mainframes etc. For example: Here in South Africa the mainframe use by all
banks (Standard Bank, ABSA Blank etc) is still in COBOL. Because of the huge
$$$$ cost involve its impossible to rewrite / port it over to a new language
(like C++).

Remember that about 30 years ago (around 1970/80) the only populate
languages used in business was COBOL and PL/I. Do you guys remember? From
1998 both COBOL & PL/I programmers where in a high demand because of the Y2K
bug.

* This is to show, that even if COBOL is older than my grandma its here to
stay ! *

Just my 2 cents ! :-)

Kind Regards,

Lennie De Villiers

EMail: ddevilliers@lando.co.za
Web: www.lando.co.za/Lennie
CV/Resume: www.lando.co.za/Lennie/CV.htm

"Deliver yesterday, code today, think tomorrow. "
```

<a id="t-4ff1761b87"></a>
## Thread: INITIALIZE vs DEPENDING — 2 this month / 12 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### INITIALIZE vs DEPENDING

- **From:** Edward Reid <edwardreid@spamcop.net>
- **Date:** 2002-12-28T11:08:35-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.BA3336B203514EAA1849E120@news-east.usenetserver.com>`

```
The question: does the DEPENDING restriction on INITIALIZE apply only 
to unsubscripted references or to all references? (In ANSI85 COBOL.)

Example:

 identification division.
 program-id. init-sub.
 data division.
 working-storage section.
 1 the-sub     pic 99.
 1 the-table.
   2 the-item pic x occurs 0 to 30 depending on the-sub.
 procedure division.
 begin-prog.
     initialize the-item of the-table (the-sub)
     initialize the-item
     .

Now, I understand that the second INITIALIZE is invalid, per the 
standard at 6.17.3(4): "The description of the data item referenced by 
identifier-1 or any items subordinate to identifier-1 may not contain 
the DEPENDING phrase of the OCCRUS clause."

My question is about the first INITIALIZE. The Unisys NX COBOL85 
compiler rejects it, but they have had a whole nest of bugs in 
INITIALIZE (some of which I reported, some others reported), so I don't 
trust that as an authority.

The problem is in the part that says "the data item referenced by 
identifier-1" when identifier-1 is not a simple data item reference. 
When I go back to section 4.3.8.4, the definition of an identifier, an 
identifier includes a leading data-name, optional qualification, 
optional subscript(s), and optional reference modification. This can 
involve numerous data-names. Which is "the data-name referenced by 
identifier-1"?

And if indeed this is prohibited, why? I understand why the DEPENDING 
restriction, to make compilation simpler. (I don't see any ambiguity 
that results from DEPENDING, just complications.) But this reason would 
not seem to apply when the data item with DEPENDING is fully 
subscripted. Is this just an oversight, or is there a good reason?

Edward Reid
```

#### ↳ Re: INITIALIZE vs DEPENDING

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-28T16:21:45-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<UMSdnSZAPe5xvpOjXTWc3g@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<01HW.BA3336B203514EAA1849E120@news-east.usenetserver.com>`

```

"Edward Reid" <edwardreid@spamcop.net> wrote in message
news:01HW.BA3336B203514EAA1849E120@news-east.usenetserver.com...
> The question: does the DEPENDING restriction on INITIALIZE apply only
> to unsubscripted references or to all references? (In ANSI85 COBOL.)
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
> subscripted. Is this just an oversight, or is there a good reason?

Must be a 'good reason' (indolence?) because my compiler (Fujitsu 6) not
only does not flag

   initialize the-item of the-table(the-sub)

as an error, it executes the resulting code as expected..
```

- **Message-ID:** `<7f64e2ff.0212282103.7663b515@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3E0DD3ED.4EE1BF64@snajdr.de> <92qP9.3132$FF4.190414@newsb.telia.net>`

```
"Fim W stberg" <fim.wastberg@fimator.se> wrote in message news:<92qP9.3132$FF4.190414@newsb.telia.net>...
> "vaclav snajdr" <vsn@snajdr.de> skrev i meddelandet
> news:3E0DD3ED.4EE1BF64@snajdr.de...
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Fim Wï¿½stberg

Kobol translates COBOL to C, then uses gcc to compile the C. Setting
things up from the command line is not that bad if you are familiar
with gcc. Also the IDE echoes its command lines into an output window.

Here's the outline of what works under Windows (95) to compile a
single source file:

1. install cygwin to c:\cygwin
2. add c:\cygwin\bin to the path
3. copy these files to the working directory from Kobol

cob2c.exe
cobol2gcc.h
libcompsuppwin32.a
cygwin1.dll

4. add the source file + batch files
5. translate the source text to line-feed delimited text
6. set the "prog" environment variable to the program base name, e.g.
foo
7. execute c1.bat, c2.bat and l.bat

c1.bat:
cob2c -M %prog%.cbl

c2.bat:
g++ -c -Ic:/cygwin/usr/include -Ic:/cygwin/usr/include/g++-3
-o %prog%.o %prog%.cc

l.bat:
g++ -s -o %prog% %prog%.o -L. -lcompsuppwin32 -Lc:/cygwin/lib
-Lc:/cygwin/lib/w32api

Note: c2.bat and l.bat contain a single line of text each

the switches do the following

M = set MAIN file
c = compile only
I = include directory
o = output file
s = strip debug info from exe
L = link to libraries in
l = link to library

c1: foo.cbl -> foo.cc + *.h
c2: foo.o   -> foo.o
l:  foo.o   -> foo.exe
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Kobol

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-12-30T13:17:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0IXP9.4211$qU5.3178656@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<3E0DD3ED.4EE1BF64@snajdr.de> <KH0x0LAmE5D+EwI1@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```
"Alistair Maclean" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message
news:KH0x0LAmE5D+EwI1@ld50macca.demon.co.uk...
> So somebody took my suggestion of changing the C in Cobol into a K, in
> order to make it appear kool to the peeps, seriously. Do I have any
> copyright on the name?

No, because a brand name is not copyrightable.

You might have a claim on a trademark IF... your first public use of the
term identified KOBOL as a trademark AND you used "KOBOL(tm)" as an
adjective.

If used as a noun, it might qualify as a service mark, if you had offered it
as a service under that brand name.

But I don't believe any of these conditions exist, so instead of "C," "TM"
or "SM" the only acronym or abbreviation which applies is "SOL."

MCM
```

<a id="t-ba02692642"></a>
## Thread: COBOL Components — 9 this month / 11 total

### COBOL Components

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-12-29T00:54:30
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e0e44fa_5@Usenet.com>`

```
I am considering a new venture in the coming year and would like to know if
anybody is using, or is capable of using, OO COBOL to make ActiveX (COM)
components. I am also interested in people who have .asp knowledge with
VBScript or J(ava)Script.(Especially if you have built components for web
servers with these tools...)

Components that can run as server side code (contain no user interface) are
of particular interest, but so are some that have windows msgbox interface.

If anyone is using SOAP or .NET COBOL to create Web Services, that would
also be of interest.

I am particularly  interested to trade components and know how, and possibly
establish a pool of business components (probably written in OO COBOL)
suitable for insertion in Web Pages and/or Windows desktop systems, along
with a group of contacts (besides myself <G>) who can build such stuff.

Around 2 years ago I posted a similar message and received no replies. I'm
kind of hoping things have moved on and there are now some people who could
be interested, or who simply love COBOL enough to do something meaningful
with it <G>. It gets lonely out here on the frontier...talk to me!

Pete.







 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

```
I have a generic routine for interfacing weigh scales that could be 
turned into such an object.  Never done it, but I do not think it would 
take long.  The only portion of the screen interface that would be 
required would be the setup (it includes all RS-232-C drivers required).

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Components

- **From:** jchafin@cgwinc.com (John Chafin)
- **Date:** 2002-12-29T18:13:31-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7c63c745.0212291813.619d4bbf@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3e0e44fa_5@Usenet.com> <3E0E8B4B.20500@Sympatico.ca> <3e0f3cd8$1_5@Usenet.com>`

```
Pete,

While developing our software product line, my company has developed a
huge number of COBOL DLLs that use the standard windows API
convention. These DLLS are accessed by COBOL, VB, JAVA and ASP modules
today. Our thoughts were to turn them into serverside com objects at
some point in the future. We just haven't had the incentive to do it
yet. One thought I had was to develop a server side and client side
com wrapper that would trap all client side API calls, do the
appropriate call to the server side com wrapper, which would then call
the appropriate DLL entry point and then vice-versa. How difficult do
you think this would be? Can you suggest an alternative approach
besides rewriting all the DLLs?

We have DLLs that do UPS, FEDEX, Airborne and LTL carrier shipping. We
have DLLs that talk to scales and barcode printers. we have DLLs that
completely support web based sales orders, including credit cards and
electronic checks. All you have to build is the UI. I have many more
that I could list.

Could what we've done possibly fit what you are looking for?

John
```

```
Thane Hubbell <thaneh@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:bfdfc3e8.0212291948.7db4fe57@posting.google.com...
> Pete,
>
> I have an interest in this - on several fronts.  What I have NOT come

No, that is always a problem <G>.... (However, it is not an insurmountable
one and I have some ideas for the new year.)

At this stage I am "testing the water" to see if there is any kind of
resource pool for COBOL components that could be raised from within the
group.

The response has been much better than it was the last time I thought about
doing this...

Obviously, I have a number of components which I would be prepared to
contribute in exchange for others, but what is more important, is that we
could have an exchange of ideas and knowledge. As the majority of CLC is not
into OO and components, it would be a bit tiresome to do this through the
NG. Also, if we decide to make it a commercial venture we might not need for
it to be public.

> My time is spent
> mostly on things I get paid for at present! However, this particular
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yes, I think we are all trying to make a living at the moment <G>.

The thought I had was that if a group of us got together and determined that
we had some capability, it would be quite possible to market that.  (I would
stress that the marketing aspect was not my primary motivation in this <G>.)

The idea I have in mind is a "Component Forge" targeted specifically at
Commercial installations. It would probably need to be Web Oriented (hence,
ASP, .NET etc.) but not necessarily.

I'll see how many responses or "notifications of interest" are received here
by Friday this week, then I'll circulate something to everybody and we can
see where it goes from there.

I was hoping you'd respond as I know you're into very much the same stuff as
me. (In fact, it was you who stimulated some of my interest <G>)

Cheers,

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Components

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2002-12-31T07:13:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c2b09b$f6e334e0$408bf243@chottel>`
- **References:** `<3e0e44fa_5@Usenet.com> <bfdfc3e8.0212291948.7db4fe57@posting.google.com> <3e104dd2_6@Usenet.com>`

```
I am definitely interested in learning this stuff and I think I am capable.
 Downside is I do not have anything concrete to contribute at this point. 
I do have some books on these topics but most of them have not been read
yet.

I have been concentrating  on learning OO concepts and I feel comfortable
with most of them them but I have not used them enough to feel confortable
with OO design principles.  I can still get thrown off by some comments or
new ideas in a new language.  For instance the other day someone mentioned
that OO prog/design is bottom up as opposed to top-down.  I am not certain
if this is true or not.  It seems to me that for the most part OO
technology is a "forced" repackaging of data and functions. Various
languages provide varying support for OO resulting in in different program
organizations. The key concept seems to be abstract data types/user defined
data types. Also depending on what you are reading there is usually a
"laundary list" of concepts inculed in OO: abstraction, encapuslation,
overloading, polymorphism, etc depending upon what the various languages
support.

If a traditional structured program is viewed via a hierarchical structure
chart then an OO program seems pretty similiar except you find objects at
many of the nodes instead of functional paragraphs. The functions within
each object still seem pretty procedural to me and the high level logic
that gets everything started is often procedural code hiding under a
generic object name ending in "Manager". I guess what I am trying to say is
it still seems pretty procedural and not as revolutionary as is often
stated.  I post this at the risk of seeming a little naive about objects
and I admit I am lacking in practical experience.

Sorry, I think I lost my focus/purpose for this post a little. 

Despite all of this I agree that the advantage of OO seems to be in
leveraging components and I want to learn more about them.  I am leery of
M$ only solutions while admitting they are worth learning about.

The amount of time I can devote is unclear at this point as I am being
absorbed into the new Dept. of Homeland Security and whatever new demands
that will bring is unknown at this point.  I seem to learn better when I
have a specfic project or see a pressing need coming soon in the future.
This helps concentrate by focus on what I need to learn.  I think this
comes not from being lazy but from learning many things that then were not
used.  If I don't use it I seem to lose it. In my current job I don't see
myself being called on to use this technology anytime soon, but I want to
be ready when the time comes. I should say if it comes because ther is
distinct possibility I may retire before that time comes.
```

```
have to consider myself a "junior programmer" or even "entry

been spending time learning Java 2, HTML and XML.  Java

a job at McDonalds.

Also, my knowledge of COBOL in general is extensive (pushing 
28 years), as is my knowledge of various businesses - such 
as insurance, accounting (as a programmer, not as a number 
cruncher), and certain manufacturing functions like 
warehouse distribution and inventory.

So... Let me know.  Perhaps when it is more fleshed out we 
will find a fit for me.

Robert
```

- **Message-ID:** `<20021228202633.01967.00000038@mb-df.aol.com>`

```
       identification division.
       program-id.     table1.
       author.         Ron Glazier.
       installation.   Los Angeles.
       date-written.   December 15, 2002.
       date-compiled.
       security.       non-classified.
      * remarks        p370.
      *********************************************************
      *
      * counting number of records in a file.
      *
      *
      *********************************************************

       environment division.
       input-output section.
       file-control.
           select catalog-file assign to "c:\catalog.dat"
           organization is line sequential.

           select out-file assign to "c:\out.dat"
           organization is line sequential.

       fd  catalog-file
           label records are standard.
       01  catalog-record.
               05 master-catalog-number            pic x(5).
               05 rest-of-record                   pic x(5).

       fd out-file
          label records are standard.
       01 out-record.
               05 catalog-num                      pic x(5).
               05 rest-of-rec                      pic x(5).


       working-storage section.
       01 more-data-remains-flag  pic x(3) value "yes".
           88 no-more-data-remains         value "no ".
           88 more-data-remains            value "yes".

       01 counter1             pic 9(6)    value zeroes.
       01 counter2             pic 9(6)    value zeroes.




       procedure division.
       a000-main.
           display " start of program table1.cbl ".
           open input catalog-file.
           open output out-file.
           move zero to counter1.
           move zero to counter2.

           read catalog-file
                   at end move "no " to more-data-remains-flag.

           move master-catalog-number to catalog-num.
           move rest-of-record to rest-of-rec.
           write out-record.

           add 1 to counter1.
           display " counter1 = " counter1.

           perform b000-process-record
               until no-more-data-remains.


           close catalog-file.
           close out-file.
           display " end of program table1 ".
           stop run.


       b000-process-record.
           read catalog-file
               at end move "no " to more-data-remains-flag.


           move master-catalog-number to catalog-num.
           move rest-of-record to rest-of-rec.
           write out-record.
           add 1 to counter1.


           if more-data-remains
               display " counter1 = " counter1.

/////////////////////////////////////
 could not get this one to compile with no errors.
 Ron
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: compile with Kobol was not error free

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-12-31T12:34:13-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0212311234.64dafa86@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<aumitg$qie$1@news5.svr.pol.co.uk> <20021230234647.06965.00000430@mb-cj.aol.com>`

```
ronglaz6@aol.comnojunk (Ron Glazier) wrote

> error message was
>    unknown option '3'
>  when using Kobol.

At last some useful indication of what the problem is.

The problem has nothing at all to do with your Cobol code. This is a
problem with Kobol where the IDE invokes the cob2c 'compiler' with an
option '-3' and the cob2c program doesn't know what this is.

There should be a version that does recognise the -3 option in the
~/kobol/bin directory:

> [riplin@Redhat bin]$ cob2c_mpe
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>   -W     (give all warnings)

The cob2c demo version has same date and time and is only a few bytes
different in size and doesn't display the -3 option in the help - it
also fails when it is passed the -3 option as you have found.

Rename the cob2c_mpe to cob2c (after removing original cob2c) and it
should work.

Somebody needs to tell TheKompany.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Net Express Printing

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2002-12-30T18:12:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-C6I82dZzOcdC@h24-82-204-17.wp.shawcable.net>`
- **References:** `<MeQP9.161038$qF3.11704@sccrnsc04> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-NeUZrZmDY3Qg@h24-82-204-17.wp.shawcable.net> <RaRP9.556469$QZ.80190@sccrnsc02>`

```
IIRC your best way to get the selection of a particular printer is to 
set the "oprn command" variable to force selection of a printer

01    open-command pic x(4) comp-x.

                 move 4 to open-command
                  call "pc_printer_open" using
                       os2-printer-handle
                       os2-document-title
                       open-command
                       by value 0 size 4
                       returning return-status

Note, the "printer handle" is a binary pointer to the printer, not 
something like "lpt1"

You can also obtain the device context for the printer and use that to
issue an API command

Lorne
```

```
As an extension to Lorne's suggestion there are three alternatives for you.

1. Crystal Reports - just had it confirmed from two N/E users that they use this
for
preference. On the plus side it handles ODBC access to DB Tables. All comments
one reads about Crystal appear 'positive'.

2. OLE - MS Word - one which I'm giving consideration to. Go to the Micro Focus
site and read White Papers - Wayne Rippen on using MS Word, (and Excel for
producing graphs). As backup to this the IDE contains a menu item for 'Tools"
---> Type Library Assistant. You can extract a list of data types and all
methods associated with using MS Word. (It's comprehensive - contains 20,906
lines of source !). My own inclination would be to wrap the above code in a new
class." MS Word" and do invokes from business logic to this class.

3. PC_PRINT - works fine for me, (changing font styles, sizes etc.), as I'm not
into traditional accounting sub controls. If you want a sample, e-mail me
privately. Note, the Word approach above, like Crystal, allows you to view
before printing. PC_PRINT doesn't have this feature.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Net Express Printing

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2002-12-31T17:39:36-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0212311739.c836ebc@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<MeQP9.161038$qF3.11704@sccrnsc04> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-NeUZrZmDY3Qg@h24-82-204-17.wp.shawcable.net> <JIRP9.557967$NH2.37869@sccrnsc01> <3E101D79.4020405@Sympatico.ca> <qp%P9.166735$qF3.12238@sccrnsc04>`

```
"Stan A" <Stan nospam Archer@hotmail.com> wrote

> Still nothing tells me (stupid me or humble me) how to define the 
> printer.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Now I could be thick here but is X(4) not the length of say 'lpt1' ?

The 'Printer Handle' is a 4 byte hardware address that will point to a
structure held in memory somewhere.  It is set to the required value
when the printer is opened and this value must be correct for all
subsequent actions.  ie it has nothing to do with any specific name
and it must not be set to any value by the programmer (except possibly
low-values after closing the printer).
```

<a id="t-7cafe83273"></a>
## Thread: Periods, Scope delimiters (with or without IDMS) — 13 this month / 32 total

### Periods, Scope delimiters (with or without IDMS)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-30T11:14:52-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aupuue$vkp$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Starting a new thread (as a follow-up on another thread),

Some comments on WHY (personally) I would suggest a style with minimal
periods and Next Sentence phrases:

1) "Next Sentences" is in the "archaic" category in the 2002 COBOL Standard.
Although it won't be SOON, it is certainly possible that COBOL code that you
write today *may* still be in use at some time in the future when "next
sentence" is *not* a portable feature to all compilers.

2) "Next Sentence" with "END-IF" is an extension today (and has been since
'85 when "END-IF" was first introduced).  Therefore, the use of  "next
sentence" in some (not all) constructs is already NOT portable to all
conforming ('85 much less 2002 Standard) compilers.  This may well be a
"dropped" extension in some future compiler provided by vendors who support
it today.

3) Whether working with printed or "viewed on a screen" source code, a
period (aka "full stop") is relatively easy to miss.  This (traditionally)
was/is one of the biggest problems in maintaining code that requires KNOWING
where the "next period is".

4) There are STILL many programmers who *think* that a "Next Sentence" *is*
the same thing as a "continue" with an End-IF (or End-Search).  "Fixing"
this is "only" a matter of training, but is a constant training issue - as
long as there are new COBOL programmers or new programmers to a "shop" that
uses "Next Sentence".

5) Periods in the middle of paragraphs (or sections - if that is your
preference) *do* change logic flow of Next Sentence phrases.  Such "next
sentence" phrases *may* be far away from both the original and any "new"
periods - in my experience this is a maintenance problem.  (it certainly
WOULD be a problem if you had a precompiler inserting either "Next Sentence"
phrases or "periods" where the maintenance programmer doesn't see them -
either before or after added new code.)

    ***

None of the reasons listed above are sufficient (IMHO) to do a "major"
rewrite of an existing/functioning program or application.  On the other
hand, I *do* view them as sufficient reasons to ENCOURAGE (not require) a
change to a "single period per paragraph style of coding with NO "next
sentences" included in new programs and new "major" insertions of code/logic
in existing programs.
```

#### ↳ Re: Periods, Scope delimiters (with or without IDMS)

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-12-30T19:58:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<auq8i9$sb$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<aupuue$vkp$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

On 30-Dec-2002, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:

> None of the reasons listed above are sufficient (IMHO) to do a "major"
> rewrite of an existing/functioning program or application.  On the other
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> in existing programs.

I would rather have a standard to eliminate NEXT SENTENCE than one to eliminate
periods.   It sounds as though you would as well (when you mention how NEXT
SENTENCE within an IF, END-IF is an extension.   On the other hand, you seem to
think that it is harder to train a programmer to stop using NEXT SENTENCE than
it is to train him not to use periods.   My thought is to the contrary.

You also think that periods are hard to see.  I haven't come across that (well,
I remember trying to decide whether an impact printer had a comma or a period -
and whether the period was in column 72 or 73 - but now we can use END-IF and it
is no longer a worry), but in an environment that uses END commands and excludes
NEXT-SENTENCE, I don't see that this makes a significant difference.   If you
dangle an END-IF after a period, your compiler is sure to notice.  If you are
missing an END-IF, your program will be more likely to run correctly with a
period than without.   Whether you leave a period after a stand alone statement
or not doesn't effect the program.
```

```
    My thoughts exactly.  I can understand why many people dislike go to's,
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods, Scope delimiters

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2002-12-30T14:26:04-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E10C7FC.E9345A91@attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<aupuue$vkp$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <auq8i9$sb$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <S72Q9.363493$pN3.41666@sccrnsc03>`

```
It looks to me as if you recognize a reason NOT to use periods, but want to keep
using them anyway.  I'm part of the "don't code 'em if you don't need 'em" camp,
and since you never need 'em except at the end of a procedure (paragraph or
section), I recommend not using periods anywhere except there.

I seriously doubt if there is a COBOL programmer anywhere who hasn't seen some
of the following:
1.  A period that caused an logic error.
2.  A missing period that caused a logic error.
3.  A set of imperative statements which all ended with periods that had to be
made subordinate to a conditional (IF, EVALUATE, PERFORM ... UNTIL, etc.)
statement, meaning that all of the periods had to be removed.
...and I'm sure there are more.

The standards writers gave us a way out of this set of potential messes by
providing the scope terminators.  In addition, they gave us more freedom to code
what the program requires, without artifical solutions such as a PERFORM that
moves code out of line, or writing the same statement twice, etc.

Not to take full advantage of the improved language is just like throwing small
change on the ground, because it is too much trouble to carry it around.
Eventually, you've cost yourself some real money.

Russell Styles wrote:

> "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
> news:auq8i9$sb$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
…[54 more quoted lines elided]…
> the venue, it would need to be a monospace font as well.
```

#### ↳ Re: Periods, Scope delimiters (with or without IDMS)

- **From:** Edward Reid <edwardreid@spamcop.net>
- **Date:** 2002-12-31T14:44:55-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.BA375DE7044B31BF1849E120@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<aupuue$vkp$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
On Mon, 30 Dec 2002 12:14:52 -0500, William M. Klein wrote
> 4) There are STILL many programmers who *think* that a "Next Sentence" *is*
> the same thing as a "continue" with an End-IF (or End-Search). 

And compilers that think so as well ... I only learned a couple of days 
ago (from identical discussions on this same topic in this same 
newsgroup several years ago ;-) that NEXT SENTENCE is *not* the same as 
"next statement". I had missed this in reading the documents. Since 
it's an extension, the compiler is free to do what ever it damned well 
pleases with it (and with the entire program that contains it, for that 
matter). Make it mean what it says, and programmers have a real hard 
time with it. Make it do something more reasonable, and it doesn't mean 
what it says.

A posting here by Randall Bart several years ago mentioned that in fact 
it is implemented both ways, and I'm familiar with the Unisys NX 
COBOL85 compiler, which implements NEXT SENTENCE to mean CONTINUE. (I 
have filed a trouble report over the fact that it does not warn me 
about the extension.)

Given the choice, I would simply eliminate NEXT SENTENCE. When used 
reasonably, CONTINUE means the same thing. If you convert COBOL74 to 
COBOL85, add scope terminators for all conditionals, and drop periods, 
then you also must change NEXT SENTENCE to CONTINUE for the code to be 
portable. The skip-to-next-period option is, to me, a recipe for 
hard-to-read code.

> 5) Periods in the middle of paragraphs (or sections - if that is your
> preference) *do* change logic flow of Next Sentence phrases.

More important to me is that I simply don't need periods except for the 
degenerate cases. (If COBOL gave us a different way to delimit 
procedure-names, then we could toss the periods entirely.) I *want* to 
use a scope terminator every time I have a conditional. I *want* to 
keep the indentation in sync with the syntax. I *want* to have both of 
these checked automatically -- I would be delighted if the compiler I'm 
using had an option to generate a syntax error for missing scope 
delimiters for conditionals and for incorrect indentation.

On Mon, 30 Dec 2002 14:58:51 -0500, Howard Brazee wrote
> You also think that periods are hard to see.  I haven't come across 
> that (well, I remember trying to decide whether an impact printer 
> had a comma or a period - and whether the period was in column 72 or 
> 73 - 

I remember a case many years ago of a virtually invisible period. We 
were printing listings on a Xerox 9700, an early, fast (2 page/sec) 
300dpi laser printer. We were printing shaded bars to simulate 
green-box paper. One programmer was beating his head over a strange 
syntax error (this wasn't even COBOL). I looked at his listing and 
could not see the problem. Later I called it up on the screen and saw 
the little gnat that had landed in a far-away column somehow. He hadn't 
seen it because he "knew" there was no text out there. Even when I 
looked back at the listing, I had a hard time finding the period in the 
shaded area.

Very soon afterward, we eliminated the shading in favor of simple 
lines.

A period is one to two orders of magnitude smaller than the average 
COBOL word. There *is* a cognitive difficulty in seeing it. Even if you 
manage to see it, depending on it increases your level of effort, 
effort which could better be spent solving the problem you are writing 
the program to solve.

> but now we can use END-IF and it is no longer a worry), but in an 
> environment that uses END commands and excludes NEXT-SENTENCE, I 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> correctly with a period than without.   

As stated, I certainly disagree -- a missing END-IF anywhere except 
immediately before the period will almost certainly cause the program 
to run incorrectly. No matter what your style (including 
one-period-per-paragraph), a missing END-IF will generally cause a bug. 
This is why I find it so important to have an automatic checker.

However, if you are in a position to follow my suggestions of forcing a 
syntax error for missing scope terminators and improper indentation, 
and you exclude NEXT SENTENCE as you say, then I agree that the period 
does no harm.

> Whether you leave a period after a stand alone statement or not 
> doesn't effect the program. 

But it does affect the programmer. It creates extra effort expended on 
trivia. (OK, like this entire thread?) Programmers are important. 
Language standards not only standardize the language and the compilers, 
they also standardize the skills needed by the programmers. We should 
leverage the power of standardized skills by eliminating what does not 
help and yet causes extra effort.

Eyesight varies. Mine requires more effort from me than it did 35 years 
ago, when I wrote my first program. Consider not only your own eyesight 
and perception, but how those vary across the entire pool of 
programmers. Perceptual studies from psychology are helpful here.

On Mon, 30 Dec 2002 15:37:06 -0500, Russell Styles wrote
> The fact that a period can be hard to see is really a font problem.

It's more than a font problem. Even if you make the period into a 
bullet, it's still a lot smaller than the COBOL word that opened the 
scope. You cannot fix this asymmetry with a font adjustment.

On Mon, 30 Dec 2002 17:26:04 -0500, Colin Campbell wrote
> I seriously doubt if there is a COBOL programmer anywhere who hasn't 
> seen some of the following: 

I've taught a few classes, and given a few talks, on the topic "COBOL85 
update for COBOL74 programmers". Each time -- in introducing scope 
terminators -- I ask the audience, "which of you are experienced COBOL 
programmers and have never encountered a bug due to a missing or 
extraneous period?" Not a single hand has ever gone up. I don't expect 
I'll see one as long as there are programmers who remember COBOL74.

Edward Reid
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Periods, Scope delimiters (with or without IDMS)

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-12-31T20:07:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<austek$i23$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<aupuue$vkp$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <01HW.BA375DE7044B31BF1849E120@news-east.usenetserver.com>`

```

> And compilers that think so as well ... I only learned a couple of days
> ago (from identical discussions on this same topic in this same
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> what it says.

It is only an extension when used in conjunction with an END-IF.   Without an
END-IF, NEXT SENTENCE has a specific meaning which says go to the next period.
I don't like changing its meaning when used with an END-IF.


> Given the choice, I would simply eliminate NEXT SENTENCE.

Amen.


>  If you convert COBOL74 to
> COBOL85, add scope terminators for all conditionals, and drop periods,
> then you also must change NEXT SENTENCE to CONTINUE for the code to be
> portable.

Agree with everything except I still don't see a significant advantage in
dropping periods.  Do everything else, and they don't matter.

> The skip-to-next-period option is, to me, a recipe for hard-to-read code.

Agreed.


>  I *want* to
> use a scope terminator every time I have a conditional. I *want* to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> delimiters for conditionals and for incorrect indentation.

Me too.  Periods sometimes allow the compiler to notice when you have screwed up
the number of terminators.


> A period is one to two orders of magnitude smaller than the average
> COBOL word. There *is* a cognitive difficulty in seeing it. Even if you
> manage to see it, depending on it increases your level of effort,
> effort which could better be spent solving the problem you are writing
> the program to solve.

I don't have trouble.  But the problem you described goes away if you use
terminators and eschew NEXT SENTENCE.


> I've taught a few classes, and given a few talks, on the topic "COBOL85
> update for COBOL74 programmers". Each time -- in introducing scope
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> I'll see one as long as there are programmers who remember COBOL74.

Periods caused problems when they were the only terminators for conditions.  
Ask the audience whether those conditions were with condition terminators.   I
bet none of them were.

The "solution" of eliminating the periods misses the point.   The true solution
is to add END terminators.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods, Scope delimiters (with or without IDMS)

- **From:** Robert Graham <rgraham2@nycap.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-12-31T23:46:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E122EA7.6000009@nycap.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<aupuue$vkp$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <01HW.BA375DE7044B31BF1849E120@news-east.usenetserver.com> <austek$i23$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
<snip>

> 
>>Given the choice, I would simply eliminate NEXT SENTENCE.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I agree.  I've run into problems when maintaining or fixing 
bugs in programs that someone "updated" to use new features 
like in-line performs which (now) contained "in-line" if's. 
  The if's were formerly a statement ending in period where 
NEST SENTENCE was used, and they didn't change it to 
CONTINUE.  Nothing like someone not knowing the full changes 
to the language before trying to implement them. (When all 
else fails, read the directions?)  :)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods, Scope delimiters (with or without IDMS)

- **From:** Edward Reid <edwardreid@spamcop.net>
- **Date:** 2002-12-31T21:18:44-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.BA37BA340460E3AF1849E120@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<aupuue$vkp$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <01HW.BA375DE7044B31BF1849E120@news-east.usenetserver.com> <austek$i23$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
On Tue, 31 Dec 2002 15:07:33 -0500, Howard Brazee wrote
> It is only an extension when used in conjunction with an END-IF.   
> Without an END-IF, NEXT SENTENCE has a specific meaning which says 
> go to the next period. I don't like changing its meaning when used 
> with an END-IF. 

In COBOL74 it may have been defined as "go to the next period", but in 
COBOL74 there was no distinction between that and "go to the end of the 
outermost IF" -- there was no way to do one without the other. 
Furthermore, it was impossible to go to the end of the current IF 
without also going to the end of the outermost IF.

And the average COBOL programmer doesn't know the difference between a 
statement and a sentence, because most of the time it either doesn't 
matter or else nobody they know uses "sentence" in its technical 
meaning.

As a result, most programmers actually thought of NEXT SENTENCE as 
meaning "go to the end of the IF". The standard is important, but the 
standard exists to support the programmers. In COBOL74, there was no 
conflict between what the standard said it meant and what people 
thought it meant. In COBOL85 with extensions to allow NEXT SENTENCE 
with END-IF, there's a serious conflict.

So no matter how the combination is implemented, in practice it's 
changing the meaning for a lot of programmers.

I would much prefer that the compiler I use did not allow the 
combination (as in fact it does not allow NEXT SENTENCE with 
END-SEARCH).

Edward
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Periods, Scope delimiters (with or without IDMS)

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2002-12-31T22:19:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c2b11a$8abcf2e0$418bf243@chottel>`
- **References:** `<aupuue$vkp$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <01HW.BA375DE7044B31BF1849E120@news-east.usenetserver.com>`

```
Long ago and far away I remember working of a CDC6400 computer whose COBOL
had "EXIT PROGRAM." as a statement. I.E. the period was considered as part
of the statment even if you did not type it!  We had a READ subroutine
something like:

READ filename 
    AT END
        MOVE 'y' TO DONE-SW.
IF DONE-SW = 'y'
    EXIT PROGRAM
ELSE
    other statements.

Took a while to finally figure out that the  period was consideed there
even though not visible.

<01HW.BA375DE7044B31BF1849E120@news-east.usenetserver.com>...

> > 4) There are STILL many programmers who *think* that a "Next Sentence"
*is*
> > the same thing as a "continue" with an End-IF (or End-Search). 
> 
…[115 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
> It's more than a font problem. Even if you make the period into a

My favourite editor, TSE, does syntax highlighting and displays full

Printing ? No I don't do that.  If I did then I may consider putting a
```

### How do I read and write bits?

- **From:** vibroplex@mindspring.com (Derek Cohn/WB0TUA)
- **Date:** 2002-12-30T13:52:35-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d7d0c297.0212301352.43889aaa@posting.google.com>`

```
Dear All,

This is my first post to this forum so please forgive me if I
misstep...

I am a COBOL programmer working on ALPHA platform running VMS.  I need
to read a data file and examine the various bits in a field such as a
signed word or longword.  I have looked in my COBOL manuals and can't
find a mechanism for reading and setting the value of a bit in a
field.  I'll need to loop over all bits in these fields and handle
them accordingly.

Can anyone point me to a mechanism that reads the individual bits in a
field and then sets them appropriately?

Please reply to the newsgroup if possible (not to my e-mail address)
so I can view posts at both my work and home.

Thank you for any help you can give me,

Derek
```

#### ↳ Re: How do I read and write bits?

- **From:** "Richard Maher" <maher_rj@hotspamnotmail.com>

```
Hi,

What you want to use is mth$jiand your_mask, integer giving result. if
result not = zeros . . .

The AND, OR and XOR routines are documented in the undocumented section of
the mth run-time library manual.

As someone else suggested, there is always subtract 2**n from integer giving
result. If result not less zero. . .

and dont forget hexidecimal literals x"ff" when setting protection masks for
a mailbox etc

Regards Richard Maher

PS. I'll try and post an example and don't forget comp.os.vms


Derek Cohn/WB0TUA <vibroplex@mindspring.com> wrote in message
news:d7d0c297.0212301352.43889aaa@posting.google.com...
> Dear All,
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> Derek
```

```
Hi,

This will probably wrap really badly but FWIW look for mth$jiand
```
